topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Processes and Flowcharts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a "Process"?

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Process** is a structured, ordered, and time-bound sequence of interconnected activities, tasks, or sub-processes that consume defined inputs (data, materials, information) and transform them into valuable outputs (results, decisions, services) to achieve a specific business or operational objective. In the context of **Robotic Process Automation (RPA)**, a process refers to a *rule-based, repetitive, deterministic, and high-volume* business procedure that can be decomposed into discrete, executable steps suitable for delegation to a software bot.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> A *process* in RPA is the *As-Is* (current) or *To-Be* (future) workflow that the developer must fully understand, document, and model before any automation script is written. Without a properly defined process, an RPA bot cannot be configured, debugged, or maintained.

### Key Characteristics of an RPA-Suitable Process
1. **Rule-Based**: Decisions follow clear "if-then-else" logic with no human judgment calls.
2. **Repetitive**: Executed frequently (daily, hourly, or many times per day).
3. **High-Volume**: Involves large quantities of transactions or records.
4. **Stable / Low Exception Rate**: The inputs, applications, and rules rarely change.
5. **Digital Inputs**: Triggers and data originate from digital systems (emails, ERP, databases, portals).
6. **Time-Critical & Mundane**: Often boring for humans, hence ideal for bots.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process vs. Procedure vs. Task — A Critical Distinction**
> - **Process**: The *complete end-to-end* workflow (e.g., "Process Vendor Invoice").
> - **Procedure**: A *specific organizational* method or policy governing how the process must be done.
> - **Task**: An *individual* action within a process (e.g., "Open the email", "Read the PDF amount").

## 1.2 What is a Flowchart?

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Flowchart** (also called a *process map* or *workflow diagram*) is a standardized, symbolic, and graphical representation of a process, depicting the sequence of operations, decision points, inputs, outputs, and the directional flow of control using a defined set of geometric shapes connected by arrows. According to **ISO 5807:1985** and **ANSI/ISO standards**, every flowchart symbol carries a fixed, universally accepted meaning.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Definition Expectation:**
> When asked to "define a flowchart" in a 3-mark question, write: *"A flowchart is a diagrammatic representation of an algorithm, process, or workflow that uses standardized geometric symbols connected by arrows to depict the sequence, decisions, and flow of control."*

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

### 🏠 Real-World Analogy: A Cooking Recipe
Imagine you want to teach a friend (who has **never seen your kitchen**) how to make a cup of tea:

- **Process** = The entire *tea-making routine* (from fetching water to serving the cup).
- **Flowchart** = A *hand-drawn map* you give your friend that says:
  - "Start here ➔ Fill kettle ➔ If water is not enough, refill ➔ Boil ➔ Add tea powder ➔ Add sugar ➔ Wait 3 minutes ➔ Strain ➔ Serve in cup ➔ End."

The flowchart is essentially a **GPS route for the process**. Just as a GPS shows turn-by-turn directions using a universal language of arrows and signs, a flowchart shows step-by-step business actions using a universal language of shapes and arrows.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this important for RPA?**
> Software robots (bots) do not "think". They are like **strict literal-minded interns** who follow instructions **exactly as written**. A flowchart provides that exact written instruction in a visual, testable, and modifiable form *before* the bot is coded.

## 1.4 Why Flowcharts are the Backbone of RPA

| # | Reason | Explanation |
|---|--------|-------------|
| 1 | **Visual Clarity** | Humans process images 60,000× faster than text. |
| 2 | **Standardization** | Universal symbols (ISO 5807) ensure team-wide understanding. |
| 3 | **Pre-Coding Blueprint** | Avoids costly re-work by validating logic *before* development. |
| 4 | **Debugging Aid** | Helps trace where a bot failed by matching the failed step to a flowchart node. |
| 5 | **Documentation** | Serves as legal/audit documentation for compliance. |
| 6 | **Communication** | Bridges the gap between business analysts and RPA developers. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandate:**
> In your board examination, you may be asked to *"draw a flowchart for a given process"*. Always use a **pencil, ruler, and proper geometry** in the exam. Sloppy diagrams will cost you marks.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Flowchart — The Building Blocks

A flowchart is constructed from a small, fixed set of geometric primitives. Each primitive has a **distinct meaning** and is **never used interchangeably**.

### 2.1.1 The Standard ISO 5807 Symbol Set

| # | Symbol Shape | Name | Purpose / Meaning | Example |
|---|--------------|------|------------------|---------|
| 1 | **Oval / Pill (Rounded Rectangle)** | **Terminator** | Marks the **Start** or **End** of the process. | `START`, `END` |
| 2 | **Rectangle** | **Process / Activity** | Represents an *action, operation, or transformation*. | `Add 5 to X`, `Send Email` |
| 3 | **Diamond** | **Decision** | Represents a *condition* with **Yes/No** or **True/False** branches. | `Is Age >= 18?` |
| 4 | **Parallelogram** | **Input / Output (Data)** | Represents data entry or data display. | `Read Salary`, `Print Report` |
| 5 | **Cylinder** (sometimes drawn) | **Database / Storage** | Represents data stored in a system. | `Fetch from SQL DB` |
| 6 | **Document Symbol** (rectangle with wavy bottom) | **Document** | A paper document produced or consumed. | `Generate Invoice PDF` |
| 7 | **Small Circle** | **Connector (On-Page)** | Connects two parts of flowchart on the *same page*. | `A → B via circle 1` |
| 8 | **Pentagon / House-shape** | **Off-Page Connector** | Connects flowchart parts on *different pages*. | `(Page 2)` |
| 9 | **Arrow / Line with arrowhead** | **Flow Line** | Shows the **direction of control flow**. | `→` |
| 10 | **Trapezoid** | **Manual Operation** | A step done *manually* by a human. | `Manager Approval` |
| 11 | **Hexagon** | **Preparation** | Set-up steps (e.g., initializing a counter). | `Set i = 0` |
| 12 | **Double-lined rectangle** | **Predefined Process** | A call to a *sub-process* or subroutine. | `Call ValidateUser()` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic to Remember Core Symbols:**
> **"Oval-Rectangle-Diamond-Parallelogram-Arrow"** → **O-R-D-P-A**
> *Oval (Start/End), Rectangle (Process), Diamond (Decision), Parallelogram (I/O), Arrow (Flow).*

## 2.2 Types of Flowcharts Used in RPA

The KTU syllabus specifically expects knowledge of the following flowchart variants:

### 2.2.1 System Flowchart vs. Program Flowchart
- **System Flowchart**: Depicts the *interaction between different systems* (hardware, software, databases, network). Used by business analysts.
- **Program Flowchart**: Depicts the *internal logic* of a single program or automation script. Used by RPA developers.

### 2.2.2 High-Level Process Map (Top-Down Flowchart)
- Shows only **major process steps** (typically 5–10 boxes).
- Used for *executive presentations* and *process selection* in the **RPA Opportunity Assessment** phase.

### 2.2.3 Detailed / Workflow Flowchart
- Shows **every single task, decision, and exception path**.
- Used for actual bot development in tools like **UiPath Studio, Automation Anywhere, Blue Prism**.

### 2.2.4 Swimlane Flowchart
- Divides the process into **vertical or horizontal lanes** representing *actors* (Customer, Bot, HR System, Database).
- Crucial in RPA to define **human-bot handoff** points.

### 2.2.5 Data Flow Diagram (DFD)
- Focuses on the **movement of data** between processes, entities, and storage.
- Context Level 0, Level 1, Level 2 DFDs are common in process discovery.

## 2.3 Structural Rules of Flowchart Design (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **These rules are frequently asked as 3-mark short-answer questions. Memorize them.**

1. **One Start, One End Rule**: Every flowchart has exactly **one Start** terminator and **at least one End** terminator.
2. **Flow Direction Rule**: Default flow is **top-to-bottom** and **left-to-right**. Arrows are mandatory for non-default directions.
3. **No Crossed Lines Rule**: Flow lines should not cross. Use **connectors** if they must.
4. **Decision Branches Rule**: A **diamond** must have **exactly two** outgoing arrows (for binary decisions), labeled **Yes/No** or **True/False**.
5. **Single Entry, Single Exit Rule** (for sub-processes): Each process box has one entry arrow and one exit arrow.
6. **Labelling Rule**: Every shape must have a *clear, verb-based label* (e.g., "Send Email" not "Email").
7. **Page Boundary Rule**: Off-page connectors must be used when the flowchart extends beyond one page.
8. **Loop Rule**: A loop must be **explicitly visible** with a clear return arrow back to a decision.

## 2.4 Process Discovery & Documentation Lifecycle in RPA

The flowchart is produced during a structured **Process Discovery** phase:

| Stage | Activity | Output |
|-------|----------|--------|
| **1. Identify Candidate Process** | Brainstorm processes with business users. | List of candidate processes. |
| **2. Define Scope** | Select *one* process to automate. | **Process Definition Document (PDD)**. |
| **3. Capture As-Is** | Interview SMEs, observe, record screen. | **As-Is Flowchart** (current state). |
| **4. Design To-Be** | Add automation logic, exception handling. | **To-Be Flowchart** (future state). |
| **5. Validate with SME** | Walk through flowchart with process owner. | **Signed-off flowchart**. |
| **6. Develop Bot** | Convert flowchart to RPA tool activities. | **Working Bot**. |
| **7. Test & Deploy** | Unit test, UAT, production release. | **Live automation**. |

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Logic Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> While flowcharts are not "mathematical" in the traditional sense, the following **logic structures** are formal control-flow patterns that can be expressed as equations of flow.

| Structure Name | Pseudocode Form | Flowchart Pattern |
|----------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Sequence** | $S_1 \rightarrow S_2 \rightarrow S_3$ | Rectangle → Rectangle → Rectangle |
| **Selection (If-Then)** | $\text{IF } C \text{ THEN } S$ | Diamond (C) → True branch → Rectangle (S) → Merge |
| **Selection (If-Then-Else)** | $\text{IF } C \text{ THEN } S_1 \text{ ELSE } S_2$ | Diamond (C) → True: $S_1$, False: $S_2$ → Merge |
| **Multi-Way Switch (Case)** | $\text{SWITCH}(X): \text{case } 1, 2, \ldots, n$ | Diamond chain or stacked diamonds |
| **Pre-Test Loop (While)** | $\text{WHILE } C \text{ DO } S$ | Decision (C) → True: $S$ → loop back to C |
| **Post-Test Loop (Do-While)** | $\text{DO } S \text{ WHILE } C$ | $S$ → Decision (C) → True: loop back to $S$ |
| **For Loop** | $\text{FOR } i=1 \text{ TO } n$ | Initialize → Decision (i≤n) → $S$ → Increment → loop |
| **Nested Loop** | For inside For | Inner loop drawn *inside* outer loop's rectangle |
| **Sub-Process Call** | $\text{CALL } \text{Sub}(x)$ | Double-bordered rectangle (Predefined Process) |
| **Parallel Fork** | $\text{FORK}(S_1 \vert\vert S_2)$ | Synchronization bar (thick horizontal line) |
| **Parallel Join** | $\text{JOIN}(S_1, S_2)$ | Synchronization bar (thick horizontal line) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Safety:**
> For absolute values in flow logic (e.g., magnitude of difference), use `\vert x \vert` rather than `\vert x \vert` to avoid markdown table breakage.

## 2.6 Process Metrics in RPA (Engineering Utility)

When documenting a process, the following **metrics** are measured and validated against thresholds:

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Typical Acceptance Value |
|--------|--------|------|--------------------------|
| **Process Cycle Time** | $T_{cycle}$ | seconds / minutes | $\le 60$ s for transactional processes |
| **Process Volume** | $V$ | transactions / day | $\ge 100$ / day |
| **Human Effort (FTEs)** | $F$ | full-time equivalents | $F \ge 1$ FTE saved |
| **Error Rate** | $E$ | percent | $E < 5\%$ (candidates for RPA) |
| **ROI** | $\text{ROI}$ | percent | $\text{ROI} = \frac{(\text{Benefit} - \text{Cost})}{\text{Cost}} \times 100$ |
| **Payback Period** | $P$ | months | $P \le 12$ months |

These are *real engineering numbers* used during the **Process Selection** stage of an RPA project.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: The Decision-Point Logic Derivation

A flowchart's decision diamond is essentially a **Boolean function** evaluated at runtime. Below is the formal, exhaustive logical derivation of the most common decision structures used in RPA.

### 3.1.1 The Simple IF-THEN Structure

**Logical Definition:**
Let $C$ be a Boolean condition, and $S$ be a single process action.

$$
\text{IF } (C = \text{TRUE}) \text{ THEN execute } S \text{ ELSE skip } S
$$

In Boolean algebra:

$$
\text{Execution of } S = C \cdot 1 + \overline{C} \cdot 0 = C
$$

This means the action $S$ is executed **if and only if** $C$ evaluates to **TRUE**.

**Flowchart Form:**

```
        +-------+
        | START |
        +-------+
             |
             v
        +-------------+
        | Action S0   |
        +-------------+
             |
             v
       < Diamond C? >  --[False]--+
             |                   |
           [True]                |
             v                   |
        +-------------+          |
        |  Action S   |          |
        +-------------+          |
             |                   |
             +---------+---------+
                       |
                       v
                  +-------+
                  |  END  |
                  +-------+
```

**KTU Examiner Note:** Note how the **False** branch *bypasses* the action and **merges** with the main flow. This is the **only correct** way to draw an IF-THEN in a flowchart.

---

### 3.1.2 The IF-THEN-ELSE Structure

**Logical Definition:**

$$
\text{Output} = (C \cdot S_1) + (\overline{C} \cdot S_2)
$$

This is a **logical disjunction** of two mutually exclusive branches.

**Truth Table Derivation:**

| $C$ | $\overline{C}$ | $S_1$ executes? | $S_2$ executes? |
|:---:|:--------------:|:---------------:|:---------------:|
| T   | F              | ✓               | ✗               |
| F   | T              | ✗               | ✓               |

This is the **exhaustive truth table** for IF-THEN-ELSE. There is **no third case**.

**Flowchart Form:**

```
           +-------+
           | START |
           +-------+
                |
                v
          <  C ?  >
          /        \
       [T]          [F]
        v            v
   +---------+   +---------+
   |   S1    |   |   S2    |
   +---------+   +---------+
        \            /
         \          /
          v        v
          +-------+
          |  END  |
          +-------+
```

---

### 3.1.3 The WHILE Loop Structure (Pre-Test)

**Logical Definition:**

$$
\text{Repeat } S \text{ as long as } C = \text{TRUE}
$$

Formally, the number of iterations $n$ is:

$$
n = \min \{ k \in \mathbb{N}_0 \mid C(\text{after } k \text{ iterations}) = \text{FALSE} \}
$$

The total execution time of the loop is:

$$
T_{loop} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( T_{check}(C) + T_{exec}(S) \right)
$$

where:
- $T_{check}(C)$ = time to evaluate condition $C$ (typically **< 1 ms** for Boolean checks).
- $T_{exec}(S)$ = time to execute action $S$ (varies, e.g., **200 ms** for keystrokes, **2 s** for HTTP calls).

**Flowchart Form:**

```
           +-------+
           | START |
           +-------+
                |
                v
          <  C ?  >------[False]------+
             |                        |
           [True]                     |
             v                        |
        +---------+                   |
        |    S    |                   |
        +---------+                   |
             |                        |
             +------>[loop back]------+
                                      |
                                      v
                                 +-------+
                                 |  END  |
                                 +-------+
```

**KTU Pitfall:** The loop back arrow must return to the **decision diamond**, not the start terminator. Returning to START creates an **infinite loop** that is a runtime error.

---

### 3.1.4 The DO-WHILE Loop Structure (Post-Test)

**Logical Definition:**

$$
\text{Execute } S \text{ once, then repeat while } C = \text{TRUE}
$$

Unlike WHILE, the action $S$ is executed **at least once**:

$$
n \ge 1
$$

**Flowchart Form:**

```
           +-------+
           | START |
           +-------+
                |
                v
        +---------+
        |    S    |<-------+
        +---------+        |
             |              |
             v              |
          <  C ?  >--[True]-+
             |
          [False]
             |
             v
          +-------+
          |  END  |
          +-------+
```

**Key Difference:**

| Feature | WHILE | DO-WHILE |
|---------|-------|----------|
| **Test Position** | Before body | After body |
| **Min Iterations** | $n = 0$ (may skip entirely) | $n \ge 1$ (executes at least once) |
| **Use Case in RPA** | "While emails exist, process them" | "Process one email, then check if more exist" |

---

### 3.1.5 The FOR Loop (Count-Controlled)

**Logical Definition:**

$$
\text{FOR } i = i_{start} \text{ TO } i_{end} \text{ STEP } \Delta i \text{ DO } S
$$

The total number of iterations is:

$$
n = \left\lfloor \frac{i_{end} - i_{start}}{\Delta i} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

**Flowchart Form:**

```
        +-----------------+
        |  Initialize i   |
        +-----------------+
                 |
                 v
           < i <= i_end? >--[False]--+
              |                       |
            [True]                    |
              v                       |
        +-----------+                 |
        |     S     |                 |
        +-----------+                 |
              |                       |
              v                       |
        +-----------------+           |
        |  i = i + step   |           |
        +-----------------+           |
              |                       |
              +----[loop back]--------+
                                      |
                                      v
                                 +-------+
                                 |  END  |
                                 +-------+
```

---

### 3.1.6 The CASE (Multi-Way Branch) Structure

**Logical Definition:**

$$
\text{Output} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} \left( X = v_i \right) \cdot S_i
$$

where $X$ is a selector variable, $v_i$ are discrete values, and $S_i$ are the corresponding actions. This is a **mutually exclusive** selection across $n$ cases.

**Flowchart Form (Linear Chain):**

```
            +-------+
            | START |
            +-------+
                 |
                 v
          < X = v1? >--[False]--+
             |                    |
           [True]                 v
             |              < X = v2? >--[False]--> ... (etc.)
             v                    |
        +---------+           [True]
        |   S1    |              |
        +---------+              v
             |             +---------+
             |             |   S2    |
             |             +---------+
             |                  |
             +--------+---------+
                      |
                      v
                  +-------+
                  |  END  |
                  +-------+
```

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Number of Process Paths

A flowchart with $d$ decision points, each having 2 outcomes, has a **maximum** of:

$$
P_{max} = 2^{d}
$$

distinct paths from Start to End.

### Worked Example
A flowchart has **3 decision points** (e.g., "Is user valid?", "Is balance sufficient?", "Is amount > 0?").

$$
P_{max} = 2^{3} = 8 \text{ paths}
$$

This means the RPA bot must be **tested on at least 8 distinct scenarios** to achieve full path coverage — a concept known as **Branch Coverage** in software testing.

## 3.3 Derivation: Cyclomatic Complexity (McCabe's Metric)

The **Cyclomatic Complexity** $M$ of a flowchart is given by:

$$
M = E - N + 2P
$$

where:
- $E$ = number of edges (flow lines + arrows)
- $N$ = number of nodes (shapes)
- $P$ = number of connected components (typically $P = 1$ for a single flowchart)

Or equivalently:

$$
M = \pi + 1
$$

where $\pi$ is the number of decision points (diamonds) plus 1.

### Interpretation Table

| $M$ value | Risk | RPA Testability |
|-----------|------|-----------------|
| $M = 1$ to $10$ | Low risk, simple | Easy to automate |
| $M = 11$ to $20$ | Moderate risk | Refactor recommended |
| $M = 21$ to $50$ | High risk | Break into sub-processes |
| $M > 50$ | Untestable | **Not suitable for RPA** |

**KTU Note:** This is a high-yield formula. Questions like *"Calculate the cyclomatic complexity of a flowchart with 8 decision diamonds"* are common.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flowchart: The RPA Process Development Lifecycle (PDLC)

The following Mermaid diagram maps how **Processes** and **Flowcharts** interact across the entire RPA project lifecycle. This is the most important diagram for the KTU exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([STEP1: Process Identification]) --> B[STEP2: Feasibility Check]
    B --> C[STEP3: As-Is Process Documentation]
    C --> D[STEP4: To-Be Flowchart Design]
    D --> E[STEP5: SME Validation and Sign-off]
    E --> F[STEP6: Convert Flowchart to Bot Logic]
    F --> G[STEP7: Unit Testing of Each Path]
    G --> H[STEP8: User Acceptance Testing UAT]
    H --> I{All Tests Pass?}
    I -- Yes --> J([STEP9: Production Deployment])
    I -- No --> K[STEP10: Refine Flowchart]
    K --> D
    J --> L([STEP11: Continuous Monitoring and Support])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for the Diagram:**
> - The **rounded rectangles with `( )`** represent *terminators* (Start/End of a major phase).
> - The **sharp rectangles** are *process activities* (e.g., documentation, testing).
> - The **diamond** at `I` is a *decision point* (Test Pass / Fail).
> - The arrow from `K` back to `D` creates a **refinement loop**, ensuring the flowchart evolves based on testing feedback.

## 4.2 Detailed Flowchart: ATM Cash Withdrawal Process (As-Is)

This is a complete, exam-ready flowchart for the classic **ATM Cash Withdrawal** process. Use this as a template for any *"draw a flowchart"* question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S([START]) --> A[Insert ATM Card into Machine]
    A --> B[Read Card Data via Magnetic Strip]
    B --> C[Prompt: Enter 4-Digit PIN]
    C --> D{Is PIN Correct?}
    D -- No --> E[Increment Invalid PIN Counter]
    E --> F{Counter > 3?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Retain Card and Display Error]
    G --> H([END Blocked])
    F -- No --> C
    D -- Yes --> I[Display Account Type Menu]
    I --> J[User Selects Withdrawal]
    J --> K[Prompt: Enter Amount]
    K --> L{Is Amount within Daily Limit?}
    L -- No --> M[Display: Limit Exceeded Message]
    M --> N[Ask: Another Transaction?]
    N -- Yes --> I
    N -- No --> O([END])
    L -- Yes --> P{Is Balance Sufficient?}
    P -- No --> Q[Display: Insufficient Funds]
    Q --> N
    P -- Yes --> R[Deduct Amount from Account]
    R --> S2[Dispense Cash from Machine]
    S2 --> T[Print Transaction Receipt]
    T --> Eject[Eject Card]
    Eject --> U[Return Card to Customer]
    U --> N
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbol Legend in this Mermaid Diagram:**
> - `([Text])` = Oval (Start/End terminator).
> - `[Text]` = Rectangle (Process / Action).
> - `{Text}` = Diamond (Decision).
> - `-- Label -->` = Labelled flow line (e.g., Yes/No branches).

## 4.3 Swimlane Diagram: Human vs. Bot in Invoice Processing

This is the **swimlane** variant — a key requirement in RPA for defining **human-bot handoff** points.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HUMAN["HUMAN LANE"]
        H1[Receive Invoice via Email] --> H2[Verify Physical Signature on PDF]
        H2 --> H3[Forward to RPA Inbox]
    end

    subgraph BOT["RPA BOT LANE"]
        B1[Read Invoice from Inbox] --> B2[Extract Invoice Number and Amount via OCR]
        B2 --> B3{Duplicate Invoice Found?}
        B3 -- Yes --> B4[Flag for Human Review]
        B3 -- No --> B5[Enter Data into ERP System]
        B5 --> B6[Validate Total against PO]
        B6 --> B7{Total Mismatch?}
        B7 -- Yes --> B8[Email Alert to Finance Team]
        B7 -- No --> B9[Mark Invoice as Processed]
    end

    H3 --> B1
    B4 --> H4{Reviewer Approves?}
    B4 -.-> H5[Human Reviews Duplicate]
    H5 --> H4
    H4 -- Yes --> B5
    H4 -- No --> B9
    B8 --> H6[Finance Resolves Mismatch]
    H6 --> B5
    B9 --> DONE([END: Invoice Successfully Processed])
```

**Architectural Notes:**
- The **subgraphs** `HUMAN` and `BOT` represent the two **swimlanes**.
- Dashed lines `-.->` indicate **escalation / handoff** to a human.
- Solid lines `-->` indicate **standard sequential flow** within a lane.
- This is the **gold standard** for documenting an RPA process before development begins.

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Flowchart-to-Bot Conversion Pipeline

This functional architecture shows the relationship between a flowchart (designed by a human) and the actual RPA bot (executed by software).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Business Analyst] -->|Designs| B[Process Definition Document PDD]
    B --> C[As-Is Flowchart]
    B --> D[To-Be Flowchart]
    D --> E[RPA Developer]
    E -->|Translates each box| F[UiPath Workflow File .xaml]
    E -->|Translates each box| G[Automation Anywhere Task File]
    E -->|Translates each box| H[Blue Prism Process File]
    F --> I[RPA Orchestrator]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J([Bot Execution on Production Environment])
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked *"How is a flowchart used in RPA development?"*, present this exact mapping: **Flowchart Box → RPA Activity**, **Diamond → If/Decision Activity**, **Arrow → Sequence Flow Connector**, **Oval (Start) → Trigger Event**, **Oval (End) → Termination Logic**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
**Define a flowchart. List any four standard flowchart symbols with their meanings.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A flowchart is a diagrammatic representation of a process or algorithm that uses standardized geometric symbols connected by arrows to depict the sequence of operations, decision points, and the flow of control.

**Four Standard Flowchart Symbols:**

| Symbol | Name | Function |
|--------|------|----------|
| Oval (Rounded Rectangle) | **Terminator** | Indicates the Start or End of the process. |
| Rectangle | **Process** | Represents an action, operation, or data transformation. |
| Diamond | **Decision** | Represents a conditional check with Yes/No or True/False branches. |
| Parallelogram | **Input / Output** | Represents data entry into or data output from the system. |

The fifth commonly used symbol is the **Arrow (Flow Line)**, which indicates the direction of execution flow.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between an As-Is process flowchart and a To-Be process flowchart in the context of RPA.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Basis of Comparison | As-Is Flowchart | To-Be Flowchart |
|---------------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| **Definition** | Represents the *current* state of the process as performed manually. | Represents the *future* state of the process *after* automation. |
| **Purpose** | Used to *understand* the existing workflow, identify bottlenecks, and document manual steps. | Used to *design* the new automated workflow, including bot tasks and exception handling. |
| **Created By** | Business Analyst / Process Analyst. | RPA Developer / Solution Architect. |
| **Content** | Includes human tasks, manual handoffs, paper documents, and legacy system interactions. | Includes bot activities, exception paths, logging steps, and human escalation only where strictly needed. |
| **Used In** | Process Discovery and Feasibility Analysis phase. | Bot Development and Testing phase. |
| **Key Output** | Identifies automation opportunities and savings (FTEs, time). | Provides the blueprint directly translatable into RPA tool activities. |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Module in the End Semester Examination (ESE) contains one full **14-mark question** with an **internal choice**. The question has **two sub-parts (a) and (b)** typically carrying **7 marks each**. You must answer **either the full Question A or the full Question B**.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Question from Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the standard symbols used in flowcharts as per ISO 5807 standards. Draw the flowchart symbols for Start/End, Process, Decision, Input/Output, and Connector. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Design a detailed flowchart for the following process: *"A banking system processes a cheque. The clerk enters the cheque number and amount. If the cheque is valid and the account has sufficient balance, the amount is debited and a receipt is printed. If the balance is insufficient, an error message is displayed. If the cheque is invalid, the cheque is returned with a rejection note."* *(7 Marks)*

---

### **MODEL ANSWER FOR QUESTION A**

#### **Part (a) — ISO 5807 Flowchart Symbols** *(7 Marks)*

The **ISO 5807:1985** standard defines a uniform set of symbols for flowcharts. The following are the most important symbols used in RPA process design:

**1. Terminator (Oval / Rounded Rectangle)** *(1 Mark)*
- Used to indicate the **Start** and **End** of a flowchart.
- There is exactly **one Start** terminator and **at least one End** terminator.
- Example: `START`, `END`.

**2. Process (Rectangle)** *(1 Mark)*
- Represents any **operation, action, or data transformation**.
- Must contain a **verb-based description** (e.g., "Calculate Total", "Send Email").
- Example: `[Calculate Tax]`.

**3. Decision (Diamond)** *(1 Mark)*
- Represents a **logical condition** that branches the flow into two paths.
- The two branches are typically labeled **Yes/No** or **True/False**.
- Example: `{Is Balance > 0?}`.

**4. Input / Output (Parallelogram)** *(1 Mark)*
- Represents **data entry** (input) or **data display** (output).
- Example: `[Read Employee ID]`, `[Display Result]`.

**5. Connector (Small Circle)** *(1 Mark)*
- Used to **connect two parts** of a flowchart on the **same page** to avoid crossing flow lines.
- Labeled with a letter or number (e.g., `A`, `1`).

**6. Off-Page Connector (Pentagon / House-shape)** *(1 Mark)*
- Connects flowchart segments on **different pages** in large, multi-page flowcharts.
- Used when the process is too large to fit on a single page.

**7. Flow Line (Arrow)** *(1 Mark)*
- Shows the **direction of execution** from one symbol to the next.
- Default direction is top-to-bottom and left-to-right.

**Summary Diagram:**

```
   [Symbol]                  Meaning
   ---------                 -------
   (Start/End)               Oval: Process boundary
   [Process]                 Rectangle: Action
   <Decision?>               Diamond: Condition
   [Input/Output]            Parallelogram: I/O
   (o)                       Circle: On-page connector
   /Page/                    Pentagon: Off-page connector
   -->                       Arrow: Flow direction
```

---

#### **Part (b) — Flowchart for Cheque Processing** *(7 Marks)*

**Step 1: Identify the Inputs and Logic** *(1 Mark)*
- Input: Cheque number, Amount.
- Decision 1: Is the cheque valid?
- Decision 2: Is the balance sufficient?
- Actions: Debit account, Print receipt, Display error, Return cheque.

**Step 2: Design the Flowchart (In Mermaid Format for Clarity, but should be hand-drawn in exam):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S([START]) --> A[Clerk Enters Cheque Number and Amount]
    A --> B{Is Cheque Valid?}
    B -- No --> C[Return Cheque with Rejection Note]
    C --> END1([END Rejected])
    B -- Yes --> D{Check Account Balance}
    D -- Insufficient --> E[Display Error: Insufficient Balance]
    E --> END2([END Failed])
    D -- Sufficient --> F[Debit Amount from Account]
    F --> G[Print Transaction Receipt]
    G --> H[Hand Over Receipt to Customer]
    H --> END3([END Successful])
```

**Step 3: Explanation of Each Block** *(3 Marks)*

| Step | Symbol | Action / Decision |
|------|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | Oval START | Process begins when a cheque is received. |
| 2 | Rectangle | Clerk enters cheque number and amount. |
| 3 | Diamond | System validates the cheque (signature, date, MICR code). |
| 4 | Branch (No) | Invalid cheque → returned with rejection note → END. |
| 5 | Diamond | System checks account balance. |
| 6 | Branch (No balance) | Insufficient funds → error message → END. |
| 7 | Branch (Yes balance) | Amount is debited. |
| 8 | Rectangle | Receipt is generated and printed. |
| 9 | Rectangle | Receipt is handed to the customer. |
| 10 | Oval END | Process terminates successfully. |

**Step 4: Justification of RPA Suitability** *(1 Mark)*
This process is **rule-based** (yes/no decisions), **repetitive** (hundreds of cheques daily), and **digital-friendly** (input is already in a banking system), making it an **ideal candidate for RPA automation**.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Practice Paper]**

**(a)** What is a process in the context of RPA? Explain the characteristics that make a process suitable for automation. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the following flowchart structures with diagrams:
   (i) IF-THEN-ELSE
   (ii) WHILE loop
   (iii) FOR loop
   *(7 Marks)*

---

### **MODEL ANSWER FOR QUESTION B**

#### **Part (a) — Process in RPA** *(7 Marks)*

**Definition** *(2 Marks)*

A **Process** in RPA is a structured, end-to-end sequence of business activities that takes defined inputs, applies a set of business rules, and produces measurable outputs to achieve a specific organizational goal. In RPA, the term specifically refers to a *candidate workflow* being considered for automation by a software bot.

**Characteristics of an RPA-Suitable Process** *(5 Marks)*

1. **Rule-Based and Deterministic** *(1 Mark)* — The process must follow clear, unambiguous "if-then-else" rules with no subjective human judgment. Example: "If invoice total > ₹1,00,000, escalate to manager."

2. **Repetitive and High-Volume** *(1 Mark)* — The process should be executed frequently, typically hundreds or thousands of times per month. Example: Processing 5,000 invoices per day.

3. **Low Exception Rate** *(1 Mark)* — The process should have less than **5% exceptions** under normal conditions. High-exception processes are unsuitable because the bot would spend more time on error handling than on the core task.

4. **Digital and Structured Inputs** *(1 Mark)* — Inputs must originate from digital systems (emails, ERPs, databases, portals) and be in machine-readable formats (Excel, PDF, XML, JSON). OCR may be used for scanned documents.

5. **Stable and Mature** *(1 Mark)* — The underlying applications, business rules, and user interfaces should not change frequently. A process that changes every month will require constant bot reconfiguration, eroding ROI.

> [!TIP]
> **Additional Valid Characteristics** *(not mandatory for full marks)*: Mature (executed for >6 months), well-documented, measurable (has clear KPIs like cycle time and error rate), and has a clear process owner willing to support automation.

---

#### **Part (b) — Flowchart Structures with Examples** *(7 Marks)*

**Example Scenario: A University wants to admit students based on entrance exam marks.**

##### **(i) IF-THEN-ELSE** *(2.5 Marks)*

**Pseudocode:**

$$
\text{IF } (\text{Marks} \ge 60) \text{ THEN Admit ELSE Reject}
$$

**Flowchart:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S([START]) --> A[Read Marks]
    A --> D{Marks >= 60?}
    D -- True --> P1[Print: Admitted]
    D -- False --> P2[Print: Rejected]
    P1 --> E([END])
    P2 --> E
```

**Explanation:** The decision diamond evaluates the condition. The TRUE branch executes the "Admit" path, while the FALSE branch executes the "Reject" path. Both paths merge at the END terminator.

##### **(ii) WHILE Loop** *(2.5 Marks)*

**Scenario:** Print numbers from 1 to N.

**Pseudocode:**

$$
i = 1; \quad \text{WHILE } (i \le N) \text{ DO Print } i; \quad i = i + 1
$$

**Flowchart:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S([START]) --> A[Initialize i = 1]
    A --> D{i <= N?}
    D -- False --> E([END])
    D -- True --> P[Print i]
    P --> U[Increment i = i + 1]
    U --> D
```

**Explanation:** The condition is checked *before* each iteration. If FALSE, the loop exits. The body (`Print i`) executes only if TRUE. The increment step ensures progress; without it, the loop would never terminate.

##### **(iii) FOR Loop** *(2 Marks)*

**Scenario:** Calculate the sum of the first 10 natural numbers.

**Pseudocode:**

$$
\text{SUM} = 0; \quad \text{FOR } i = 1 \text{ TO } 10 \text{ DO SUM} = \text{SUM} + i
$$

**Flowchart:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S([START]) --> A[Initialize SUM = 0 and i = 1]
    A --> D{i <= 10?}
    D -- False --> E([END Display SUM])
    D -- True --> P[SUM = SUM + i]
    P --> U[i = i + 1]
    U --> D
```

**Explanation:** A FOR loop is a *count-controlled* loop. The number of iterations is known in advance. The initializer sets the counter, the condition checks the bound, the body performs the action, and the increment updates the counter.

**Comparative Summary:**

| Feature | IF-THEN-ELSE | WHILE | FOR |
|---------|--------------|-------|-----|
| **Iteration Count** | None | Unknown in advance | Known in advance |
| **Test Position** | N/A (single decision) | Pre-test | Pre-test |
| **Min Executions** | 1 (one branch) | 0 (may skip body) | 1 if range valid, else 0 |
| **Use Case** | Branching logic | Unknown-length processing | Fixed-count processing |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the START and END terminators** (−1 mark). Every flowchart **must** begin and end with an oval.
> 2. **Drawing the decision diamond with only ONE branch** (−2 marks). A diamond *always* has two outgoing arrows (Yes/No or True/False).
> 3. **Crossing flow lines** without using connectors (−0.5 to −1 mark). The KTU examiner specifically checks for line crossings.
> 4. **Writing paragraph-style labels inside process boxes** (−1 mark). Labels must be concise and verb-based.
> 5. **Not merging decision branches before END** (−1 mark). Yes and No paths must **rejoin** at a single point before the END terminator.
> 6. **Returning the loop back to START instead of the decision diamond** (−2 marks). This is a logical error that causes infinite loops.
> 7. **Missing exception paths** (−1 to −2 marks). In RPA, every flowchart should show *what happens if the decision fails* (error handling path).
> 8. **Confusing "Process" with "System Flowchart"** (−1 mark). A program flowchart is for logic; a system flowchart is for hardware/software interaction. The question wording determines which to draw.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read this 10 minutes before the exam.**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- A **Process** is an end-to-end workflow; a **Task** is a single action within it.
- A **Flowchart** is a standardized diagram of a process using ISO 5807 symbols.
- In RPA, the flowchart is the **design blueprint** that precedes coding.

### 🔟 The 10 Must-Know Flowchart Symbols
1. **Oval** → Start/End
2. **Rectangle** → Process/Action
3. **Diamond** → Decision
4. **Parallelogram** → Input/Output
5. **Cylinder** → Database
6. **Document** → Paper document
7. **Small Circle** → On-page connector
8. **Pentagon** → Off-page connector
9. **Trapezoid** → Manual operation
10. **Arrow** → Flow direction

### 🧠 The 5 Must-Know Flowchart Structures
1. **Sequence** — Step A → Step B → Step C
2. **Selection (IF-THEN-ELSE)** — Branch on a condition
3. **WHILE loop** — Pre-test, may execute 0 times
4. **DO-WHILE loop** — Post-test, executes at least once
5. **FOR loop** — Count-controlled, iterations known in advance

### 📐 The 2 Must-Know Formulas
- **Maximum Paths:** $P_{max} = 2^{d}$ where $d$ = number of decisions.
- **Cyclomatic Complexity:** $M = E - N + 2P$ or $M = \pi + 1$.

### 📋 The 4 Must-Know Design Rules
1. One Start, One (or more) End.
2. Default flow top-to-bottom, left-to-right.
3. No crossed lines — use connectors.
4. Decision diamonds always have 2 labelled branches.

### 🎯 The 5 RPA-Suitability Criteria
1. Rule-based
2. Repetitive
3. High-volume
4. Low exception rate
5. Digital/structured inputs

### 🔄 The 2 Flowchart Types Most Asked in KTU
- **As-Is Flowchart** (current manual process).
- **To-Be Flowchart** (proposed automated process).

### 💡 The 1 Mnemonic
- **"O-R-D-P-A"** → **O**val, **R**ectangle, **D**iamond, **P**arallelogram, **A**rrow.

> 🎓 **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to *"draw a flowchart"*, always begin by **listing the inputs, decisions, and outputs in plain text first**. This 2-minute pre-drawing step prevents mid-flow logical errors and earns you the **process clarity** marks that distinguish an A-grade answer from a B-grade one.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programming Constructs in RPA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Programming Constructs in RPA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Programming Constructs in RPA** refer to the fundamental logical control structures — **Sequence**, **Selection (Decision)**, and **Iteration (Looping)** — that govern the execution flow of an automated bot within a Robotic Process Automation workflow. These constructs are the backbone of any RPA workflow (e.g., UiPath, Automation Anywhere, Blue Prism) and allow the bot to mimic human decision-making, repetitive actions, and conditional branching.

The **three primary programming constructs** as standardized by the KTU 2024 Scheme for RPA are:

| # | Construct | Purpose | RPA Equivalent |
|---|-----------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | **Sequence** | Linear step-by-step execution | Main workflow pipeline |
| 2 | **Selection** | Conditional branching (if-else, switch) | If Activity, Flow Decision, Switch |
| 3 | **Iteration** | Repetition of tasks | While, Do-While, For Each |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Robot" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of an RPA bot as a newly hired office assistant named "Robo".** On his first day, his manager gives him a stack of 500 invoices and says: "Read each one. If the amount is above ₹10,000, send it to the manager's email. Otherwise, file it in the cabinet. After every 50 invoices, take a 1-minute break."

Robo needs **three mental abilities** to do this:
1. **Sequence** — He reads invoice #1, then #2, then #3... (one after another, in order).
2. **Selection** — For each invoice, he **decides** "amount > 10,000?" → Yes path or No path.
3. **Iteration** — He **repeats** the same reading-and-deciding action 500 times, taking a break every 50.

> **These three abilities = the three programming constructs of RPA.**

---

## 1.3 Why Programming Constructs Matter in RPA

In traditional programming, you write code in C/Java/Python. In RPA, you **drag and drop visual activities** that *internally* implement the same logic. For example, the **"If" activity** in UiPath is essentially a visual `if-else` statement.

**Real-world RPA Use Cases (Tagged with the relevant construct):**

- **Sequence** → "Open browser → Login to ERP → Download report → Save to folder." (Linear, no decisions.)
- **Selection** → "If today's date is Sunday, do not run the bot. Else, run payroll." (Conditional.)
- **Iteration** → "For each row in the Excel sheet, fill the corresponding web form." (Repetitive.)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** Almost every RPA exam question in Module 1 maps directly to one of these three constructs. Always identify the construct first, then draw the **flowchart** — that is the highest-mark-yielding strategy.

---

## 1.4 Variables, Data Types & Expressions (Supporting Constructs)

Although not "control structures" per se, variables and expressions are the *fuel* that powers all three constructs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **variable** is a named storage location that holds a value which can change during workflow execution. In UiPath, variables have **strict types** (String, Int32, Boolean, DateTime, DataTable, etc.).

**Standard RPA Variable Types:**

- **String** → Text (e.g., `"Hello World"`)
- **Int32** → Integer numbers (e.g., `42`)
- **Boolean** → True / False (critical for If conditions)
- **DateTime** → Date and time values
- **DataTable** → Tabular data (Excel rows)
- **GenericValue** → UiPath's flexible type
- **Array / List** → Collections of items

**Expressions** combine variables using **operators**:
- Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `mod`
- Comparison: `=`, `<>`, `>`, `<`, `>=`, `<=`
- Logical: `AND`, `OR`, `NOT`
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Construct 1 — Sequence

**Definition:** A sequence is a linear flow of activities where each step executes **exactly once, in order, from top to bottom**, with no branching or repetition.

**Operational Logic:**
- Step 1 executes → completes → Step 2 begins → completes → ... → Final step.
- No condition is evaluated; no loop is formed.
- This is the **default execution model** of an RPA workflow.

**Why it matters:** The vast majority of simple bots (e.g., "open app → click button → type text → save file") are pure sequences. KTU examiners frequently test students by asking them to *convert a sequence of English instructions into a flowchart*.

---

## 2.2 Construct 2 — Selection (Decision Making)

Selection introduces **branching**. The bot evaluates a Boolean condition and chooses one of two (or more) paths.

### 2.2.1 Simple `If` (Two-way Decision)

- Evaluates one condition.
- **True branch** executes; **False branch** is skipped (or vice versa).
- Implemented in UiPath as the **"If" activity**.

### 2.2.2 `If-Else` (Two-way Decision with both branches active)

- Same as simple If, but **both branches are explicitly defined** so the bot always does *something* regardless of the condition.

### 2.2.3 Nested `If` (Multi-way Decision)

- An If placed **inside** another If.
- Used when more than two conditions need to be checked sequentially.
- Risk: **Spaghetti code** if nested too deeply.

### 2.2.4 `Switch` (Multi-way Decision)

- Evaluates an expression and jumps to the matching **case**.
- Cleaner alternative to deeply nested Ifs.
- Implemented in UiPath as the **"Switch" activity** (TypeArgument can be String, Int32, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** If an exam question mentions "more than 3 conditions", the expected answer is a **Switch construct**, not nested Ifs.

---

## 2.3 Construct 3 — Iteration (Looping)

Iteration allows the bot to **repeat a block of activities** until a condition is met (or for a known number of times).

### 2.3.1 `While` Loop (Pre-test / Entry-controlled)

```
WHILE condition is TRUE
    execute body
END WHILE
```
- Condition is checked **before** each iteration.
- If the condition is **FALSE initially**, the body **never executes** (zero iterations possible).
- **Risk:** Infinite loop if the body never modifies the condition.

### 2.3.2 `Do-While` Loop (Post-test / Exit-controlled)

```
DO
    execute body
WHILE condition is TRUE
```
- Body executes **at least once** (condition is checked **after**).
- Useful when the action must happen first, then be validated.

### 2.3.3 `For Each` Loop (Collection-based)

```
FOR EACH item IN collection
    execute body (using 'item')
NEXT
```
- Iterates over every element in a collection (Array, List, DataTable rows, etc.).
- **Most common in RPA** because most automation involves processing rows of Excel/CSV files.
- In UiPath, this is the **"For Each" activity** or **"For Each Row"** for DataTables.

### 2.3.4 `For` Loop (Counter-based, Pre-test)

```
FOR counter = start TO end STEP stepValue
    execute body
NEXT
```
- Used when the number of iterations is **known in advance**.
- Less common in modern RPA but still tested in KTU theory questions.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet — All Constructs at a Glance

| Construct | Synonym | Condition Checked? | Min. Executions | Risk | RPA Activity Name |
|-----------|---------|--------------------|------------------|------|-------------------|
| Sequence | Linear flow | No | 1 (per step) | None | Main / Sequence container |
| If (simple) | One-way If | Yes (one) | 0 or 1 | None | If activity |
| If-Else | Two-way If | Yes (one) | exactly 1 | None | If activity (with Else) |
| Nested If | If inside If | Yes (multiple) | 0 or 1 (per branch) | Spaghetti code | Multiple If activities |
| Switch | Case statement | Yes (multi) | exactly 1 | None | Switch activity |
| While | Pre-test loop | Before body | 0 | Infinite loop | While activity |
| Do-While | Post-test loop | After body | 1 | Infinite loop | Do While activity |
| For Each | Iterator loop | Implicit (collection) | 0 (if empty) | None | For Each / For Each Row |
| For | Counter loop | Before body | 0 | Off-by-one error | (Implemented via While in UiPath) |

> **Note on operators in expressions:** The KTU syllabus treats `=` and `==` as equivalent for equality. Inequality is written as `\neq` or `<>`.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of These Constructs

| Construct | Industry Use Case (RPA Context) |
|-----------|---------------------------------|
| Sequence | Bootstrapping a bot: load config → log in → initialize Excel scope |
| If-Else | Invoice validation: "If VAT > 18%, route to senior accountant" |
| Switch | Day-of-week scheduling: "Case Monday → run report A; Case Friday → run report B" |
| While | Retry logic: "While login fails, re-enter credentials (max 5 tries)" |
| Do-While | Mandatory first run: "Run payroll once, then While (not last day of month) repeat" |
| For Each | Mass email: "For each row in customer list, send personalized email" |

> [!TIP]
> **For 14-mark questions**, KTU examiners expect you to:
> 1. State the construct.
> 2. Draw the **standard flowchart symbol set** (oval = start/end, rectangle = process, diamond = decision, parallelogram = I/O).
> 3. Write the **pseudocode**.
> 4. Provide a **real RPA scenario** where it is used.
> Missing any of these four typically costs 2-3 marks.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Flowchart Logic & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Universal "If-Else" Decision Logic

We begin with the formal Boolean logic of a two-way decision and derive the execution rule that every RPA engine follows internally.

**Given:** A Boolean expression $B(x)$ evaluated on input $x$, with two action blocks $A_1$ (taken when $B$ is TRUE) and $A_2$ (taken when $B$ is FALSE).

**Rule 1 — Definition of If-Else in set theory:**

$$
\text{Execute}(x) = \begin{cases} A_1, & \text{if } B(x) = \text{TRUE} \\ A_2, & \text{if } B(x) = \text{FALSE} \end{cases}
$$

**Rule 2 — Mutual Exclusivity:** The two branches can never execute simultaneously. Formally:

$$
\forall x : \neg(B(x) = \text{TRUE} \;\land\; B(x) = \text{FALSE})
$$

**Rule 3 — Exhaustive Coverage:** For any valid input, exactly one branch executes:

$$
\forall x : (\text{executes}(A_1, x) \; \oplus \; \text{executes}(A_2, x)) = \text{TRUE}
$$

where $\oplus$ is the **XOR** operator.

> **Engineering interpretation:** This is why a Switch statement in UiPath has a "Default" case — to guarantee Rule 3 holds even if no explicit case matches.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Loop Termination Conditions

### 3.2.1 `While` Loop Termination

**Given:** Loop body $L$, condition $C$, initial state $S_0$.

**Step 1** — At iteration $k$, the engine checks $C(S_{k-1})$.

**Step 2** — If $C(S_{k-1}) = \text{TRUE}$, execute $L$ to produce $S_k = L(S_{k-1})$, then go to Step 1 with $k \leftarrow k+1$.

**Step 3** — If $C(S_{k-1}) = \text{FALSE}$, exit the loop.

**Termination condition (formal):**

$$
\exists k \in \mathbb{N} : C(S_{k}) = \text{FALSE}
$$

For the loop to **terminate**, such a $k$ must exist. A common sufficient condition is that the body $L$ monotonically decreases a **loop variant** $V$ (a non-negative integer strictly decreasing each iteration):

$$
V(S_0) \in \mathbb{N}, \quad V(S_{k+1}) < V(S_k), \quad V \geq 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{loop terminates in at most } V(S_0) \text{ iterations.}
$$

> **RPA Application:** In UiPath, this is why we use a **counter variable** (e.g., `intCounter`) inside a While loop, incrementing it by 1 each iteration. The counter acts as the loop variant $V$.

### 3.2.2 `Do-While` Loop Termination

The body executes once unconditionally, then the condition is checked. The variant condition becomes:

$$
V(S_0) \in \mathbb{N}, \quad V(S_{k+1}) < V(S_k) \quad \text{(checked at the end of iteration } k)
$$

**Difference from While:** Minimum iteration count is 1 (not 0).

### 3.2.3 `For Each` Loop Termination

**Given:** A collection $C = \{c_1, c_2, \dots, c_n\}$ of size $n = \vert C \vert$.

**Termination:** The loop ends when the internal index $i$ exceeds $n - 1$:

$$
\text{Loop ends} \iff i > n - 1
$$

**Number of iterations:** Exactly $n$ (or 0 if the collection is empty).

> **RPA Application:** When using "For Each Row in DataTable", the loop runs exactly once per row of the DataTable, regardless of content.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Truth Tables for Compound Conditions

RPA workflows often combine conditions with `AND` / `OR` / `NOT`. The truth table is the **foundation** for evaluating any If-condition.

| $A$ | $B$ | $A \;\text{AND}\; B$ | $A \;\text{OR}\; B$ | $\text{NOT}\; A$ |
|-----|-----|---------------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| F | F | F | F | T |
| F | T | F | T | T |
| T | F | F | T | F |
| T | T | T | T | F |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A common mistake is to confuse `AND` (conjunction — both must be true) with `OR` (disjunction — at least one must be true). In UiPath, the operators are typed exactly as `AND`, `OR`, `NOT` in the Expression Editor.

---

## 3.4 Conversion Rules — English Statement to Flowchart / Pseudocode

The KTU examiner frequently gives a plain-English problem and expects a structured solution. The conversion rule is:

**Step 1:** Identify the **keywords**:
- "in order" / "then" / "next" → **Sequence**
- "if" / "otherwise" / "when" → **Selection**
- "for each" / "while" / "repeat until" → **Iteration**

**Step 2:** Identify the **inputs and outputs** (I/O = parallelogram in flowchart).

**Step 3:** Identify the **decision points** (diamond in flowchart).

**Step 4:** Map each English sentence to **one** flowchart symbol.

**Step 5:** Verify termination conditions (for loops) and exhaustive coverage (for Switch).

### Worked Example Conversion

**English Problem (typical KTU 14-mark question):**
> "Read 10 numbers from the user. For each number, if it is even, add it to SumEven; otherwise, add it to SumOdd. After all 10 numbers, display both sums."

**Pseudocode Derivation (line-by-line):**

```
START
    Initialize SumEven = 0
    Initialize SumOdd  = 0
    Initialize Counter = 1

    DO
        READ number
        IF (number MOD 2 == 0)
            THEN SumEven = SumEven + number
            ELSE SumOdd  = SumOdd  + number
        END IF
        Counter = Counter + 1
    WHILE (Counter <= 10)

    DISPLAY SumEven
    DISPLAY SumOdd
END
```

**Construct identification:**
- `Initialize` statements → **Sequence**
- `IF ... THEN ... ELSE ... END IF` → **Selection (If-Else)**
- `DO ... WHILE` → **Iteration (Do-While loop)** — note that the body executes at least once, which is what we want for "read 10 numbers".

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Using a `While` loop with `Counter = 1` and `Counter <= 10` as the condition. This works only if the initial value of `Counter` (1) already satisfies the condition. The cleaner and **unconditionally safe** form is the `Do-While` shown above. If you must use While, initialize the counter to 0 and check `Counter < 10` to be safe.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid)

> [!NOTE]
> The following diagrams are designed to be **renderable by any standard Mermaid engine** (GitHub, VS Code, Obsidian, etc.). All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to avoid conflicts with Mermaid reserved keywords.

---

## 4.1 Sequence Construct — Linear Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1([START]) --> B1[Step 1: Open Application]
    B1 --> C1[Step 2: Login]
    C1 --> D1[Step 3: Read Data]
    D1 --> E1[Step 4: Write Data]
    E1 --> F1[Step 5: Close Application]
    F1 --> G1([END])
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each rectangle is an *activity*; the arrows define the strict linear order. There is no diamond, no branching, no loop.

---

## 4.2 Selection Construct — If-Else Two-Way Decision

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2([START]) --> B2[Process Input X]
    B2 --> C2{Is X greater than 1000?}
    C2 -- YES --> D2[Execute Branch A: Send to Manager]
    C2 -- NO --> E2[Execute Branch B: File in Cabinet]
    D2 --> F2[Merge Point]
    E2 --> F2
    F2 --> G2([END])
```

**Reading the diagram:** The diamond `{Is X > 1000?}` is the decision node. Two labeled edges (YES / NO) lead to mutually exclusive branches that **rejoin** at the merge point before END. This rejoin is the visual signature of `If-Else`.

---

## 4.3 Selection Construct — Switch (Multi-way Decision)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A3([START]) --> B3[Read DayOfWeek variable]
    B3 --> C3{Switch on DayOfWeek}
    C3 -- Monday --> D3[Run Report A]
    C3 -- Wednesday --> E3[Run Report B]
    C3 -- Friday --> F3[Run Report C]
    C3 -- Default --> G3[No report scheduled]
    D3 --> H3([END])
    E3 --> H3
    F3 --> H3
    G3 --> H3
```

**Reading the diagram:** One decision node, multiple labeled outgoing edges. The "Default" edge corresponds to UiPath's `Default` case, guaranteeing exhaustive coverage.

---

## 4.4 Iteration Construct — While Loop (Entry-Controlled)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A4([START]) --> B4[Initialize Counter = 0]
    B4 --> C4{Is Counter less than 10?}
    C4 -- YES --> D4[Execute Loop Body]
    D4 --> E4[Counter = Counter + 1]
    E4 --> C4
    C4 -- NO --> F4([END])
```

**Reading the diagram:** The decision is checked **before** the body. Notice the back-arrow from `Counter = Counter + 1` to the decision — this is the loop's return path. The body can execute **zero times** if the initial condition is FALSE.

---

## 4.5 Iteration Construct — Do-While Loop (Exit-Controlled)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A5([START]) --> B5[Initialize Counter = 0]
    B5 --> C5[Execute Loop Body]
    C5 --> D5[Counter = Counter + 1]
    D5 --> E5{Is Counter less than 10?}
    E5 -- YES --> C5
    E5 -- NO --> F5([END])
```

**Reading the diagram:** The body executes **first**, then the condition is checked. The body always runs at least once.

---

## 4.6 Iteration Construct — For Each (Collection-Based)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A6([START]) --> B6[Load Collection: DataTable]
    B6 --> C6{Any items left in collection?}
    C6 -- YES --> D6[Get next item: CurrentRow]
    D6 --> E6[Process CurrentRow]
    E6 --> F6[Move to next item]
    F6 --> C6
    C6 -- NO --> G6([END])
```

**Reading the diagram:** The decision node asks "any items left?" instead of testing a numeric counter. This is the conceptual model of `For Each` in UiPath.

---

## 4.7 Composite Architecture — All Three Constructs in One RPA Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K1([START BOT]) --> K2[Sequence: Login to ERP]
    K2 --> K3{Sequence: Read Config File}
    K3 --> K4[Extract: ThresholdValue]
    K4 --> L1{Selection: Is ThresholdValue greater than 0?}
    L1 -- NO --> M1[Log Error: Invalid Config]
    M1 --> M2([END BOT])
    L1 -- YES --> N1[Selection: Switch on DepartmentName]
    N1 -- Finance --> O1[Open Finance Folder]
    N1 -- HR --> O2[Open HR Folder]
    N1 -- Sales --> O3[Open Sales Folder]
    N1 -- Default --> O4[Log Warning: Unknown Department]
    O1 --> P1[Iteration: For Each Row in DepartmentData]
    O2 --> P1
    O3 --> P1
    O4 --> P1
    P1 --> Q1[Process Current Row]
    Q1 --> R1[Selection: If Row is Valid]
    R1 -- YES --> S1[Write to Output File]
    R1 -- NO --> S2[Append to Error Log]
    S1 --> P1
    S2 --> P1
    P1 --> T1([END BOT])
```

**Architectural Reading:** This single diagram demonstrates how the three constructs **compose** in a real RPA workflow:
- **Sequence** wraps the entire bot (`Login` → `Read Config`).
- **Selection (If)** validates the config value.
- **Selection (Switch)** routes by department.
- **Iteration (For Each)** processes every row.
- **Nested Selection (If)** validates each row inside the loop.

> [!TIP]
> This composite diagram is the **gold-standard answer** for any 14-mark question that asks: *"Design an RPA workflow for processing department-wise invoice data."*
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following questions are modeled on actual KTU University Exam patterns. **Part A** is 3-mark short-answer (typical KTU internal/module test format). **Part B** is the 14-mark End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern with **internal choice** — two full alternative questions are provided.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Define the three fundamental programming constructs used in RPA. Give one example of each from a real-world automation scenario.**

**Model Answer:**

The three fundamental programming constructs in RPA are:

1. **Sequence:** A linear, top-to-bottom execution of activities with no branching or repetition. *Example:* "Open web browser → Navigate to ERP login page → Enter credentials → Click Submit" — a pure sequence of steps.

2. **Selection:** A conditional branching construct where the workflow follows one of two or more paths based on the evaluation of a Boolean condition. *Example:* "If invoice amount > ₹50,000, send email to CFO; Else, file in local folder."

3. **Iteration:** A looping construct that repeatedly executes a block of activities until a condition is satisfied or a collection is exhausted. *Example:* "For Each row in the vendor Excel file, fetch the vendor's GST number from the web portal."

> **Marking note (3 marks):** 1 mark per construct definition + 1 mark total for all three examples (½ mark each). To score full marks, **name the construct AND give the RPA-specific example**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**Differentiate between a `While` loop and a `Do-While` loop. In which RPA scenario would you prefer a `Do-While` loop?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | `While` Loop | `Do-While` Loop |
|---------|--------------|------------------|
| Type | Pre-test (entry-controlled) | Post-test (exit-controlled) |
| Condition check | Before body execution | After body execution |
| Minimum executions | 0 (body may never run) | 1 (body always runs at least once) |
| Risk of infinite loop | Higher (if condition is initially FALSE, loop never enters) | Lower (body always runs once) |
| RPA Activity | `While` in UiPath | `Do While` in UiPath |

**Preferred scenario for Do-While in RPA:** Mandatory first-attempt tasks where the action must occur at least once before validation. *Example:* "Run the daily payroll calculation once. Then, while the result file does not exist on the network drive, retry saving the file (max 5 retries)." — here the payroll must be calculated at least once before checking if the file was saved.

> **Marking note (3 marks):** 2 marks for the tabular difference (at least 3 points) + 1 mark for the justified scenario.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice)

> Each question has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Two full alternative questions are provided — answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — ESE Pattern Model]

**"A bank wants to automate its daily loan eligibility check. For every customer in an Excel file containing 1,000 rows, the bot must read the applicant's salary and credit score. If the salary is above ₹3,00,000 AND the credit score is above 750, mark the applicant as 'Eligible'; otherwise, mark as 'Not Eligible'. At the end, display the total count of eligible and non-eligible applicants."**

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Identify the programming constructs required. Draw the complete flowchart of the workflow.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Construct Identification:**

- **Sequence** → Initialize variables (countEligible = 0, countNotEligible = 0), display final results.
- **Iteration (For Each)** → To iterate over all 1,000 rows of the Excel DataTable.
- **Selection (Nested If with AND)** → To check "salary > 3,00,000 AND creditScore > 750".

**Step 2 — Flowchart:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    QA1([START]) --> QA2[Initialize: countEligible = 0, countNotEligible = 0]
    QA2 --> QA3[Open Excel: Read 1000 rows into DataTable]
    QA3 --> QA4{For Each Row in DataTable}
    QA4 -- next row --> QA5[Read currentRow: salary, creditScore]
    QA5 --> QA6{Is salary greater than 300000?}
    QA6 -- NO --> QA7[Set Status = Not Eligible]
    QA6 -- YES --> QA8{Is creditScore greater than 750?}
    QA8 -- NO --> QA7
    QA8 -- YES --> QA9[Set Status = Eligible]
    QA7 --> QA10[Increment countNotEligible]
    QA9 --> QA11[Increment countEligible]
    QA10 --> QA12[Write Status back to row]
    QA11 --> QA12
    QA12 --> QA4
    QA4 -- no more rows --> QA13[Display countEligible and countNotEligible]
    QA13 --> QA14([END])
```

**Step 3 — Explanation:**
The bot initializes two counters, opens the Excel file as a DataTable, and uses a For Each loop to process each row. Inside the loop, a **nested If** evaluates the AND condition. Based on the result, the status is set and the appropriate counter is incremented.

**Mark split:** Construct identification (2) + Flowchart (3) + Explanation (2).

---

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** Write the equivalent pseudocode for the above workflow. Justify why a `For Each` loop is preferable to a `While` loop in this scenario.

**Model Solution:**

**Pseudocode:**

```
START
    countEligible    = 0
    countNotEligible = 0
    OPEN Excel file, READ all rows into DataTable dtApplicants

    FOR EACH currentRow IN dtApplicants
        salary      = currentRow("Salary")
        creditScore = currentRow("CreditScore")

        IF (salary > 300000) AND (creditScore > 750)
            THEN
                currentRow("Status") = "Eligible"
                countEligible = countEligible + 1
            ELSE
                currentRow("Status") = "Not Eligible"
                countNotEligible = countNotEligible + 1
        END IF
    END FOR EACH

    DISPLAY "Eligible applicants: ", countEligible
    DISPLAY "Not Eligible applicants: ", countNotEligible
END
```

**Justification — Why `For Each` over `While`:**

1. **Known collection size:** The DataTable is a finite, well-defined collection. A `For Each` loop automatically iterates over every row without needing an explicit counter.
2. **No off-by-one risk:** A `While` loop would require manual counter initialization, increment, and bounds checking. Mistakes in any of these cause off-by-one errors.
3. **No infinite loop risk:** A `While` loop with a faulty condition can run indefinitely. A `For Each` loop terminates the moment the collection is exhausted.
4. **Cleaner code:** In RPA tools like UiPath, the "For Each Row" activity is purpose-built for DataTables and exposes the current row as a strongly-typed object — a `While` loop would require manual indexing into the DataTable.

**Mark split:** Correct pseudocode (4) + Justification with at least 3 valid points (3).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU ESE Pattern Model]

**"An e-commerce company wants a bot that processes customer orders from an online queue. The bot must keep reading orders from the queue until the queue is empty. For each order, if the order value exceeds ₹10,000, apply a 10% discount; otherwise, apply a 5% discount. After processing all orders, the bot must display the total revenue generated."**

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Identify the most appropriate iteration construct for reading orders from the queue. Draw the flowchart.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Construct Identification:**

- **Iteration (`Do-While` or `While` with queue-not-empty condition)** — The bot must process orders as long as the queue is non-empty.
- **Selection (If-Else)** — To apply the correct discount tier.
- **Sequence** — To compute the discounted price and accumulate revenue.

> **Best choice:** A `While` loop with the condition "queue is not empty" is ideal because the body should **not** execute if the queue starts empty (zero iterations is acceptable). If the business rule were "process at least one order", then a `Do-While` would be required.

**Step 2 — Flowchart:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    QB1([START]) --> QB2[Initialize: totalRevenue = 0]
    QB2 --> QB3[Connect to Order Queue]
    QB3 --> QB4{Is Order Queue empty?}
    QB4 -- NO --> QB5[Dequeue one Order]
    QB5 --> QB6[Read orderValue]
    QB6 --> QB7{Is orderValue greater than 10000?}
    QB7 -- YES --> QB8[discountedPrice = orderValue * 0.90]
    QB7 -- NO --> QB9[discountedPrice = orderValue * 0.95]
    QB8 --> QB10[totalRevenue = totalRevenue + discountedPrice]
    QB9 --> QB10
    QB10 --> QB4
    QB4 -- YES --> QB11[Display totalRevenue]
    QB11 --> QB12([END])
```

**Mark split:** Construct identification (2) + Justification of choice (2) + Flowchart (3).

---

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** Write the pseudocode. Explain what would happen if a `Do-While` loop were used instead.

**Model Solution:**

**Pseudocode (using While):**

```
START
    totalRevenue = 0
    CONNECT to OrderQueue

    WHILE (OrderQueue is NOT empty)
        currentOrder = DEQUEUE from OrderQueue
        orderValue   = currentOrder.value

        IF (orderValue > 10000)
            THEN
                discountedPrice = orderValue * 0.90
            ELSE
                discountedPrice = orderValue * 0.95
        END IF

        totalRevenue = totalRevenue + discountedPrice
    END WHILE

    DISPLAY "Total Revenue: Rs. ", totalRevenue
END
```

**What happens if a `Do-While` loop is used instead:**

- A `Do-While` loop executes the body **at least once**, then checks the condition.
- If the OrderQueue is **empty at the start** of the day (a common case on Sundays or holidays), the bot would:
  1. **Attempt to dequeue an order from an empty queue** — causing a runtime error or returning a `null` order.
  2. **Try to read `currentOrder.value`** — throwing a `NullReferenceException` (in UiPath, this is the "Object reference not set" error).
  3. **Crash the entire workflow**, requiring manual restart.
- In contrast, the `While` loop checks the queue's emptiness **first**, and if it is empty, skips the body entirely and goes directly to displaying `totalRevenue = 0`. This is the **safe and correct** behavior.

**Mark split:** Correct pseudocode (3) + Error analysis of Do-While with at least 3 valid points (4).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose the most marks on "Programming Constructs in RPA" questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing `While` and `Do-While`** (most common, costs 2-3 marks). Always state explicitly: "Condition is checked **before** the body" vs. "Condition is checked **after** the body".
>
> 2. **Forgetting to show loop termination logic.** A `While` loop flowchart that does not show the back-arrow from the body to the decision node is **incomplete** and loses 1-2 marks.
>
> 3. **Using the wrong symbol in the flowchart.** A decision must be a **diamond**, a process must be a **rectangle**, and I/O must be a **parallelogram**. Drawing a rectangle for a decision node costs 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Not justifying the choice of construct.** When asked "why For Each and not While?", simply stating "it is easier" gets zero. You must give **technical reasons**: known collection size, no off-by-one, no infinite loop, etc.
>
> 5. **Missing the merge point in If-Else flowcharts.** Both branches must rejoin before the END node. A flowchart where the two branches lead to two separate END nodes is **structurally wrong**.
>
> 6. **Spelling `pseudo code` or `pseudocode` incorrectly** in the answer heading — minor, but examiners do deduct ½ mark for unprofessional formatting under strict KTU valuation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and revise from it the night before the exam.**

- The **three programming constructs** in RPA are **Sequence, Selection, and Iteration**.
- **Sequence** = linear, no decision, no loop. Used for bootstrapping and finalization.
- **Selection** has four variants: **simple If, If-Else, Nested If, Switch**. Use **Switch** when there are 3+ cases.
- **Iteration** has four variants: **While, Do-While, For Each, For**.
  - **While** → condition checked first, may run **0** times.
  - **Do-While** → condition checked after, runs **at least 1** time.
  - **For Each** → iterates over a **collection** (most common in RPA).
  - **For** → iterates a **fixed number** of times using a counter.
- **Variables** are typed (String, Int32, Boolean, DateTime, DataTable). They are the *fuel* for all conditions.
- **Operators** in conditions: arithmetic (`+ - * / mod`), comparison (`= <> > < >= <=`), logical (`AND OR NOT`).
- **Flowchart symbols**: oval = start/end, rectangle = process, diamond = decision, parallelogram = I/O.
- **If-Else** must have a **merge point** before the END node.
- **Loops** must have a clear **back-arrow** from the body to the decision node, otherwise the loop will not repeat.
- **For Each** is the **safest** iteration construct in RPA because it cannot produce an infinite loop and has no off-by-one error.
- **Truth tables** for `AND`, `OR`, `NOT` must be memorized — they appear in 3-mark and 14-mark questions alike.
- A **loop variant** $V$ (a non-negative integer that strictly decreases) is the formal proof that a `While` loop will terminate.
- In any 14-mark question, the **four-part answer** is: **(1)** state the construct, **(2)** draw the flowchart, **(3)** write the pseudocode, **(4)** give a real RPA scenario.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: What Processes Can Be Automated
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** What Processes Can Be Automated

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Robotic Process Automation (RPA)**, a **process** refers to a structured, logical sequence of well-defined business activities, sub-tasks, or workflow steps that transform a set of **inputs** into a measurable **output**, performed either manually by humans or through a combination of human and digital systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition — Automatable Process**
> An **automatable process** is any rule-based, repetitive, high-volume, deterministic, and structured business process that requires minimal human judgment, is driven by digital inputs (emails, forms, ERP screens, databases, spreadsheets, or web portals), and can be executed end-to-end by a software bot with the same or better accuracy, speed, and consistency than a human worker.

The fundamental question — *"What processes can be automated?"* — sits at the very heart of **Process Discovery**, the first phase of the **RPA Lifecycle** (Discovery → Design → Develop → Test → Deploy → Maintain). Before a single bot is built, a process analyst must rigorously identify, evaluate, and rank candidate processes against a set of **automation feasibility criteria**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Assembly Line"

Imagine a large postal sorting office. Every day, thousands of letters arrive, each with a clear zip code, a clear destination, and a clear set of sorting rules. A machine can read the zip code and physically place each letter into the correct bin — **faster, cheaper, and with fewer errors than a human sorter**.

Now replace the letters with **invoices**, the zip codes with **invoice numbers**, and the sorting machine with a **software robot**. That is precisely what RPA does — it acts as a *digital assembly line* for office work.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students**
> Just as a postal machine **cannot** sort a letter with no zip code or one that is torn and illegible, an RPA bot **cannot** automate a process that is ambiguous, judgment-driven, highly emotional, or constantly changing. The first lesson in RPA is therefore **not** "how to build a bot", but **"how to recognise a process that deserves one."**

## 1.3 The Two Broad Universes of Work

KTU 2024 scheme explicitly groups all workplace processes into two categories:

| Category | Description | Bot-Suitability |
|----------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Deterministic / Rule-Based Work** | Repetitive, predictable, follows a clear if-then logic. Examples: data entry, invoice matching, report generation. | **HIGH — Prime RPA candidate** |
| **Judgmental / Cognitive Work** | Requires creativity, empathy, negotiation, complex reasoning, or physical dexterity. Examples: strategy, conflict resolution, sales negotiations. | **LOW — Not suitable for traditional RPA** |

> [!TIP]
> Modern **Intelligent Automation (IA)** and **Hyperautomation** combine RPA with AI/ML (OCR, NLP, Computer Vision) to *partially* cross the boundary into judgmental work — but traditional RPA (the focus of PEAIT415) operates strictly on the **deterministic** side.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Pillars of an Automatable Process

A process is considered a strong RPA candidate when it satisfies the following **seven core characteristics**. These are the most frequently asked points in KTU 2024 ESE Part A questions.

1. **High Volume & High Frequency** — The process runs hundreds or thousands of times per day/month.
