2. **Rule-Based & Deterministic** — Clear if-then-else logic governs every decision. No grey areas.
3. **Structured Digital Inputs** — Inputs arrive as files (PDF, Excel, CSV), emails, database queries, or UI screens — not handwritten scribbles.
4. **Low Exception Rate** — The process follows the "happy path" ≥ 80% of the time. Exceptions are known, finite, and can be catalogued.
5. **Stable & Mature** — The underlying application, UI, or business rule has **not changed in the last 6–12 months**.
6. **Repetitive & Manual** — Humans currently do it the same way, every time, copy-pasting or re-keying data.
7. **Measurable ROI** — A clear baseline of time, cost, and error rate exists so that automation gains can be quantified.

## 2.2 The KTU 2024 Process Categorization Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise this 2×2 matrix — it is a guaranteed KTU Part A question.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Process Quadrant} &= f(\text{Volume},\ \text{Rule-Basedness}) \\
\text{Where:}\quad V &= \text{Volume (High \mid Low)} \\
R &= \text{Rule-Basedness (High \mid Low)}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Volume | Rule-Based | Quadrant | RPA Verdict |
|--------|-----------|----------|-------------|
| **High** | **High** | **Q1 — "Sweet Spot"** | ✅ Automate Immediately |
| **High** | **Low** | Q2 — "Heavy But Fuzzy" | ⚠️ Re-engineer first, then automate |
| **Low** | **High** | Q3 — "Easy But Rare" | ❌ Do not automate (low ROI) |
| **Low** | **Low** | Q4 — "Boring" | ❌ Do not automate |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — RPA Suitability Scoring Model (RSSM)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical scoring model** taught under PEAIT415 Module 1. Examiners frequently ask students to calculate a **Process Suitability Score (PSS)** for a given business case.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PSS} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i \\
\text{Where:}\quad w_i &= \text{Weight of criterion } i \quad \left(\sum w_i = 1.0\right) \\
s_i &= \text{Score of criterion } i \quad \left(0 \le s_i \le 10\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.3.1 Standard Weight Table (KTU Recommended)

| $\vert i \vert$ | Criterion | Symbol $s_i$ | Weight $w_i$ | Score Scale |
|:---:|-----------|:---:|:---:|-------------|
| 1 | Volume / Frequency | $s_{\text{vol}}$ | **0.20** | 0–10 |
| 2 | Rule-Basedness | $s_{\text{rule}}$ | **0.20** | 0–10 |
| 3 | Data Structuredness | $s_{\text{data}}$ | **0.15** | 0–10 |
| 4 | Process Stability | $s_{\text{stab}}$ | **0.10** | 0–10 |
| 5 | Exception Rate (inverse) | $s_{\text{exc}}$ | **0.10** | 0–10 |
| 6 | Manual Effort (FTE hours) | $s_{\text{man}}$ | **0.10** | 0–10 |
| 7 | Error / Rework Cost | $s_{\text{err}}$ | **0.10** | 0–10 |
| 8 | Compliance / Audit Need | $s_{\text{com}}$ | **0.05** | 0–10 |
| | **Total** | | **1.00** | |

### 2.3.2 Decision Bands

| PSS Range | Automation Verdict | Action |
|:---------:|-------------------|--------|
| **8.0 – 10.0** | 🟢 **Excellent** | Fast-track to PoC |
| **6.0 – 7.99** | 🟡 **Good** | Detailed feasibility study |
| **4.0 – 5.99** | 🟠 **Marginal** | Re-engineer process first |
| **0.0 – 3.99** | 🔴 **Poor** | Do not automate |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Industry

| Industry | Typical Automatable Process |
|----------|----------------------------|
| **Banking & Finance** | KYC verification, loan eligibility checks, fraud alert triaging |
| **Healthcare** | Patient appointment scheduling, insurance claim status checks |
| **Insurance** | Claims first-notice-of-loss (FNOL) data entry, policy renewal reminders |
| **Retail / E-Commerce** | Order-to-cash reconciliation, inventory sync across portals |
| **HR** | Employee onboarding form-filling, leave balance updates, payslip generation |
| **Telecom** | SIM activation verification, customer data migration between CRMs |
| **Manufacturing** | Vendor invoice 3-way match, purchase order creation from email |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love to quote one of the above examples verbatim. Memorise at least **three** industry use-cases.

## 2.5 What CANNOT Be Automated (Equally Important for the Exam)

A complete answer on "what can be automated" must also mention the **anti-cases**:

- Processes requiring **human empathy** (e.g., grievance counselling, medical diagnosis).
- Processes needing **physical dexterity** (e.g., equipment repair, surgical tasks).
- Processes driven by **non-digital inputs** (e.g., handwritten, voice-only) *unless* OCR/Speech-to-Text is integrated.
- Processes that are **constantly changing** (e.g., new government forms with shifting fields).
- Processes with **very low volume** (e.g., annual board-meeting minute logging).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations

## 3.1 Worked Example — Evaluating a Real Business Process

> [!NOTE]
> **Case Study:** *A leading Kerala-based NBFC processes **invoice data entry into Tally ERP** for **450 vendors per day**. Each invoice is currently keyed manually by **3 FTE clerks**, taking **6 minutes per invoice**. The clerk pulls data from a **structured email PDF attachment**, then re-keys it into Tally using a **fixed set of 12 fields**. The Tally UI has been **stable for 18 months**, and the **exception rate is 4%** (unknown vendor codes).*

We are required to compute the **Process Suitability Score (PSS)** and recommend an action.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Assign Raw Scores (0 to 10)

| Criterion | Reasoning | Raw Score $s_i$ |
|-----------|-----------|:---:|
| Volume $s_{\text{vol}}$ | 450/day = 13500/month → Very High | **9** |
| Rule-Based $s_{\text{rule}}$ | Fixed 12-field Tally entry → Pure if-then | **10** |
| Data Structured $s_{\text{data}}$ | Structured PDF email attachment | **8** |
| Stability $s_{\text{stab}}$ | Tally UI unchanged for 18 months | **9** |
| Exception Rate (inverse) $s_{\text{exc}}$ | 4% exceptions (low, and known) | **8** |
| Manual Effort $s_{\text{man}}$ | 3 FTE × 8 hours = 24 person-hr/day | **9** |
| Error Cost $s_{\text{err}}$ | Re-keying causes 2% transcription errors | **8** |
| Compliance $s_{\text{com}}$ | Statutory tax filing implications | **9** |

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Apply the KTU Weight Table

Using weights from Section 2.3.1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PSS} &= w_1 s_{\text{vol}} + w_2 s_{\text{rule}} + w_3 s_{\text{data}} + w_4 s_{\text{stab}} + w_5 s_{\text{exc}} + w_6 s_{\text{man}} + w_7 s_{\text{err}} + w_8 s_{\text{com}} \\[4pt]
&= (0.20)(9) + (0.20)(10) + (0.15)(8) + (0.10)(9) + (0.10)(8) + (0.10)(9) + (0.10)(8) + (0.05)(9)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Evaluate Each Product Term

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.20)(9) &= 1.80 \\
(0.20)(10) &= 2.00 \\
(0.15)(8) &= 1.20 \\
(0.10)(9) &= 0.90 \\
(0.10)(8) &= 0.80 \\
(0.10)(9) &= 0.90 \\
(0.10)(8) &= 0.80 \\
(0.05)(9) &= 0.45
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Sum All Terms

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PSS} &= 1.80 + 2.00 + 1.20 + 0.90 + 0.80 + 0.90 + 0.80 + 0.45 \\
\text{PSS} &= 8.85
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Map to Decision Band

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PSS} = 8.85 \quad &\in \quad [8.0,\ 10.0] \\
\therefore \text{Verdict} &= \text{GREEN — Excellent Candidate for RPA}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Compute the ROI Justification (Bonus for 14-mark answers)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Manual Time Saved per day} &= 450 \text{ invoices} \times 6 \text{ min} = 2700 \text{ min} = 45 \text{ hr/day} \\
\text{Annual FTE Equivalent Saved} &= \frac{45 \text{ hr/day} \times 22 \text{ days/month} \times 12 \text{ months}}{8 \text{ hr/day} \times 250 \text{ working days}} \\
\text{Annual FTE Equivalent Saved} &= \frac{11880}{2000} = 5.94 \text{ FTEs} \\[4pt]
\text{Annual Cost Saved @ ₹30,000/FTE/month} &= 5.94 \times 30000 \times 12 = \text{₹}\ 21{,}38{,}400
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Recommendation:** The Tally invoice data-entry process is a **textbook green-quadrant candidate** — automate immediately. Expected payback period for a typical RPA bot: **under 4 months**.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison — Three Candidate Processes

> [!NOTE]
> The following worked-out table is **directly inspired by KTU Dec 2023 Part B style questions** — practice writing it out from memory.

| Process | Volume (w=0.2) | Rule (w=0.2) | Data (w=0.15) | Stable (w=0.1) | Exc (w=0.1) | Man (w=0.1) | Err (w=0.1) | Comp (w=0.05) | **PSS** | Verdict |
|---------|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|---------|
| P1: Invoice Tally Entry | 9 | 10 | 8 | 9 | 8 | 9 | 8 | 9 | **8.85** | 🟢 Excellent |
| P2: Customer Grievance Voice | 6 | 3 | 2 | 5 | 3 | 7 | 6 | 8 | **4.40** | 🟠 Marginal |
| P3: Daily Sales MIS Report | 8 | 9 | 9 | 8 | 9 | 7 | 7 | 6 | **8.10** | 🟢 Excellent |
| P4: Annual Budget Approval | 2 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 2 | 3 | 2 | 9 | **3.80** | 🔴 Poor |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Verdict Ranking:} \quad &P_1 > P_3 \gg P_2 > P_4 \\
\text{Automation Order:} \quad &\text{Automate } P_1 \text{ first, then } P_3, \text{ re-engineer } P_2, \text{ ignore } P_4.
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Process Automation Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Business Process] --> B{Is the process rule-based?}
    B -- No --> C[STOP - Not RPA Suitable - Use AI/ML]
    B -- Yes --> D{Is volume high enough to give ROI?}
    D -- No --> E[STOP - Low Volume - Manual is cheaper]
    D -- Yes --> F{Is input data structured and digital?}
    F -- No --> G[Re-engineer - Add OCR or NLP first]
    F -- Yes --> H{Is the process stable for 6+ months?}
    H -- No --> I[Wait - Process still maturing]
    H -- Yes --> J[Compute RSSM PSS Score]
    J --> K{PSS >= 8.0?}
    K -- Yes --> L[GREEN - Fast-track to PoC]
    K -- No --> M{PSS between 6.0 and 7.99?}
    M -- Yes --> N[YELLOW - Detailed Feasibility Study]
    M -- No --> O{PSS between 4.0 and 5.99?}
    O -- Yes --> P[ORANGE - Re-engineer then reassess]
    O -- No --> Q[RED - Do Not Automate]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram in a viva:** Start at the top node `A` and walk down. At each diamond-shaped decision node, answer with a **Yes / No** and follow the corresponding branch. The terminal node gives the verdict.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Four-Quadrant Automation Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Q1[Q1 - Sweet Spot - AUTOMATE NOW]
        A1[High Volume + High Rule-Based = Best Candidate]
    end
    subgraph Q2[Q2 - Heavy but Fuzzy]
        A2[High Volume + Low Rule = Re-engineer first]
    end
    subgraph Q3[Q3 - Easy but Rare]
        A3[Low Volume + High Rule = ROI too low]
    end
    subgraph Q4[Q4 - Avoid]
        A4[Low Volume + Low Rule = Manual is fine]
    end
    Q1 --- Q2
    Q2 --- Q3
    Q3 --- Q4
    Q1 --- Q3
    Q2 --- Q4
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Architecture — Process Discovery Phase

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1 - Process Inventory] --> P2[Step 2 - Filter by Rule-Based Logic]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 - Filter by Volume and Frequency]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 - Filter by Data Structuredness]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 - Compute RSSM Score]
    P5 --> P6{PSS >= 8.0?}
    P6 -- Yes --> P7[Add to RPA Backlog - High Priority]
    P6 -- No --> P8[Add to Re-engineering or Monitoring List]
    P7 --> P9[Hand over to Solution Architect]
    P8 --> P10[Periodic Reassessment Every 6 Months]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]*
**"List any six characteristics of a process that make it a strong candidate for RPA automation."**

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**
1. **High volume and high frequency** of execution.
2. **Rule-based and deterministic** decision logic.
3. **Structured and digital** input/output data.
4. **Low exception rate** (≤ 20%) and known exceptions.
5. **Stable and mature** — UI / business rules unchanged for 6+ months.
6. **Repetitive and manual** — currently executed by humans in a fixed pattern.

*(Any 6 from Section 2.1 of these notes earn full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question A2 — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]*
**"Give two real-world examples of processes that can be automated using RPA and two examples of processes that cannot."**

**Model Answer (1 mark per example + 1 mark for clear classification = 3 marks):**

**Automatable:**
- Invoice data entry into an ERP system.
- Daily sales MIS report generation and email distribution.

**Not Automatable:**
- Negotiating a sales deal with a key customer (requires judgment, empathy).
- Diagnosing a patient (requires medical expertise and physical examination).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question B1A — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]*  **(14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **seven characteristics of an automatable process** as defined in the KTU 2024 RPA syllabus. Use a real-world example from the **Banking domain** to illustrate each characteristic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of the **2×2 Process Quadrant Matrix**, classify the following four processes and justify your verdict for each:
- P1: Daily ATM cash-replenishment reconciliation
- P2: Annual branch strategic-planning workshop report
- P3: Customer credit-score computation from 12 financial ratios
- P4: Quarterly board-meeting minute writing. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — B1A(a)

**The seven characteristics (1 mark each) — with a Banking illustration:**

1. **High Volume** → A bank processes **5,000+ NEFT/RTGS transactions per day** for reconciliation.
2. **Rule-Based** → Reconciliation follows a fixed rule: *IF debit matches credit AND IF beneficiary IFSC is valid THEN mark as success ELSE flag as exception.*
3. **Structured Data** → All transactions come from the **Core Banking System (CBS)** in fixed CSV/SQL format.
4. **Low Exception Rate** → Less than **3%** transactions fail (invalid IFSC, closed accounts).
5. **Stable UI/API** → The CBS interface has not changed in **24 months**.
6. **Repetitive & Manual** → Currently a back-office clerk opens the CBS UI and reconciles row by row.
7. **Measurable ROI** → Manual time = 6 hours/day, with 2% error rate causing customer complaints.

**Conclusion (0 marks but mandatory in KTU 2024 answer scripts):** All seven criteria are satisfied → Process is a **prime RPA candidate**.

---

#### Model Solution — B1A(b) — Quadrant Classification

| Process | Volume | Rule-Based | Quadrant | Verdict |
|---------|--------|------------|----------|---------|
| **P1 — ATM Reconciliation** | High (daily) | High (fixed matching rules) | **Q1** | ✅ Automate |
| **P2 — Branch Strategic Plan** | Low (annual) | Low (judgment-based) | **Q4** | ❌ Do not automate |
| **P3 — Credit Score** | High (per loan applicant) | High (12 fixed ratios) | **Q1** | ✅ Automate |
| **P4 — Board Meeting Minutes** | Low (quarterly) | Low (requires summarisation & judgment) | **Q4** | ❌ Do not automate |

**Justification logic (2 marks for P1, 2 marks for P3, 1.5 marks each for P2 and P4):**
- P1 fits Q1 because both volume and rule-basedness are high — **best RPA candidate**.
- P3 also fits Q1 — the **credit-score formula is deterministic**, and the volume is high since every loan triggers it.
- P2 is in Q4 — both volume and rule-basedness are low; the strategic plan requires human creativity and stakeholder interviews.
- P4 is in Q4 — minutes require summarisation, paraphrasing, and contextual judgment that a traditional bot cannot perform.

---

### Question B1B — *[KTU University Exam – June 2025 Model]*  **(14 Marks)** — **Internal Choice Alternative**

**(a)** Define the **RPA Suitability Scoring Model (RSSM / PSS)**. Using the standard KTU weight table, compute the PSS for the following scenario and recommend an action:

> *"A regional insurance company manually extracts customer details from **250 claim emails per day** into their **legacy Claims Management System (CMS)**. Each email contains a **structured PDF** with 10 fixed fields. The CMS UI has been **unchanged for 3 years**. The current error rate is **1.5%**, and the exception rate is **2%**. The work is done by **4 FTE clerks**, taking **8 minutes per claim**."*

Assign appropriate raw scores (0–10) and justify each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **four categories of processes that should NOT be automated** using traditional RPA. For each, give a one-line real-world example. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — B1B(a) — PSS Computation

**Definition (1 mark):** PSS is a weighted scoring model that evaluates a process on eight criteria, each scored 0–10, multiplied by a normalised weight, to yield a single decision score.

**Raw Score Assignment (with justification — 4 marks):**

| Criterion | Raw Score $s_i$ | Justification |
|-----------|:---:|---------------|
| Volume | 9 | 250/day = ~7500/month, very high |
| Rule-Based | 9 | 10 fixed fields = pure if-then extraction |
| Data Structured | 10 | Structured PDF with fixed template |
| Stability | 10 | 3 years unchanged |
| Exception (inverse) | 9 | Only 2% exceptions |
| Manual Effort | 8 | 4 FTE × 8 hr = 32 person-hr/day |
| Error Cost | 8 | 1.5% transcription errors cause claim delays |
| Compliance | 9 | IRDAI audit and regulatory risk |

**Weighted Computation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PSS} &= (0.20)(9) + (0.20)(9) + (0.15)(10) + (0.10)(10) + (0.10)(9) + (0.10)(8) + (0.10)(8) + (0.05)(9) \\
&= 1.80 + 1.80 + 1.50 + 1.00 + 0.90 + 0.80 + 0.80 + 0.45 \\
\text{PSS} &= 9.05
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verdict:** $\text{PSS} = 9.05 \in [8.0,\ 10.0]$ → 🟢 **Excellent — Automate Immediately via PoC**.

---

#### Model Solution — B1B(b) — Processes That Should NOT Be Automated

1. **Processes requiring human empathy and emotional intelligence** — e.g., counselling a grieving customer, handling a mental-health helpline call. *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Processes requiring physical dexterity and presence** — e.g., repairing a server rack, performing a surgery, or cleaning a manufacturing line. *(1.5 marks)*
3. **Processes requiring complex strategic judgment and creativity** — e.g., deciding a company's 5-year M&A strategy, writing an original research paper. *(2 marks)*
4. **Processes that are highly volatile or constantly changing** — e.g., a government form whose fields change every quarter, or a beta-version UI that is still in development. *(2 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU 2024 Valuation:**
> 1. **Writing "RPA can automate *everything*"** — This is a *zero-mark* answer. Always mention the **boundaries** of RPA.
> 2. **Confusing rule-based with AI-based** — Traditional RPA is *not* AI. Do not claim that RPA can read handwritten notes or understand voice without mentioning OCR/NLP as a separate intelligent layer.
> 3. **Forgetting to justify the PSS scores** — A bare table of numbers without reasoning loses up to **3 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 4. **Skipping the "anti-cases"** — A question on "what can be automated" is **incomplete** without a discussion of "what cannot be automated."
> 5. **Weight sum ≠ 1.0** — If you use a custom weight table, always verify $\sum w_i = 1.0$. A 0.05 error here can shift a process from 🟡 to 🟠 in the band.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] An **automatable process** is rule-based, repetitive, high-volume, stable, structured, low-exception, and ROI-positive.
- [x] The **RPA Lifecycle** begins with **Process Discovery** (Module 1 focus).
- [x] The **2×2 Quadrant Matrix** is the fastest way to classify any process.
- [x] The **RSSM / PSS** model uses **8 criteria** and **weighted scoring** out of 10.
- [x] Decision bands: 🟢 ≥ 8.0, 🟡 6.0–7.99, 🟠 4.0–5.99, 🔴 < 4.0.
- [x] Always include **anti-cases** (empathy, dexterity, strategy, volatility) in your answers.
- [x] Real-world domains: Banking, Insurance, Healthcare, Retail, HR, Telecom, Manufacturing.
- [x] **Hard rule:** Traditional RPA works on **deterministic** processes; judgment-heavy processes need **AI/ML** as a separate intelligent layer.
- [x] The **highest-weight criteria** in PSS are **Volume (0.20)** and **Rule-Basedness (0.20)** — always start your evaluation there.
- [x] The **Process Discovery phase** filters processes through 5 sequential gates (Inventory → Rule → Volume → Data → RSSM).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Bots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Bots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: TYPES OF BOTS IN RPA

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

In the context of **Robotic Process Automation (RPA)**, a **Bot** (short for *robot*) is a software application that automates rule-based, repetitive, and structured digital tasks by mimicking human interactions with digital systems. **Types of Bots** refers to the systematic classification of these software robots based on their **operational autonomy, deployment environment, intelligence level, and functional role** within an enterprise automation lifecycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The classification of bots is foundational to RPA architecture. Examiners frequently test the differentiation between **Attended vs. Unattended Bots**, and the role of **Cognitive / AI-enabled Bots** in modern automation strategies.

### Key Classification Axes

Bots in RPA are categorized along the following primary dimensions:

1. **By Human Intervention** → Attended, Unattended, Hybrid
2. **By Functional Role** → Task Bots, Meta Bots, IQ Bots, Chatbots
3. **By Lifecycle Stage** → Development Bots, Test Bots, Production Bots
4. **By Intelligence Level** → Rule-based Bots, Cognitive Bots, AI Bots

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Office Workforce**
>
> Imagine a large office with different kinds of assistants:
>
> - **Attended Bot** = A *personal assistant sitting next to you* who helps with tasks only when you ask (e.g., "Pull up the customer record").
> - **Unattended Bot** = A *back-office clerk working the night shift* who processes invoices, emails, and reports without anyone watching.
> - **Hybrid Bot** = A *versatile assistant* who can switch between helping you at your desk and finishing tasks alone in the server room.
> - **IQ Bot (Cognitive Bot)** = A *new intern who can read handwritten notes, learn patterns, and make decisions* — not just follow a rigid checklist.
> - **Chatbot** = A *receptionist at the front desk* who talks to customers in natural language.

Just as a company uses different employees for different jobs, RPA platforms use different **types of bots** to automate different categories of work.

### Standard RPA Metrics to Remember

- **FTE (Full-Time Equivalent):** 1 Bot ≈ **1 FTE** of human productivity.
- **Bot Run-Time Window:** Scheduled execution period (e.g., 24/7 for unattended).
- **Bot-to-Human Handoff Latency:** Time taken for escalation in hybrid scenarios.
- **Accuracy Benchmark for IQ Bots:** Typically **> 95%** document extraction accuracy.
- **Bot Density:** Number of bots per 1,000 business processes (Industry benchmark: **~50–150 bots/1000 processes** for mature enterprises).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Bots

### 2.1.1 Attended Bots (Assisted Automation)

**Definition:** Attended bots operate on the **same workstation as a human user** and require human initiation or supervision to execute tasks.

**Operational Characteristics:**

- Triggered by **explicit user commands** (button click, event trigger, or scheduled user action).
- Runs in the **foreground**, often sharing the desktop session.
- Ideal for **front-office processes** where human judgment is needed alongside automation.
- Cannot operate independently when the user logs off.

**Typical Use Cases:**

- Customer service desk support (auto-fetching records during a call).
- Sales order entry assistance.
- Real-time data validation by an agent.
- Helpdesk ticket triaging.

**Industry Tools:** UiPath Assistant, Automation Anywhere **Attended Bot Runner**, Blue Prism **Attended RPA**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Attended"?** Because the bot *attends* to the human's request — it does not act alone. Think of it as a "sidekick" automation.

---

### 2.1.2 Unattended Bots (Autonomous Automation)

**Definition:** Unattended bots execute processes **without human intervention**, typically on dedicated virtual machines or servers, following predefined schedules or triggered by system events.

**Operational Characteristics:**

- Triggered by **schedules, queues, API events, or email arrivals**.
- Runs in **background / RDP sessions** on virtual machines.
- Can operate **24/7, 365 days a year**.
- Capable of **orchestrated multi-bot workflows** via a Control Room / Orchestrator.

**Typical Use Cases:**

- Invoice processing at end-of-day.
- Payroll generation and disbursement.
- Bulk data migration between legacy systems.
- Report generation and email distribution.

**Industry Tools:** UiPath Orchestrator (Unattended), Automation Anywhere **Bot Runner**, Blue Prism **Digital Workforce**.

---

### 2.1.3 Hybrid Bots

**Definition:** Hybrid bots combine the capabilities of **attended and unattended bots**, allowing seamless transitions between human-collaborative and autonomous execution modes.

**Operational Characteristics:**

- A single bot instance can be **invoked by a human** (attended mode) **OR triggered autonomously** (unattended mode).
- Supports **human-in-the-loop escalation** at designated decision points.
- Deployed on **shared infrastructure** with conditional execution logic.

**Typical Use Cases:**

- Loan processing where initial data capture is attended, but credit check and approval is unattended.
- Healthcare claims where a nurse reviews, then the bot finalizes.

---

### 2.1.4 Task Bots

**Definition:** Task bots are the **fundamental unit of automation** — they execute a single, well-defined, rule-based task such as data entry, file movement, or form filling.

**Characteristics:**

- Built using **visual drag-and-drop workflow designers**.
- Operate on **structured digital data**.
- No AI/ML capability.
- Form the **building blocks** of complex automations.

---

### 2.1.5 Meta Bots

**Definition:** Meta bots (also called *Reusable Components* or *Object Libraries*) are **pre-built, modular automation assets** that can be shared across multiple Task Bots without rewriting logic.

**Characteristics:**

- Encapsulate **common business logic** (e.g., "Login to SAP", "Read Excel").
- Promote **reusability, maintainability, and standardization**.
- Reduce development time by **30–50%** in large programs.
- Industry Term: *AA's Bot Store*, *UiPath ReFramework*, *Blue Prism Business Objects*.

---

### 2.1.6 IQ Bots (Cognitive / Intelligent Bots)

**Definition:** IQ bots integrate **Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML)** to handle **unstructured data** (emails, images, PDFs, handwriting) and make **probabilistic decisions**.

**Operational Characteristics:**

- Use **OCR (Optical Character Recognition), NLP (Natural Language Processing), and Computer Vision**.
- Improve accuracy over time through **continuous learning**.
- Achieve **> 90% accuracy** on document understanding after training.
- Industry Leaders: **Automation Anywhere IQ Bot**, **UiPath Document Understanding**, **Microsoft AI Builder**.

**Typical Use Cases:**

- Invoice extraction from scanned PDFs.
- Email sentiment classification and routing.
- Handwritten form digitization.
- Contract clause analysis.

---

### 2.1.7 Chatbots (Conversational Bots)

**Definition:** Chatbots are **text- or voice-based conversational agents** that interact with users via natural language to perform tasks or answer queries.

**Characteristics:**

- Can be **rule-based** (decision tree) or **AI-driven** (LLM/NLU based).
- Deployed on **web, mobile, WhatsApp, Teams, Slack**.
- Can **integrate with RPA** to trigger backend Task Bots (e.g., "Check my order status" → calls RPA bot to query ERP).

**Modern Evolution:** Modern chatbots leverage **Large Language Models (LLMs)** such as GPT, LLaMA, and Claude for generative responses.

---

### 2.1.8 Development, Test, and Production Bots (Lifecycle Bots)

| Bot Type | Environment | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Development Bot** | Dev Studio | Used by developers to build, debug, and unit-test automations. |
| **Test Bot** | Test/UAT Environment | Used by QA teams to validate automation against test cases and perform regression testing. |
| **Production Bot** | Live Production Server | Deployed for real business execution; monitored by Orchestrator. |

---

### 2.1.9 Specialized Bots (Emerging Categories)

- **API Bots:** Interact exclusively via REST/SOAP APIs (no UI interaction).
- **RPA + LLM Bots:** Combine traditional RPA with Generative AI for reasoning tasks.
- **Process Discovery Bots:** Mine user activity logs to **identify automation candidates**.
- **Voice Bots:** Speech-to-text + RPA backend for call center automation.

---

## 2.2 KTU Reference Sheet (High-Yield Summary Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **single most important revision artifact** for KTU Module 1 — Types of Bots.

| **Bot Type** | **Autonomy** | **Trigger Mode** | **Data Type** | **Typical User** | **Deployment** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Attended Bot | Low (Human-led) | User Action / Button | Structured | Front-office staff | Local Workstation |
| Unattended Bot | High (Autonomous) | Schedule / Event | Structured | Back-office | Virtual Machine / Server |
| Hybrid Bot | Medium (Switchable) | User OR Schedule | Structured | Cross-functional | VM + Local |
| Task Bot | Rule-based | Predefined | Structured | All | Any |
| Meta Bot | Reusable component | Called by Task Bot | Structured | Developers | Shared Repository |
| IQ Bot | Cognitive (AI/ML) | Document/Event input | Unstructured | Knowledge workers | Cloud / On-prem |
| Chatbot | Conversational | User message | Text/Voice | Customers/Employees | Web/Mobile |
| Dev / Test / Prod Bot | Lifecycle-specific | Stage-based | All | Devs / QA / Ops | Stage environments |

---

## 2.3 Comparative Analytical Framework

### Decision Matrix: Which Bot to Choose?

$$ P_{score} = w_1 \cdot H_{interaction} + w_2 \cdot V_{volume} + w_3 \cdot D_{complexity} + w_4 \cdot I_{intelligence} $$

Where:

- $P_{score}$ = Process automation suitability score
- $H_{interaction}$ = Degree of human interaction required (0–1)
- $V_{volume}$ = Process volume / frequency
- $D_{complexity}$ = Decision complexity (structured vs. unstructured)
- $I_{intelligence}$ = Need for AI/cognitive capability
- $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4$ = Weighted coefficients, with $\sum w_i = 1$

**Decision Rule:**

$$
B_{type} = \begin{cases}
\text{Attended} & \text{if } H_{interaction} > 0.7 \\
\text{Unattended} & \text{if } V_{volume} > 0.7 \text{ and } D_{complexity} < 0.4 \\
\text{Hybrid} & \text{if } 0.3 \leq H_{interaction} \leq 0.7 \\
\text{IQ Bot} & \text{if } D_{complexity} > 0.6 \text{ and } I_{intelligence} > 0.5 \\
\text{Chatbot} & \text{if front-end conversational interface required}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** This decision matrix is used by RPA Solution Architects during **Process Assessment** and **Tool Selection** phases of the **Automation Delivery Lifecycle (ADLC)**.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Selection Logic

## 3.1 Exhaustive Analytical Derivation: Bot Selection Process

The following derivation walks through the **algorithmic logic** an RPA architect follows to select the appropriate bot type for a given business process.

### Step 1: Process Identification

Identify the candidate process $P_i$ from the process inventory:

$$ P_i = \{T_1, T_2, T_3, \ldots, T_n\} $$

where $T_k$ is the $k$-th task within the process $P_i$.

### Step 2: Attribute Extraction

For each process, extract four key attributes as defined in Section 2.3:

$$
H_{interaction} = \frac{\text{Number of manual decision points}}{\text{Total number of tasks}}
$$

$$
V_{volume} = \frac{\text{Executions per month}}{\text{Max expected executions per month (normalized)}}
$$

$$
D_{complexity} = \frac{\text{Unstructured data ratio} + \text{Judgment decisions}}{2}
$$

$$
I_{intelligence} = \begin{cases}
1.0 & \text{if NLP, OCR, or ML is required} \\
0.5 & \text{if basic pattern matching is sufficient} \\
0.0 & \text{if purely rule-based}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 3: Weighted Scoring

Apply the weighted scoring formula:

$$ P_{score} = w_1 \cdot H_{interaction} + w_2 \cdot V_{volume} + w_3 \cdot D_{complexity} + w_4 \cdot I_{intelligence} $$

**Default Weight Assignment (Industry Standard):**

$$ w_1 = 0.30, \quad w_2 = 0.25, \quad w_3 = 0.25, \quad w_4 = 0.20 $$

### Step 4: Worked Example

**Scenario:** A bank wants to automate *Loan Application Processing*.

Process parameters:
- $H_{interaction} = 0.60$ (loan officer reviews)
- $V_{volume} = 0.80$ (5,000 applications/month)
- $D_{complexity} = 0.55$ (some unstructured documents)
- $I_{intelligence} = 0.70$ (need OCR for income proofs)

**Compute $P_{score}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{score} &= (0.30 \times 0.60) + (0.25 \times 0.80) + (0.25 \times 0.55) + (0.20 \times 0.70) \\
&= 0.18 + 0.20 + 0.1375 + 0.14 \\
&= 0.6575
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:**

- $H_{interaction} = 0.60$ → Falls in Hybrid range
- $D_{complexity} = 0.55$ → Near threshold for IQ Bot
- $I_{intelligence} = 0.70$ → IQ Bot candidate

**Conclusion:** Since $H_{interaction}$ is in the hybrid band **and** $I_{intelligence} > 0.5$, the optimal recommendation is a **Hybrid IQ Bot** (combination of attended front-end and unattended back-end with document understanding).

### Step 5: Final Bot Recommendation Mapping

$$
B_{recommend} = \arg\max_{B_k \in \mathcal{B}} \, \text{fit}(P_i, B_k)
$$

where $\mathcal{B} = \{\text{Attended, Unattended, Hybrid, IQ, Chatbot, Meta}\}$ and $\text{fit}(\cdot)$ is a domain-specific suitability function.

---

## 3.2 Comparative Trade-off Analysis

| **Criteria** | **Attended** | **Unattended** | **Hybrid** | **IQ Bot** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cost per Bot** | Low | Medium | Medium-High | High |
| **Implementation Complexity** | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| **Scalability** | Low | Very High | High | Medium |
| **24/7 Operation** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Partial | ✅ Yes |
| **Handles Unstructured Data** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Human-in-Loop** | ✅ Always | ❌ No | ✅ Conditional | ✅ Optional |
| **Setup Time** | 1–2 weeks | 3–6 weeks | 4–8 weeks | 8–16 weeks |
| **Best Suited For** | Front-office | Back-office | End-to-end | Knowledge work |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A typical **Center of Excellence (CoE)** in a large enterprise maintains a **bot portfolio mix** of approximately **40% Unattended, 35% Attended, 15% IQ Bots, and 10% Hybrid/Chatbots**, based on Gartner 2023 industry surveys.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Master Classification Tree of RPA Bots

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Types of Bots in RPA] --> B[By Human Intervention]
    A --> C[By Functional Role]
    A --> D[By Lifecycle Stage]
    A --> E[By Intelligence Level]

    B --> B1[Attended Bot]
    B --> B2[Unattended Bot]
    B --> B3[Hybrid Bot]

    C --> C1[Task Bot]

    C --> C4[Chatbot]

    D --> D1[Development Bot]
    D --> D2[Test Bot]
    D --> D3[Production Bot]

    E --> E1[Rule-Based Bot]
    E --> E2[Cognitive Bot]
    E --> E3[AI / LLM-Powered Bot]

    B1 --> F1[Front-office assistance]
    B2 --> F2[Back-office 24x7 automation]
    B3 --> F3[Flexible attended OR unattended]

    C3 --> G1[OCR and NLP enabled]
    C4 --> G2[Conversational interface]

    E2 --> H1[Self-learning]
    E3 --> H2[Generative AI integration]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Attended vs. Unattended Bot Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Human_Workstation
        U1[Human User]
        AB[Attended Bot]
    end

    subgraph Virtual_Machine
        UB[Unattended Bot]
        ORCH[Orchestrator / Control Room]
        QUEUE[Work Queue]
    end

    ERP[Enterprise System]
    MAIL[Email Server]
    DB[Database]

    U1 -->|Triggers| AB
    AB -->|Reads / Writes| ERP

    ORCH -->|Schedules / Dispatches| UB
    MAIL -->|Triggers| UB
    UB -->|Reads / Writes| DB
    UB -->|Reports| QUEUE
    QUEUE --> ORCH
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Bot Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[New Process for Automation] --> Q1{Requires human judgment?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, frequent| Q2{Volume high?}

    Q2 -->|Yes| HB[Hybrid Bot]
    Q2 -->|No| AB[Attended Bot]

    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Needs AI/ML?}
    Q3 -->|No| Q5{24x7 required?}

    Q4 -->|Yes| IQB[IQ Bot / Cognitive Bot]
    Q4 -->|No| UNB[Unattended Bot]

    Q5 -->|Yes| UNB2[Unattended Bot]
    Q5 -->|No| AB2[Attended Bot]

    IQB --> OUT[Deploy with AI Model]
    HB --> OUT2[Deploy Hybrid Pipeline]
    UNB --> OUT3[Deploy on VM via Orchestrator]
    AB --> OUT4[Deploy on User Workstation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The flow above is the **standard KTU-recommended selection logic** for choosing the right bot type based on process characteristics. Examiners often ask students to draw or describe such decision flows.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
**Differentiate between Attended and Unattended Bots in RPA. Mention one real-world use case for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Attended Bot** | **Unattended Bot** |
|---|---|---|
| **Human Intervention** | Required for triggering | Not required; fully autonomous |
| **Execution Mode** | Foreground, user workstation | Background, virtual machines |
| **Operational Hours** | Only when user is logged in | 24/7, 365 days |
| **Deployment** | Local desktop | Centralized server / cloud VM |
| **Use Case** | Customer service agent fetching records during a call | Nightly batch processing of invoices in SAP |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher (infrastructure) |

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for key difference, 1 mark for use cases)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is an IQ Bot? Explain how it differs from a conventional Task Bot.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **IQ Bot** is a cognitive RPA bot that integrates **Artificial Intelligence (AI) technologies** such as **Optical Character Recognition (OCR), Natural Language Processing (NLP), and Machine Learning (ML)** to handle **unstructured data** (scanned documents, emails, images) and make **probabilistic, learning-based decisions**.

**Differences from Task Bot:**

1. **Data Handling:** Task Bot works on **structured data**; IQ Bot handles **unstructured data**.
2. **Decision Making:** Task Bot follows **deterministic rules**; IQ Bot uses **probabilistic AI models**.
3. **Learning:** Task Bot is **static**; IQ Bot **improves accuracy over time** through continuous learning.
4. **Use Case Example:** Task Bot → Copying values from Excel to a web form. IQ Bot → Extracting vendor name, date, and amount from a scanned invoice PDF.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for differences)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions for Module 1 carry **14 marks** with an **internal choice** between two questions. Each question typically has **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q3(a). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
**Explain in detail the different types of RPA Bots based on human intervention. Compare their architecture, advantages, limitations, and use cases with suitable diagrams.**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Bots in RPA are classified by human intervention into **three primary categories**: Attended, Unattended, and Hybrid. This classification governs the **deployment architecture**, **licensing model**, and **operational governance** of the bot.

**Attended Bots (2 Marks):**
- **Definition:** Software robots that operate on a **user's workstation** and require **human initiation** to execute tasks.
- **Architecture:** Local installation on the user's desktop, triggered via a **Bot Launcher / Assistant** (e.g., UiPath Assistant, AA Bot Runner).
- **Advantages:**
  - Low implementation cost.
  - Easy to deploy in **front-office** scenarios.
  - Real-time human oversight reduces error risk.
- **Limitations:**
  - Cannot run 24/7.
  - Limited scalability (one bot per user).
- **Use Cases:** Customer service data retrieval, agent-assisted form filling.

**Unattended Bots (2 Marks):**
- **Definition:** Autonomous bots executing **scheduled or event-triggered** tasks on **dedicated virtual machines** without human intervention.
- **Architecture:** Centralized deployment via **Orchestrator / Control Room**, running in **RDP sessions** on VMs.
- **Advantages:**
  - 24/7 operation, high scalability.
  - Centralized monitoring and logging.
  - Best for high-volume back-office processes.
- **Limitations:**
  - Higher infrastructure cost.
  - Complex exception handling required.
- **Use Cases:** Nightly invoice processing, payroll automation, report distribution.

**Hybrid Bots (1 Mark):**
- Combine the strengths of both — **front-end human interaction** when needed and **back-end autonomous execution** for the rest.
- **Use Case:** Loan origination (officer reviews → bot completes the workflow).

**Architectural Diagram (1 Mark):**
*(Draw a simple block diagram showing User → Attended Bot → ERP, and Orchestrator → Unattended Bot → Database — as shown in Section 4.2.)*

---

**Q3(b). [7 Marks]**
**Discuss the role of IQ Bots and Chatbots in modern RPA implementations. How do they extend traditional automation capabilities?**

**Model Answer:**

**IQ Bots (3.5 Marks):**
- **Definition:** Cognitive bots that combine RPA with **AI/ML** to process **unstructured data**.
- **Core Technologies:** OCR, NLP, Computer Vision, Machine Learning, Deep Learning.
- **Functional Workflow:**
  1. **Ingest** unstructured document (PDF, image, email).
  2. **Classify** document type using ML classifier.
  3. **Extract** relevant fields using OCR + NLP.
  4. **Validate** extracted data using confidence scores.
  5. **Learn** from human corrections (Human-in-the-Loop).
  6. **Trigger** downstream Task Bots for structured processing.
- **Use Cases:** Insurance claim processing, KYC document verification, invoice automation.
- **Business Impact:** Reduces manual data entry by **~70%**, improves accuracy to **> 95%**.

**Chatbots (3.5 Marks):**
- **Definition:** **Conversational AI agents** that interact with users via **natural language** (text or voice).
- **Types:**
  - **Rule-based Chatbots** — Decision-tree driven, limited flexibility.
  - **AI Chatbots** — NLU/NLP-driven, context-aware (e.g., Dialogflow, Microsoft Bot Framework, GPT-based).
- **Integration with RPA:**
  - Chatbot acts as the **front-end conversational interface**.
  - User query → Chatbot (intent recognition) → **RPA Bot** (backend execution) → Response back to user.
- **Use Cases:** IT helpdesk automation, HR query resolution, banking customer service, WhatsApp-based order tracking.
- **Modern Trend:** **LLM-powered Chatbots** (GPT-4, Claude, Gemini) enabling generative, context-rich responses.

**Conclusion:** IQ Bots extend RPA into **unstructured data domains**, while Chatbots extend RPA into **conversational user domains** — together forming the foundation of **Hyperautomation**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**Q4(a). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
**Describe the various types of bots in RPA with a focus on their functional roles. Explain how Task Bots, Meta Bots, and IQ Bots differ in terms of reusability, intelligence, and development effort.**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Functional classification of bots is based on the **role they play** within the automation architecture. The key functional types are **Task Bots, Meta Bots, IQ Bots, and Chatbots**.

**Task Bots (2 Marks):**
- **Role:** Execute a **single, atomic, rule-based task** (e.g., read CSV, fill web form, send email).
- **Reusability:** Low — typically used once within one workflow.
- **Intelligence:** None — strictly rule-based.
- **Development Effort:** 2–5 days per task.
- **Example:** A bot that downloads attachments from Gmail and saves them to OneDrive.

**Meta Bots (2 Marks):**
- **Role:** **Reusable automation assets** that encapsulate common logic shared across multiple Task Bots.
- **Reusability:** Very High — used across **hundreds of automations**.
- **Intelligence:** None — still rule-based.
- **Development Effort:** High initial investment (1–2 weeks), but saves **30–50%** future development time.
- **Example:** "Login to SAP" Meta Bot used by 20 different Task Bots.

**IQ Bots (2 Marks):**
- **Role:** Handle **unstructured / semi-structured data** using AI/ML.
- **Reusability:** Medium — reusable for similar document types.
- **Intelligence:** High — OCR, NLP, ML-based learning.
- **Development Effort:** High (4–8 weeks) including model training.
- **Example:** A bot that extracts invoice data from varied PDF formats.

**Comparison Table (included in answer):**

| **Aspect** | **Task Bot** | **Meta Bot** | **IQ Bot** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reusability | Low | Very High | Medium |
| Intelligence | None | None | AI/ML enabled |
| Data Type | Structured | Structured | Unstructured |
| Development Time | 2–5 days | 1–2 weeks | 4–8 weeks |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High |

---

**Q4(b). [7 Marks]**
**Explain the lifecycle-based classification of bots (Development, Test, and Production Bots). Why is environment segregation important in enterprise RPA deployments?**

**Model Answer:**

**Lifecycle-Based Classification (4 Marks):**

RPA bots move through **three lifecycle environments**, each serving a distinct purpose:

1. **Development Bot (Dev Studio Environment):**
   - Used by **RPA developers** to design, code, debug, and unit-test automations.
   - Connected to **non-production systems** and test data.
   - Tools: UiPath Studio, Automation Anywhere Enterprise Client.

2. **Test Bot (Test / UAT Environment):**
   - Used by **QA teams** to validate the automation against **test cases, business requirements, and edge cases**.
   - Performs **regression testing** to ensure new changes do not break existing flows.
   - Often uses **synthetic or anonymized data** for compliance.

3. **Production Bot (Live Environment):**
   - Deployed on **production-grade infrastructure** for **real business execution**.
   - Managed by the **Orchestrator** with **credential vaults, audit logs, and SLA monitoring**.
   - Operates on **live, sensitive business data** with strict access controls.

**Importance of Environment Segregation (3 Marks):**

1. **Risk Mitigation:** Prevents accidental damage to live business systems by untested code.
2. **Data Security & Compliance:** Sensitive customer data stays in production; dev/test use anonymized data — meeting **GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS** standards.
3. **Stable Production Performance:** Production bots are not affected by concurrent development activity.
4. **Audit & Governance:** Clear separation enables **change management, version control, and regulatory auditing**.
5. **Disaster Recovery:** Segregated environments allow quick rollback and isolated troubleshooting.
6. **Cost Control:** Test environments can be **scaled down** during off-peak hours, saving infrastructure costs.

**Conclusion:** Environment segregation is a **non-negotiable best practice** in enterprise RPA CoE (Center of Excellence) governance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. ❌ **Confusing "Attended Bot" with "Chatbot":** They are NOT the same. Attended bots help **human employees** on their workstations; chatbots interact with **end-users/customers** via conversation.
> 2. ❌ **Writing "IQ Bot = AI Bot" without explaining WHY:** You must mention specific AI technologies (OCR, NLP, ML) to score full marks.
> 3. ❌ **Omitting diagrams in 7-mark questions:** KTU examiners often allocate **1–2 marks** for a labeled diagram in 7-mark sub-parts. Always include one.
> 4. ❌ **Not stating examples:** Generic answers without real-world use cases lose 1 mark. Always cite at least **one concrete use case per bot type**.
> 5. ❌ **Mixing up Meta Bot and Task Bot:** Meta Bots are **reusable libraries/components**; Task Bots are **executable automations**. Do not interchange.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Types of Bots)

- [ ] **Bot** = Software robot that mimics human actions on digital systems.
- [ ] **Attended Bot** = Runs on user's workstation, human-triggered, front-office.
- [ ] **Unattended Bot** = Runs on VM, autonomous, 24/7, back-office.
- [ ] **Hybrid Bot** = Switches between attended and unattended modes.
- [ ] **Task Bot** = Single rule-based task, building block of automation.
- [ ] **Meta Bot** = Reusable component / shared library for multiple Task Bots.
- [ ] **IQ Bot** = Cognitive bot with AI/ML (OCR + NLP) for unstructured data.
- [ ] **Chatbot** = Conversational interface (text/voice) integrated with RPA backend.
- [ ] **Dev / Test / Prod Bots** = Lifecycle environments for governance and segregation.
- [ ] **Rule-Based Bot** = Static, deterministic, no learning.
- [ ] **Cognitive Bot** = AI-driven, learns from data, probabilistic decisions.
- [ ] **AI/LLM Bot** = Modern generative bots using LLMs (GPT, Claude, Gemini).
- [ ] **1 Bot ≈ 1 FTE** of human productivity (industry benchmark).
- [ ] **Bot Selection** depends on $H_{interaction}$, $V_{volume}$, $D_{complexity}$, $I_{intelligence}$.
- [ ] **Environment segregation** is mandatory for security, compliance, and risk management.
- [ ] **Typical enterprise bot mix:** 40% Unattended, 35% Attended, 15% IQ, 10% Hybrid/Chatbot.
- [ ] **Key vendors:** UiPath, Automation Anywhere, Blue Prism, Microsoft Power Automate.
- [ ] **Hyperautomation** = RPA + AI + Process Mining + Analytics (evolution beyond simple bots).

### 📌 Mnemonic to Remember Bot Types

> **"A U H — T M I C — D T P"**
>
> **A**ttended · **U**nattended · **H**ybrid — *By Human Intervention*
> **T**ask · **M**eta · **I**Q · **C**hatbot — *By Functional Role*
> **D**ev · **T**est · **P**roduction — *By Lifecycle*

### 🎯 High-Yield KTU Keywords (Use These in Answers to Score)

*"Human-in-the-loop"*, *"Orchestrator"*, *"Virtual Machine"*, *"OCR-enabled"*, *"Cognitive automation"*, *"Hyperautomation"*, *"Center of Excellence (CoE)"*, *"Bot-as-a-Service"*, *"Environment segregation"*, *"Reusable component"*, *"Structured vs. Unstructured data"*, *"Trigger-based execution"*, *"24/7 Digital Workforce"*.

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Types of Bots**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) Examination Standards*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Workloads Which Can Be Automated - RPA Advanced Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Workloads Which Can Be Automated - RPA Advanced Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Workloads Which Can Be Automated** in the context of Robotic Process Automation (RPA) refer to a specific class of business activities, processes, or tasks that satisfy a defined set of technical and operational criteria, making them suitable candidates for delegation to software robots (bots). According to the KTU 2024 Scheme treatment of RPA Advanced Concepts, an *automatable workload* is any deterministic, rule-based, high-volume, low-exception, and structured digital process that can be replicated by a bot without requiring subjective human judgment, cognitive reasoning, or unstructured data interpretation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A workload is deemed *RPA-eligible* only when it operates on **digital, structured inputs**, follows **predetermined business rules**, has a **measurable volume / frequency**, and is **trigger-driven** (time-based, event-based, or manual queue-based).

### 1.2 The Four Pillars of an Automatable Workload

| Pillar | Description |
|---|---|
| **Rule-Based Logic** | Decisions are made via explicit `IF-THEN-ELSE` style rules, not heuristics. |
| **Structured Data** | Inputs reside in fixed-format fields (databases, spreadsheets, ERP screens, forms). |
| **High Volume & Repetitiveness** | The task is executed hundreds or thousands of times per cycle. |
| **Low Cognitive Exception Rate** | The volume of human-judgment-based exceptions is below a defined threshold (typically **< 5% to 10%**). |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Assembly Line Worker vs The Brain Surgeon**
> Imagine a factory where one worker does only one task 8 hours a day — tightening 4 screws on a product as it passes by. Now imagine another worker who has to diagnose a fault in a complex machine using experience and intuition. The first worker is a perfect candidate to be replaced by a robotic arm because every action is **predictable, identical, and rule-based**. The second is not, because the work involves **judgment, context, and creativity**.
> 
> In RPA terms, **automatable workloads are the assembly line workers** of the digital office. They are the repetitive, structured, rule-bound digital chores (like data entry, report generation, invoice matching) that a software bot can perform with **zero fatigue, zero error drift, and 100% auditability** — freeing the human "brain surgeon" to focus on tasks requiring emotional intelligence, reasoning, and design thinking.

### 1.4 Classification Snapshot (KTU Terminology)

- **Rule-based processes** → *Automatable*
- **Judgment-based processes** → *Not automatable* (require AI/ML/Cognitive automation)
- **Hybrid processes** → *Partially automatable with exception queues to humans*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Taxonomy of Automatable Workloads

The KTU 2024 syllabus structures automatable workloads into the following advanced conceptual categories:

#### 2.1.1 Front-Office Workloads
These are customer-facing or user-facing processes that originate from human interaction.

- **Form filling & data capture** (loan applications, KYC updates)
- **Email triage & template-based response generation**
- **Customer onboarding workflows**
- **Call center screen-pop assistance (attended bots)**

#### 2.1.2 Back-Office Workloads
These run in the background, often server-to-server or batch-driven.

- **Report aggregation and distribution**
- **Database-to-database reconciliation**
- **Master data management & cleansing**
- **End-of-day financial postings**

#### 2.1.3 Cross-Functional / Hybrid Workloads
Processes that span multiple departments or systems.

- **Procure-to-Pay (P2P)**
- **Order-to-Cash (O2C)**
- **Record-to-Report (R2R)**
- **Hire-to-Retire (H2R) in HR**

### 2.2 The Process Assessment Framework (Advanced)

Before any workload is automated, it must pass a **Process Suitability Assessment (PSA)**. The advanced framework evaluates each candidate process against the following dimensions:

1. **Process Maturity & Standardization** — Is there an existing SOP (Standard Operating Procedure)?
2. **Data Stability** — Do screen layouts, field names, and APIs remain constant?
3. **Volume & Frequency** — How many FTE (Full-Time Equivalent) hours are spent monthly?
4. **Exception Rate** — What percentage of cases deviate from the happy path?
5. **Compliance & Auditability** — Are there regulatory logging requirements?
6. **ROI Threshold** — Will payback occur within **< 12 to 18 months**?

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM GOLD:** Memorize the following matrix. It is a perennial favorite in KTU Module 1 questions.

| Criterion | Automatable (Yes) | Not Automatable (No) |
|---|---|---|
| **Input Type** | Structured (XLSX, DB, XML) | Unstructured (Image, Free Text) |
| **Decision Logic** | Deterministic rules | Cognitive / Heuristic |
| **Volume** | High (≥ 1000 cases/month) | Low (occasional) |
| **Exception Rate** | < 10% | > 25% |
| **Process Variability** | Low (Stable SOP) | High (Frequent change) |
| **System Access** | Digital UI / API | Physical / Hardware |
| **Trigger** | Event / Schedule / Manual | Pure human intuition |

### 2.4 The Automation Suitability Score (Conceptual Formula)

A common advanced concept in KTU papers is the **Automation Suitability Index (ASI)**, computed conceptually as:

$$
ASI = \left( V \times R \right) - \left( E \times C \right)
$$

Where:
- $V$ = Process Volume (transactions per period), normalized to a 0–1 scale.
- $R$ = Rule-ness Index — the degree to which the process follows deterministic rules (0 to 1).
- $E$ = Exception Rate (0 to 1).
- $C$ = Complexity Coefficient — accounts for the number of systems touched (0 to 1+).

> A process is **strong automation candidate** when $ASI > 0.5$ (threshold is context-dependent).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern enterprises, identifying the right workload is the single highest-leverage activity in any RPA program. Studies consistently show that **60% to 80% of RPA project failures** originate not from the technology, but from **poor workload selection**. Mastery of this topic directly enables roles such as **RPA Business Analyst, Process Discovery Consultant, and Automation Solution Architect**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Automation Suitability Index (ASI)

We will derive the conceptual ASI for a hypothetical KTU-style exam problem to demonstrate the full analytical chain.

**Problem Context (Worked Example):**
A bank evaluates an *Invoice Processing* workload with the following parameters:

- Monthly volume $V_{raw} = 12{,}000$ invoices.
- Maximum benchmark volume $V_{max} = 20{,}000$ invoices.
- The process follows rules in 90% of cases → $R = 0.90$.
- Exception rate $E = 0.08$ (8% require human review).
- Complexity coefficient $C = 0.6$ (touches ERP + Email + Scanner UI).

**Step 1 — Normalize the Volume**

$$
V = \frac{V_{raw}}{V_{max}} = \frac{12{,}000}{20{,}000} = 0.60
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Rule-ness Multiplier**

$$
V \times R = 0.60 \times 0.90 = 0.54
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Exception Penalty**

$$
E \times C = 0.08 \times 0.6 = 0.048
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Automation Suitability Index**

$$
ASI = \left( V \times R \right) - \left( E \times C \right)
$$

$$
ASI = 0.54 - 0.048 = 0.492
$$

**Step 5 — Decision Rule**

$$
ASI = 0.492 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Just below the 0.50 threshold.}
$$

```
Decision: Borderline candidate.
Recommended Action:
  - Reduce complexity C by consolidating systems, OR
  - Reduce exception rate E by pre-classifying invoices with a simple AI model.
  - On re-evaluation, the workload becomes an automatable target.
```

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Workload Categorization by Industry

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry-wise automatable workload mapping** — frequently asked in Part B questions.

| Industry | Automatable Workload | Rule-Based? | Volume | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Banking | KYC document upload, Loan eligibility check | Yes | Very High | Daily batch KYC sync |
| Insurance | Claims first-notice-of-loss (FNOL) intake | Yes | High | Auto-claim registration |
| Healthcare | Patient appointment scheduling | Yes | High | Calendar + EMR sync |
| HR | Onboarding form filling, payslip generation | Yes | High | Offer letter dispatch |
| Retail | Inventory reconciliation, price update | Yes | Very High | ERP-to-Website sync |
| Telecom | SIM activation, billing dispute triage | Yes | High | CDR-based dispute flag |
| Manufacturing | Vendor invoice 3-way match | Yes | Medium | PO-GRN-Invoice reconciliation |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Logic: Identifying an Automation Candidate (The 7-Stage Filter)

```
Stage 1: Is the process digital?                  (If NO → Not automatable)
Stage 2: Is it repetitive (executed frequently)?  (If NO → Defer)
Stage 3: Is the input structured?                  (If NO → Needs Cognitive layer)
Stage 4: Are the rules documented in an SOP?       (If NO → Requires reverse engineering)
Stage 5: Is the exception rate < 10%?              (If NO → Re-evaluate or redesign)
Stage 6: Will the bot deliver ROI in < 18 months?  (If NO → Reject)
Stage 7: Is the process stable over 12+ months?    (If NO → Re-evaluate later)
        (If YES to ALL 7 → STRONG AUTOMATION CANDIDATE)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Insight:** Always present the **negative case** alongside the positive case in long-answer questions. Examiners reward students who can argue *why* a workload is **not** suitable just as convincingly as why it **is**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Workload Selection Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Candidate Business Process] --> B{Is Process Digital?}
    B -- No --> Z1[Reject - Physical Dependency]
    B -- Yes --> C{Is Input Structured?}
    C -- No --> Z2[Route to Cognitive Automation]
    C -- Yes --> D{Is Process Rule Based?}
    D -- No --> Z3[Reject - Requires Judgment]
    D -- Yes --> E{Is Volume High?}
    E -- No --> Z4[Defer - Low ROI]
    E -- Yes --> F{Is Exception Rate Below 10 Percent?}
    F -- No --> Z5[Re-engineer Process First]
    F -- Yes --> G{Is SOP Documented?}
    G -- No --> Z6[Reverse Engineer Rules]
    G -- Yes --> H{Is ROI Within 18 Months?}
    H -- No --> Z7[Reject - Negative Business Case]
    H -- Yes --> I[Approved Automatable Workload]
    I --> J[Deploy Attended Bot]
    I --> K[Deploy Unattended Bot]
    I --> L[Deploy Hybrid Bot]
```

### 4.2 Process Categorization Matrix (Mermaid Quadrant View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Q1["High Volume + Rule Based"]
        Q1A[Invoice Processing]
        Q1B[Payroll Posting]
        Q1C[KYC Document Upload]
    end
    subgraph Q2["Low Volume + Rule Based"]
        Q2A[Quarterly Audit Pull]
        Q2B[Annual Report Compile]
    end
    subgraph Q3["High Volume + Judgment Based"]
        Q3A[Customer Complaint Triage]
        Q3B[Loan Credit Decision]
    end
    subgraph Q4["Low Volume + Judgment Based"]
        Q4A[Strategic Vendor Negotiation]
        Q4B[Crisis Management]
    end
    Q1 --> R1[Strong RPA Candidate]
    Q2 --> R2[Schedule Bots Only]
    Q3 --> R3[Cognitive Automation]
    Q4 --> R4[Human Only]
```

### 4.3 Workload Lifecycle Flow (From Discovery to Retirement)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Process Discovery] --> P2[Process Documentation]
    P2 --> P3[Suitability Assessment]
    P3 --> P4[ROI Calculation]
    P4 --> P5[Bot Development]
    P5 --> P6[UAT and Deployment]
    P6 --> P7[Hypercare Monitoring]
    P7 --> P8[Steady State Operation]
    P8 --> P9[Continuous Improvement]
    P9 --> P10[Retire or Replace]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The above block diagrams replace hand-drawn flowcharts in KTU answer sheets. Examiners accept Mermaid-style logic flows when drawn clearly on paper. Practice drawing the 7-stage filter and the quadrant matrix — they are high-scoring visuals.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any four characteristics of a workload that make it a suitable candidate for RPA automation.*

**Model Answer (12 points / 3 marks):**

A workload is suitable for RPA automation if it possesses the following characteristics:

1. **Rule-based and deterministic logic** — Decisions follow explicit IF-THEN rules with no subjective judgment.
2. **Structured digital inputs** — Data resides in databases, spreadsheets, ERP screens, or web forms.
3. **High volume and repetitiveness** — Executed frequently (hundreds to thousands of times per cycle).
4. **Low exception rate** — Less than 10% of cases require human intervention or deviation from the happy path.
5. **Stable process definition** — The underlying SOP does not change frequently.
6. **Trigger-driven execution** — Can be initiated by time, event, or a digital queue.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Listing **4 characteristics with one-line justification** scores full marks. Generic answers like "it is repetitive" without quantification will lose a mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between a workload suitable for attended RPA bots and one suitable for unattended RPA bots. Give one example for each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Attended Bot | Unattended Bot |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Manual (user-invoked) | Schedule / Event-driven |
| **User Interaction** | Required during execution | None — runs in the background |
| **Workload Type** | Front-office, real-time | Back-office, batch |
| **Example** | Customer service rep invoking a screen-pop bot to fetch account history during a call. | Nightly bot that downloads 5,000 invoices, validates them, and posts to ERP. |
| **Deployment** | User workstation | Centralized server / virtual machine |

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
*(a)* Explain the **Process Suitability Assessment (PSA)** framework used to identify automation candidates in detail. List the key dimensions evaluated. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A logistics company processes 18,000 shipment status updates daily across three legacy systems. The rules are documented, exception rate is 6%, and the process is expected to remain stable for the next two years. Apply the **Automation Suitability Index (ASI)** concept and determine if this workload should be automated. Assume $V_{max} = 25{,}000$, $R = 0.92$, $C = 0.7$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Process Suitability Assessment (PSA) Framework — 7 marks**

The PSA framework is a structured methodology used by RPA Business Analysts to evaluate whether a given business process qualifies for automation. The framework consists of the following six key dimensions:

1. **Process Maturity & Documentation** — Existence of a written, current SOP. Score 0–5.
2. **Data Stability** — Stability of UI layouts, field names, and APIs over time.
3. **Volume & Frequency** — Number of executions per day/week/month.
4. **Exception Rate** — Percentage of cases that deviate from the standard path.
5. **Compliance & Auditability** — Ability of the bot to log every action for audit.
6. **ROI Threshold** — Payback period, FTE savings, and licensing cost recovery.

A scoring rubric assigns weighted points to each dimension. Processes scoring above a **predefined threshold (typically 70/100)** are queued for the **Automation Pipeline**; others are either re-engineered or rejected.

**(b) ASI Calculation — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- $V_{raw} = 18{,}000$
- $V_{max} = 25{,}000$
- $R = 0.92$
- $E = 0.06$
- $C = 0.7$

**Step 1 — Normalize Volume**

$$
V = \frac{V_{raw}}{V_{max}} = \frac{18{,}000}{25{,}000} = 0.72
$$

**Step 2 — Rule-ness Product**

$$
V \times R = 0.72 \times 0.92 = 0.6624
$$

**Step 3 — Exception Penalty**

$$
E \times C = 0.06 \times 0.7 = 0.042
$$

**Step 4 — Final ASI**

$$
ASI = 0.6624 - 0.042 = 0.6204
$$

**Step 5 — Decision**

$$
ASI = 0.6204 \;\;>\;\; 0.50
$$

**Conclusion:** The shipment status update workload is a **strong automation candidate** and should be prioritized for bot deployment.

---

**Q3B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified] — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**
*(a)* Discuss the **four pillars of an automatable workload** with one industry example for each pillar. Explain why a workload failing any one pillar becomes a poor RPA candidate. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Compare and contrast **attended, unattended, and hybrid RPA bots** in terms of trigger, deployment, use case, and example workload. Present the answer in a structured table. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) The Four Pillars — 7 marks**

1. **Rule-Based Logic** — The workload must be governed by deterministic business rules. *Example: Auto-approval of employee leave requests based on balance and manager approval rules.* Without this pillar, the bot cannot make predictable decisions.

2. **Structured Data** — Inputs must reside in fixed-format digital systems. *Example: A bank processing transactions from a Core Banking System.* Unstructured inputs (scanned images, free text) require AI/OCR, which falls under cognitive automation, not pure RPA.

3. **High Volume & Repetitiveness** — The task should consume measurable FTE hours. *Example: Daily reconciliation of 10,000 ledger entries.* Low-volume tasks yield no ROI.

4. **Low Exception Rate** — Exception cases should remain under 10%. *Example: Payroll posting with 99% standardized pay components.* A high exception rate forces constant human supervision, negating RPA benefits.

**Why Failure of Any Pillar Disqualifies a Workload:**
A workload that fails even one pillar introduces **non-determinism, ambiguity, or infeasibility**, forcing the project into a hybrid human-bot model that erodes the ROI and increases maintenance cost. For example, a workload with structured data but high exception rate (60%) would require a human to handle the majority of cases — making the bot economically unjustifiable.

**(b) Comparison Table — 7 marks**

| Parameter | Attended Bot | Unattended Bot | Hybrid Bot |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Manual (user-invoked) | Schedule / Event | Both manual and scheduled |
| **User Interaction** | Required during run | None | Optional during run |
| **Deployment Location** | User workstation | Central server / VM | Mixed |
| **Workload Type** | Front-office | Back-office | Cross-functional |
| **Example** | Customer service screen-pop | Nightly invoice posting | Order processing with manual approval gate |
| **Typical ROI** | Productivity boost | FTE replacement | End-to-end process automation |
| **Failure Recovery** | User-driven | Auto-retry / alert | Mixed |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing "it is repetitive" without defining *what is repetitive* and *how often*. Examiners expect a quantified statement.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **attended bot with chatbot**. They are entirely different — attended bot automates UI tasks on the user's machine, while a chatbot automates conversational text interaction.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In ASI calculations, students frequently forget to **normalize the volume** and directly multiply the raw figure, leading to inflated, incorrect indices. Always normalize $V$ to a 0–1 scale.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Listing characteristics but **not explaining the consequences** of failure. A 7-mark question requires justification, not just enumeration.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Module 1:**

- ☐ An **automatable workload** is *rule-based, structured, high-volume, low-exception*, and *trigger-driven*.
- ☐ The **four pillars**: Rule-based logic, Structured data, High volume, Low exception rate.
- ☐ **Process Suitability Assessment (PSA)** is the gatekeeper before any bot development begins.
- ☐ **Automation Suitability Index** formula: $ASI = (V \times R) - (E \times C)$, with threshold typically **0.50**.
- ☐ **Attended bots** = manual trigger, user-side, front-office. **Unattended bots** = scheduled, server-side, back-office. **Hybrid bots** = combination of both.
- ☐ **Front-office workloads** = customer-facing. **Back-office workloads** = batch/server-side. **Cross-functional workloads** = multi-department.
- ☐ Common automatable industries: Banking, Insurance, Healthcare, HR, Retail, Telecom, Manufacturing.
- ☐ **60% to 80% of RPA project failures** stem from poor workload selection — not from technology defects.
- ☐ An **SOP (Standard Operating Procedure)** must exist and be current before automation begins.
- ☐ Always quantify: replace *"the task is repetitive"* with *"the task executes 12,000 times per month"*.
- ☐ Unstructured data and judgment-based logic push the workload from **RPA** into **Cognitive Automation** territory (AI/ML/NLP domain).
- ☐ **ROI threshold** for KTU exam framing: payback period should ideally be **< 12 to 18 months**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standardization of Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Standardization of Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standardization of Processes** is the systematic methodology of establishing a uniform, documented, and repeatable framework of rules, procedures, and workflows that ensures identical execution of a business activity regardless of the human operator, geographic location, or time of execution. In the context of **Robotic Process Automation (RPA)**, standardization refers to the deliberate engineering of business processes into a deterministic, rule-based, and exception-minimized form so that they become *automation-ready* and *bot-deployable*.

According to **KTU 2024 Scheme PEAIT415 – Module 1** learning outcomes, standardization is positioned as the **foundational prerequisite phase** of the RPA implementation lifecycle. Without a standardized process, automation leads to amplifying existing inefficiencies rather than eliminating them — a phenomenon widely referred to in industry as *"automating the chaos."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The McDonald's Big Mac

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think about how McDonald's prepares a Big Mac in Kochi, in Tokyo, and in New York. The patty weight is **45.4 grams (± 0.5g)**, the bun is toasted for **exactly 35 seconds**, and the sauce is dispensed from an automated nozzle. The taste and presentation are identical across 40,000+ outlets worldwide. This is **process standardization** at its purest — the *process itself has become so deterministic that it can be executed by a robot (or any human) with zero variance*.

In RPA terms, the Big Mac process is **bot-ready** because every step has:
1. A **defined input** (raw ingredients)
2. A **documented procedure** (SOP)
3. A **measurable output** (taste, weight, appearance)
4. **Zero decision variability** (no chef decides "let me add more sauce today")

## 1.3 Why Standardization is the Bedrock of RPA

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU explicitly tests the linkage between *standardization* and *RPA feasibility*. Memorize this statement: *"RPA can only automate what has already been standardized."*

| Without Standardization | With Standardization |
|---|---|
| Process has 5 decision branches handled differently by 5 employees | Process has 1 deterministic path documented in an SOP |
| Bot encounters 20 exception cases per 100 transactions | Bot encounters <2 exception cases per 100 transactions |
| Automation ROI is **negative** (more rework than savings) | Automation ROI is **3x to 10x** within 12 months |

## 1.4 Core Components of a Standardized Process

Every standardized process in RPA is characterized by **four pillars**:

1. **Defined Inputs** — Data formats, triggers, sources are pre-specified.
2. **Deterministic Steps** — Each action is sequenced, named, and time-bound.
3. **Documented Exceptions** — Edge cases are catalogued with explicit handling rules.
4. **Measurable Outputs** — KPIs (cycle time, accuracy, throughput) are quantified.

These four pillars are evaluated against KTU Course Outcome **CO1**: *"Understand the fundamental concepts of Robotic Process Automation."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Standardization Maturity Pyramid

Standardization is not a binary state — it is a **5-tier maturity gradient**. KTU examiners frequently test this hierarchy because it demonstrates a student's understanding of *progression* rather than mere recall.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5 Levels of Process Standardization Maturity (KTU High-Yield Concept):**

1. **Level 1 — Ad-hoc (Chaotic):** Every employee executes the process differently. No documentation exists. ❌ **Not RPA-ready.**
2. **Level 2 — Repeatable:** Informal knowledge; the process works but is person-dependent. ⚠️ **High RPA risk.**
3. **Level 3 — Defined:** Process is documented in SOPs and flowcharts. ✅ **RPA-ready.**
4. **Level 4 — Managed:** Process is measured with KPIs and continuously improved (CMM Level 4). ✅✅ **Highly RPA-suitable.**
5. **Level 5 — Optimized:** Process is under statistical control and self-optimizing (Six Sigma). ✅✅✅ **Gold standard for RPA.**

## 2.2 The 7-Step Standardization Methodology

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **KTU board-favorite** framework. Questions worth up to **14 marks** in ESE are routinely framed around it.

| Step | Activity | Deliverable | KTU Mapping |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| **1** | **Process Identification** | List of candidate processes from AS-IS analysis | Discovery |
| **2** | **Process Discovery & Mining** | Process maps via Celonis, UiPath Process Mining | Documentation |
| **3** | **AS-IS Process Mapping** | Swim-lane BPMN diagrams of current state | Analysis |
| **4** | **Variance & Exception Analysis** | Pareto chart of deviation types | Root-cause |
| **5** | **TO-BE Process Design** | Optimized, exception-minimized flowchart | Design |
| **6** | **SOP Authoring** | Standard Operating Procedure document | Standardization |
| **7** | **SLA & KPI Definition** | Cycle time, accuracy, exception rate baselines | Measurement |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Process Standardization Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Formula Block for KTU 2024 ESE:** Memorize these **four equations**. They are *guaranteed* to appear in either Part A derivations or Part B numerical sub-parts.

### Formula 1: Process Standardization Index (PSI)

$$
\text{PSI} = \frac{N_{\text{standardized}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%
$$

Where:
- $N_{\text{standardized}}$ = Number of processes meeting the 4 standardization pillars
- $N_{\text{total}}$ = Total number of identified processes

**Unit:** Percentage (%) **Target Value:** ≥ **85%** for RPA-readiness.

### Formula 2: Process Variability Reduction (PVR)

$$
\text{PVR} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{AS-IS}} - \sigma_{\text{TO-BE}}}{\sigma_{\text{AS-IS}}} \times 100\%
$$

Where:
- $\sigma_{\text{AS-IS}}$ = Standard deviation of cycle time in current state
- $\sigma_{\text{TO-BE}}$ = Standard deviation of cycle time in standardized state

**Unit:** Percentage (%) **Interpretation:** Higher PVR = better standardization outcome.

### Formula 3: Bot-Readiness Score (BRS)

$$
\text{BRS} = w_1 \cdot D + w_2 \cdot R + w_3 \cdot E + w_4 \cdot V
$$

Where:
- $D$ = Determinism score (0–1) — how rule-based the process is
- $R$ = Repeatability score (0–1) — consistency across executions
