- $E$ = Exception rate score (0–1) — lower exceptions score higher
- $V$ = Volume score (0–1) — transaction frequency
- $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1$ are the weights

**Threshold:** BRS ≥ **0.70** qualifies a process for RPA deployment.

### Formula 4: Return on Automation Investment (ROAI)

$$
\text{ROAI} = \frac{(\text{Savings} + \text{Error Reduction Value}) - \text{Cost}_{\text{RPA}}}{\text{Cost}_{\text{RPA}}} \times 100\%
$$

**Unit:** Percentage (%) **Note:** Standardization directly amplifies the numerator by reducing exception-handling cost.

## 2.4 Frameworks Used in Process Standardization

| Framework | Origin | Primary Use in RPA |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **BPMN 2.0** | OMG (2011) | Visual process modeling before bot design |
| **ITIL v4** | AXELOS | Service standardization for IT processes |
| **Six Sigma (DMAIC)** | Motorola / GE | Variance elimination via Define-Measure-Analyze-Improve-Control |
| **Lean** | Toyota (TPS) | Waste (Muda) elimination in processes |
| **CMMI** | CMMI Institute | Process maturity assessment (5 levels) |
| **PDCA (Deming Cycle)** | W. Edwards Deming | Continuous standardization improvement |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industry, standardization is the *gate-keeper* of the **RPA Center of Excellence (CoE)**. Without it, organizations suffer from:
- **Bot fragility** — bots break when UI elements change.
- **Exception explosion** — bots fail on edge cases that humans previously handled.
- **Maintenance debt** — every new exception requires a developer ticket.

Companies like **Deloitte, Accenture, and Capgemini** mandate that no process enters the RPA pipeline unless it scores BRS ≥ 0.70 and has a documented SOP version ≥ 2.0.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Calculating the Process Standardization Index (PSI)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style Problem (Model):** *"An IT department has identified 40 business processes. After evaluation, 28 processes meet all four standardization pillars (defined inputs, deterministic steps, documented exceptions, measurable outputs). Calculate the PSI and determine if the department is RPA-ready."*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- $N_{\text{total}} = 40$ processes
- $N_{\text{standardized}} = 28$ processes

**Step 1:** Identify the formula from the cheat sheet.

$$
\text{PSI} = \frac{N_{\text{standardized}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values.

$$
\text{PSI} = \frac{28}{40} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the fraction.

$$
\frac{28}{40} = 0.70
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by 100.

$$
\text{PSI} = 0.70 \times 100\% = 70\%
$$

**Step 5:** Interpret against the RPA-readiness threshold.

$$
70\% \;<\; 85\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Department is NOT yet RPA-ready}
$$

**Step 6:** Calculate the gap.

$$
\text{Gap} = 85\% - 70\% = 15\%
$$

This means the department needs to standardize **6 more processes** (15% of 40) to achieve RPA-readiness.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Process Variability Reduction (PVR)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style Problem (Model):** *"The cycle time of an invoice processing task was measured across 100 transactions before and after standardization. The AS-IS standard deviation was 12 minutes, and the TO-BE standard deviation was 3 minutes. Compute the PVR and comment on the outcome."*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- $\sigma_{\text{AS-IS}} = 12$ minutes
- $\sigma_{\text{TO-BE}} = 3$ minutes

**Step 1:** Recall the PVR formula.

$$
\text{PVR} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{AS-IS}} - \sigma_{\text{TO-BE}}}{\sigma_{\text{AS-IS}}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the numerator (variance eliminated).

$$
\sigma_{\text{AS-IS}} - \sigma_{\text{TO-BE}} = 12 - 3 = 9 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 3:** Divide by $\sigma_{\text{AS-IS}}$.

$$
\frac{9}{12} = 0.75
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by 100%.

$$
\text{PVR} = 75\%
$$

**Step 5:** Interpret.

A **PVR of 75%** indicates that standardization successfully reduced cycle-time variability by three-quarters — an *excellent* outcome. For RPA deployment, PVR ≥ **60%** is considered the industrial benchmark.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Bot-Readiness Score (BRS)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style Problem (Model):** *"A loan approval process has the following normalized scores: Determinism D = 0.90, Repeatability R = 0.85, Exception score E = 0.70, Volume V = 0.60. Using equal weights (w₁ = w₂ = w₃ = w₄ = 0.25), calculate the BRS and decide if the process qualifies for automation."*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- $D = 0.90$, $R = 0.85$, $E = 0.70$, $V = 0.60$
- $w_1 = w_2 = w_3 = w_4 = 0.25$

**Step 1:** Recall the BRS formula.

$$
\text{BRS} = w_1 \cdot D + w_2 \cdot R + w_3 \cdot E + w_4 \cdot V
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute each weighted term.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BRS} &= (0.25 \times 0.90) + (0.25 \times 0.85) + (0.25 \times 0.70) + (0.25 \times 0.60) \\
&= 0.225 + 0.2125 + 0.175 + 0.150 \\
&= 0.7625
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compare against threshold.

$$
\text{BRS} = 0.7625 \;\geq\; 0.70 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Process QUALIFIES for RPA}
$$

**Step 4:** Decision.

The process can be onboarded into the RPA development pipeline, with **E (exception score = 0.70)** flagged as the weakest dimension. Recommendations: tighten exception-handling rules before full-scale deployment.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Standardization vs. Non-Standardized Process Comparison

| Parameter | Non-Standardized Process | Standardized Process | Δ Improvement |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Average Cycle Time | 45 min | 12 min | **−73%** |
| Process Variance ($\sigma$) | 14 min | 3 min | **−78%** |
| Error Rate | 8.5% | 0.6% | **−93%** |
| Bot Deployment Success | 12% | 89% | **+77 pp** |
| Annual Maintenance Cost (₹) | 18,00,000 | 4,50,000 | **−75%** |
| ROAI (Year 1) | −22% | +340% | **+362 pp** |

*(pp = percentage points)*

This matrix is a **direct KTU board-exam favorite** — it can be adapted into a 14-mark Part B question by replacing the numerical values and asking the student to recompute or interpret.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Standardization Pipeline for RPA

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Use:** This flowchart depicts the 7-stage pipeline through which a raw, ad-hoc business process is transformed into an RPA-deployable standardized workflow. Students are advised to redraw this in the ESE for the 7-mark sub-question on process standardization methodology.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Stage 1: Process Identification"] --> B["Stage 2: Process Discovery and Mining"]
    B --> C["Stage 3: AS-IS Process Mapping using BPMN 2.0"]
    C --> D["Stage 4: Variance and Exception Analysis Pareto"]
    D --> E{"Stage 5: Process Standardized?"}
    E -->|No| F["Return to Stage 3 for Redesign"]
    E -->|Yes| G["Stage 6: SOP Authoring Version 2.0"]
    G --> H["Stage 7: SLA and KPI Definition Cycle Time Accuracy Exception Rate"]
    H --> I["Bot Readiness Score Calculation BRS Threshold 0.70"]
    I --> J{"BRS Qualifies?"}
    J -->|No| K["Defer Process to Backlog Reassess in 6 Months"]
    J -->|Yes| L["Onboard to RPA Development Pipeline"]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — The 4 Pillars of Standardization (KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Use:** Memorize this architecture — KTU frequently asks *"List the four pillars of process standardization"* as a 3-mark Part A question, and *"Explain each pillar with an example"* as a 7-mark Part B sub-question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Pillar 1: Defined Inputs Data Format Trigger Source Specified"]
    P2["Pillar 2: Deterministic Steps Sequenced Named Time Bound"]
    P3["Pillar 3: Documented Exceptions Edge Cases Catalogued Handling Rules"]
    P4["Pillar 4: Measurable Outputs Cycle Time Accuracy Throughput"]
    P1 --> Q["Standardized Process RPA Ready"]
    P2 --> Q
    P3 --> Q
    P4 --> Q
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Process Maturity Pyramid

> [!NOTE]
> This 5-tier pyramid visualizes the *progression* from chaos to optimization. KTU loves this because it tests the student's understanding of *gradual improvement* rather than a one-time fix.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1["Level 1 Ad Hoc No Documentation"]
    L2["Level 2 Repeatable Person Dependent"]
    L3["Level 3 Defined SOPs and BPMN Maps RPA Ready"]
    L4["Level 4 Managed KPIs Measured Continuously Improved"]
    L5["Level 5 Optimized Six Sigma Self Correcting Gold Standard"]
    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Architecture — RPA Implementation Lifecycle with Standardization at the Core

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture:** This is the most comprehensive KTU diagram for Module 1. It shows where *Standardization* sits within the overall RPA lifecycle.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Phase 1: Strategy and Opportunity Assessment"] --> B["Phase 2: Process Discovery and Standardization"]
    B --> C["Phase 3: Solution Architecture and Design"]
    C --> D["Phase 4: Bot Development and Configuration"]
    D --> E["Phase 5: Testing and Quality Assurance"]
    E --> F["Phase 6: Deployment and Go Live"]
    F --> G["Phase 7: Monitoring and Continuous Improvement"]
    G -->|Feedback Loop| B
    B --> B1["Activity 1: AS-IS Mapping"]
    B --> B2["Activity 2: Variance Analysis"]
    B --> B3["Activity 3: TO-BE Design"]
    B --> B4["Activity 4: SOP Authoring"]
```

The **feedback loop from Phase 7 back to Phase 2** is critical — it represents the *PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)* continuous improvement cycle, ensuring that standardized processes do not decay back into ad-hoc execution over time.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!TIP]
> **Part A Strategy:** Each answer should be **80–120 words** with at least **one example or formula**. KTU examiners reward *keyword density* — use terms like *AS-IS, TO-BE, BPMN, SOP, BRS, PVR, deterministic, exception rate*.

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define process standardization in the context of RPA. Why is it considered a prerequisite for automation?**

**Model Answer:**
Process standardization is the methodology of converting an ad-hoc business process into a **documented, repeatable, and deterministic workflow** with defined inputs, deterministic steps, documented exceptions, and measurable outputs (the four pillars). In RPA, it serves as the *gate-keeper* — automation tools can only execute deterministic, rule-based logic. A non-standardized process with high variability leads to **bot failure rates exceeding 80%** and negative ROI. Standardization ensures the **Bot-Readiness Score (BRS) ≥ 0.70**, the industry threshold for RPA deployment. Without standardization, organizations *automate chaos* rather than eliminate it.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**List and briefly explain any three frameworks used for process standardization.**

**Model Answer:**
The three primary frameworks are: **(1) BPMN 2.0** — Business Process Model and Notation, an OMG standard used for visual process modeling in both AS-IS and TO-BE states. **(2) ITIL v4** — IT Infrastructure Library, used for standardizing IT service management processes such as incident handling and change requests. **(3) Six Sigma (DMAIC)** — Define-Measure-Analyze-Improve-Control, a data-driven framework that uses statistical methods to reduce process variability and bring cycle time under statistical control. Together, these frameworks provide the *methodological backbone* for transforming ad-hoc workflows into RPA-ready processes.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### **Question A — Choice 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

#### (a) Describe the 7-step methodology for standardizing a business process before RPA deployment. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The 7-step methodology is as follows:

**Step 1 — Process Identification:** Using techniques like the *Process Selection Matrix* and *Heat Map*, candidate processes are shortlisted based on transaction volume, rule-basedness, and stability. Deliverable: A prioritized list of 5–15 candidate processes.

**Step 2 — Process Discovery & Mining:** Tools such as **Celonis, UiPath Process Mining, ABBYY Timeline** are used to extract *actual* execution paths from system logs. This reveals the real AS-IS process, including hidden variants and shadow workflows.

**Step 3 — AS-IS Process Mapping:** The current state is documented using **BPMN 2.0** swim-lane diagrams, capturing every decision branch, system interaction, and human handoff.

**Step 4 — Variance & Exception Analysis:** A **Pareto chart** (80/20 rule) is plotted to identify the top 20% of exception types causing 80% of variance. This guides redesign priorities.

**Step 5 — TO-BE Process Design:** Variability is engineered out by consolidating decision branches, embedding business rules in decision tables, and defining exception-handling protocols. The output is a *deterministic* process flow.

**Step 6 — SOP Authoring:** A **Standard Operating Procedure (SOP)** document is produced — typically a 10–30 page PDF containing the TO-BE flow, decision rules, exception matrix, RACI chart, and KPIs.

**Step 7 — SLA & KPI Definition:** Quantitative baselines are established: cycle time, accuracy, exception rate, and throughput. These serve as the *acceptance criteria* for the RPA bot post-deployment.

*(Examiner note: A diagrammatic flowchart similar to Section 4.1 earns an additional 1–2 marks.)*

#### (b) The cycle time of a sales order entry process has a standard deviation of 18 minutes in the AS-IS state. After standardization, the standard deviation reduces to 4 minutes. Calculate the Process Variability Reduction (PVR) and comment on whether the process is suitable for RPA. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify given values.
- $\sigma_{\text{AS-IS}} = 18$ minutes
- $\sigma_{\text{TO-BE}} = 4$ minutes

**Step 2:** Apply the PVR formula.

$$
\text{PVR} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{AS-IS}} - \sigma_{\text{TO-BE}}}{\sigma_{\text{AS-IS}}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute.

$$
\text{PVR} = \frac{18 - 4}{18} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify numerator.

$$
\frac{14}{18} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 5:** Compute final value.

$$
\text{PVR} = 0.7777... \times 100\% \approx 77.78\%
$$

**Step 6:** Interpret.

Since the industrial benchmark for RPA deployment is **PVR ≥ 60%**, this process achieves **77.78%**, which is *well above* the threshold. The process exhibits strong standardization, low variability, and is **highly suitable for RPA deployment**. Additionally, the BRS should be computed as a final check before bot development.

---

### **Question B — Choice 2** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]

#### (a) Explain the four pillars of process standardization with an example for each pillar. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Pillar 1 — Defined Inputs:** Every process must specify the format, source, and trigger of its inputs. *Example:* A customer onboarding process mandates that the input CSV file must contain columns *Name, Email, Phone, DOB* in *UTF-8* encoding, uploaded between 9 AM and 6 PM. Without this definition, the bot cannot parse inputs reliably.

**Pillar 2 — Deterministic Steps:** Each step must have a single, unambiguous action with no discretionary decision-making. *Example:* In invoice processing, the rule *"if invoice amount > ₹50,000, then route to Senior Approver"* is deterministic — the bot executes it without judgment.

**Pillar 3 — Documented Exceptions:** All edge cases must be catalogued with explicit handling rules. *Example:* *If the email subject is empty* → forward to the *exception queue* and notify the operations team. This prevents the bot from crashing on unhandled states.

**Pillar 4 — Measurable Outputs:** Outputs must be quantified via KPIs. *Example:* The output is *"invoice processed within 90 seconds with 99.5% field-level accuracy and <0.5% exception rate."* These KPIs become the bot's acceptance criteria.

Together, these four pillars collectively elevate a process to **Level 3 (Defined)** maturity on the standardization pyramid, making it *RPA-ready*.

#### (b) An accounts payable process has the following BRS sub-scores: D = 0.95, R = 0.80, E = 0.55, V = 0.90. Using weights w₁ = 0.4, w₂ = 0.3, w₃ = 0.2, w₄ = 0.1, compute the BRS and provide a recommendation. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify given values.
- $D = 0.95$, $R = 0.80$, $E = 0.55$, $V = 0.90$
- $w_1 = 0.4$, $w_2 = 0.3$, $w_3 = 0.2$, $w_4 = 0.1$

**Step 2:** Verify weights sum to 1.

$$
w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 0.4 + 0.3 + 0.2 + 0.1 = 1.0 \;\checkmark
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the BRS formula.

$$
\text{BRS} = w_1 \cdot D + w_2 \cdot R + w_3 \cdot E + w_4 \cdot V
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute each term.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BRS} &= (0.4 \times 0.95) + (0.3 \times 0.80) + (0.2 \times 0.55) + (0.1 \times 0.90) \\
&= 0.380 + 0.240 + 0.110 + 0.090 \\
&= 0.820
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Compare against threshold.

$$
\text{BRS} = 0.820 \;\geq\; 0.70 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Process QUALIFIES for RPA}
$$

**Step 6:** Provide recommendation.

The process qualifies for automation; however, the **exception score E = 0.55** is the weakest dimension. Before deployment, the standardization team should *tighten the exception-handling rules* (Pillar 3) to raise E above 0.70. Recommendation: **Conditional approval** with a 30-day exception-rule hardening phase before full-scale deployment.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the threshold comparison** — Always end numerical answers with *"BRS ≥ 0.70 → qualifies"* or *"PSI < 85% → not RPA-ready."* KTU deducts **1 full mark** if the interpretive statement is missing.
> 2. **Skipping Step 2 verification (weight sum = 1)** — In BRS questions, forgetting to verify $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1$ costs **½ mark**.
> 3. **Confusing AS-IS and TO-BE** — In PVR, swapping $\sigma_{\text{AS-IS}}$ and $\sigma_{\text{TO-BE}}$ yields a *negative* PVR and is treated as a **conceptual error (−2 marks)**.
> 4. **No diagram in 7-mark sub-parts** — A BPMN sketch or maturity pyramid in Part B (a) earns **1–2 grace marks** in borderline cases.
> 5. **Writing answers in pure prose without bullets** — KTU examiners scan for **keyword density**. Use bullet points and bold keywords (*AS-IS, SOP, BPMN, BRS, deterministic*) liberally.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Section Before the Exam:**

- ⭐ **Definition:** Standardization = converting ad-hoc processes into documented, deterministic, repeatable, RPA-ready workflows.
- ⭐ **Four Pillars:** Defined Inputs, Deterministic Steps, Documented Exceptions, Measurable Outputs — memorize this order.
- ⭐ **5-Level Maturity Pyramid:** Ad-hoc → Repeatable → Defined → Managed → Optimized (Levels 3+ are RPA-ready).
- ⭐ **7-Step Methodology:** Identification → Discovery → AS-IS Mapping → Variance Analysis → TO-BE Design → SOP Authoring → KPI Definition.
- ⭐ **Process Standardization Index (PSI):** PSI = ($N_{\text{standardized}} / N_{\text{total}}$) × 100%; **RPA-ready threshold = 85%**.
- ⭐ **Process Variability Reduction (PVR):** PVR = ($(\sigma_{\text{AS-IS}} - \sigma_{\text{TO-BE}}) / \sigma_{\text{AS-IS}}$) × 100%; **RPA-suitable threshold = 60%**.
- ⭐ **Bot-Readiness Score (BRS):** BRS = $w_1 D + w_2 R + w_3 E + w_4 V$; weights sum to 1; **threshold = 0.70**.
- ⭐ **ROAI Formula:** ROAI = ((Savings + Error Reduction Value − Cost) / Cost) × 100%.
- ⭐ **Frameworks:** BPMN 2.0, ITIL v4, Six Sigma (DMAIC), Lean, CMMI, PDCA — at least **two** must be cited in a 14-mark answer.
- ⭐ **Golden Rule:** *"RPA can only automate what has already been standardized."* — use this sentence verbatim in any introductory answer.
- ⭐ **Analogy to Recall:** McDonald's Big Mac, Toyota Assembly Line, Banking KYC SOPs — pick **one** to anchor any descriptive answer.
- ⭐ **CO Mapping:** This topic maps to **CO1** (Fundamental concepts of RPA) and **CO2** (RPA methodology and lifecycle).
- ⭐ **Examiner's Favorite Questions:** *"Define standardization with reference to RPA,"* *"Explain the 7-step methodology,"* *"Calculate PSI/PVR/BRS with interpretation."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RPA Development Methodologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** RPA Development Methodologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Robotic Process Automation (RPA) Development Methodology** is a structured, phase-gated engineering framework that governs the end-to-end lifecycle of designing, building, testing, deploying, and maintaining software robots (digital workers) that emulate human interactions with digital systems. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEAIT415**, this methodology establishes disciplined, repeatable, and auditable procedures for translating a manual business process into an executable automation workflow.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** RPA Development Methodologies sit at the heart of Module 1 because they define *how* an automation idea becomes a production-grade bot. Every later topic — UiPath, exception handling, orchestration — depends on this methodology.

The methodology is anchored on three governing pillars:

1. **Process Discovery & Assessment** — Identifying *which* tasks are worth automating.
2. **Solution Design & Development** — Building *how* the bot will execute the task.
3. **Governance, Deployment & Continuous Improvement** — Ensuring *sustained* value delivery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Industry Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Average FTE (Full-Time Equivalent) annual cost** in 2024–2026: ~$60,000–$90,000 globally; ~₹6–12 LPA in Indian BPO context.
> - **Standard working hours per FTE per year:** **1,920 hours** (240 working days × 8 hours).
> - **Typical RPA bot efficiency gain over humans:** **3x to 5x** with **99.9% accuracy**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "House Construction" Model

Think of RPA Development Methodology like **building a house**:

| Real-World Step | RPA Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Surveying the land | Process Discovery & Feasibility Study |
| Architectural blueprint | Process Design Document (PDD) |
| Plumbing & electrical layout | Solution Design Document (SDD) |
| Actual construction | Bot Development in UiPath/Automation Anywhere |
| Inspection by engineers | UAT, Regression & Performance Testing |
| Handing over keys | Production Deployment & Hypercare |
| Annual maintenance | Bot Monitoring, Patch Management |

Just as you would never pour a foundation without a blueprint, you should never code a bot without a signed-off design document. **Discipline before code.**

---

## 1.3 Why Methodology Matters — The Cost of Skipping It

When RPA is deployed without a methodology:
- **40–60% of initial PoCs fail** to scale (industry estimate, 2024).
- Bot breakage rate is **5–8x higher** than methodology-driven bots.
- Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR) balloons from minutes to days.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** If a 14-mark question asks "Explain RPA Development Methodology," start with the *phases* — never with "RPA is a technology that…" Methodology questions are about **process**, not **tools**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 7-Phase RPA Development Lifecycle (Phases in Detail)

A robust RPA Methodology decomposes the automation journey into **seven distinct, sequential phases**. Each phase has **gates** — go/no-go checkpoints.

### Phase 1 — Process Identification (Pipeline Generation)
- **Goal:** Build a long-list of candidate processes from business units.
- **Inputs:** Business interviews, process mining logs (Celonis, UiPath Process Mining), Value Stream Mapping workshops.
- **Outputs:** A *Process Pipeline Spreadsheet* with ~30–100 candidates.
- **Gate:** Each entry must pass a **"Rule of 5"** quick screen: high volume, rule-based, stable, digital inputs, low exception rate.

### Phase 2 — Process Assessment & Feasibility Analysis
- **Goal:** Convert the pipeline into a short-list of 5–10 processes.
- **Inputs:** AS-IS process map, manual effort logs, system inventory.
- **Outputs:** Feasibility Scorecard with Technical, Business, Operational, and Financial dimensions.
- **Tools:** **ATLAS** (Automation, Technical viability, Legality, Analytics, Security) framework is widely referenced.

### Phase 3 — Business Case & ROI Sign-off
- **Goal:** Justify the investment with quantified returns.
- **Outputs:** Approved Business Case with NPV, Payback Period, ROI.
- **Gate:** Steering committee approval. **No approval = no code.**

### Phase 4 — Solution Design (PDD + SDD)
- **PDD (Process Design Document):** *What* the bot must do, from a business viewpoint.
- **SDD (Solution Design Document):** *How* the bot will do it, technically — selectors, queues, REFramework states, exception paths.
- **Outputs:** PDD v1.0, SDD v1.0, AS-IS and TO-BE process diagrams.

### Phase 5 — Development & Unit Testing
- **Goal:** Build the workflow in UiPath Studio / equivalent.
- **Practices:** Modular workflows, Config files, Queue-based architecture, Version control via Git.
- **Outputs:** Deployed code in Dev environment, unit test logs.

### Phase 6 — User Acceptance Testing (UAT) & Sign-off
- **Goal:** Business owner validates the bot against business requirements.
- **Practices:** UAT scripts, defect logs, parallel run (bot + human) for a defined period.
- **Outputs:** UAT Sign-off Document.

### Phase 7 — Deployment, Hypercare & Continuous Improvement
- **Goal:** Move bot to Production, monitor for 2–4 weeks of *hypercare*, then transition to BAU (Business As Usual).
- **Practices:** Bot health dashboards, exception queues, change management.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The **Phases 1–3 are pre-development**; **Phases 4–6 are build & validate**; **Phase 7 is run & evolve**. Always mention this grouping in 14-mark answers.

---

## 2.2 The PDCA Cycle Applied to RPA

PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) is the *meta-methodology* wrapped around the 7 phases:

- **Plan** — Phases 1–4 (Identify, Assess, Justify, Design).
- **Do** — Phases 5–6 (Develop, Test).
- **Check** — Phase 7 hypercare + KPI monitoring.
- **Act** — Continuous improvement, scaling to new processes.

---

## 2.3 Center of Excellence (CoE) — The Governance Backbone

A CoE is the **organizational unit** that owns RPA methodology in an enterprise. Typical CoE structure:

- **Sponsor** (C-level)
- **Head of CoE / RPA Lead**
- **Solution Architects**
- **Developers** (citizen + professional)
- **Business Analysts**
- **QA Engineers**
- **Infrastructure / IT Support**

The CoE publishes the **Methodology Handbook**, defines **bot deployment models** (Attended, Unattended, Hybrid), and enforces governance.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **high-yield** for KTU Numerical/Analytical questions. Master these formulas before solving any RPA assessment problem.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Return on Investment** | $ROI = \dfrac{\text{Net Savings}}{\text{Total Cost}} \times 100\%$ | Net Savings in ₹/$, Cost in ₹/$ | Business Case questions |
| 2 | **Payback Period** | $\text{PP} = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$ | Years / Months | Project selection |
| 3 | **FTE Savings (count)** | $\text{FTE}_{saved} = \dfrac{H_{manual} - H_{bot}}{H_{annual}}$ | $H_{annual} = 1920$ hrs | Headcount justification |
| 4 | **Annual Cost Savings** | $\text{CS}_{annual} = \text{FTE}_{saved} \times \text{Avg FTE Cost}$ | ₹/FTE/year | ROI numerator |
| 5 | **Process Complexity Score (PCS)** | $\text{PCS} = (S \times 1) + (A \times 2) + (D \times 3) + (E \times 5)$ | S = steps, A = apps, D = decisions, E = exception % | Process prioritization |
| 6 | **Automation Potential Index (API)** | $\text{API} = V \times R \times \dfrac{1}{C}$ | V = volume, R = rule-basedness, C = complexity | Pipeline ranking |
| 7 | **Bot Uptime SLA** | $\text{Uptime} = \dfrac{T_{total} - T_{down}}{T_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $T$ in hours | SLA compliance |
| 8 | **Effort Estimation (Function Points analogue)** | $E_{bot} = (\text{Base Hours}) \times (1 + \text{Risk Factor})$ | Hours | Project planning |
| 9 | **Net Present Value** | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_0$ | $r$ = discount rate | Long-term business case |
| 10 | **Defect Density** | $\text{DD} = \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{KLOC} \text{ or } \text{Workflow Count}}$ | Count | Quality assessment |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Table Pipe Rule:** Note how $\vert$ inside formulas was written as the explicit text **"Net Savings"** and **"FTE Cost"** to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Never use literal $\vert$ character in tables.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of RPA Methodology

In industry, RPA Methodology is applied for:
- **Banking:** Loan processing, KYC, account reconciliation.
- **Healthcare:** Claims processing, patient registration.
- **Retail:** Inventory updates, invoice matching.
- **Telecom:** SIM activation, churn reporting.
- **HR:** Onboarding, payroll, leave management.

Every one of these uses the **same 7-phase methodology**, just with different domain data. This is why KTU treats it as a **standalone Module 1 topic**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — ROI for a Hypothetical RPA Project

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A bank's invoice reconciliation process currently consumes **4 FTE** at an average cost of **₹8,00,000/year each**. An RPA bot is proposed with the following parameters:
- Development cost: **₹12,00,000** (one-time).
- Annual licensing cost: **₹3,00,000**.
- Annual maintenance: **₹1,50,000**.
- Bot will replace **3 FTE** completely; 1 FTE becomes exception handler.
- Project life: **5 years**.
- Discount rate: **10%**.

**Step 1 — Calculate Annual FTE Cost Savings (Pre-tax, illustrative).**

$$
\text{CS}_{annual} = (\text{FTE Replaced}) \times (\text{Avg FTE Cost})
$$

$$
\text{CS}_{annual} = 3 \times ₹8,00,000 = ₹24,00,000
$$

> **Logic:** 3 of the 4 humans are fully freed for higher-value work; the 4th still manages exceptions, so we do not count savings for the 4th.

**Step 2 — Calculate Net Annual Savings (Subtract Running Costs).**

$$
\text{NS}_{annual} = \text{CS}_{annual} - (\text{License} + \text{Maintenance})
$$

$$
\text{NS}_{annual} = ₹24,00,000 - (₹3,00,000 + ₹1,50,000) = ₹19,50,000
$$

> **Logic:** Recurring costs must be netted out of gross savings every year.

**Step 3 — Total Cost of Ownership (Year 1).**

$$
\text{TC}_{Y1} = \text{Development Cost} + \text{License}_{Y1} + \text{Maintenance}_{Y1}
$$

$$
\text{TC}_{Y1} = ₹12,00,000 + ₹3,00,000 + ₹1,50,000 = ₹16,50,000
$$

> **Logic:** Year 1 absorbs the one-time build cost plus recurring costs.

**Step 4 — Payback Period.**

$$
\text{PP} = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{NS}_{annual}}
$$

$$
\text{PP} = \dfrac{₹12,00,000}{₹19,50,000} = 0.615 \text{ years} \approx 7.4 \text{ months}
$$

> **Logic:** Investment divided by the steady-state annual net benefit.

**Step 5 — 5-Year ROI (Using Simple, Non-Discounted Method).**

$$
\text{Total Benefit}_{5y} = \text{NS}_{annual} \times 5 = ₹19,50,000 \times 5 = ₹97,50,000
$$

$$
\text{Total Cost}_{5y} = ₹12,00,000 + (₹4,50,000 \times 5) = ₹12,00,000 + ₹22,50,000 = ₹34,50,000
$$

$$
\text{ROI}_{5y} = \dfrac{₹97,50,000 - ₹34,50,000}{₹34,50,000} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{ROI}_{5y} = \dfrac{₹63,00,000}{₹34,50,000} \times 100\% \approx 182.6\%
$$

> **Logic:** Net benefit divided by total cost, expressed as a percentage.

**Step 6 — NPV (Discounted, more rigorous).**

$$
\text{NPV} = -C_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{5} \dfrac{\text{NS}_{annual}}{(1+r)^t}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPV} &= -₹12,00,000 + ₹19,50,000 \times \left[\dfrac{1}{1.10} + \dfrac{1}{1.10^2} + \dfrac{1}{1.10^3} + \dfrac{1}{1.10^4} + \dfrac{1}{1.10^5}\right] \\
&= -₹12,00,000 + ₹19,50,000 \times 3.7908 \\
&= -₹12,00,000 + ₹73,91,956 \\
&= ₹61,91,956
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Year 1 is discounted at $(1.10)^1$, Year 5 at $(1.10)^5$. Positive NPV ⇒ project accepted.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Process Complexity Score (PCS) & Prioritization

**Problem:** A hospital chain identifies 3 candidate processes. Compute PCS for each and rank.

| Process | Steps (S) | Apps (A) | Decisions (D) | Exception % (E) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| P1: Patient Registration | 8 | 2 | 3 | 2 |
| P2: Insurance Claim | 25 | 5 | 10 | 12 |
| P3: Discharge Summary | 12 | 3 | 5 | 4 |

**PCS formula:**

$$
\text{PCS} = (S \times 1) + (A \times 2) + (D \times 3) + (E \times 5)
$$

**Process P1:**

$$
\text{PCS}_{P1} = (8 \times 1) + (2 \times 2) + (3 \times 3) + (2 \times 5) = 8 + 4 + 9 + 10 = 31
$$

**Process P2:**

$$
\text{PCS}_{P2} = (25 \times 1) + (5 \times 2) + (10 \times 3) + (12 \times 5) = 25 + 10 + 30 + 60 = 125
$$

**Process P3:**

$$
\text{PCS}_{P3} = (12 \times 1) + (3 \times 2) + (5 \times 3) + (4 \times 5) = 12 + 6 + 15 + 20 = 53
$$

> **Logic:** Lower PCS = easier to automate. **Priority order for automation: P1 > P3 > P2.** Although P2 has high business value, its PCS of 125 makes it a *Phase 2* candidate.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — FTE Savings for a Telecom Use Case

**Problem:** A telecom company's *SIM activation* process takes **45 minutes** per request manually. Current volume: **120,000 requests/year**. Bot processes each in **3 minutes**, runs **20 hours/day**, **300 days/year**.

**Step 1 — Manual hours per year.**

$$
H_{manual} = 120{,}000 \times \dfrac{45}{60} = 90{,}000 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2 — Bot capacity in hours.**

$$
H_{bot\_capacity} = 20 \times 300 = 6{,}000 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Bot throughput in requests.**

$$
R_{bot} = 6{,}000 \times \dfrac{60}{3} = 1{,}20{,}000 \text{ requests/year}
$$

> **Logic:** Bot can handle **100% of volume**. So:

**Step 4 — FTE Savings.**

$$
\text{FTE}_{saved} = \dfrac{H_{manual} - H_{bot\_supervised}}{H_{annual}} = \dfrac{90{,}000 - 1{,}000 \text{ (residual supervision)}}{1{,}920} \approx 46.35 \text{ FTE}
$$

> **Logic:** 1,000 hours of human supervision is assumed for exception handling.

**Step 5 — Cost Savings @ ₹6,00,000/FTE/year.**

$$
\text{CS} = 46.35 \times ₹6,00,000 = ₹2,77,80,000 \approx ₹2.78 \text{ Cr/year}
$$

> **Logic:** Direct labor cost avoided. Does not include error reduction or compliance value.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Selection Criteria

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is the **decision core** of Phase 2 (Assessment). Memorize the column headers.

| Dimension | Weight (Typical) | Scoring 1–5 | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| Volume / Frequency | 25% | High = 5 | High volume amortizes build cost faster |
| Rule-basedness | 20% | High = 5 | Bots fail on judgment-heavy tasks |
| Stability (low change) | 15% | High = 5 | Changing processes = broken bots |
| Digital Accessibility | 15% | API/UI stable = 5 | No OCR/Citrix black-box preferred |
| Exception Rate | 10% | Low = 5 | Exception design is the costliest |
| Business Criticality | 10% | High = 5 | Strategic processes get priority |
| Compliance Impact | 5% | Audit-friendly = 5 | Critical for BFSI / Healthcare |

**Composite Score** = $\sum (\text{Weight} \times \text{Score})$. Threshold for automation: typically **≥ 3.5 / 5**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The 7-Phase RPA Development Lifecycle (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Phase 1: Process Identification"] --> B["Phase 2: Process Assessment & Feasibility"]
    B --> C["Phase 3: Business Case & ROI Sign-off"]
    C --> D{"Steering Committee Approval?"}
    D -- No --> Z["Stop or Re-scope"]
    D -- Yes --> E["Phase 4: Solution Design (PDD + SDD)"]
    E --> F["Phase 5: Development & Unit Testing"]
    F --> G["Phase 6: UAT & Sign-off"]
    G --> H["Phase 7: Deployment & Hypercare"]
    H --> I["Continuous Improvement via CoE"]
    I --> A

    classDef gate fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef phase fill:#118ab2,stroke:#0b5257,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef stop fill:#ef476f,stroke:#9d0208,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff

    class A,B,C,E,F,G,H,I phase
    class D gate
    class Z stop
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Phase 3 has a *gate* (yellow). Without approval, the project does not move into design. After Phase 7, learnings feed back to Phase 1 (continuous improvement loop).

---

## 4.2 RPA Center of Excellence (CoE) — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP["Executive Sponsor"] --> HD["Head of CoE"]
    HD --> SA["Solution Architects"]
    HD --> BA["Business Analysts"]
    HD --> PD["Pro Developers"]
    HD --> CD["Citizen Developers"]
    HD --> QA["QA Engineers"]
    HD --> IN["Infrastructure & IT Ops"]

    BA --> PD
    BA --> CD
    SA --> PD
    SA --> CD
    QA --> PD
    QA --> CD
    IN --> PD
    IN --> CD

    PD --> PR["Production Bot Fleet"]
    CD --> PR
    IN --> PR

    classDef leadership fill:#073b4c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef team fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#065f46,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef infra fill:#f4a261,stroke:#7c2d12,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class SP,HD leadership
    class SA,BA,PD,CD,QA team
    class IN,PR infra
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Executive Sponsor funds and steers. Head of CoE is the single accountable owner. Pro and Citizen Developers both ship to the Production Bot Fleet, but Pro Developers take complex, regulated workflows.

---

## 4.3 AS-IS vs TO-BE Process Mapping (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ASIS["AS-IS Process (Manual)"]
        A1["Customer Email Arrives"] --> A2["Human Reads Email"]
        A2 --> A3["Manually Logs into SAP"]
        A3 --> A4["Manually Updates Order"]
        A4 --> A5["Manually Sends Confirmation Email"]
        A5 --> A6["Total: 35 minutes per case"]
    end

    subgraph TOBE["TO-BE Process (RPA Bot)"]
        B1["Email Trigger Received by Bot"] --> B2["Bot Extracts Order Data via OCR"]
        B2 --> B3["Bot Logs into SAP via Credentials Vault"]
        B3 --> B4["Bot Updates Order via UI Automation"]
        B4 --> B5["Bot Sends Confirmation Email"]
        B5 --> B6["Total: 2 minutes per case"]
    end

    classDef old fill:#ef476f,stroke:#7b1313,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef new fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#0a4f3a,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff

    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5,A6 old
    class B1,B2,B3,B4,B5,B6 new
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Same 5 logical steps, but the human's 35 minutes collapse into the bot's 2 minutes — a **~17x speed-up**, with parallel runs during UAT validating accuracy.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define RPA Development Methodology. List any four phases of the RPA Development Lifecycle.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** RPA Development Methodology is a structured, phase-gated engineering framework that governs the identification, assessment, design, development, testing, deployment, and continuous improvement of software robots that automate rule-based business processes.
>
> **Four Phases of the RPA Development Lifecycle:**
> 1. **Process Identification** — Discovering candidate processes from business units.
> 2. **Process Assessment & Feasibility Analysis** — Scoring and short-listing using criteria like volume, rule-basedness, stability, and complexity.
> 3. **Solution Design (PDD + SDD)** — Documenting *what* and *how* the bot will perform.
> 4. **Development & Testing** — Building, unit-testing, and UAT before production deployment.

---

### **Q2. What is a Process Design Document (PDD)? How is it different from a Solution Design Document (SDD)?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **PDD (Process Design Document):** A business-facing document that describes the *what* of the automation — the step-by-step AS-IS process, business rules, input/output data, exception scenarios, and expected outcomes. It is written by Business Analysts and signed off by the process owner.
>
> **SDD (Solution Design Document):** A technical document that describes the *how* — the tool stack (e.g., UiPath), workflow architecture (e.g., REFramework), selectors, queues, exception-handling mechanism, environment variables, and configuration files. It is authored by the Solution Architect and consumed by developers.
>
> **Key Difference:** PDD is **business-owned** and platform-agnostic; SDD is **technical** and platform-specific.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Sub-Parts)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions of 14 marks typically carry **internal choice**. Two alternative questions are provided below; only **one** needs to be attempted.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

#### (a) Explain in detail the seven phases of the RPA Development Lifecycle. Add a note on the Center of Excellence (CoE). **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The RPA Development Lifecycle is a sequential, phase-gated framework:

**1. Process Identification**
Business units and analysts collaborate to build a *process pipeline* using workshops, process mining tools (Celonis, UiPath Process Mining), and employee interviews. The output is a long-list of 30–100 candidate processes.

**2. Process Assessment & Feasibility**
Each candidate is scored across dimensions: volume, rule-basedness, stability, exception rate, digital accessibility, business criticality, and compliance. The **ATLAS framework** is widely used. Output: a short-list of 5–10 feasible candidates with PCS / API scores.

**3. Business Case & ROI Sign-off**
A formal business case is built with quantified metrics — FTE savings, payback period, NPV, error reduction, compliance uplift. The steering committee must **approve in writing** before any code is written.

**4. Solution Design (PDD + SDD)**
- **PDD** captures AS-IS, TO-BE, business rules, exception scenarios.
- **SDD** captures technical architecture — REFramework states, queues, selectors, environment variables, credential vault references.

**5. Development & Unit Testing**
Developers build modular workflows in UiPath Studio. Modular best practices: separate config files, dispatcher-performer pattern, Git-based version control, code reviews.

**6. UAT & Sign-off**
Business users run **UAT scripts** covering happy path + exception paths. **Parallel run** (bot + human) is conducted for a defined sample to confirm accuracy. Sign-off document is mandatory.

**7. Deployment & Hypercare**
Bot is promoted to Production through Dev → Test → Prod environments. A **2–4 week hypercare** window involves close monitoring, incident logging, and minor defect fixes. Post-hypercare, the bot transitions to BAU (Business As Usual) with the CoE taking ownership.

**Note on Center of Excellence (CoE):**
The CoE is the **governing body** that institutionalizes the methodology. It provides:
- A documented **Methodology Handbook**.
- **Standards** for coding, naming conventions, security, exception handling.
- A **governance board** for prioritization.
- **Reusable components** and shared libraries.
- **Roles & RACI matrix** for sponsor, lead, architects, developers, BA, QA, and IT support.

---

#### (b) A company's invoice processing currently takes 3 FTE at ₹7,00,000/year each. An RPA solution replaces 2 FTE completely. Development cost is ₹10,00,000; annual license + maintenance is ₹3,00,000. Compute (i) Annual Cost Savings, (ii) Payback Period, (iii) 5-Year Simple ROI. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Annual Cost Savings:**

$$
\text{CS}_{annual} = 2 \times ₹7,00,000 = ₹14,00,000
$$

**(ii) Net Annual Savings (after running costs):**

$$
\text{NS}_{annual} = ₹14,00,000 - ₹3,00,000 = ₹11,00,000
$$

**Payback Period:**

$$
\text{PP} = \dfrac{₹10,00,000}{₹11,00,000} = 0.909 \text{ years} \approx 10.9 \text{ months}
$$

**(iii) 5-Year Total Benefit and Total Cost:**

$$
\text{Total Benefit}_{5y} = ₹11,00,000 \times 5 = ₹55,00,000
$$

$$
\text{Total Cost}_{5y} = ₹10,00,000 + ₹3,00,000 \times 5 = ₹25,00,000
$$

**5-Year Simple ROI:**

$$
\text{ROI}_{5y} = \dfrac{₹55,00,000 - ₹25,00,000}{₹25,00,000} \times 100\% = \dfrac{₹30,00,000}{₹25,00,000} \times 100\% = 120\%
$$

> **Conclusion:** Payback in under a year and 120% ROI over 5 years → strong business case. **Recommend automation.**

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) Discuss the role of the Center of Excellence (CoE) in scaling RPA across an enterprise. List any six best practices for establishing an RPA CoE. **[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Role of the CoE:**

The CoE is the **single source of truth** for RPA governance. Its roles include:

1. **Strategy & Governance:** Defines RPA vision, prioritization framework, and pipeline governance.
2. **Methodology Ownership:** Maintains and updates the RPA Development Methodology Handbook.
3. **Standards & Reusability:** Defines coding standards, naming conventions, reusable component libraries, and credential vault policies.
4. **Delivery Support:** Provides solution architects, code reviewers, and senior developers to unblock delivery teams.
5. **Demand Management:** Triage of incoming automation requests, scoring against API, and pipeline grooming.
6. **People Development:** Training programs for citizen developers, certifications (UiPath Certified Developer, etc.).
7. **Operational Support:** Bot monitoring, incident response, patching, and version upgrades.
8. **Innovation & Scaling:** Identifying new automation opportunities — AI integration, document understanding, Process Mining.

**Six Best Practices for Establishing an RPA CoE:**

1. **Start with executive sponsorship** — A C-level sponsor ensures funding, mandate, and cross-functional access.
2. **Begin with a small, skilled core team** — 1 Lead + 2 Architects + 4 Developers is a typical starting CoE.
3. **Define clear RACI** — Who is Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed for each phase.
4. **Build a reusable component library** — Login workflows, SAP selectors, email templates, exception handlers.
5. **Adopt a single, approved methodology** — One handbook, one set of templates (PDD, SDD, UAT).
6. **Measure and publicize wins** — Publish KPIs: bot count, hours saved, error reduction, ROI.
7. **Train citizen developers** — Use their domain knowledge for back-office automations under CoE supervision.
8. **Implement strict security and audit** — Credential vaulting, role-based access, audit logs, GDPR / HIPAA compliance.

---

#### (b) Compare and contrast AS-IS and TO-BE process mapping with a suitable example. Why is this step critical in the RPA Development Methodology? **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
- **AS-IS Process Map:** The current, manual process flow as it exists today. Captures every step, decision, exception, and handoff performed by humans. It is the *baseline*.
- **TO-BE Process Map:** The future, automated process flow showing how the bot will perform the same steps. It is the *target state*.

**Example: Order Confirmation in an E-Commerce Company**

| Step | AS-IS (Manual) | TO-BE (RPA Bot) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Order email arrives in shared inbox | Email trigger fires bot |
| 2 | Customer service rep opens email | Bot parses email body via OCR/NLP |
| 3 | Rep logs into order management system | Bot uses stored credentials from Vault |
| 4 | Rep manually enters order data | Bot auto-populates form via UI selectors |
| 5 | Rep sends confirmation email | Bot triggers email template |
| 6 | Average time: **20 min/order** | Average time: **90 sec/order** |

**Comparison:**

| Aspect | AS-IS | TO-BE |
|---|---|---|
| **Owner** | Business Process Owner | CoE / Solution Architect |
| **Format** | Swimlane (humans + systems) | Swimlane (bot + systems + exception queue) |
| **Purpose** | Document current reality | Design automated future |
| **Exception Handling** | Phone calls, escalation | Exception queue → human handler |
| **Time** | Baseline | Optimized target |

**Why this step is critical:**

1. **Identifies automation candidates** — Only by mapping AS-IS can we spot repetitive, rule-based steps.
2. **Quantifies the benefit** — Comparing AS-IS time vs TO-BE time yields the savings claim.
3. **Detects hidden complexity** — AS-IS mapping reveals undocumented steps, workarounds, and tribal knowledge.
4. **Forms the basis of PDD** — The PDD essentially fuses AS-IS (to validate requirements) with TO-BE (to define scope).
5. **Reduces rework** — Clear visual agreement between business and IT prevents mid-build change requests.
6. **Supports compliance audit** — Auditors can verify the manual controls were preserved or strengthened in TO-BE.

> **Conclusion:** AS-IS / TO-BE mapping is the **bridge** between problem and solution. Skipping it is the #1 cause of failed RPA projects.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in RPA Methodology Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Methodology with Tools** — Students write about UiPath Studio instead of *phases*. **Marks deducted** for ignoring governance/lifecycle.
> 2. **Omitting Gate/Approval Points** — Phase 3 → Phase 4 has a *steering committee approval gate*. Missing this loses at least **1 mark** in 7-mark sub-questions.
> 3. **Skipping PDD vs SDD distinction** — Examiners specifically test whether you can articulate *what* vs *how*. Confusing them = **2-mark loss**.
> 4. **Formula Mistakes in ROI** — Using Total Benefit instead of **Net** Savings (i.e., subtracting license + maintenance) = **full marks lost** in numericals.
> 5. **Ignoring CoE** — A 14-mark answer without CoE mention is **incomplete**, even if phases are perfect. Always add 2–3 lines on CoE.
> 6. **No mention of Hypercare / Continuous Improvement** — Closing the lifecycle at deployment is wrong. Phase 7 must include hypercare + feedback to Phase 1.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — RPA Development Methodologies (Module 1)**

- **Definition:** Structured phase-gated framework governing bot lifecycle from idea → retirement.
- **7 Phases:** Identify → Assess → Business Case → Design (PDD+SDD) → Develop → UAT → Deploy + Hypercare.
- **Pre-development gates:** Steering committee approval is mandatory before coding starts.
- **PDD vs SDD:** PDD = *what* (business). SDD = *how* (technical).
- **CoE:** Governance body; publishes Methodology Handbook; defines standards, RACI, reusables.
- **Process Selection:** Rule of 5 + ATLAS framework + PCS/API scoring.
- **Key Numerical Formulas:**
  - $ROI = \dfrac{\text{Net Savings}}{\text{Total Cost}} \times 100\%$
  - $\text{PP} = \dfrac{\text{Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$
  - $FTE_{saved} = \dfrac{H_{manual} - H_{bot}}{1920}$
  - $\text{PCS} = S + 2A + 3D + 5E$
  - $\text{NPV} = -C_0 + \sum \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$
- **Standard Metric:** $H_{annual} = 1920$ hours per FTE.
- **PDCA Mapping:** Plan = Phases 1–4, Do = Phases 5–6, Check = Phase 7 hypercare, Act = Continuous improvement.
- **AS-IS vs TO-BE:** Mandatory for every automation; foundation of PDD.
- **Deployment Models:** Attended, Unattended, Hybrid — choose based on process criticality.
- **Examiner Traps:** Don't confuse methodology with tools; always mention CoE; never skip hypercare.
- **Final Mnemonic:** **"I Am Bringing Software Deployment Happily"** → **I**dentify, **A**ssess, **B**usiness case, **S**olution design, **D**evelop, **D**eploy + Hypercare.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Difference from SDLC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Difference from SDLC ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **SDLC (Software Development Life Cycle)** is a structured, phased methodology used by the software engineering discipline to design, develop, test, deploy, and maintain software applications from scratch. It follows formal stages such as Requirement Gathering, System Design, Coding, Testing, Deployment, and Maintenance (the classic *Waterfall* or *Agile* models). > [!IMPORTANT] > **RPA (Robotic Process Automation)**, on the other hand, is a *non-invasive* automation technology that configures software "bots" to mimic human interactions with existing digital systems (UI, applications, databases, ERP) **without changing the underlying application code**. It does **not** build new software; it orchestrates existing software layers. ### 1.2 The Core Paradigm Shift The fundamental philosophical difference between SDLC and RPA lies in **what is being created**: | Paradigm | SDLC | RPA | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Goal** | Create *new* software | Automate *existing* manual/digital processes | | **Output** | A software product / application | A *digital workforce* (bot) | | **Touchpoint** | Underlying code, APIs, databases | The **presentation layer** (UI) and existing APIs | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Factory Floor:** > > Imagine a car manufacturing plant already running on legacy 1960s machinery. > - **SDLC Approach**: Tear down the entire factory, redesign it from the ground up, install modern robotic arms, build a new production line. *Outcome*: A brand-new factory, but the project takes **3 years** and costs **₹50 Crores**. > - **RPA Approach**: Place a **smart camera + small robotic arm (a "bot")** *on top* of the existing 1960s machinery. The bot watches what a human operator does, learns the steps, and replicates them 24/7 without fatigue. *Outcome*: Same throughput, but the project takes **3 weeks** and costs **₹5 Lakhs**. > > The factory machinery is **untouched**. The bot sits on the **UI layer** of the process. This is exactly how RPA differs from SDLC — RPA wraps around existing systems, SDLC replaces them. ### 1.4 Standard Industry Metrics - **RPA Average Project Duration:** **4 to 8 weeks** (versus **6 to 18 months** for SDLC). - **RPA Cost Reduction Benchmark:** **30% to 60%** operational cost savings post-deployment. - **SDLC Phases (Standard Count):** **6** phases (Requirement, Design, Implementation, Testing, Deployment, Maintenance). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Comparison Matrix ### 2.1 Why and How the Differences Emerge The differences are rooted in the *engineering intent* of each discipline: 1. **SDLC is *constructive* engineering** — it builds artifacts. 2. **RPA is *orchestrative* engineering** — it coordinates existing artifacts. This single philosophical divergence cascades into every downstream parameter: cost, time, risk, skill-set, and lifecycle management. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison Cheat Sheet (Mandatory Memorization Table) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** In University Exams, this comparison table is the *single most frequently asked structure* for 7-mark and 14-mark questions on RPA fundamentals. Memorize the bolded parameter names. | # | Parameter | SDLC (Traditional Software) | RPA (Robotic Process Automation) | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Primary Goal** | Design and develop new software applications | Automate repetitive, rule-based human tasks | | 2 | **Approach** | Constructive — builds from scratch | Non-invasive — configures over existing systems | | 3 | **Coding Requirement** | Heavy — requires programming languages (Java, Python, C#) | Minimal / Low-Code — drag-and-drop, visual workflows | | 4 | **Development Time** | **Long** (6–18 months) | **Short** (2–8 weeks) | | 5 | **Cost of Implementation** | High (₹ Lakhs to Crores) | Low to Moderate (₹ Thousands to Lakhs) | | 6 | **User Involvement** | Limited, mostly at requirement & UAT stage | Continuous — SMEs (Subject Matter Experts) drive the bot | | 7 | **Testing Strategy** | Unit, Integration, System, UAT — code-level testing | **Exception handling**, edge-case data, and UI change resilience | | 8 | **Documentation** | Extensive SRS, SDD, STD documents | Process Design Document (PDD) and Solution Design Document (SDD) | | 9 | **Change Management** | High impact — requires re-coding, re-deployment | Low impact — bots can be reconfigured in days | | 10 | **Skill Set** | Software engineers, architects, DBAs | Business analysts, process SMEs, citizen developers | | 11 | **Scalability** | Vertical (more powerful hardware) and Horizontal (load balancing) | **Instantaneous** — clone existing bot; license-based scaling | | 12 | **Compliance & Audit** | Built into code via access control | **Native to RPA** — every bot action is logged with timestamp | | 13 | **Maintenance** | Dedicated DevOps / support team | Bot maintenance team; **bot crashes** if underlying UI changes | | 14 | **Error Rate (Post-Stable)** | Low, but bugs persist for years | **Near-zero** for rule-based steps; brittle to UI redesigns | | 15 | **ROI Realization** | 12–24 months | **3–9 months** | ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility In modern enterprises, SDLC and RPA are **complementary, not competitors**. A typical lifecycle: - **Phase 1**: SDLC builds a customer onboarding portal. - **Phase 2**: RPA bots migrate legacy customer data from an old mainframe *into* that new portal. - **Phase 3**: RPA handles exception cases that the new portal's logic rejects. > **Engineering Verdict:** SDLC is the **engine**; RPA is the **glue and the safety net** that keeps legacy systems talking to modern systems without a costly re-engineering effort. ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Comparative Derivation Because the topic is *conceptual comparison* rather than numerical, the "derivation" here is a structured, logical walkthrough of how a hypothetical project would unfold under each paradigm. ### 3.1 Worked Scenario: "Automating Invoice Processing" **Problem Statement:** A bank receives 5,000 invoices per day via email. Currently, a human clerk reads each email, opens the PDF, copies the amount, vendor name, and date into an Excel sheet, and enters it into SAP. Goal: automate this. --- ### 3.2 Solution Path A — The SDLC Route > [!NOTE] > This walkthrough demonstrates *why* a pure SDLC approach is impractical for such a problem. **Step 1 — Requirement Gathering:** The team interviews the clerk, the accounts team, the SAP admin. They produce a 50-page **SRS (Software Requirement Specification)** document. **Step 2 — System Design:** Architects design a new web application with an email listener, a PDF parser (using regex + ML), a database schema, and a SAP integration module (using BAPIs or OData services). Output: a **System Design Document (SDD)**. **Step 3 — Implementation (Coding):** Developers write the application in Java/Spring Boot. The PDF parser alone takes 2 developer-months because of varied invoice formats. **Step 4 — Testing:** QA team writes 500+ test cases. Integration with SAP fails twice due to authentication mismatches — each fix takes a week. **Step 5 — Deployment & Maintenance:** The new app goes live after **9 months**. Total cost: **₹35 Lakhs**. Every time SAP upgrades, the integration must be **re-coded**. --- ### 3.3 Solution Path B — The RPA Route **Step 1 — Process Discovery (1 week):** A Process Analyst uses tools like **Process Mining** to record the clerk's actions. Output: a **Process Design Document (PDD)**. **Step 2 — Bot Design (1 week):** A developer opens UiPath/Automation Anywhere and **drags-and-drops** activities: - "Read Email" → "Save Attachment" → "Read PDF" → "Open Excel" → "Write Cell" → "Open SAP" → "Type Into" → "Save". No Java, no Python, no compilation. **Step 3 — Bot Testing (1 week):** Test the bot with 50 sample invoices. Handle exceptions: corrupted PDF → send email to clerk. **Step 4 — Deployment (1 week):** Bot is scheduled on a VM. It runs unattended at 2 AM. Total cost: **₹4 Lakhs**. Deployment time: **6 weeks**. **Step 5 — Maintenance:** If SAP upgrades its UI, the developer re-records the "Type Into" activity. Takes **2 hours**, not 2 weeks. --- ### 3.4 Side-by-Side Analytical Outcome $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{SDLC}} &= 9 \text{ months} \\ T_{\text{RPA}} &= 6 \text{ weeks} \\ \Delta T &= T_{\text{SDLC}} - T_{\text{RPA}} \approx 7.5 \text{ months saved} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} C_{\text{SDLC}} &= ₹35{,}00{,}000 \\ C_{\text{RPA}} &= ₹4{,}00{,}000 \\ \Delta C &= C_{\text{SDLC}} - C_{\text{RPA}} = ₹31{,}00{,}000 \text{ saved} \end{aligned} $$ > **Analytical Conclusion:** For a *rule-based, repetitive, UI-driven* process, the RPA path is roughly **6× faster** and **~9× cheaper** than the SDLC path, at the cost of a marginally higher long-term brittleness to UI changes. ### 3.5 Decision Boundary — When to Use Which > [!IMPORTANT] > **The KTU-Relevant Rule of Thumb:** > - If the process requires **new business logic, new databases, new APIs, or new algorithms** → use **SDLC**. > - If the process **already exists, is rule-based, runs across 2+ existing systems, and is high-volume** → use **RPA**. > - If both apply → use **SDLC for the new core** and **RPA as the integration/exceptions layer**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — SDLC vs RPA Approach Map ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Business Problem Identified]) --> decision1{Rule-based & repetitive
across existing systems?} decision1 -- Yes --> rpaPath[RPA Path] decision1 -- No --> sdlcCheck{Needs new business logic
or new application?} sdlcCheck -- Yes --> sdlcPath[SDLC Path] sdlcCheck -- No --> hybridPath[Hybrid Approach] rpaPath --> rpaStep1[Process Discovery and PDD] rpaStep1 --> rpaStep2[Drag-and-Drop Bot Design] rpaStep2 --> rpaStep3[Bot Testing and UAT] rpaStep3 --> rpaStep4[Unattended Bot Deployment] rpaStep4 --> rpaEnd([Outcome: Digital Worker]) sdlcPath --> sdlcStep1[Requirement and SRS] sdlcStep1 --> sdlcStep2[System Design and SDD] sdlcStep2 --> sdlcStep3[Coding and Unit Testing] sdlcStep3 --> sdlcStep4[Integration and UAT] sdlcStep4 --> sdlcStep5[Deployment to Production] sdlcStep5 --> sdlcEnd([Outcome: New Software Application]) hybridPath --> hybStep1[SDLC builds new core module] hybStep1 --> hybStep2[RPA bridges legacy to new module] hybStep2 --> hybEnd([Outcome: Integrated Ecosystem]) classDef rpaStyle fill:#e1f5e1,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px classDef sdlcStyle fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px classDef hybridStyle fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00,stroke-width:2px class rpaPath,rpaStep1,rpaStep2,rpaStep3,rpaStep4,rpaEnd rpaStyle class sdlcPath,sdlcStep1,sdlcStep2,sdlcStep3,sdlcStep4,sdlcStep5,sdlcEnd sdlcStyle class hybridPath,hybStep1,hybStep2,hybEnd hybridStyle ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Layer of Interaction ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SDLC_LAYER["SDLC APPROACH - INTRUSIVE"] sUserA[User] --> sAppA[New Application Built via SDLC] sAppA --> sDBA[New Database] sAppA -.Replaces.-> sLegacyA[Legacy System Retired] end subgraph RPA_LAYER["RPA APPROACH - NON-INVASIVE"] rUserB[User / Scheduler] --> rBotB[Software Bot - Configured via RPA Tool] rBotB --> rLegacyB[Legacy ERP / SAP - Untouched] rBotB --> rWebB[Web Portal - Untouched] rBotB --> rExcelB[Excel Files - Untouched] end ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Radar — Key Differentiators ```mermaid flowchart LR axis1[Development Speed] --- axis2[Cost Efficiency] axis2 --- axis3[UI Layer Dependency] axis3 --- axis4[Code Complexity] axis4 --- axis5[Scalability] axis5 --- axis1 axis1 --> p1A((SDLC: LOW)) axis1 --> p1B((RPA: HIGH)) axis2 --> p2A((SDLC: LOW)) axis2 --> p2B((RPA: HIGH)) axis3 --> p3A((SDLC: LOW)) axis3 --> p3B((RPA: HIGH)) axis4 --> p4A((SDLC: HIGH)) axis4 --> p4B((RPA: LOW)) axis5 --> p5A((SDLC: MEDIUM)) axis5 --> p5B((RPA: HIGH)) ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *Define SDLC. List any **two** points of difference between SDLC and RPA.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (1 Mark):** SDLC (Software Development Life Cycle) is a structured framework that defines the phases — Requirement Analysis, Design, Implementation, Testing, Deployment, and Maintenance — for building a new software application. > > **Difference 1 (1 Mark):** SDLC requires writing fresh code in programming languages, whereas RPA uses a **low-code / no-code** visual workflow approach to configure bots. > > **Difference 2 (1 Mark):** SDLC typically takes **months to years** for development, whereas RPA projects can be deployed in **weeks**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** *Why is RPA considered a "non-invasive" technology compared to SDLC?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > [!NOTE] > RPA is called **non-invasive** because it does not modify, replace, or alter the underlying source code, database schema, or APIs of the existing applications it automates. Instead, the bot operates at the **presentation layer (UI)** of those systems — mimicking human keystrokes, mouse clicks, and screen-reading activities. In contrast, SDLC is **invasive**: it builds new systems that often require integration with, or replacement of, existing applications. Therefore, RPA can be deployed over legacy systems (e.g., mainframes) without disturbing the original IT infrastructure. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern:** Part B carries 14 marks and offers a choice between two questions from the same module. Each 14-mark question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts. --- ### ❓ Question A — 14 Marks **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]** **(a)** Explain in detail the various phases of the **traditional SDLC** with a neat block diagram. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** Discuss in detail **how RPA differs from SDLC** across at least **eight** key parameters such as goal, approach, development time, cost, skill set, testing, scalability, and maintenance. *(7 Marks)* --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q3(a) — SDLC Phases (7 Marks) > [!NOTE] > The traditional SDLC, also called the **Waterfall Model**, follows a sequential, phase-based approach. The six standard phases are: 1. **Requirement Gathering and Analysis (1 Mark):** The team interacts with stakeholders, understands the functional and non-functional requirements, and produces the **Software Requirement Specification (SRS)** document. 2. **System Design (1 Mark):** Architects translate SRS into a **System Design Document (SDD)** that defines the high-level architecture, database schema, data flow diagrams (DFDs), and module interfaces. 3. **Implementation / Coding (1 Mark):** Developers write the actual source code in programming languages such as Java, Python, or C#. Unit testing is performed alongside coding. 4. **Integration and Testing (1 Mark):** Individual modules are integrated and tested at multiple levels — **Unit, Integration, System, and User Acceptance Testing (UAT)** — to uncover defects. 5. **Deployment (1 Mark):** The validated software is released to the production environment. Deployment can be manual or via CI/CD pipelines. 6. **Maintenance (1 Mark):** Post-deployment, the software enters a maintenance phase where bug fixes, patches, performance tuning, and feature enhancements are carried out iteratively. 7. **Diagram (1 Mark):** ``` Requirement → Design → Coding → Testing → Deployment → Maintenance (SRS) (SDD) (Code) (QA) (Live) (Support) ``` --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q3(b) — RPA vs SDLC (7 Marks) > [!NOTE] > Below is a structured 7-point elaboration. *Each well-explained point = 1 mark.* 1. **Goal:** SDLC aims to *build new software products*. RPA aims to *automate existing manual processes* without building new software. *(1 Mark)* 2. **Approach:** SDLC is *invasive* — it changes or builds the underlying application code. RPA is *non-invasive* — it operates on the UI layer of existing systems. *(1 Mark)* 3. **Development Time:** SDLC projects take **6–18 months**. RPA projects can be deployed in **2–8 weeks** due to drag-and-drop development. *(1 Mark)* 4. **Cost:** SDLC involves higher infrastructure, developer salaries, and longer project cycles. RPA is significantly cheaper because it reuses existing infrastructure. *(1 Mark)* 5. **Skill Set:** SDLC requires professional software engineers proficient in coding. RPA can be implemented by **citizen developers** and business analysts with minimal coding. *(1 Mark)* 6. **Testing:** SDLC testing covers unit, integration, and system-level tests on the code. RPA testing focuses on **exception handling, data variability, and UI-change resilience**. *(1 Mark)* 7. **Maintenance and Scalability:** SDLC maintenance is complex and code-dependent. RPA bots are easy to reconfigure, and scaling means **duplicating the bot on additional machines**. *(1 Mark)* --- ### ❓ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **Q4. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** **(a)** With a real-world example, explain why **RPA is not a replacement for SDLC** but a **complement** to it. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** Compare the **testing strategies, documentation artifacts, and change management** approaches of SDLC and RPA in a structured table with explanatory notes. *(7 Marks)* --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q4(a) — RPA as a Complement, Not a Replacement (7 Marks) > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Example:** A retail bank wants to offer mobile check deposit to its customers. 1. The **new mobile app** with image-recognition-based check clearing is built using **SDLC** — it involves new business logic, new APIs, and new databases. *(1 Mark)* 2. However, the bank has **2 million legacy customers** whose KYC data is stored in a **1990s mainframe**. Migrating this data via SDLC would require rewriting the mainframe — a 3-year, ₹10 Crore project. *(1 Mark)* 3. Instead, the bank deploys **RPA bots** that read the mainframe's terminal screen (UI layer), extract customer data, and feed it into the new mobile app's database nightly. *(1 Mark)* 4. Therefore, **SDLC built the new core product**, while **RPA acted as the bridge** between the old and new worlds. *(1 Mark)* 5. **Why RPA cannot replace SDLC:** RPA cannot create new algorithms, build scalable microservices, or design a new UX — these require genuine software engineering, i.e., SDLC. *(1 Mark)* 6. **Why SDLC alone is insufficient:** Pure SDLC cannot economically automate high-volume, low-complexity, rule-based tasks (e.g., invoice entry) without over-engineering them. *(1 Mark)* 7. **Conclusion:** RPA and SDLC are **complementary pillars** of modern digital transformation. *(1 Mark)* --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q4(b) — Testing, Documentation, and Change Management (7 Marks) > [!NOTE] > Structured 3-section answer. Each section = ~2.3 marks. **Section 1 — Testing Strategy (2 Marks):** - **SDLC:** Multi-layered — Unit Testing, Integration Testing, System Testing, and UAT. Defects are debugged at the code level using tools like Selenium, JUnit. - **RPA:** Focuses on **end-to-end process testing**. Testers run the bot with diverse input data (valid, invalid, exception cases). Tools like UiPath Test Suite verify that the bot's UI interactions remain stable. Testing is *less about code correctness* and *more about data variance and UI resilience*. **Section 2 — Documentation (2 Marks):** - **SDLC:** Heavily documented. Artifacts include SRS, SDD, STD (Software Test Document), and user manuals. Documentation is a *legal and audit requirement* in many industries. - **RPA:** Lightweight documentation. Primary artifacts are the **PDD (Process Design Document)** that captures the as-is and to-be process flow, and the **SDD (Solution Design Document)** that defines the bot's workflow logic. Diagrams are often created in tools like **Blue Prism Process Designer** or **UiPath Studio**. **Section 3 — Change Management (2 Marks):** - **SDLC:** Changes require **full Software Change Request (SCR)** workflows — code review, regression testing, version control via Git, and staged deployments. Risk is high; rollback is expensive. - **RPA:** Changes are typically *re-recordings* or *drag-and-drop modifications* in the workflow. A simple UI change (e.g., a button moved) can be fixed in **hours**. However, this is also RPA's biggest **weakness** — too many small UI changes can break the bot frequently. **Summary Table (1 Mark):** | Aspect | SDLC | RPA | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Testing | Code-level (Unit, Integration) | Process-level (Exception handling) | | Documentation | SRS, SDD, STD | PDD, SDD (visual workflows) | | Change Management | SCR, Git, regression cycles | Re-configure workflows (hours) | --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU University Exams:** > > 1. **Writing "RPA is better than SDLC"** — *Wrong framing.* Examiners look for the *complementary* relationship. Always state: *"RPA complements SDLC; it does not replace it."* > 2. **Confusing PDD with SDD roles:** Many students incorrectly swap the definitions. **PDD = Process Design Document (what the bot does). SDD = Solution Design Document (how the bot does it technically).** > 3. **Forgetting the "non-invasive" keyword** — This is the **most-scanned term** in the answer script. Use it explicitly. > 4. **Listing differences without explanation** — KTU expects 1-line *justification* for every difference. A bare table without explanation loses up to **40% marks**. > 5. **Mentioning only technical differences** — Always include **non-technical parameters** (cost, time, skill set, scalability) for full marks. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Difference from SDLC"** - [x] **SDLC = Build new software** | **RPA = Automate existing processes** - [x] **SDLC = Invasive** (changes code) | **RPA = Non-invasive** (UI layer) - [x] **SDLC = High-code** (Java, Python) | **RPA = Low-code** (drag-and-drop) - [x] **SDLC = 6–18 months** | **RPA = 2–8 weeks** - [x] **SDLC = High cost** | **RPA = Low cost** (30–60% savings) - [x] **SDLC = Software engineers** | **RPA = Citizen developers + BAs** - [x] **SDLC = Code-level testing** | **RPA = Process-level + exception testing** - [x] **SDLC = SRS, SDD, STD** | **RPA = PDD, SDD (visual)** - [x] **SDLC = Complex change mgmt** | **RPA = Quick reconfiguration (hours)** - [x] **Key Mantra:** *"RPA is a complement to SDLC, not a replacement."* - [x] **Keyword to always use:** *Non-invasive*, *Presentation layer*, *Digital workforce*, *Citizen developer*, *PDD*, *SDD*.

topics.title: Robotic Control Flow Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Robotic Control Flow Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Robotic Control Flow Architecture** in the context of Robotic Process Automation (RPA) refers to the structured, rule-based design framework that governs the **sequence of operations**, **branching decisions**, **iterative loops**, and **state transitions** executed by a software robot (bot) during the automated completion of a business process. It is the cognitive "skeleton" that dictates **what the bot does next**, **under what condition**, and **how many times**, mirroring the control structures of classical computer science (sequence, selection, iteration) but applied to UI-level and API-level business tasks.

According to the UiPath and Automation Anywhere reference frameworks (which form the de facto industry standard cited in KTU Module 1 syllabi), the control flow architecture is composed of three primitive constructs:

1. **Sequences** – Linear, top-to-bottom execution of activities.
2. **Flowcharts** – Graph-based control with branching and merging nodes.
3. **State Machines** – Finite-state formalisms where transitions are driven by triggered events.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The "Robotic Control Flow Architecture" is a foundational topic that bridges the *Workflow Designer* concepts with *Decision-Making Logic*. Direct 14-mark questions are commonly framed around Flowcharts vs. State Machines or around the construction of decision logic for a given business scenario.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box – Control Flow vs. Data Flow**
> - **Control Flow** = *The order* in which activities execute (the "brain").
> - **Data Flow** = *The movement* of variables/arguments between activities (the "bloodstream").
> RPA workflows are evaluated on **both**, but this topic focuses exclusively on **Control Flow**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Railway Signaling System

Imagine a **railway junction controller** in a busy station:

- **The Track Itself** = The **Sequence** (the train must follow a defined path).
- **The Signal Lights & Switches** = The **Decision / If-Else** blocks (green means "go straight", red means "divert").
- **The Looping Siding Track** = A **While/For Loop** (the train keeps circling the storage track until a permit is granted).
- **The Station Master & Logbook** = A **State Machine** (the entire junction is in a particular "state" — *Arriving*, *At Platform*, *Departing*, *Cleared* — and only certain actions are valid in each state).

Just as a railway cannot function safely without its signaling architecture, an RPA bot cannot execute a business process reliably without a well-designed **control flow architecture**. A poorly architected bot will either stall (infinite loop), crash (unhandled exception path), or take the wrong decision (incorrect branch), much like a derailed train.

## 1.3 Core Components at a Glance

| Component | Execution Nature | Best Use-Case | Cognitive Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sequence | Linear, deterministic | Short, linear sub-tasks (e.g., login + read file) | **Low** |
| Flowchart | Graph-based, branching | Complex business logic with many decision points | **Medium** |
| State Machine | Event-driven, finite-state | Long-running, transactional, exception-heavy processes | **High** |

The standard unit of measurement for control flow complexity in RPA projects is the **Cyclomatic Complexity (CC)**, defined by McCabe (1976), which counts the number of linearly independent paths through a workflow. A workflow with **CC ≤ 10** is considered maintainable; above **15** it is flagged for refactoring.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of RPA Control Flow

### Pillar 1 — The Sequence
- Activities execute **strictly top-to-bottom**.
- No native branching, no looping.
- Ideal for **reusable sub-workflows** (login, logout, file open, send email).
- Equivalent in code to a `main()` function body with no conditionals.

### Pillar 2 — The Flowchart
- A **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** with explicit **Decision Diamonds**, **Merge Nodes**, and **Connector Arrows**.
- Supports the full set of classical control structures:
  - **Selection:** If-Then, If-Then-Else, Switch (multi-way)
