- **Industry Stat:** ~34% of bot failures are timing-related, making Delay/synchronization critical for production stability.
- **Category:** Delay belongs to **Workflow Control** activities in UiPath.
- **KTU Exam Frequency:** Appears in **Module 2** of PEAIT415; expect 3-mark conceptual and 7-mark comparison questions.
- **Mnemonic for Properties:** **"D-D-C"** → **D**uration, **D**isplayName, **C**ontinueOnError.
- **Mnemonic for Best Practice:** **"Smart Over Hard"** → Smart waits are preferred over hard delays.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Do While Activity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The Do While Activity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Do While Activity** is a **control flow activity** in UiPath Studio that belongs to the **Workflow > Control Flow** category. It executes a sequence (or a single activity) placed inside its *Body* container at least **one time unconditionally**, and then repeatedly re-executes the body **as long as** the Boolean expression specified in its *Condition* field evaluates to **True**. The loop terminates immediately when the Condition evaluates to **False**.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the Do While Activity is classified as a **post-test loop** because the condition is verified *after* the body has been executed for the first time. This is in direct contrast to the *While* activity, which is a *pre-test loop* (condition checked *before* body execution).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The "Eat at Least One Bite" Rule**
> 
> Imagine a mother telling her child at dinner:
> *"You **must eat at least one spoon** of vegetables. After that, keep eating **only if** you are still hungry."*
> 
> - The **first bite is mandatory** → Do While guarantees one execution.
> - The **continuation depends on hunger** → Loop continues while `Condition == True`.
> - **The moment hunger is gone** → The child stops eating → Loop terminates.
> 
> In contrast, a *While* loop would be: *"Eat only if you are hungry from the start"* — meaning the child might not eat at all if not hungry initially.

### 1.3 Key Operational Metrics

- **Loop Type**: Post-test (Exit-controlled) loop
- **Minimum Iterations**: **1** (guaranteed)
- **Maximum Iterations**: **Unbounded** (theoretically infinite — *risk of infinite loop*)
- **Condition Type**: `Boolean` (True / False only)
- **Evaluation Position**: End of the loop body
- **Default Condition Value**: `True` (UiPath Studio default — *caution: causes infinite loop*)

### 1.4 Activity Location in UiPath Studio

| Property | Specification |
|---|---|
| Activity Panel Path | Workflow → Control Flow → Do While |
| Namespace | `System.Activities.Statements.DoWhile` |
| Parent Class | `System.Activities.Statements.While` (sibling) |
| Compatible Containers | Sequence, Flowchart, State Machine |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

> [!NOTE]
> The Do While Activity follows a strictly deterministic execution flow. Below is the canonical control sequence as defined in the .NET `System.Activities.Statements.DoWhile` class used by UiPath.

1. **Entry Phase**: The workflow engine reaches the Do While Activity.
2. **First Execution (Unconditional)**: The body container executes **exactly one time**, regardless of the Condition's initial value.
3. **Condition Evaluation**: After the body completes, the Boolean expression in the *Condition* field is evaluated.
4. **Decision Branch**:
   - If **True** → Control jumps back to the start of the body → repeat from Step 2.
   - If **False** → Loop terminates → workflow continues to the next activity *after* the Do While.
5. **Exit Phase**: Final activity in the body has completed *and* Condition returned False.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Design

The Do While structure exists because in many RPA automation scenarios, the bot **must perform an action at least once** before it can decide whether to repeat it. Classic scenarios include:

- Logging into an application (first login attempt is mandatory; retry only if failed).
- Reading the first record from a database (the first read is needed; subsequent reads depend on existence).
- Submitting a form at least once (validation runs after submission).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No traditional physics/math formulas apply here.** Instead, use the following **control-flow truth table** and **structural equivalence table** for exam derivations.

#### 2.3.1 Do While Truth Table

| Iteration | Body Executed? | Condition Value (post-body) | Action |
|:---------:|:--------------:|:---------------------------:|:-------|
| 1 | **Yes (Mandatory)** | True | Jump back to Body start |
| 1 | **Yes (Mandatory)** | False | Terminate loop |
| 2 | Yes | True | Jump back to Body start |
| 2 | Yes | False | Terminate loop |
| N | Yes | True | Jump back to Body start |
| N | Yes | False | Terminate loop |

#### 2.3.2 Do While vs While vs For Each — Structural Equivalence

| Parameter | Do While | While | For Each |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Test Position** | Post-test | Pre-test | Pre-test (collection-based) |
| **Min Iterations** | **1** | **0** | **0** (if collection empty) |
| **Condition Type** | Boolean | Boolean | Implicit (T) |
| **Risk of No Execution** | **None** | High | High (empty collection) |
| **Infinite Loop Risk** | **High** (if condition always True) | High | Low |
| **Counter Required** | Optional | Optional | Built-in |

#### 2.3.3 Key Properties (Examiner-Favorite Table)

| Property | Type | Purpose | Default |
|---|---|---|---|
| `DisplayName` | String | Activity label in workflow | "Do While" |
| `Condition` | Boolean expression | Termination condition | **True** (dangerous) |
| `Body` | Activity container | Activities to repeat | Empty |

### 2.4 Real-World RPA Engineering Utility

In real-world UiPath deployments, the Do While Activity is heavily used for:

- **Retry-After-Failure Logic**: Try an action; if it fails, retry; if success, stop.
- **Queue Item Processing**: Read queue items until the queue is empty.
- **Page-by-Page Web Scraping**: Scrape page 1 always; continue while "Next" button is enabled.
- **Polling Systems**: Check a job status at least once; poll again if still "Running".
- **Database Cursor Looping**: Fetch the first record; keep fetching while records exist.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Execution Flow Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivation proves why Do While guarantees **at least one execution**, contrasting it mathematically with the While loop.

Let:

- **B** = Body execution event
- **C** = Boolean condition value
- **T** = True
- **F** = False
- **n** = iteration counter (n ∈ ℕ, starting at 1)

#### 3.1.1 Do While Execution Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & n \leftarrow 1 \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Execute } B_n \quad \text{(unconditional first execution)} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Evaluate } C_n = \text{eval}(\text{ConditionExpression}) \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{If } C_n = T \Rightarrow n \leftarrow n+1 \text{ and go to Step 2} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{If } C_n = F \Rightarrow \text{EXIT loop, continue workflow}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion**: The body $B_1$ executes **before** $C_1$ is even evaluated. This is the formal proof of the *post-test* property.

#### 3.1.2 While Execution Derivation (for contrast)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & n \leftarrow 1 \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Evaluate } C_n = \text{eval}(\text{ConditionExpression}) \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{If } C_n = T \Rightarrow \text{Execute } B_n,\; n \leftarrow n+1 \text{ go to Step 2} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{If } C_n = F \Rightarrow \text{EXIT loop without executing } B_n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion**: Body $B_1$ executes *only if* $C_1 = T$. If $C_1 = F$, **zero executions occur**.

### 3.2 Iteration Count Bound Derivation

For a Do While loop where the Condition is initially evaluated as **True** for $k$ consecutive iterations and then **False**:

$$
\text{Total Iterations} = k + 1
$$

Where the `+1` accounts for the **mandatory first execution**. In the worst case (Condition always True):

$$
\lim_{k \to \infty} (k + 1) = \infty
$$

This is the formal statement of the **infinite loop risk**.

### 3.3 Conversion Equivalence Proof — Do While → While

A Do While loop can be algorithmically converted into a While loop with a flag variable:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{// Original Do While:} \\
& \text{do} \; \{ \text{Body} \} \; \text{while} \; (C); \\
\\
& \text{// Equivalent While:} \\
& \text{flag} \leftarrow T; \\
& \text{while} \; (\text{flag} == T) \; \{ \\
& \quad \text{Body}; \\
& \quad \text{flag} \leftarrow C; \\
& \}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification**: 
- The While loop's `flag` is initialized to `T`, guaranteeing one execution of `Body`.
- After `Body`, `flag` is assigned the current value of `C`.
- If `C = F`, `flag = F`, the While condition fails, and the loop terminates — equivalent to Do While termination.

This proves the **computational equivalence** between the two structures when auxiliary state is introduced.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Do While

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This decision matrix is a **frequently tested** KTU concept. Memorize the rule-of-thumb.

| Scenario | Use Do While? | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Login at least once, retry if failed | ✅ Yes | First attempt is mandatory |
| Process queue until empty | ✅ Yes | Need to read first item before checking emptiness |
| Skip entirely if condition false initially | ❌ No | Use While instead |
| Iterate over known collection | ❌ No | Use For Each instead |
| Infinite background worker (server) | ✅ Yes | Start once, never stop |
| Validate input before processing | ❌ No | Use While (validate first) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Do While Activity — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["WORKFLOW ENTRY POINT"] --> nodeB["DO WHILE ACTIVITY"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["EXECUTE BODY UNCONDITIONALLY"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["BODY ACTIVITY 1 LOG ASSIGN"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["BODY ACTIVITY 2 READ FILE"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["BODY ACTIVITY 3 CLICK BUTTON"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["EVALUATE BOOLEAN CONDITION"]
    nodeG --> nodeH{"IS CONDITION TRUE"}
    nodeH -- YES --> nodeC
    nodeH -- NO  --> nodeI["EXIT LOOP"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["WORKFLOW CONTINUES TO NEXT ACTIVITY"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["WORKFLOW END"]
```

### 4.2 Do While vs While — Comparative Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["ENTRY"] --> B{"DO WHILE"}
    B --> C["EXECUTE BODY ONCE"]
    C --> D{"CHECK CONDITION"}
    D -- TRUE --> C
    D -- FALSE --> E["EXIT"]

    F["ENTRY"] --> G{"WHILE CHECK CONDITION"}
    G -- TRUE --> H["EXECUTE BODY"]
    G -- FALSE --> I["EXIT NO EXECUTION"]
    H --> G
```

### 4.3 Do While Nested Inside a Sequence — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    seq1["SEQUENCE MAIN"] --> init["INITIALIZE COUNTER N = 1"]
    init --> dowhile["DO WHILE LOOP"]
    dowhile --> body1["BODY: INCREMENT N BY 1"]
    body1 --> body2["BODY: LOG CURRENT N"]
    body2 --> cond["CONDITION: N LESS THAN 5"]
    cond -- TRUE --> dowhile
    cond -- FALSE --> endact["END ACTIVITIES LOG FINAL N"]
    endact --> fin["WORKFLOW COMPLETE"]
```

### 4.4 Property Panel Schematic (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["DO WHILE ACTIVITY PANEL"] --> Q["DISPLAY NAME FIELD"]
    P --> R["CONDITION FIELD BOOLEAN"]
    P --> S["BODY CONTAINER WORKFLOW SCOPE"]
    R --> R1["EXPRESSION EDITOR"]
    R1 --> R2["VARIABLES INT INPUT"]
    S --> S1["ACTIVITY 1"]
    S --> S2["ACTIVITY 2"]
    S --> S3["ACTIVITY N"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Q: Define the Do While Activity in UiPath. Why is it classified as a post-test loop?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The Do While Activity is a control flow activity in UiPath Studio that executes a sequence of activities placed in its Body at least once and then repeats the execution as long as the specified Boolean condition evaluates to True. **(2 marks)**
> 
> It is classified as a *post-test loop* because the condition is checked *after* the body has been executed for the first time, ensuring that the body always runs a minimum of one time. **(1 mark)**

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Q: Differentiate between the Do While and While activities in UiPath with one key structural difference.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Do While | While |
|---|---|---|
| Test Type | Post-test | Pre-test |
| Min Executions | **1** (guaranteed) | **0** (may never execute) |
| Condition Check | After body | Before body |
> 
> **Key structural difference (1 mark):** In *Do While*, the body executes before the condition is evaluated; in *While*, the condition is evaluated before the body executes.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 
> Students commonly lose marks in the following ways:
> 1. **Confusing Do While with While** — stating that Do While may not execute at all (it always executes at least once). **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Writing the condition as a string ("True") instead of a Boolean expression** — UiPath requires an actual expression like `counter < 5`, not the literal text "True". **−1 mark**.
> 3. **Forgetting the mandatory first iteration property** in the truth table question. **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Missing the `Body` container** in the property listing. **−1 mark**.
> 5. **Using Do While where For Each or While is more appropriate** in the scenario-based question. **−2 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the working of the Do While activity in UiPath with a suitable example. List its key properties. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

The Do While Activity is a control flow activity located under *Workflow → Control Flow* in UiPath Studio. **(0.5 marks)**

**Working Mechanism (4 marks):**
1. The activity is added to the workflow.
2. The *Body* container holds the activities to be repeated.
3. The *Condition* field accepts a Boolean expression.
4. The body executes **once unconditionally**.
5. The condition is evaluated; if **True**, the body re-executes; if **False**, the loop terminates.
6. Execution continues to the activity *after* the Do While block.

**Example (1.5 marks):**
> Scenario: A bot needs to read rows from an Excel file starting from row 2 (header is row 1). It should read at least one row, then continue reading while there are still data rows.
> - **Condition**: `rowIndex <= dt.Rows.Count`
> - **Body**: `Read Cell` + `Add Data Row` + `Increment rowIndex`
> - The bot **always reads the first data row**, then loops until `rowIndex > dt.Rows.Count`.

**Key Properties (1 mark):**
- `DisplayName`, `Condition` (Boolean), `Body` (Activity container)

---

**(b)** Compare Do While, While, and For Each activities in UiPath across at least four parameters with a tabular presentation. State one scenario where Do While is the *only* correct choice. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Do While | While | For Each |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Min Iterations** | 1 (guaranteed) | 0 | 0 (empty collection) |
| **Test Type** | Post-test | Pre-test | Pre-test (collection) |
| **Counter Needed** | Optional | Optional | Not needed (built-in) |
| **Risk of Infinite Loop** | High | High | Low |
| **Best Use Case** | Mandatory first action | Conditional-only action | Known collection iteration |

**Tabular presentation (4 marks)** — full table as above.

**Mandatory-first scenario (3 marks):**
> *Scenario: A bot must attempt to log into a banking portal at least once. If the login fails (e.g., wrong OTP), it should retry up to 3 times; otherwise, it should stop immediately.*
> 
> In this case, the **first login attempt is mandatory** — using a *While* loop might skip login entirely if the condition is initially False. **Do While** is the *only* correct choice because it guarantees one execution before checking the retry condition.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Adapted]`

**(a)** Write a step-by-step procedure to implement a Do While loop in UiPath that reads all rows from a DataTable `dt_Employees` and logs each employee name. The loop must stop when all rows are processed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Create a `Int32` variable `rowIndex` with default value `0`.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Drag the **Do While** activity from *Workflow → Control Flow* into the main Sequence.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** In the **Condition** field, enter the Boolean expression:
> `rowIndex < dt_Employees.Rows.Count`

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Inside the **Body** container, add a **Log Message** activity.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** In the Log Message, set the message to:
> `dt_Employees.Rows(rowIndex)("Name").ToString`

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Add an **Assign** activity *after* the Log Message to increment the counter:
> `rowIndex = rowIndex + 1`

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Run the workflow. The first row (index 0) is **always** logged, even if the table had only one row. Subsequent rows are logged only while the index is within bounds.

**Working Verification (bonus, optional):**
- If `dt_Employees` has 5 rows, the loop runs 5 times (indices 0–4), logging all 5 names.
- The loop terminates when `rowIndex` becomes `5`, making the condition `5 < 5 = False`.

---

**(b)** Explain with a neat diagram how the Do While activity differs structurally from the While activity. Why can a Do While loop never result in zero executions? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Diagrammatic Explanation (4 marks):**

**Do While Flow:**
> Entry → Execute Body → Check Condition → (True: jump back to Body; False: Exit)

**While Flow:**
> Entry → Check Condition → (True: Execute Body → back to Condition; False: Exit without execution)

(Use the comparative mermaid diagrams from SECTION 4.2 as reference.)

**Why Do While can never have zero executions (3 marks):**

Mathematically, let $E$ = number of executions, $C$ = condition expression.

For a *While* loop:
$$
E = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } C_0 = F \\ k & \text{if } C_0 = T, C_k = F \end{cases}
$$

For a *Do While* loop:
$$
E = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } C_1 = F \\ k+1 & \text{if } C_k = T, C_{k+1} = F \end{cases}
$$

Since the body executes **before** the first condition check, $E \geq 1$ is mathematically guaranteed. This is the formal proof that Do While **cannot** have zero executions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — The Do While Activity**

- ✅ **Location**: *Workflow → Control Flow → Do While* in UiPath Studio
- ✅ **Loop Type**: **Post-test** (exit-controlled) loop
- ✅ **Guaranteed Min Iterations**: **1** (unconditional first execution)
- ✅ **Max Iterations**: **Unbounded** (theoretically infinite)
- ✅ **Condition Property**: Boolean expression — e.g., `counter < 10`, `file.Exists = True`
- ✅ **Default Condition**: `True` — *dangerous, causes infinite loop*
- ✅ **Body Container**: Holds one or more activities to repeat
- ✅ **Key Difference from While**: Condition is checked *after* body, not *before*
- ✅ **Key Difference from For Each**: No built-in iterator; works on conditions, not collections
- ✅ **Mandatory Use Cases**: First-time login, queue polling, retry-after-failure logic
- ✅ **Common Mistake**: Forgetting to update the loop variable → infinite loop
- ✅ **Best Practice**: Always use a counter or a clearly terminating condition
- ✅ **Equivalent While Construction**: Use a `flag` variable initialized to `True` to convert any Do While into a While loop
- ✅ **Namespace**: `System.Activities.Statements.DoWhile`
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Weight**: High-yield — frequently tested in Part A (definitions) and Part B (comparisons/scenarios)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The If Activity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** The If Activity # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **If Activity** in UiPath is a fundamental **workflow control flow activity** that enables **conditional branching** within an automation sequence. It evaluates a specified Boolean expression (the **Condition**) and routes the execution flow to either the **Then branch** (when the condition evaluates to `True`) or the **Else branch** (when the condition evaluates to `False`). The If Activity is a direct implementation of the classical **IF–THEN–ELSE** decision structure used in structured programming, adapted for visual, drag-and-drop RPA development. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *"The If Activity allows a robot to make decisions by evaluating a specified condition. Depending on whether the condition is true or false, the robot executes a different set of activities."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy Imagine you walk up to a **railway ticket counter**. The clerk looks at your **age**: - If your age is **less than 60**, you pay the **full fare** (Then branch). - Otherwise (age ≥ 60), you receive a **senior citizen discount** (Else branch). The clerk doesn't perform both actions — they make a **decision** based on a **single condition** and execute **exactly one path**. The If Activity works identically: it asks **one question** (the condition), and based on the answer, executes **only one block** of activities. ## 1.3 Location in UiPath Studio The If Activity is found in the **Activities Panel** under the path: ``` Activities Panel → Workflow Control → If ``` > [!NOTE] > **Key Design Insight:** The If Activity is a **container-type activity** — it holds child activities inside its `Then` and `Else` branches, unlike simple actions such as `Assign` or `Log Message`. ## 1.4 Core Properties of the If Activity | Property | Description | Data Type | |----------|-------------|-----------| | `Condition` | Boolean expression evaluated by the engine | `Boolean` | | `Then` | Container for activities executed when Condition = True | `Activity Container` | | `Else` | Container for activities executed when Condition = False | `Activity Container` | | `DisplayName` | User-friendly label shown in the workflow designer | `String` | > [!TIP] > The **Condition** field is the *only* mandatory property that must always be filled. The `Then` and `Else` branches are *optional containers* — if left empty, the workflow simply skips that branch. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic of the If Activity The execution engine follows a deterministic **three-stage decision process**: 1. **Stage 1 — Condition Evaluation:** The runtime engine reads the expression in the `Condition` property and resolves it to a strict Boolean value (`True` or `False`). 2. **Stage 2 — Branch Selection:** The engine checks the resolved value: - If `True` → execution proceeds into the **Then branch**. - If `False` → execution proceeds into the **Else branch**. 3. **Stage 3 — Sequential Execution:** Activities inside the selected branch are executed **top-to-bottom** in the order they appear in the container. After the branch completes, control returns to the activity immediately following the If Activity in the parent scope. ## 2.2 Types of Conditions The `Condition` field accepts any expression that resolves to a Boolean. These are classified into three families: ### A. Comparison Conditions Compare two values using relational operators. | Operator | Meaning | Example | |----------|---------|---------| | `=` | Equal to | `age = 18` | | `<>` | Not equal to | `status <> "Active"` | | `>` | Greater than | `salary > 50000` | | `<` | Less than | `balance < 1000` | | `>=` | Greater than or equal to | `score >= 75` | | `<=` | Less than or equal to | `quantity <= 10` | ### B. Logical Conditions Combine multiple Boolean sub-expressions. | Operator | Meaning | Example | |----------|---------|---------| | `AndAlso` | Logical AND (short-circuit) | `age >= 18 AndAlso age <= 60` | | `OrElse` | Logical OR (short-circuit) | `role = "Admin" OrElse role = "Manager"` | | `Not` | Logical negation | `Not isLocked` | ### C. Method-Based Conditions Invoke methods that return Boolean values. - `String.Contains("text")` - `String.StartsWith("prefix")` - `String.IsNullOrEmpty(variable)` - `ListVariable.Contains(item)` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** UiPath uses `AndAlso` and `OrElse` (rather than `And`/`Or`) because they implement **short-circuit evaluation** — the second operand is evaluated *only* if necessary. This prevents runtime errors when the first operand already determines the result. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Decision Logic | Construct | Mathematical / Logical Form | Truth Table Summary | |-----------|----------------------------|---------------------| | Simple If | $f(C) = \begin{cases} T \rightarrow \text{Then} \\ F \rightarrow \text{Else} \end{cases}$ | Binary decision | | AND | $A \cdot B$ | True only if both True | | OR | $A + B - A \cdot B$ | False only if both False | | NOT | $\neg A$ | Inverts truth value | | Combined | $(A \lor B) \land \neg C$ | Composite condition | Where $A$, $B$, $C$ are Boolean sub-conditions and $\land$, $\lor$, $\neg$ represent logical AND, OR, and NOT respectively. ## 2.4 Real-World RPA Engineering Utility The If Activity is the **decision backbone** of virtually every business automation: - **Invoice Processing:** If invoice total > 10,000 → route to manager approval; Else → auto-approve. - **Email Triage:** If sender domain = "client.com" → mark priority; Else → move to general folder. - **Loan Eligibility:** If age ≥ 21 AND income ≥ 30,000 → eligible; Else → reject. - **Exception Handling:** If file exists → process; Else → send notification. ## 2.5 Nesting the If Activity The If Activity supports **nesting** — placing another If Activity inside either the `Then` or `Else` branch. This is the KTU-recommended approach for implementing **multi-way decisions** (an alternative to the `Switch` activity for binary-only splits). ``` If (Condition1) ├── Then → If (Condition2) │ ├── Then → Action A │ └── Else → Action B └── Else → Action C ``` > [!WARNING] > **Excessive nesting (depth > 3)** reduces workflow readability. KTU examiners mark down on "spaghetti workflows" — prefer the **Switch** activity or **State Machine** for complex multi-branch logic. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example — Loan Eligibility Checker **Problem Statement:** A bank wants to automate loan approval. The robot reads an applicant's `age` and `monthlyIncome`. The loan is **auto-approved** if: - Age is between **21 and 60** (inclusive), **AND** - Monthly income is **≥ ₹30,000** Otherwise, the application is **rejected**. Build the If Activity logic. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1:** Identify the Boolean condition for approval. The condition must be true when **all** of the following hold: - $age \geq 21$ - $age \leq 60$ - $monthlyIncome \geq 30000$ Combined using logical AND: $$ Condition = (age \geq 21) \;\land\; (age \leq 60) \;\land\; (monthlyIncome \geq 30000) $$ **Step 2:** Translate into UiPath expression syntax. In the `Condition` field of the If Activity, write: ``` age >= 21 AndAlso age <= 60 AndAlso monthlyIncome >= 30000 ``` **Step 3:** Build the Then branch (auto-approve path). Drag activities into the `Then` container: 1. `Assign` → `applicationStatus = "Auto-Approved"` 2. `Log Message` → `"Loan auto-approved for applicant ID: " + applicantID` 3. `Send SMTP Mail Message` → notify applicant of approval **Step 4:** Build the Else branch (rejection path). Drag activities into the `Else` container: 1. `Assign` → `applicationStatus = "Rejected"` 2. `Log Message` → `"Application rejected. Reason: Criteria not met."` 3. `Send SMTP Mail Message` → notify applicant of rejection **Step 5:** Trace the execution for a sample input. Let $age = 35$ and $monthlyIncome = 45000$. $$ Condition = (35 \geq 21) \land (35 \leq 60) \land (45000 \geq 30000) $$ $$ = (True) \land (True) \land (True) $$ $$ = True $$ Execution enters the **Then branch** → application is auto-approved. Now test $age = 19$ and $monthlyIncome = 50000$. $$ Condition = (19 \geq 21) \land (19 \leq 60) \land (50000 \geq 30000) $$ $$ = (False) \land (True) \land (True) $$ $$ = False $$ Execution enters the **Else branch** → application is rejected. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Outcomes | Input Age | Input Income | Condition Evaluates To | Branch Executed | Final Status | |-----------|--------------|------------------------|-----------------|--------------| | 35 | 45000 | True | Then | Auto-Approved | | 19 | 50000 | False | Else | Rejected | | 65 | 70000 | False | Else | Rejected | | 25 | 25000 | False | Else | Rejected | | 45 | 30000 | True | Then | Auto-Approved | | 60 | 29999 | False | Else | Rejected | > [!NOTE] > The last row demonstrates a critical edge case: even when age condition is satisfied, a single failed sub-condition causes the entire composite condition to evaluate to `False`. This is the **short-circuit AND** behaviour of `AndAlso`. ## 3.3 Nested If Activity — Multi-Criteria Triage **Problem:** A helpdesk robot must classify tickets into four categories based on **priority** ("High"/"Low") and **type** ("Technical"/"Billing"). ### Step-by-Step Construction **Step 1:** Outer If checks priority. $$ Condition_1 = (priority = \text{"High"}) $$ **Step 2:** Inside the Then branch, nest another If for type discrimination. $$ Condition_2 = (ticketType = \text{"Technical"}) $$ **Step 3:** Inside the Else branch of the outer If, nest another If. $$ Condition_3 = (ticketType = \text{"Technical"}) $$ ### Resulting Decision Tree | priority | ticketType | Outer Result | Inner Result | Final Action | |----------|------------|--------------|--------------|--------------| | High | Technical | Then | Then | Assign to Tech-L1 | | High | Billing | Then | Else | Assign to Finance | | Low | Technical | Else | Then | Queue for next day | | Low | Billing | Else | Else | Auto-reply with FAQ | > [!IMPORTANT] > This nested pattern generates $2^n$ outcomes from $n$ binary decisions. KTU examiners often test whether students can **map a requirements table to a nested If tree**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 If Activity — Execution Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start of If Activity]) --> evalA{Condition
Evaluates} evalA -- True --> thenA[Execute Then Branch
Activities Top-to-Bottom] evalA -- False --> elseA[Execute Else Branch
Activities Top-to-Bottom] thenA --> endA([Continue to Next Activity]) elseA --> endA ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR inputA[Input Variables:
age, monthlyIncome] --> engineA{If Activity
Decision Engine} engineA -- True --> thenBlockA[Then Container:
Assign status
Log Message
Send Email] engineA -- False --> elseBlockA[Else Container:
Assign status
Log Message
Send Email] thenBlockA --> outputA[Output:
applicationStatus set] elseBlockA --> outputA outputA --> nextA([Next Workflow Step]) ``` ## 4.3 Nested If Activity — Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD rootA{priority = High} rootA -- True --> inner1A{type = Technical} rootA -- False --> inner2A{type = Technical} inner1A -- True --> action1A[Assign to Tech-L1] inner1A -- False --> action2A[Assign to Finance] inner2A -- True --> action3A[Queue for Next Day] inner2A -- False --> action4A[Auto-Reply with FAQ] ``` ## 4.4 UiPath Designer — Conceptual Layout ```mermaid flowchart TD seqA([Main Sequence]) --> if1A{{If Activity
DisplayName: Check Eligibility}} if1A --> condProp["Condition:
age >= 21 AndAlso
age <= 60 AndAlso
monthlyIncome >= 30000"] if1A --> thenContA[Then Branch Container] if1A --> elseContA[Else Branch Container] thenContA --> a1A[Assign: status = Approved] thenContA --> a2A[Log Message] a1A --> a3A[Send Email] elseContA --> b1A[Assign: status = Rejected] elseContA --> b2A[Log Message] b1A --> b3A[Send Email] a3A --> nextA([Next Activity]) b3A --> nextA ``` > [!TIP] > In the actual UiPath Studio, the If Activity appears as a **yellow diamond-shaped container** with two clearly labelled drop zones: **"Then"** (top) and **"Else"** (bottom). Activities are dragged into these zones exactly as shown in the architectural diagram. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Explain the If Activity in UiPath. List any two properties of the If Activity.* **Model Answer:** The If Activity is a workflow control flow activity in UiPath used to make decisions based on a condition. It evaluates a Boolean expression and executes one of two branches: the **Then branch** if the condition is `True`, or the **Else branch** if the condition is `False`. It implements the classic IF–THEN–ELSE decision structure in a visual manner. Two key properties: 1. **Condition** — a Boolean expression that determines which branch executes. 2. **DisplayName** — the user-visible label for the activity in the workflow designer. *(Alternative property: `Then` / `Else` containers — the activity containers that hold child activities for each branch.)* --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between `And` and `AndAlso` operators used in the If Activity condition.* **Model Answer:** | Aspect | `And` | `AndAlso` | |--------|-------|-----------| | Evaluation | Evaluates **both** operands always | Evaluates second operand **only if first is True** | | Short-circuit | No | Yes | | Performance | Slower for complex second operands | Faster (skips unnecessary evaluations) | | Safety | May throw runtime errors if second operand is invalid | Safer — avoids null/divide-by-zero errors | In UiPath, `AndAlso` is the **preferred** operator for If Activity conditions because of its short-circuit behaviour. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]** **(a)** *[7 Marks]* Explain the If Activity in UiPath with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the `Condition`, `Then`, and `Else` properties. **(b)** *[7 Marks]* Design an If Activity workflow for the following scenario: *"A retail company wants to automate discount calculation. If a customer's purchase amount is greater than ₹5,000, apply a 20% discount; otherwise, apply a 10% discount. Display the final bill amount."* Write the condition and list the activities inside both branches. --- #### Model Solution for (a) **Definition:** The If Activity is a conditional branching activity in UiPath that allows the robot to make decisions by evaluating a Boolean expression. It is located under **Activities Panel → Workflow Control → If**. **Role of Properties:** - **Condition:** This is a Boolean expression that the runtime engine evaluates. The expression may use comparison operators (`=`, `<>`, `>`, `<`), logical operators (`AndAlso`, `OrElse`, `Not`), or methods that return Boolean values. The result determines branch flow. - **Then:** A container that holds the activities to be executed **only when the condition evaluates to True**. It is a drag-and-drop zone in the workflow designer. - **Else:** A container that holds the activities to be executed **only when the condition evaluates to False**. It is optional; if empty, the workflow simply skips the branch. **Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart TD s1([Start]) --> c1{Condition} c1 -- True --> t1[Then Branch:
Activities A1, A2, ...] c1 -- False --> e1[Else Branch:
Activities B1, B2, ...] t1 --> end1([Continue]) e1 --> end1 ``` **Key Points:** - The If Activity executes **only one** branch (mutually exclusive). - Both branches are **optional containers**. - Activities inside each branch execute **sequentially** (top-to-bottom). - If Activities can be **nested** for multi-level decision logic. --- #### Model Solution for (b) **Scenario Analysis:** The robot must read a `purchaseAmount` variable and compute the final bill. **Condition:** $$ Condition = (purchaseAmount > 5000) $$ **Then Branch (20% discount path):** 1. `Assign` → `discount = purchaseAmount * 0.20` 2. `Assign` → `finalAmount = purchaseAmount - discount` 3. `Log Message` → `"Final Bill: ₹" + finalAmount.ToString` **Else Branch (10% discount path):** 1. `Assign` → `discount = purchaseAmount * 0.10` 2. `Assign` → `finalAmount = purchaseAmount - discount` 3. `Log Message` → `"Final Bill: ₹" + finalAmount.ToString` **Verification Table:** | purchaseAmount | Condition | Discount % | Final Amount | |----------------|-----------|-----------|--------------| | 3000 | False | 10% | 2700 | | 5000 | False | 10% | 4500 | | 5001 | True | 20% | 4000.8 | | 10000 | True | 20% | 8000 | > [!NOTE] > Note that `purchaseAmount = 5000` evaluates to `False` because the condition uses strict `>` (greater than), not `>=`. This is a common KTU exam pitfall. --- ### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]** **(a)** *[7 Marks]* With a suitable example, explain how the If Activity handles nested conditions. What are the limitations of deep nesting? **(b)** *[7 Marks]* An HR department wants to classify employees for bonus eligibility. The rule is: *"An employee is eligible for a bonus if (experience ≥ 5 years AND performance rating = 'A') OR (experience ≥ 10 years AND performance rating ≥ 'B')."* Write the If Activity condition and explain the execution logic with a truth table. --- #### Model Solution for (a) **Nested If Activity:** The If Activity supports **nesting** — placing another If Activity inside the `Then` or `Else` branch of a parent If Activity. This allows the implementation of **multi-level decision trees**. **Example — Customer Loyalty Program:** - **Outer If:** `totalSpent > 100000` - **Then Branch → Inner If:** `yearsAsCustomer >= 5` - Then → Assign loyalty tier = "Platinum" - Else → Assign loyalty tier = "Gold" - **Else Branch → Inner If:** `totalSpent > 50000` - Then → Assign loyalty tier = "Silver" - Else → Assign loyalty tier = "Bronze" **Limitations of Deep Nesting:** 1. **Readability Collapse:** Workflows become hard to follow beyond 2–3 nesting levels — the "arrow" pattern of branches gets visually confusing. 2. **Maintenance Difficulty:** Any change in business rules may require restructuring the entire nested tree. 3. **Debugging Complexity:** Tracing execution paths during debugging becomes time-consuming. 4. **Better Alternatives:** For complex multi-way decisions, the **Switch** activity or **State Machine** pattern is preferred by KTU-recommended best practices. --- #### Model Solution for (b) **Composite Condition Derivation:** Let $E$ = `experience ≥ 5`, $A$ = `performance = "A"`, $F$ = `experience ≥ 10`, $B$ = `performance ≥ "B"`. The eligibility rule is: $$ Eligible = (E \land A) \lor (F \land B) $$ **UiPath Condition Expression:** ``` (experience >= 5 AndAlso performance = "A") OrElse (experience >= 10 AndAlso performance >= "B") ``` **Truth Table:** | Experience | Performance | E | A | F | B | (E ∧ A) | (F ∧ B) | Eligible | |------------|-------------|---|---|---|---|---------|---------|----------| | 3 | A | F | T | F | T | F | F | **False** | | 5 | A | T | T | F | T | T | F | **True** | | 10 | B | T | F | T | T | F | T | **True** | | 10 | C | T | F | T | F | F | F | **False** | | 15 | B | T | F | T | T | F | T | **True** | | 2 | A | F | T | F | T | F | F | **False** | **Execution Logic:** 1. The runtime engine evaluates the **first operand** of `OrElse`: `(experience >= 5 AndAlso performance = "A")`. 2. If this is `True`, the **short-circuit** behaviour skips the second operand and the condition is `True` overall. 3. If the first operand is `False`, the engine then evaluates the second operand: `(experience >= 10 AndAlso performance >= "B")`. 4. The combined result drives the branch selection: `True` → bonus approved; `False` → bonus denied. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - Students often write `And` and `Or` instead of `AndAlso` and `OrElse` — **lose 1–2 marks**. > - Confusing `=` (assignment in some languages) with `=` (equality in UiPath) — UiPath uses `=` for comparison. > - Forgetting parentheses around composite `(E ∧ A)` and `(F ∧ B)` sub-expressions — operator precedence errors are common. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — The If Activity** - The If Activity is located at **Activities Panel → Workflow Control → If**. - It is a **container activity** with three key properties: **Condition**, **Then**, **Else**. - The **Condition** must evaluate to a **Boolean** (`True` / `False`). - Exactly **one branch** executes — the branches are **mutually exclusive**. - Use `=` for equality, `<>` for not-equal, `>`, `<`, `>=`, `<=` for comparisons. - Use `AndAlso` (AND) and `OrElse` (OR) for **short-circuit logical composition**. - Use `Not` for **logical negation**. - **Nesting** If Activities enables multi-level decision trees. - Avoid nesting **beyond 2–3 levels** — prefer **Switch** or **State Machine** for complex logic. - Common use cases: **eligibility checks**, **exception routing**, **discount calculation**, **email triage**. - The If Activity does **not loop** — for iteration, use **While**, **Do While**, or **For Each**.

topics.title: The Switch Activity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The Switch Activity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Switch Activity** is a **control flow activity** in Robotic Process Automation (RPA) platforms such as UiPath that executes **one and only one** branch of activities from a list of mutually exclusive options, based on the runtime evaluation of a **predefined expression** against a set of **case values**.

In UiPath Studio, the Switch Activity resides in the **Workflow → Control Flow** category of the Activities Panel. It functions analogously to the `switch`/`case` construct found in imperative programming languages (C, Java, C#, Python). The activity takes a single input **expression**, evaluates it at runtime, and dispatches execution to the block of activities nested inside the matching case. If no match is found, an optional **Default** case handles the execution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – RPA):** The Switch Activity is grouped under *"Decision-Making and Control Flow Activities"* alongside the **If**, **While**, **Do While**, **For Each**, and **Flow Decision** activities. Examiners expect students to contrast the Switch with the If activity and identify scenarios where Switch is the optimal choice.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Railway Signal Controller

Imagine a **railway junction control room**. The station master monitors a single lever whose position can take exactly one of several values: **North Line, South Line, East Line, or West Line**. Depending on the lever's position (the *expression value*), only **one set of tracks is aligned** at a time. Trains arriving at the junction are automatically routed to the correct platform corresponding to the active lever position. If, due to a malfunction, the lever sits in an unrecognized state, the trains are redirected to a **safety siding (the Default case)** so the system never crashes.

| Real-World Analogy Element | Switch Activity Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Lever Position | Evaluated Expression Value |
| Available Track Directions | Defined Case Values |
| Aligned Track Section | Activities Inside a Case |
| Malfunction / Unknown State | Default Case |
| Station Master | UiPath Runtime Engine (Robot) |

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Data Types

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Switch Activity supports **multiple TypeArguments**, each representing a distinct data category. Choosing the correct TypeArgument is a **2-mark examiner favorite** in KTU exams.

| TypeArgument (Data Type) | Description | Example Value |
|---|---|---|
| **String** | Sequence of characters | `"ADMIN"`, `"PROCESSED"` |
| **Int32** | 32-bit signed integer | `1`, `404`, `200` |
| **Object** | Generic .NET object (default) | Any variable type |
| **GenericValue** | UiPath dynamic data type | Strings or Numbers |

### 1.4 Switch vs. If — When to Use Which?

> [!NOTE]
> The Switch Activity must be chosen over the **If** activity when the decision branches exceed **three (3)** options. The If-ElseIf chain becomes unmanageable beyond this threshold, whereas the Switch scales linearly and remains readable.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The Switch Activity operates on a strict evaluation pipeline. Understanding each stage is critical for KTU 14-mark descriptive questions.

### Stage 1 — Expression Declaration
The developer specifies a single **Expression** property whose runtime value drives the routing logic. This expression must evaluate to a value compatible with the selected **TypeArgument**.

### Stage 2 — Case Configuration
Multiple **Case** entries are appended to the activity. Each case has:
- A unique **Case key value** (literal, variable, or expression).
- A nested container holding one or more child activities to execute when matched.

### Stage 3 — Runtime Dispatch
When the workflow executes the Switch block, the runtime engine performs an **equality comparison** between the evaluated Expression and each Case key value, sequentially (in UiPath) or in parallel (depending on platform implementation).

### Stage 4 — Match & Execute
Upon detecting the **first matching Case**, the corresponding child activities are executed. Execution then **exits the Switch block entirely** (no fall-through by default, unlike C/Java's switch).

### Stage 5 — Default Fallback
If no Case matches the expression value, control is transferred to the **Default** sub-block. If no Default block is defined and no match occurs, the Switch silently completes without executing any branch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Focus:** The Switch Activity in UiPath **does NOT support fall-through behavior** (unlike the C `switch` statement where omitting `break` allows execution to cascade into subsequent cases). Each case is isolated.

## 2.2 Core Properties — High-Yield Reference Table

| Property | Purpose | Mandatory? |
|---|---|---|
| `DisplayName` | Human-readable label shown on the canvas | Yes |
| `TypeArgument` | Declares the data type of the Expression | Yes |
| `Expression` | The variable or value to evaluate | Yes |
| `Cases` (Collection) | Holds the list of case branches | Yes |
| `Default` (sub-activity container) | Fallback branch when no match | Optional |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Decision Logic Sheet

The Switch Activity is governed by a mathematical decision function. Let $E$ denote the evaluated expression and $C_i$ denote the $i$-th Case key value where $i \in \{1, 2, ..., n\}$. The execution path $\mathcal{P}$ is defined as:

$$
\mathcal{P} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Block}_i, & \text{if } E = C_i \text{ for some } i \\
\text{Default}, & \text{if } E \neq C_i \text{ for all } i \text{ and Default exists} \\
\varnothing, & \text{if } E \neq C_i \text{ for all } i \text{ and Default is absent}
\end{cases}
$$

The runtime comparison operator is **strict equality** ($=$), and the search is linear:

$$
\mathcal{T}(n) = \mathcal{O}(n)
$$

where $\mathcal{T}(n)$ is the time complexity to find a match in $n$ cases.

## 2.4 Real-World RPA Utility

The Switch Activity is extensively deployed in production-grade bots for:
- **HTTP Response Code Handling:** Routing 200 → Success Block, 404 → Retry Block, 500 → Alert Block.
- **Order Status Processing:** `"NEW"` → Create Record, `"PROCESSING"` → Update Timer, `"SHIPPED"` → Notify Customer, `"DELIVERED"` → Close Ticket.
- **Menu-Driven Automation:** Navigating a legacy terminal UI by parsing user keystrokes.
- **Language/Region Routing:** Selecting localized templates based on a country code.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Worked Example — HTTP Status Code Router

**Scenario:** An RPA bot invokes a REST API and receives an HTTP status code stored in the variable `statusCode` of type `Int32`. Construct a Switch Activity that handles four distinct cases.

### Step 1 — Variable Declaration
Assume `statusCode` has been assigned a value from the API response. For this derivation, let:

$$
\text{statusCode} = 200
$$

### Step 2 — Switch Configuration

| Property | Assigned Value |
|---|---|
| `DisplayName` | `HTTP Status Router` |
| `TypeArgument` | `Int32` |
| `Expression` | `statusCode` |

### Step 3 — Define the Case Set

| Case Key Value | Branch Activities | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `200` | Log Message → Continue Workflow | Request Successful |
| `201` | Log Message → Save Response Body | Resource Created |
| `400` | Throw BusinessRuleException | Bad Request |
| `404` | Retry Scope (3x) | Resource Not Found |
| `500` | Send SMTP Mail → Escalate | Internal Server Error |

### Step 4 — Evaluate the Decision Function

Substituting into the formula defined in Section 2.3:

$$
E = 200, \quad C_1 = 200, \quad C_2 = 201, \quad C_3 = 400, \quad C_4 = 404, \quad C_5 = 500
$$

The engine performs sequential equality checks:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Check 1:} \quad E &\stackrel{?}{=} C_1 \\
200 &\stackrel{?}{=} 200 \\
\text{Result:} \quad &\text{TRUE} \rightarrow \text{Execute Block}_1
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Execution Termination

Since Block 1 matches, the engine executes the **Log Message → Continue Workflow** activities and **immediately exits** the Switch container. Cases 2 through 5 are skipped.

### Step 6 — Default Path Verification

For demonstration, if `statusCode = 418` (an unmapped value):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Check 1:} \quad 418 &\neq 200 \\
\text{Check 2:} \quad 418 &\neq 201 \\
\text{Check 3:} \quad 418 &\neq 400 \\
\text{Check 4:} \quad 418 &\neq 404 \\
\text{Check 5:} \quad 418 &\neq 500 \\
\text{Result:} \quad &\text{No Match} \rightarrow \text{Execute Default Branch}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** Notice how the linear search yields **worst-case complexity** of $\mathcal{O}(n)$ when the match is absent or located at the final case. For high-frequency dispatch tables exceeding 10 cases, a dictionary-based lookup is preferable.

## 3.2 Decision Flow Analysis

The decision flow can be represented as a deterministic finite automaton (DFA). Let $\Sigma$ denote the alphabet of possible expression values and $q_0$ denote the initial state:

$$
\text{DFA} = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)
$$

where:
- $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, ..., q_n, q_d\}$ (states for each case plus default).
- $\Sigma$ = domain of the TypeArgument.
- $\delta$ = the deterministic transition function.
- $F$ = set of accepting/final states (the executed block).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Switch Activity Execution Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Workflow Reaches Switch Activity] --> B[Evaluate Expression E]
    B --> C{E equals Case 1?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Execute Case 1 Block]
    C -- No --> E{E equals Case 2?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Execute Case 2 Block]
    E -- No --> G{E equals Case N?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Execute Case N Block]
    G -- No --> I{Default Case Exists?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Execute Default Block]
    I -- No --> K[Exit Switch Silently]
    D --> L[Exit Switch Activity]
    F --> L
    H --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Continue Next Workflow Activity]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** Each diamond node represents an equality comparison. Execution enters from the top and exits from the bottom after exactly one branch is executed. This visually reinforces the **mutually exclusive** nature of Switch cases.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Switch in a UiPath Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    UIPathCanvas["UiPath Workflow Canvas"] --> SeqContainer["Sequence Activity"]
    SeqContainer --> HTTPReq["HTTP Request Activity"]
    HTTPReq --> AssignVar["Assign: statusCode = ResponseCode"]
    AssignVar --> SwitchAct["Switch Activity"]
    SwitchAct --> CaseBlockA["Case 200: Success Handler"]
    SwitchAct --> CaseBlockB["Case 400: Bad Request Handler"]
    SwitchAct --> CaseBlockC["Case 404: Retry Logic"]
    SwitchAct --> CaseBlockD["Case 500: Error Escalation"]
    SwitchAct --> DefaultBlock["Default: Log Unhandled Status"]
    CaseBlockA --> EndNode["End of Workflow"]
    CaseBlockB --> EndNode
    CaseBlockC --> EndNode
    CaseBlockD --> EndNode
    DefaultBlock --> EndNode
```

## 4.3 Component Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SwitchContainer["Switch Activity Boundary"]
        ExprProp["Expression Property"]
        TypeArg["TypeArgument Property"]
        CaseList["Cases Collection"]
        DefaultSlot["Default Sub-Activity Slot"]
    end
    Engine["UiPath Runtime Engine"] --> ExprProp
    Engine --> TypeArg
    Engine --> CaseList
    CaseList --> CaseA["Case Key: A"]
    CaseList --> CaseB["Case Key: B"]
    CaseList --> CaseN["Case Key: N"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define the Switch Activity in UiPath. Mention any two of its key properties.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> The Switch Activity is a control flow activity in UiPath that selects and executes one branch from a list of mutually exclusive options based on the runtime value of a specified expression. It evaluates the expression and matches it against predefined case values, executing only the matching case block. **Two key properties** are: **(1) TypeArgument** — which defines the data type of the expression (such as String, Int32, Object, or GenericValue), and **(2) Expression** — which holds the variable or value to be evaluated at runtime. *(3 marks)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between the If Activity and the Switch Activity in UiPath. When is Switch preferred?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> The **If Activity** evaluates a single Boolean condition and executes either the Then-branch or the Else-branch (binary decision). In contrast, the **Switch Activity** evaluates one expression against multiple case values and executes the branch corresponding to the first matching case (multi-way decision). **Switch is preferred when the decision logic involves three or more mutually exclusive branches**, as it provides better readability, scalability, and maintainability compared to nested If-ElseIf constructs. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students commonly **forget to declare the TypeArgument**, leading to runtime type-mismatch exceptions. Always specify it explicitly.
> - Confusing the **Default** case with an **Else** block — Default executes only when NO case matches; it is not a catch-all exception handler.
> - Writing cases with **duplicate key values** results in undefined behavior (only the first match executes). Always use unique keys.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the Switch Activity in UiPath with a suitable diagram. Describe its key properties and execution flow. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Switch Activity** is a control flow activity located under *Workflow → Control Flow* in UiPath Studio. It enables multi-way branching by evaluating a single expression and dispatching execution to one of several case blocks based on equality match.

**Key Properties:**
1. **`DisplayName`:** The friendly name visible on the workflow canvas.
2. **`TypeArgument`:** Declares the data type (String, Int32, Object, GenericValue).
3. **`Expression`:** The variable or expression whose value is evaluated at runtime.
4. **`Cases`:** A collection of case entries, each with a key value and an associated activity block.
5. **`Default`:** An optional sub-container executed when no case matches.

**Execution Flow:**

The workflow engine enters the Switch activity and evaluates the Expression property. It then performs sequential equality comparisons against each Case key. Upon the first match, the corresponding block is executed, and control immediately exits the Switch. If no match occurs and a Default block exists, Default is executed. Otherwise, the Switch silently completes.

**Diagram (refer Section 4.1):**

The flowchart shows the linear search through case comparisons, with the Default branch as the fallback terminal node.

*(7 marks — breakdown: definition 2 marks, properties 3 marks, execution flow 2 marks)*

**(b) Design a Switch Activity for an RPA bot that processes incoming emails. The bot must route emails based on the `mailSubject` variable of type `String` into four cases: "INVOICE", "COMPLAINT", "APPOINTMENT", and "QUERY". Provide the configuration table and explain the decision logic. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Configuration Table:**

| Property / Case | Value / Activity Block |
|---|---|
| `DisplayName` | Email Router Switch |
| `TypeArgument` | String |
| `Expression` | mailSubject |
| **Case 1 Key** | `"INVOICE"` |
| Case 1 Activity | Extract PDF Attachment → Save to Finance Folder |
| **Case 2 Key** | `"COMPLAINT"` |
| Case 2 Activity | Forward Email to support@company.com |
| **Case 3 Key** | `"APPOINTMENT"` |
| Case 3 Activity | Add Event to Outlook Calendar |
| **Case 4 Key** | `"QUERY"` |
| Case 4 Activity | Generate Auto-Reply via Template |
| **Default Key** | *(implicit)* |
| Default Activity | Log "Unknown Subject" → Move to Review Folder |

**Decision Logic:**

The Switch Activity evaluates `mailSubject` as a String. Let:

$$
E = \text{mailSubject}, \quad C_i \in \{\text{"INVOICE"}, \text{"COMPLAINT"}, \text{"APPOINTMENT"}, \text{"QUERY"}\}
$$

The engine tests:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\stackrel{?}{=} \text{"INVOICE"} \\
E &\stackrel{?}{=} \text{"COMPLAINT"} \\
E &\stackrel{?}{=} \text{"APPOINTMENT"} \\
E &\stackrel{?}{=} \text{"QUERY"}
\end{aligned}
$$

The first TRUE comparison routes execution to its corresponding activity block. If `mailSubject = "PROMOTION"`, none match, and the Default block executes, logging the unknown subject. *(7 marks — breakdown: configuration 4 marks, decision logic 3 marks)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a) Discuss the concept of control flow activities in UiPath. Compare the Switch Activity with the If and Flow Decision activities, highlighting scenarios where each is optimal. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Control Flow Activities** govern the **order and conditions** under which activities in an RPA workflow are executed. They include sequential execution, branching, looping, and parallel processing constructs.

**Comparison:**

| Feature | If Activity | Flow Decision | Switch Activity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Decision Type** | Binary (True/False) | Binary (True/False) | Multi-way (n-cases) |
| **Branches** | 2 (Then + Else) | 2 (True + False) | n + Default |
| **Expression Type** | Boolean | Boolean | Any (Int32, String, Object) |
| **Optimal Scenario** | Simple conditional check | Visual workflow charts | Multiple value-based routes |
| **Scalability** | Poor beyond 3 branches | Poor beyond 3 branches | Excellent for many branches |

**Optimal Use Cases:**
- **If:** Validating a single condition (e.g., `age >= 18`).
- **Flow Decision:** Same as If but preferred in **Flowchart** diagrams for visual clarity.
- **Switch:** Routing based on discrete values (e.g., status codes, menu choices).

*(7 marks — breakdown: control flow concept 2 marks, comparison table 3 marks, scenarios 2 marks)*

**(b) A bank loan processing bot receives a credit score stored in an Int32 variable `creditScore`. Design a Switch Activity that classifies the applicant into categories: 750+ (Excellent), 650–749 (Good), 550–649 (Fair), Below 550 (Poor). Explain why Switch requires careful TypeArgument selection here. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Challenge — Range-Based Classification:**

The Switch Activity performs **exact equality matching**, not range comparisons. Therefore, the raw `creditScore` (e.g., 720) cannot directly match case keys like "650–749". The solution requires a **pre-processing step**.

**Solution — Pre-Computed Category Variable:**

Introduce an Assign activity before the Switch:

$$
\text{scoreCategory} = \text{CInt((creditScore - 1) / 100) + 1}
$$

This produces:
- `creditScore >= 750` → `scoreCategory = 7` (Excellent)
- `creditScore = 650–749` → `scoreCategory = 6` (Good)
- `creditScore = 550–649` → `scoreCategory = 5` (Fair)
- `creditScore < 550` → `scoreCategory = 4` (Poor, handled by Default)

**Switch Configuration:**

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| `TypeArgument` | Int32 |
| `Expression` | scoreCategory |
| **Case `7`** | Send Email: "Excellent — Auto-Approve" |
| **Case `6`** | Send Email: "Good — Manual Review" |
| **Case `5`** | Send Email: "Fair — Request Co-Signer" |
| **Default** | Send Email: "Poor — Reject Application" |

**Why TypeArgument Matters:**

Selecting `Int32` ensures the engine compares integer values efficiently and avoids string-to-number coercion errors. If `TypeArgument` were set to `String` while case keys were integers, a **type mismatch exception** would occur at runtime. *(7 marks — breakdown: problem identification 2 marks, formula derivation 2 marks, configuration 2 marks, TypeArgument justification 1 mark)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — The Switch Activity**

- **Location:** Activities Panel → *Workflow* → *Control Flow* → **Switch**.
- **Purpose:** Multi-way branching based on exact value matching of a single expression.
- **Core Properties:** `DisplayName`, `TypeArgument`, `Expression`, `Cases`, `Default`.
- **Supported TypeArguments:** `String`, `Int32`, `Object`, `GenericValue`.
- **Comparison Logic:** Strict equality ($=$), sequential linear search, $\mathcal{O}(n)$ complexity.
- **No Fall-Through:** Unlike C/Java, executing one case does NOT cascade into subsequent cases.
- **Default Case:** Executes only when no case matches; not mandatory but strongly recommended for robustness.
- **Optimal Use:** When decision branches exceed 3 mutually exclusive options based on discrete values.
- **Common Pitfalls:**
  - Forgetting `TypeArgument` declaration.
  - Using ranges instead of discrete values (Switch cannot perform relational comparisons).
  - Duplicating case key values.
  - Confusing Default with a generic catch-all exception handler.
- **Vs. If Activity:** If = binary Boolean check; Switch = multi-way value match.
- **Vs. Flow Decision:** Flow Decision is binary and visual (for Flowcharts); Switch is multi-way and value-driven.
- **Exam Tip:** Always draw the **Mermaid-style flowchart** of the Switch execution in descriptive answers to score full marks on the diagram component.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The While Activity - The For Each Activity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The While Activity - The For Each Activity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The While Activity

### Formal Definition
The **While Activity** in UiPath is an **entry-controlled (pre-test) loop construct** that repeatedly executes a body of activities as long as a specified Boolean condition evaluates to `True`. The condition is evaluated *before* every iteration, meaning if the condition is initially `False`, the body may never execute.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The While Activity is part of the *Flow Control / Looping* concepts in RPA. It is a non-iterative count-based control structure — the developer must manually manage termination.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of the **While Activity** like a **security guard at a college gate**:
- The guard checks your **ID card (condition)** *before* letting you in.
- As long as your ID is valid, you may enter.
- The moment your ID expires (condition becomes `False`), entry is denied, and the guard stops checking.
- The guard *never* checks the ID *after* you enter — only at the gate (entry point).

### Key Properties (UiPath)

| Property | Description | Data Type |
|---|---|---|
| `Condition` | Boolean expression checked before each iteration | `Boolean` |
| `Body` | Sequence of activities executed while condition is `True` | `Activity` |
| `DisplayName` | Friendly name shown in the workflow | `String` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule:** A `While` loop with a condition that never becomes `False` creates an **Infinite Loop**, which freezes the bot. KTU examiners frequently test this pitfall.

---

## 1.2 The For Each Activity

### Formal Definition
The **For Each Activity** is a **collection-iterating loop** that traverses every element in a data structure (Array, List, Dictionary, DataTable rows, etc.) and executes the body once per element. It is also known as a **definite iteration** loop because the number of iterations is predetermined by the collection's size.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The For Each Activity is the **most-used loop** in real-world RPA because enterprise data is almost always stored in collections (Excel rows, queue items, lists).

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **teacher distributing graded papers to a class**:
- The teacher has a **stack of papers (collection)**.
- For **each paper (each item)**, the teacher calls the student (executes the body) and hands over the paper.
- The loop ends automatically when the stack is empty — no need for the teacher to count.

### Key Properties (UiPath)

| Property | Description |
|---|---|
| `TypeArgument` | Data type of items (e.g., `String`, `Int32`, `DataRow`) |
| `Values` | The collection to iterate (Array, List, Dictionary, DataTable) |
| `Item` (variable) | Holds the current element during iteration |
| `Body` | Activities executed per element |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule:** The `TypeArgument` MUST match the actual element type of the collection, or the workflow will throw a **Type Mismatch** runtime exception.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics of the While Activity

The `While` activity follows a strict **PRE-CHECK execution model**:

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. The bot enters the `While` block.
2. The `Condition` expression is **evaluated FIRST**.
3. If `Condition = True` → execute the `Body` → return to Step 2.
4. If `Condition = False` → exit the loop; proceed to the next activity.
5. **Zero iterations are possible** if the condition is initially `False`.

> [!NOTE]
> The condition must be a **side-effect mutating expression** (e.g., `counter < 10`) — otherwise the loop never terminates.

### KTU Cheat Sheet — While Activity

| Aspect | Specification |
|---|---|
| Loop Type | Entry-controlled (pre-test) |
| Min Iterations | 0 (body may never run) |
| Max Iterations | Infinite (if condition never becomes False) |
| Termination | Condition-driven (Boolean) |
| Counter Variable | Manual (developer-managed) |
| Best Use Case | Reading a stream until EOF, retry logic, polling |
| Pitfall | Infinite loop if condition logic is flawed |

---

## 2.2 Operational Mechanics of the For Each Activity

The `For Each` activity follows an **implicit iteration model**:

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. The bot obtains the collection via the `Values` property.
2. Internally, UiPath initializes an **iterator (cursor)** at the first element.
3. For each iteration:
   - The current element is assigned to the `Item` variable.
   - The `Body` executes.
   - The iterator advances to the next element.
4. When the iterator reaches the **end of the collection**, the loop exits.
5. **No manual counter is needed.**

### KTU Cheat Sheet — For Each Activity

| Aspect | Specification |
|---|---|
| Loop Type | Collection-based (definite iteration) |
| Iterations | Exactly equal to `Collection.Count` |
| Counter Variable | None (implicit) |
| Termination | Automatic (end of collection) |
| Supported Collections | `Array`, `List<T>`, `Dictionary<TKey,TValue>`, `DataTable.Rows`, `IEnumerable` |
| Best Use Case | Processing rows of an Excel/CSV, iterating Queue Items |
| Pitfall | Modifying the collection *during* iteration causes runtime error |

---

## 2.3 Comparative Analysis: While vs For Each

| Criterion | While Activity | For Each Activity |
|---|---|---|
| **Condition Source** | Boolean expression (manual) | Implicit (collection size) |
| **Iteration Count** | Unknown (0 to ∞) | Known (0 to N) |
| **Counter Management** | Manual (`counter = counter + 1`) | Automatic |
| **Risk of Infinite Loop** | High | Impossible (finite collection) |
| **Works Without Collection** | Yes (any Boolean condition) | No (must have a collection) |
| **Performance** | Slightly faster (no iterator) | Slightly slower (iterator overhead) |
| **KTU Recommended** | Polling / retry / streaming | Data-row processing |
| **Nested Capability** | Yes | Yes (but use `For Each Row` for tables) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked *"Which loop should I use?"* — answer **For Each** for collections and **While** for condition-only loops (e.g., `retryUntilSuccess` patterns).

### Real-World RPA Utility
- **Banking Bot (While):** Retry a transaction until the bank server responds `OK` or 5 attempts are exhausted.
- **Invoice Bot (For Each):** Read 10,000 rows from an Excel sheet and email each vendor individually.
- **HR Bot (For Each):** Iterate through a `List<Employee>` and generate individual payslips.
- **Log Watcher (While):** Keep reading a system log file until the keyword `"ERROR"` appears.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of While Loop Trace Table

Let us formally derive the **execution trace** of a `While` loop using an example:

**Example Pseudocode (UiPath-style):**

```
counter = 0
WHILE counter < 4
    Log Message → "Iteration: " + counter.ToString
    counter = counter + 1
END WHILE
```

### Step-by-Step Trace

| Iteration # | `counter` (Start) | Condition `counter < 4` | Action | `counter` (End) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | `0 < 4` → **True** | Log "Iteration: 0" | 1 |
| 2 | 1 | `1 < 4` → **True** | Log "Iteration: 1" | 2 |
| 3 | 2 | `2 < 4` → **True** | Log "Iteration: 2" | 3 |
| 4 | 3 | `3 < 4` → **True** | Log "Iteration: 3" | 4 |
| 5 | 4 | `4 < 4` → **False** | **EXIT LOOP** | 4 |

**Final Output:** `"Iteration: 0", "Iteration: 1", "Iteration: 2", "Iteration: 3"`

**Generalized formula** for the number of iterations of a counter-based While loop:

$$
N_{iter} = \max(0,\ \lceil C_{end} - C_{start} \rceil)
$$

Where:
- $C_{start}$ = initial counter value
- $C_{end}$ = boundary value (where condition becomes False)

> [!NOTE]
> In the example: $N_{iter} = \lceil 4 - 0 \rceil = 4$ iterations. ✓ Matches the trace table.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of For Each Loop Trace Table

**Example:** A `List<String>` named `fruits = {"Apple", "Banana", "Cherry"}` is iterated.

### Step-by-Step Trace

| Iteration # | `Item` (Current Element) | Action | Iterator Position |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `"Apple"` | Log "Apple" | Advances to index 1 |
| 2 | `"Banana"` | Log "Banana" | Advances to index 2 |
| 3 | `"Cherry"` | Log "Cherry" | Advances past end |
| 4 | *(none)* | **EXIT LOOP** | — |

**Generalized formula:**

$$
N_{iter} = \vert \text{Collection} \vert
$$

Where $\vert \text{Collection} \vert$ denotes the cardinality (size) of the collection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical Note:** $\vert \text{Collection} \vert$ here is the *cardinality operator* (count of elements), NOT absolute value. In KTU answers, always clarify this to avoid losing marks.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Loop

| Scenario | Recommended Loop | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Read each row of a 50,000-row Excel file | **For Each Row** | Definite iteration over a `DataTable` |
| Poll a web service every 5 sec until `200 OK` | **While** | Condition depends on external response |
| Retry a login 3 times max | **Do While** (with counter) | Needs post-test logic |
| Iterate over a `Dictionary<string,int>` | **For Each** | Native dictionary support |
| Read a text file line-by-line until EOF | **While** (`line <> null`) | No fixed-size collection |
| Process a `QueueItem` batch | **For Each** | Collection of queue items |
| Validate user input until it matches regex | **Do While** | Post-test loop needed |
| Traverse `List<MailMessage>` from Outlook | **For Each** | Built-in `TypeArgument = MailMessage` |

---

## 3.4 Algebraic Complexity Comparison

Let $n$ be the number of iterations.

$$
T_{while}(n) = O(n) \quad \text{(linear time)}
$$

$$
T_{foreach}(n) = O(n) \quad \text{(linear time, with small iterator overhead constant } c)
$$

$$
T_{foreach}(n) = c \cdot n, \quad c > 1
$$

Both loops are **asymptotically equivalent** in Big-O notation, but the `For Each` has a marginally higher constant factor due to the **enumerator/iterator object** instantiated by the .NET CLR.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** For the board exam, this Big-O comparison is a *high-yield* question. Memorize: both are $O(n)$, but `For Each` has iterator overhead.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Control Flow — While Activity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START]) --> B[Initialize counter = 0]
    B --> C{Is condition True?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Execute Body Activities]
    D --> E[Update counter = counter + 1]
    E --> C
    C -- No --> F([EXIT loop, go to next activity])
    F --> G([END])
```

**Reading Guide:**
- The **diamond** represents the Boolean condition check.
- Notice the loop-back arrow returning to the condition (pre-test).
- If the condition is `False` on the very first check, the body **never executes**.

---

## 4.2 Control Flow — For Each Activity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START]) --> B[Get Collection from Values]
    B --> C{Are more elements?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Assign current element to Item]
    D --> E[Execute Body using Item]
    E --> F[Move iterator to next element]
    F --> C
    C -- No --> G([EXIT loop])
    G --> H([END])
```

**Reading Guide:**
- No manual counter — the iterator handles traversal.
- The loop **always** terminates when the collection is exhausted.

---

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOOP_WHILE["WHILE ACTIVITY"]
        W1[Manual Condition] --> W2[Manual Counter Update]
        W2 --> W3[Body Activities]
    end
    subgraph LOOP_FOREACH["FOR EACH ACTIVITY"]
        F1[Implicit Iterator] --> F2[Item auto-assigned]
        F2 --> F3[Body Activities]
    end
    W3 -.Compares to.-> F3
    style LOOP_WHILE fill:#FFE5B4
    style LOOP_FOREACH fill:#B4E5FF
```

---

## 4.4 Nested Loop Architecture (For Each inside While)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Outer: WHILE retry < 3]) --> B{retry < 3?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Outer Body: Fetch Page]
    C --> D([Inner: FOR EACH row in DataTable])
    D --> E{More rows?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Process row Item]
    F --> G[Next row]
    G --> E
    E -- No --> H[Increment retry]
    H --> B
    B -- No --> I([END])
```

**Use Case:** A bot retries scraping a webpage up to 3 times, processing each scraped row on every attempt.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the While Activity in UiPath. Mention one real-world scenario where it is preferred over For Each.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **While Activity** is an entry-controlled looping construct in UiPath that repeatedly executes a sequence of activities as long as a specified Boolean condition evaluates to `True`. The condition is checked *before* every iteration, so the body may execute **zero or more times**.

**Real-world scenario (1 mark):** A **polling bot** that checks a server's status every 5 seconds until it returns `200 OK`. Since there is no predefined collection, `For Each` cannot be used — a `While` loop with the condition `status <> "OK"` is the correct choice.

---

### Question 2: List any three properties of the For Each Activity and explain the role of `TypeArgument`.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Three key properties of the For Each Activity:

1. **`Values`** (1 mark) — Specifies the input collection to iterate (Array, List, Dictionary, DataTable, etc.).
2. **`TypeArgument`** (1 mark) — Declares the data type of the elements in the collection (e.g., `String`, `Int32`, `DataRow`). It must match the actual type, otherwise a **runtime type-mismatch exception** is thrown.
3. **`Body`** (1 mark) — The container holding the activities executed once per element.

**Role of `TypeArgument`:** It enables **type safety** by allowing the `Item` variable to be strongly typed, so IntelliSense and compile-time checks work correctly in UiPath Studio.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often confuse **While** (pre-test) with **Do While** (post-test). Always mention which type you are describing.
> - When drawing flowcharts, **forgetting the return arrow** from body to condition is a common 2-mark deduction.
> - Forgetting to **initialize the counter** before the `While` loop causes a "Variable not found" runtime error. Examiners specifically test this.
> - In `For Each`, never try to **modify the original collection inside the body** — it causes `InvalidOperationException`.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the operational mechanics of the **While Activity** in UiPath with a neat flowchart. Compare it with the **Do While Activity** by highlighting any four differences. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the step-by-step trace table for the following UiPath workflow and determine the final output. **(7 marks)**

```
counter = 1
sum = 0
```
