> **Board Exam Tip:** Examiners often award full marks only when the student mentions that Web Recording uses **HTML-based selectors** as opposed to Desktop Recording which uses **uiautomation/uia tag-based selectors**. Always mention this contrast in your answers.

---

## 1.4 Why Web Recording Matters in RPA Engineering

In real-world industry deployments, **web-based automation accounts for approximately 60–70% of all RPA projects** (especially in BFSI, e-commerce, and healthcare). Web Recording is the **fastest prototyping technique** for such projects because:

- It eliminates the need to manually inspect browser elements using DevTools.
- It automatically generates **robust selectors** using stable attributes.
- It drastically reduces the *Time-to-First-Bot* (TTFB) metric.
- It allows citizen developers (non-programmers) to build automations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Web Recording is part of the **Attended Automation** paradigm – the robot assists a human in real time. For *Unattended Automation* (robots running without humans), the workflow generated via Web Recording can be scheduled on UiPath Orchestrator.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept – How Web Recording Works (Layered Architecture)

Web Recording is built on a **three-layer architecture**:

### Layer 1: Event Listener Layer (Kernel)
- The UiPath **Browser Extension** (installed in Chrome/Edge) intercepts **DOM events** fired by the browser.
- DOM events captured include: `click`, `change`, `input`, `submit`, `keydown`, `keyup`, `mouseover`.
- For each event, the extension gathers the **target element's full HTML path** (ancestor chain up to `<html>`).

### Layer 2: Translation Layer (Engine)
- The captured event is sent to UiPath Studio.
- The engine classifies the event into one of the following RPA activities:
  - `Click` → maps to `Click` activity
  - `Input change` → maps to `Type Into` activity
  - `Selection change` → maps to `Select Item` activity
  - `Check/Toggle` → maps to `Check` / `Uncheck` activity
- The engine then constructs a **Selector XML** using the ancestor chain.

### Layer 3: Workflow Generation Layer (Output)
- The translated activities are inserted as nodes inside a sequence.
- The **Attach Browser** container wraps all activities and references the `Browser` selector.
- The workflow is saved as a `.xaml` file (eXtensible Application Markup Language).

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow (The "Why" and "How")

1. **Why does the recorder need a browser extension?**
   - Because browsers enforce **same-origin policy** and DOM security. Only an extension injected into the page can directly read the DOM tree of the current tab. Without the extension, UiPath would have to use accessibility APIs (slower and less reliable).

2. **How does the recorder identify the right element?**
   - It walks up the DOM tree from the clicked element to the root.
   - It records the `tag`, `id`, `name`, `class`, `innertext`, and `aaname` of every ancestor.
   - It then generates a **CSS-like XPath** string that uniquely identifies the element.

3. **Why are selectors sometimes "unstable"?**
   - Dynamic IDs (e.g., `id="abc123"`) change every page load. KTU expects students to know that **Wildcards (`*`)** and **Anchor Base** activities are used to stabilize such selectors.

4. **How is the recording started?**
   - The user clicks `Recording > Web` in UiPath Studio.
   - A floating recording toolbar appears on screen with: **Save & Exit**, **Esc (3 sec)**, and **Pause** buttons.

5. **What happens on Save & Exit?**
   - The recorded actions are inserted as activities into the workflow at the location where recording began (usually after an `Attach Browser` or `Open Browser` activity).

6. **What is the role of `Attach Browser`?**
   - It defines the **scope** – the browser window/tab inside which the robot must look for the recorded selectors. Without it, the selectors would be invalid.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Since Web Recording is a practical/computer-science topic, the "formulas" are largely **selector construction rules**. The table below summarizes the KTU high-yield points:

| Concept | Rule / Syntax | When Used | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Full Selector | `Selector string with full DOM path` | Unique, stable web element | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Partial Selector | Inside an `Attach Browser` container | When container already narrows scope | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Wildcard `*` | `aaname='*Login*'` | Element with dynamic name | ⭐⭐ |
| Wildcard `?` | `id='btn_??23'` | Exactly one character variation | ⭐⭐ |
| `idx` attribute | `idx='2'` | Multiple matches of same selector | ⭐⭐ |
| `parentid` | `parentid='loginForm'` | Element identified via parent | ⭐⭐ |
| `css-selector` | `css-selector='div.row > button'` | CSS-style targeting | ⭐⭐ |
| `aaname` | Visible text on screen | Buttons, links, labels | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| `innertext` | Hidden text content | Headings, spans, divs | ⭐⭐ |
| Validate Selector | `Ctrl + F2` in Studio | Always validate after recording | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Recording Mode | `Basic` vs `Advanced` (for App/Web Recorder) | Determines auto-container creation | ⭐⭐ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory KTU Board Concept:** In UiPath Web Recording, every activity has **two selectors**:
> - **Selector 1 (Target Element)**: The element being acted upon (e.g., the input box).
> - **Selector 2 (Anchor Element)**: A nearby stable element used to confirm the location (e.g., the label next to the input).
> This pairing is called the **Anchor Base** technique. Examiners love this question.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Web Recording is the **foundation of the following real-world industry use cases**:

- **Automated Invoice Entry**: Recording user actions on a vendor's web portal to upload PDF invoices.
- **Web Scraping for Price Comparison**: Recording navigation through e-commerce sites to extract prices.
- **HR Onboarding Bots**: Recording the steps of creating a new employee record in a web-based HRMS portal.
- **Claims Processing**: Recording the steps of submitting an insurance claim through a web form.
- **Email Marketing Automation**: Recording the steps of sending bulk emails via a webmail interface.

The recorded workflow is a **starting point** – professional developers enhance it with:
- **Exception Handling** (`Try-Catch` blocks).
- **REFramework** integration for transaction-based processing.
- **Orchestrator Queues** for large-scale unattended execution.
- **Config Files** (`Config.xlsx`) to externalize hard-coded values.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Recorded Activity Logic

## 3.1 The Selector Derivation (From DOM Event to XPath)

This is the most important derivation for KTU board exams. Let us derive the selector that is generated when a user clicks a **"Login" button** on a sample web page.

### Step 1: The User Performs an Action
A user opens a website and clicks the **Login** button. The browser fires a `click` DOM event.

### Step 2: The UiPath Browser Extension Intercepts the Event
The extension, injected into the DOM, reads the **target element's properties**.

**Target Element Properties (Login Button):**

- `tag` = `BUTTON`
- `id` = `loginBtn`
- `class` = `btn btn-primary`
- `innertext` = `Login`
- `aaname` = `Login`
- `name` = `login`

### Step 3: The Extension Walks Up the DOM Tree
Starting from `<button>`, it walks upward:

```
<button id="loginBtn" class="btn btn-primary" innertext="Login">
  └── <div class="form-group">
        └── <form id="loginForm">
              └── <body>
                    └── <html>
```

### Step 4: UiPath Engine Generates the Selector

The selector is constructed by concatenating ancestor tags from the outermost to the innermost.

$$
\text{Selector} = \text{html} \;\rightarrow\; \text{body} \;\rightarrow\; \text{form} \;\rightarrow\; \text{div} \;\rightarrow\; \text{button}
$$

In UiPath XML format, the generated selector is:

```xml
<html app='chrome.exe' title='My Website' />
  <webctrl tag='form' id='loginForm' />
    <webctrl tag='div' class='form-group' idx='1' />
      <webctrl tag='button' id='loginBtn' name='login' aaname='Login' />
```

### Step 5: Activity is Inserted
The engine wraps this selector inside a `Click` activity:

$$
\text{Click Activity} = \big\{ \text{Selector} : \text{html > body > form > div > button} \big\}
$$

### Step 6: Container is Wrapped
A `Type Into` or `Click` activity is always enclosed inside an `Attach Browser` activity that contains the **browser-level** selector.

$$
\text{Attach Browser} = \big\{ \text{Selector} : \text{html title='My Website'} \big\}
$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Web Recording vs Desktop Recording

This is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in KTU board exams. The full matrix is derived below:

| Parameter | Web Recording | Desktop Recording |
|---|---|---|
| **Target Application** | Web Browsers (Chrome, Edge, Firefox) | Windows Desktop Apps (Calculator, Notepad) |
| **Selector Tag** | `<webctrl>` (HTML based) | `<wnd>`, `<ctrl>` (uiautomation/uia based) |
| **Extension Required** | Yes – UiPath Browser Extension | No |
| **Container Type** | `Attach Browser` | `Attach Window` |
| **Default Speed** | Normal (1x) | Normal (1x) |
| **Relative Clipping** | Yes (via `clip` region) | Yes |
| **Best For** | Web forms, scraping, CRUD on web apps | Excel, SAP, legacy Win32 apps |
| **Selector Stability** | High (HTML ids are mostly stable) | Moderate (uia selectors can be brittle) |
| **Cross-Browser Support** | Yes (with extension) | N/A |
| **License Requirement** | Same (Studio Pro) | Same (Studio Pro) |

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Recording Procedure in UiPath (With Validation)

This is the **practical workflow** every KTU student must memorize.

**Step 1:** Open UiPath Studio → New Project → `Process`.

**Step 2:** Add an `Open Browser` activity. Enter the URL `https://example.com/login`.

**Step 3:** Click the **Recording** button in the top ribbon → Select **Web Recording**.

**Step 4:** A floating Recording toolbar appears. Hover over the desired element to highlight it.

**Step 5:** Perform actions: click input box, type username, click password box, type password, click Login button.

**Step 6:** Click **Save & Exit** on the recording toolbar.

**Step 7:** UiPath inserts activities: `Type Into 'username'`, `Type Into 'password'`, `Click 'Login Button'`.

**Step 8:** Validate each selector by clicking the activity → pressing `Ctrl + F2` → confirming the element is highlighted in the browser.

**Step 9:** Run the workflow (`F5`). The robot replays the recorded actions.

**Step 10:** Add a `Log Message` activity with text `"Login Successful"` for verification.

---

## 3.4 The Selector Construction Formula (LaTeX)

For KTU exams that require a formal selector derivation, the formula can be expressed as:

$$
S_{web} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} \big\{ a_i \in \text{HTML}_{tag} \;\big|\; \text{ancestor}(target, i) \big\}
$$

Where:
- $S_{web}$ is the generated web selector.
- $n$ is the depth of the target element in the DOM tree.
- $a_i$ is the $i$-th ancestor element of the target.
- $\text{HTML}_{tag}$ represents the set of valid HTML tag attributes (`id`, `name`, `class`, `aaname`, `innertext`, `idx`).
- $\text{ancestor}(target, i)$ is a function returning the $i$-th ancestor of the target element.

The final selector is the **ordered union** of all ancestor attribute sets, traversing from the root `<html>` to the target element.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Web Recording Architecture (Mermaid Flow Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Opens UiPath Studio] --> B[Create New Process Project]
    B --> C[Add Open Browser Activity with Target URL]
    C --> D[Click Recording Button in Ribbon]
    D --> E[Select Web Recording from Dropdown]
    E --> F[UiPath Browser Extension Activates in Chrome]
    F --> G[Recording Toolbar Appears on Screen]
    G --> H[User Performs Click and Type Actions on Web Page]
    H --> I[Extension Captures DOM Events and Element Properties]
    I --> J[UiPath Engine Translates Events to Activities]
    J --> K[Selector XML Generated Using Ancestor Chain]
    K --> L[Activities Inserted Inside Attach Browser Container]
    L --> M[Click Save and Exit on Toolbar]
    M --> N[Workflow Saved as xaml File]
    N --> O[Validate Selectors Using Ctrl F2]
    O --> P[Run Workflow with F5]
    P --> Q[Bot Replays Actions on Web Application]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for KTU Students:** Trace the flow from `A` (user action) to `Q` (bot execution). The middle steps (`F` to `L`) represent the *recording phase*, while `N` to `Q` represent the *playback phase*.

---

## 4.2 Selector Generation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HTML Page Loaded] --> B[User Clicks Target Element]
    B --> C[Browser Extension Intercepts DOM Click Event]
    C --> D[Read Target Element Attributes]
    D --> E[Walk Up DOM Tree to Root]
    E --> F[Collect Ancestor Tag and Attribute Pairs]
    F --> G[Construct Ordered Selector Path]
    G --> H[Add to Activity Selector Field]
    H --> I[Attach Browser Wraps Activity]
    I --> J[Activity Ready for Execution]
```

---

## 4.3 Selector Validation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Activity in Workflow] --> Check1{Selector Valid?}
    Check1 -- Yes --> Highlight[Element Highlighted in Browser]
    Check1 -- No --> Error[Selector Not Found Error]
    Error --> Fix[Add Wildcard or Anchor Base]
    Fix --> Check1
    Highlight --> End[Activity Ready to Run]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked *"How do you verify a recorded selector?"* in exams, mention all three steps: **(1) Ctrl + F2 inside Studio**, **(2) Element gets highlighted in browser**, **(3) Repair mechanism** if selector is invalid.

---

## 4.4 Activity Mapping Matrix (What Action Becomes What Activity)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[User Action: Click Button] --> B1[Activity: Click]
    A2[User Action: Type Text] --> B2[Activity: Type Into]
    A3[User Action: Select Dropdown] --> B3[Activity: Select Item]
    A4[User Action: Check Checkbox] --> B4[Activity: Check]
    A5[User Action: Hover] --> B5[Activity: Hover]
    A6[User Action: Copy Data] --> B6[Activity: Get Text]
    A7[User Action: Press Enter] --> B7[Activity: Send Hotkey]
    A8[User Action: Right Click] --> B8[Activity: Right Click]
```

> This diagram should be memorized for the **3-mark short answer** question on *"List the activities generated by Web Recording"*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]
**Define Web Recording in the context of Robotic Process Automation. List any four activities that are automatically generated by the Web Recorder.**

**Model Answer:**

Web Recording is an RPA feature in UiPath Studio that **captures user interactions performed on web-based applications** through a browser and converts them into a sequence of automation activities using HTML-based selectors.

The recorder works through the **UiPath Browser Extension** installed in Chrome/Edge. Each user action is translated into a corresponding UiPath activity, all wrapped inside an `Attach Browser` container.

**Four activities generated by Web Recorder:**

1. **Click** – Generated when the user clicks a button, link, or any clickable web element.
2. **Type Into** – Generated when the user types text into a text field or text area.
3. **Select Item** – Generated when the user chooses an option from a drop-down list.
4. **Check / Uncheck** – Generated when the user toggles a checkbox or radio button.

> [!NOTE]
> Other valid activities: `Get Text`, `Send Hotkey`, `Hover`, `Right Click`, `Extract Structured Data`.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]
**Differentiate between Web Recording and Desktop Recording in UiPath. State any three differences.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Parameter | Web Recording | Desktop Recording |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Target Application** | Web browsers (Chrome, Edge, Firefox) | Windows desktop applications (Notepad, Calculator, SAP GUI) |
| 2 | **Selector Tag** | Uses `<webctrl>` (HTML based) | Uses `<wnd>` and `<ctrl>` (uiautomation based) |
| 3 | **Extension Required** | Yes – UiPath Browser Extension must be installed | No browser extension is required |

| Additional valid differences | Selector Stability | Cross-Browser Support |
|---|---|---|
| Web: HTML ids are mostly stable. Desktop: uia selectors can be brittle. | Web recording works on any modern browser; desktop recording is limited to Windows desktop apps. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the **architecture of Web Recording in UiPath** with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of the **UiPath Browser Extension** in detail. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the **process of recording a web-based login form** and the concept of **selectors** generated during the process. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

The Web Recording architecture in UiPath consists of **three main layers**:

**Layer 1 – Event Listener (Browser Extension Layer):**
The UiPath Browser Extension (available for Chrome, Edge, Firefox) is injected into every web page. It listens to **DOM events** such as `click`, `input`, `change`, `submit`, and `keydown`. For each event, it captures the target element's HTML attributes (`id`, `name`, `class`, `innertext`, `aaname`, `value`).

**Role of the Browser Extension:**
- It bypasses the **same-origin policy** by being part of the page context.
- It provides **reliable, fast access** to the DOM tree (faster than OS-level accessibility APIs).
- It enables **cross-browser compatibility** – the same workflow runs on Chrome, Edge, or Firefox.
- It supports **modern web frameworks** like React, Angular, and Vue.js, which render elements dynamically.

**Layer 2 – Translation Engine (UiPath Studio Core):**
The captured event is sent to the Studio's recording engine. The engine classifies the event and maps it to a **pre-defined RPA activity**:
- `click` → `Click` activity
- `input` → `Type Into` activity
- `change` on `<select>` → `Select Item` activity

It also constructs a **Selector XML** by walking up the DOM tree from the target element to the `<html>` root.

**Layer 3 – Workflow Generation (Output):**
The translated activities are inserted into the workflow inside an **`Attach Browser`** container. The container's selector points to the browser window (e.g., `<html title='My Website' />`).

#### Model Solution for (b):

Consider a web-based login form at `https://example.com/login` with three elements: a **Username** text box, a **Password** text box, and a **Login** button.

**Step 1:** Add an `Open Browser` activity in UiPath Studio with the URL `https://example.com/login`.

**Step 2:** Click `Recording > Web` in the Studio ribbon. The recording toolbar appears.

**Step 3:** Click on the Username text box. UiPath generates a `Type Into` activity with the selector:

```xml
<html title='Login Page' />
  <webctrl tag='INPUT' name='username' aaname='Username' />
```

**Step 4:** Type the value `admin`. The value is auto-filled into the `Type Into` activity's `InputString` property.

**Step 5:** Click on the Password box. UiPath generates another `Type Into` activity with the selector referencing the password field.

**Step 6:** Click the Login button. UiPath generates a `Click` activity:

```xml
<html title='Login Page' />
  <webctrl tag='BUTTON' id='loginBtn' innertext='Login' />
```

**Selectors Generated:**
- **Selector 1 (Target):** Identifies the exact element (e.g., `<webctrl tag='INPUT' name='username' />`).
- **Selector 2 (Anchor):** Identifies a nearby stable element (e.g., the label `<label>Username</label>`) to confirm the target's location.

**Concept of Selectors:**
A **selector** is a string in XML format that uniquely identifies a UI element. It is constructed by traversing the DOM tree from the root `<html>` to the target element. Selectors can be:
- **Full Selectors** – Independent of containers (work anywhere in the workflow).
- **Partial Selectors** – Dependent on an enclosing `Attach Browser` or `Open Browser` container.

Wildcards like `*` and `?` can be used to handle **dynamic attributes** (e.g., `id='btn_??45'`).

---

### Question B (14 Marks – Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam – June 2025, Expected]

**(a)** Discuss the different **types of recording wizards** available in UiPath Studio. Compare **Basic Recording, Desktop Recording, and Web Recording** in a tabular format with at least six parameters. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** What is a **selector** in UiPath? Explain the **structure of a web selector** with an example. Also explain how **wildcards** and the **Anchor Base** activity are used to handle dynamic web elements. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

UiPath Studio provides **four recording wizards** that allow users to capture actions and convert them into automation workflows:

1. **Basic Recording** – The oldest wizard; generates **full selectors** for every activity. Each activity contains its own complete selector path. It works for both web and desktop applications but is less preferred in modern projects due to brittle selectors.

2. **Desktop Recording** – Designed specifically for **Windows desktop applications**. Generates **partial selectors** that are wrapped inside an `Attach Window` container. It uses accessibility APIs (`uiautomation` / `uia` tag) to identify elements. Best for SAP, legacy Win32 apps, and Excel.

3. **Web Recording** – Specialized for **web applications running in browsers**. Requires the **UiPath Browser Extension**. Generates **partial selectors** inside an `Attach Browser` container. Uses HTML-based `<webctrl>` tags.

4. **App/Web Recording** (Modern) – The current unified recorder that works for both desktop and web applications. It is the **recommended recorder** in modern UiPath Studio versions (2021.10+).

#### Comparison Table (6 Parameters):

| S.No. | Parameter | Basic Recording | Desktop Recording | Web Recording |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Target** | Web + Desktop | Desktop only | Web only |
| 2 | **Selector Type** | Full selector | Partial selector (inside `Attach Window`) | Partial selector (inside `Attach Browser`) |
| 3 | **Container Activity** | None (auto) | `Attach Window` | `Attach Browser` |
| 4 | **Tag Type** | `webctrl` or `wnd/ctrl` | `wnd/ctrl` (uiautomation) | `webctrl` (HTML) |
| 5 | **Extension Required** | No (Web needs extension) | No | Yes – Browser Extension |
| 6 | **Modern Equivalent** | Deprecated | Replaced by App/Web Recorder | Replaced by App/Web Recorder |

#### Model Solution for (b):

A **selector** in UiPath is a **string in XML format** that uniquely identifies a UI element on the screen. It tells the robot **"which element"** to interact with by describing its position in the application's UI tree.

**Structure of a Web Selector:**
A web selector follows the DOM tree structure from root to target:

```xml
<html app='chrome.exe' title='My Website' />
  <webctrl tag='FORM' id='loginForm' />
    <webctrl tag='DIV' class='form-group' idx='1' />
      <webctrl tag='INPUT' name='username' type='text' />
```

- `<html>` – Root of the page; identifies the browser tab/window.
- `<webctrl>` – Each `<webctrl>` tag represents a child element in the DOM.
- **Attributes** – `tag`, `id`, `name`, `class`, `aaname`, `innertext`, `idx`.

**Wildcards:**
Wildcards are used when attribute values are **dynamic** (change on every page load).

- `*` – Matches **zero or more characters**: `aaname='*Login*'`.
- `?` – Matches **exactly one character**: `id='btn_??23'`.

**Anchor Base Activity:**
When wildcards are insufficient (e.g., multiple buttons have the same `aaname='Submit'`), the **Anchor Base** activity is used. It pairs the **target element** with an **anchor element** (a nearby stable element like a label). The robot locates the anchor first, then finds the target relative to it.

Example: To click the **"Submit"** button next to the **"Email"** field, the Anchor Base uses the `<label>Email</label>` as the anchor and the `<button>Submit</button>` as the target.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Web Recording with App/Web Recording** – Modern UiPath has a single *App/Web Recorder*. Older versions had separate Web and Desktop recorders. Always mention the version context.
> 2. **Forgetting the Browser Extension** – The UiPath Chrome/Edge extension is **mandatory** for web recording. Forgetting to install it is the **#1 reason** students lose marks in viva-voce.
> 3. **Writing `Attach Window` for Web Recording** – This is a common mistake. The correct container is **`Attach Browser`**, not `Attach Window`.
> 4. **Not explaining the role of HTML tags** – Examiners expect the answer to explicitly state that selectors are built using **HTML tags** (`<html>`, `<body>`, `<div>`, `<input>`, `<button>`).
> 5. **Skipping the validation step** – Always mention `Ctrl + F2` validation as the quality-check step after recording.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Web Recording** captures browser-based user actions and converts them into RPA activities.
- [x] It uses the **UiPath Browser Extension** (Chrome/Edge/Firefox) to intercept DOM events.
- [x] Generated activities are wrapped inside an **`Attach Browser`** container.
- [x] Selectors are constructed using **HTML tags** (`<webctrl>`).
- [x] Key activities: `Click`, `Type Into`, `Select Item`, `Check`, `Get Text`, `Send Hotkey`.
- [x] **Full Selectors** vs **Partial Selectors** – web recording generates partial selectors.
- [x] **Wildcards**: `*` (zero or more characters), `?` (exactly one character).
- [x] **Anchor Base** is used when wildcards are insufficient to handle dynamic elements.
- [x] **Validation**: `Ctrl + F2` inside UiPath Studio highlights the element in the browser.
- [x] The four UiPath recorders: **Basic, Desktop, Web, App/Web** (modern).
- [x] The modern equivalent of Web Recording is the **App/Web Recorder** (Studio 2021.10+).
- [x] Web recording supports **CRUD operations** on web forms (Create, Read, Update, Delete).
- [x] Recorded workflows can be enhanced with `Try-Catch`, REFramework, and Orchestrator Queues.
- [x] File format of recorded workflow: **`.xaml`** (eXtensible Application Markup Language).
- [x] The recorder is part of the **Attended Automation** paradigm but the resulting workflow can be run Unattended via Orchestrator.
- [x] **Best Practice:** Always validate selectors, use Anchor Base, and avoid dynamic IDs in production workflows.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Input/Output Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Input/Output Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Robotic Process Automation (RPA), **Input Methods** are the systematic, structured, and semi-structured mechanisms by which a software robot (Bot) acquires, ingests, or captures data from heterogeneous digital and analog sources to begin an automated workflow. Conversely, **Output Methods** are the mechanisms by which the bot delivers processed information, triggers downstream actions, or communicates results to internal/external systems, users, or repositories.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme PEAIT415 syllabus framework, Input/Output (I/O) Methods form the foundational data-handling layer of any RPA bot. They act as the **sensory (input)** and **motor (output)** interface between the bot engine and the surrounding enterprise ecosystem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** I/O Methods are categorized into **GUI-based I/O**, **API-based I/O**, **File-based I/O**, **Database I/O**, **Email I/O**, **OCR/Screen Scraping I/O**, and **Sensor/Hardware I/O**. Mastery of these is mandatory for designing end-to-end unattended and attended bots.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Office Robot Assistant"

Imagine hiring a **new office assistant** who is extremely fast, never sleeps, but cannot see, hear, or think on their own.

- **Input Methods** = *How you give work to the assistant.* You might hand them a printed invoice (**scanner/OCR**), point at a website and say "copy this table" (**screen scraping**), give them a spreadsheet (**Excel/CSV file**), tell them to "watch the HR mailbox" (**email trigger**), or hand them a database login (**SQL query**). Each method is a *channel* through which the assistant receives raw material.

- **Output Methods** = *How the assistant delivers the finished work.* They might save a report to a shared folder (**file write**), email the manager (**SMTP send**), update a CRM record (**API call**), type into a legacy green-screen terminal (**GUI automation**), or print a PDF (**document generation**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** Every RPA workflow is fundamentally a chain: `INPUT → PROCESS → OUTPUT`. A bot that cannot reliably *read* (input) or reliably *write* (output) is useless. The art of RPA engineering is choosing the **right I/O method** for the right data source.

### 1.3 Standard Classification Metrics (KTU 2024)

| Metric | Standard Notation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Protocol** | `PRT` | The communication standard used (HTTP, FTP, SMTP, ODBC) |
| **Data Velocity** | `DV` | Speed of data ingestion (bytes/sec) |
| **Data Variety** | `DVAR` | Structured / Semi-structured / Unstructured |
| **Bot Latency** | `BL` | Time gap between trigger and I/O execution |
| **Throughput** | `TP` | Number of transactions processed per unit time |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bold Constants Used:** Standard enterprise-grade RPA platforms (UiPath, Automation Anywhere, Blue Prism) support **24+ native I/O connectors**, with average **Bot Latency (BL) under 200 ms** for API-based operations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" and "How" of Each I/O Method

### 2.1.1 GUI-Based I/O (Graphical User Interface Automation)
- **Why Used:** When the target application has **no API** (legacy systems, ERPs like SAP GUI, mainframe terminals, desktop apps).
- **How It Works:** The bot uses **UI selectors** (XPath, CSS, image-based recognition, coordinate-based clicks) to mimic human mouse/keyboard actions. It performs *input* by typing into fields and *output* by reading on-screen text (text scraping) or capturing screenshots.
- **Tools:** UiPath Computer Vision, Automation Anywhere IQ Bot, Win32 hooks, Selenium for web GUIs.

### 2.1.2 API-Based I/O (Application Programming Interface)
- **Why Used:** Fastest, most reliable, and most scalable I/O method. Preferred for modern microservices and cloud-native systems.
- **How It Works:** The bot sends **HTTP requests** (`GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`) to RESTful or SOAP endpoints. Input = request payload (typically JSON/XML). Output = response payload + HTTP status code.
- **Authentications:** OAuth 2.0, JWT, API Keys, Basic Auth.

### 2.1.3 File-Based I/O
- **Why Used:** Ubiquitous in enterprises — nearly every business process involves Excel, CSV, PDF, or TXT files.
- **How It Works:**
  - **Read (Input):** `File.Read Range` (Excel), `Read CSV`, `PDF Text Extraction`, `StreamReader`.
  - **Write (Output):** `File.Write Range`, `Append to Text File`, `PDF Generation (iTextSharp)`.
- **Formats:** `.xlsx`, `.csv`, `.json`, `.xml`, `.pdf`, `.docx`, `.txt`.

### 2.1.4 Database I/O
- **Why Used:** Most enterprise data resides in relational databases (SQL Server, Oracle, MySQL, PostgreSQL) or NoSQL stores (MongoDB).
- **How It Works:** The bot uses **ODBC / OLE DB / JDBC connectors** to execute SQL queries.
  - **Input:** `SELECT` statements return datasets.
  - **Output:** `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE` statements modify the database.
- **Transactions:** Must follow **ACID properties** (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability).

### 2.1.5 Email I/O
- **Why Used:** Email remains the #1 business communication channel. Bots use it for triggers and notifications.
- **How It Works:**
  - **Input:** Connect via **IMAP** (port 143/993) or **POP3** (port 110/995) to read inbox; parse subject, body, attachments.
  - **Output:** Connect via **SMTP** (port 25/465/587) to send emails with attachments, HTML bodies, or templated content.

### 2.1.6 OCR & Screen Scraping I/O
- **Why Used:** To extract data from **images, scanned PDFs, and non-accessible UIs**.
- **How It Works:**
  - **OCR (Optical Character Recognition):** Converts pixels → text using Tesseract, Google Vision, AWS Textract.
  - **Screen Scraping:** Reads text from specific UI regions using DOM parsers or coordinate-based OCR.

### 2.1.7 Sensor / Hardware I/O (Advanced / Industrial RPA)
- **Why Used:** For RPA integrated with IoT, robotics, or industrial control systems.
- **How It Works:** Uses GPIO pins, serial ports (RS-232), Bluetooth, or MQTT brokers.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | HTTP Request Time | $T_{req} = T_{conn} + T_{trans} + T_{proc} + T_{resp}$ | $T_{conn}$: connection time, $T_{trans}$: transmission, $T_{proc}$: server processing, $T_{resp}$: response | milliseconds (ms) |
| 2 | Bot Throughput | $TP = \dfrac{N_{tx}}{T_{total}}$ | $N_{tx}$: number of transactions, $T_{total}$: total time | transactions/min |
| 3 | Error Rate (I/O) | $E_r = \dfrac{E_{io}}{N_{io}} \times 100\%$ | $E_{io}$: failed I/O ops, $N_{io}$: total I/O ops | percent (%) |
| 4 | Data Velocity | $DV = \dfrac{D_{size}}{T_{transfer}}$ | $D_{size}$: data size, $T_{transfer}$: transfer time | KB/sec or MB/sec |
| 5 | OCR Confidence | $C_{ocr} = \dfrac{C_{chars\_correct}}{C_{total}} \times 100$ | Correct chars vs total | % |
| 6 | Latency Bound (Real-time) | $L_{max} = T_{SLA} - T_{process}$ | $T_{SLA}$: SLA threshold | ms |
| 7 | Email Protocol Port | $P_{smtp} = 587 \text{ (TLS)}, P_{imap} = 993 \text{ (SSL)}$ | Standard secure ports | numeric |
| 8 | JSON Parse Complexity | $O(n)$ where $n$ = token count | Linear time | algorithmic |
| 9 | SQL Query Execution | $T_{sql} = f(P, R, I)$ | $P$: parsing, $R$: retrieval, $I$: indexing cost | ms |
| 10 | RPA Pipeline Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{T_{value\_added}}{T_{total}} \times 100$ | Value-added vs total time | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used by RPA Solution Architects to design **scalable, SLA-compliant automation pipelines**. Banks, insurance firms, and BPOs use $TP$ and $E_r$ to size their bot fleets and measure automation ROI.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right I/O Method

Since Input/Output Methods is a *methodology* topic (not purely mathematical), the "derivations" below are **structured decision algorithms** — the analytical reasoning a student must write in a KTU 14-mark answer.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step: Deriving the I/O Selection Decision Tree

**Step 1: Identify the Data Source Type**

$$\text{Data Source} = \begin{cases} \text{GUI Application} & \Rightarrow \text{Use GUI-based I/O} \\ \text{Web Service} & \Rightarrow \text{Use API-based I/O} \\ \text{Flat File} & \Rightarrow \text{Use File-based I/O} \\ \text{Database} & \Rightarrow \text{Use Database I/O} \\ \text{Email} & \Rightarrow \text{Use Email I/O} \\ \text{Image / Scanned PDF} & \Rightarrow \text{Use OCR I/O} \end{cases}$$

**Logic Explanation:** The first decision node categorizes the source. This is the **boundary classification** — the student must show this branching in the exam for full marks.

**Step 2: Check for API Availability**

If the target system is modern (post-2015), check for REST/SOAP endpoints.

$$\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{API Exists} & \Rightarrow \text{Use API I/O (preferred)} \\ \text{No API} & \Rightarrow \text{Fallback to GUI I/O} \end{cases}$$

**Logic Explanation:** API I/O is **faster, more stable, and easier to maintain**. GUI I/O is fragile (breaks when UI changes) and should be the *last resort*.

**Step 3: Evaluate Data Structure**

$$\text{Structure} = \begin{cases} \text{Structured (DB tables, JSON, CSV)} & \Rightarrow \text{Native parser available} \\ \text{Semi-structured (Email, XML)} & \Rightarrow \text{XPath / regex / MIME parser} \\ \text{Unstructured (Image, PDF scan)} & \Rightarrow \text{OCR + NLP} \end{cases}$$

**Step 4: Compute Throughput Requirement**

The required bot throughput must satisfy the SLA:

$$TP_{required} \leq TP_{SLA}$$

Where $TP_{SLA}$ is the maximum transactions/minute the business process demands. If GUI-based I/O yields a $TP$ below $TP_{SLA}$, the engineer **must** upgrade to API-based I/O.

**Step 5: Authentication & Security Layer**

Most enterprise APIs require authentication tokens. The HTTP call sequence is:

$$\text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{POST /login}} \text{Server} \xrightarrow{\text{JWT Token}} \text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{GET /data + Bearer Token}} \text{Server} \xrightarrow{\text{JSON Response}} \text{Client}$$

**Logic Explanation:** Every API I/O involves (a) acquiring a token, (b) attaching the token in the header, (c) parsing the JSON response. This is a **3-step handshake** the student must describe.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step: Deriving OCR Accuracy Bound

For OCR-based input, the accuracy of the output data depends on image quality:

$$C_{ocr} = \dfrac{C_{chars\_correct}}{C_{total}} \times 100$$

If input image resolution is $R$ (DPI) and noise factor is $N$:

$$C_{ocr} = f(R, N) \quad \text{where} \quad f \uparrow R \Rightarrow C_{ocr} \uparrow$$

**Logic Explanation:** Higher resolution and lower noise → higher OCR confidence. Below a threshold (typically $R < 150$ DPI), OCR becomes unusable, requiring **manual exception handling** in the bot.

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step: Deriving Email I/O Workflow

**Input Side (IMAP):**

$$\text{Bot} \xrightarrow{\text{IMAP CONNECT (port 993)}} \text{Mail Server} \xrightarrow{\text{LOGIN}} \text{Inbox} \xrightarrow{\text{FETCH (UID)}} \text{Bot}$$

**Output Side (SMTP):**

$$\text{Bot} \xrightarrow{\text{SMTP CONNECT (port 587)}} \text{Mail Server} \xrightarrow{\text{AUTH + FROM/TO}} \text{SMTP Relay} \xrightarrow{\text{DELIVER}} \text{Recipient Inbox}$$

**Logic Explanation:** Email I/O uses two different protocols for reading and sending. This dual-protocol asymmetry is a **favourite KTU question**.

### 3.1.4 Step-by-Step: Database I/O Transaction

For a database write operation, the bot must ensure ACID compliance:

$$\text{Transaction} = \text{BEGIN} \rightarrow \text{INSERT} \rightarrow \text{COMMIT}$$

If error occurs:

$$\text{Transaction} = \text{BEGIN} \rightarrow \text{INSERT (fail)} \rightarrow \text{ROLLBACK}$$

**Logic Explanation:** A bot that fails to rollback on error will leave the database in an **inconsistent state** — a critical KTU pitfall.

### 3.1.5 Exhaustive Comparison Matrix (Analytical Foundation)

| I/O Method | Input Use Case | Output Use Case | Speed | Reliability | Security |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| GUI Automation | Legacy ERP, desktop apps | Filling forms, clicking buttons | Slow | Low (UI-fragile) | Medium |
| API (REST/SOAP) | Cloud services, microservices | JSON/XML payloads | **Very Fast** | **High** | High (OAuth/JWT) |
| File (Excel/CSV) | Reports, bulk data | Generated reports | Medium | High | Medium |
| Database (SQL) | Master data, transactional data | Record updates | Fast | High | High (encrypted) |
| Email (IMAP/SMTP) | Triggers, attachments | Notifications, alerts | Medium | Medium | Medium (TLS) |
| OCR / Screen Scraping | Scanned docs, images | Digitized text | Slow | Medium | Low (no encryption) |
| Sensor / IoT | Industrial signals | Actuator commands | Real-time | High | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The "Reliability" and "Security" columns above are high-yield content. KTU 14-mark questions often ask the student to "compare and contrast" — memorizing this table is essential.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of RPA I/O

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **end-to-end I/O architecture** of a typical RPA bot, mapping how data flows from external sources (input) through the bot engine to downstream systems (output).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[External Data Sources] --> B{Input Classification Layer}
    B --> C1[GUI Input Module]
    B --> C2[API Input Module]
    B --> C3[File Input Module]
    B --> C4[Database Input Module]
    B --> C5[Email IMAP Input Module]
    B --> C6[OCR Input Module]
    C1 --> D[RPA Bot Engine]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    C6 --> D
    D --> E[Process Logic Layer]
    E --> F{Routing and Decision Layer}
    F --> G1[GUI Output Module]
    F --> G2[API Output Module]
    F --> G3[File Output Module]
    F --> G4[Database Output Module]
    F --> G5[Email SMTP Output Module]
    F --> G6[Report and Dashboard Output]
    G1 --> H[Destination Systems]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    G5 --> H
    G6 --> H
    H --> I[Audit Log and Exception Queue]
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**

- **A (External Data Sources):** Represents all real-world data origins — invoices, emails, databases, web pages, sensors.
- **B (Input Classification Layer):** The first decision gateway — classifies the data format and routes to the appropriate input module.
- **C1–C6 (Input Modules):** Each input module is a specialized connector (e.g., IMAP client, ODBC driver, Selenium browser).
- **D (RPA Bot Engine):** The orchestrator (UiPath Orchestrator, Automation Anywhere Control Room).
- **E (Process Logic Layer):** Business rules, AI/ML models, and workflow logic.
- **F (Routing Layer):** Decides *where* the processed output must go.
- **G1–G6 (Output Modules):** Specialized output connectors — SMTP client, HTTP client, file writer, SQL executor.
- **H (Destination Systems):** ERP, CRM, email inboxes, dashboards, cloud storage.
- **I (Audit Log):** Every I/O is logged for compliance and exception handling.

## 4.2 Input-Output Pairing Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[User Inbox] -- IMAP Read --> R1[Bot Reads Email]
    R1 -- Parse Attachment --> P2[Extract Invoice PDF]
    P2 -- OCR --> P3[Structured Invoice Data]
    P3 -- Validate --> P4{Checks Passed}
    P4 -- Yes --> P5[SQL Insert into ERP]
    P4 -- No --> P6[SMTP Send Exception Email]
    P5 -- API Call --> P7[Update Customer Portal]
    P6 -- End --> P8[Audit Log]
    P7 -- End --> P8[Audit Log]
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**

This diagram maps a **real-world use case** — automated invoice processing. The student can replicate this in the KTU exam for a 14-mark question on "Explain with a use case the input and output methods used in RPA."

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Define Input Methods in RPA. List any four common input methods.**

**Model Answer:**

**Input Methods** in RPA refer to the mechanisms and channels through which a software bot acquires data from external sources to begin an automated process. They are the "sensory layer" of the bot.

The four common input methods are:

1. **GUI-based Input** — Reading on-screen text or capturing screenshots from desktop/web applications.
2. **API-based Input** — Receiving data via REST/SOAP API calls (JSON/XML payloads).
3. **File-based Input** — Reading Excel, CSV, PDF, or TXT files from local or shared drives.
4. **Email Input** — Fetching emails (subject, body, attachments) via IMAP/POP3 protocols.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 0.5 each for the four methods.)*

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between SMTP and IMAP protocols in the context of RPA email I/O.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) | IMAP (Internet Message Access Protocol) |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Used for **sending** emails (Output) | Used for **reading** emails (Input) |
| **Default Port** | 25, 465 (SSL), 587 (TLS) | 143, 993 (SSL) |
| **Direction** | Bot → Mail Server → Recipient | Mail Server → Bot |
| **Operation** | Push (outbound) | Pull (inbound) |
| **RPA Use** | Sending notifications, alerts, reports | Reading triggers, fetching attachments |

*(3 marks: 1.5 marks for differentiation + 1.5 marks for port numbers and use cases.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Explain in detail the various Input Methods used in Robotic Process Automation. Compare GUI-based input with API-based input with suitable examples. (14 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

#### **Part (a) — Detailed Explanation of Input Methods (7 Marks)**

Input Methods in RPA are the data acquisition channels that feed raw information into the bot's processing engine. They are classified as follows:

**1. GUI-Based Input:**
The bot interacts with the application's Graphical User Interface using UI selectors, screen coordinates, or computer vision. It reads text from on-screen labels, captures images, or scrapes web pages. This is essential for legacy systems like SAP GUI, mainframes, and older desktop applications that have no API.

*Example:* A bot opens the SAP GUI, navigates to transaction code `FBL5N`, and reads the customer outstanding balance from the on-screen ALV grid.

**2. API-Based Input:**
The bot sends HTTP requests (`GET`, `POST`) to RESTful or SOAP endpoints of a web service. The response is typically a JSON or XML payload that the bot parses.

*Example:* A bot calls `GET https://api.weather.com/v1/city/Bangalore` to fetch live weather data for an insurance claim validation workflow.

**3. File-Based Input:**
The bot reads structured or semi-structured files such as Excel (`.xlsx`), CSV, JSON, XML, or PDF.

*Example:* Reading a daily sales report from `C:\Reports\sales_2024.csv` to feed the order processing bot.

**4. Database Input:**
The bot executes SQL `SELECT` queries against relational databases via ODBC/OLE DB/JDBC connectors.

*Example:* A bot queries `SELECT * FROM Customers WHERE Region='Kerala'` to retrieve customer records for onboarding.

**5. Email Input (IMAP/POP3):**
The bot connects to the mail server, fetches unread emails, extracts subject, body, and attachments.

*Example:* Reading vendor invoices from `invoices@company.com` and extracting PDF attachments.

**6. OCR / Screen Scraping Input:**
The bot uses Optical Character Recognition to extract text from scanned documents, images, or PDFs.

*Example:* Digitizing a scanned handwritten application form using Tesseract OCR.

**7. Sensor / IoT Input:**
In industrial RPA, bots receive signals from sensors via MQTT or GPIO.

*Example:* Reading temperature sensor data from a manufacturing line to trigger an automated alert.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per method + 0.5 mark example each = 7.5, capped to 7)*

---

#### **Part (b) — Comparison: GUI vs API Input (7 Marks)**

| Comparison Criterion | GUI-Based Input | API-Based Input |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Mimics human mouse/keyboard via UI selectors | Sends HTTP requests to endpoints |
| **Speed** | Slow (latency 1–5 seconds per action) | **Fast (50–200 ms per request)** |
| **Reliability** | Low — breaks if UI changes | **High** — contract-based, versioned |
| **Data Format** | Unstructured (screen text, images) | **Structured (JSON/XML)** |
| **Security** | Limited (no encryption in transit) | **High (OAuth 2.0, TLS, JWT)** |
| **Maintenance** | High (UI changes break bot) | **Low (API contracts are stable)** |
| **Scalability** | Limited | **Highly scalable** |
| **Example Tool** | UiPath Computer Vision, Selenium | Postman, HTTP Request activity |

**Conclusion:** API-based input is the **gold standard** for modern RPA. GUI-based input is a fallback for legacy systems that lack APIs.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per row of comparison + 1 mark conclusion)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the Output Methods in RPA. Discuss how Email and Database output methods are implemented in a real-world automation scenario. (14 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

#### **Part (a) — Output Methods with Diagram (7 Marks)**

**Output Methods** in RPA are the mechanisms by which the bot delivers processed results to downstream systems, users, or repositories. They are the "motor layer" of the bot.

**Classification of Output Methods:**

1. **File Output:** Writing processed data into Excel, CSV, PDF, or text files.
2. **Database Output:** Executing `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE` SQL statements.
3. **Email Output:** Sending emails via SMTP with attachments and HTML bodies.
4. **API Output:** Sending HTTP `POST`/`PUT` calls to update remote systems.
5. **GUI Output:** Typing results into a legacy application form (e.g., SAP, mainframe).
6. **Report/Dashboard Output:** Generating BI dashboards, Power BI reports.
7. **Document Generation:** Creating templated PDFs or Word docs.

**Diagram (Text Representation for Exam):**

```
[Bot Engine] → [Processed Data] → {Output Router}
                                          ├──> File Write (.xlsx, .pdf)
                                          ├──> SQL UPDATE (ERP Database)
                                          ├──> SMTP Send (Email Notification)
                                          ├──> HTTP POST (REST API)
                                          ├──> GUI Automation (Legacy Form)
                                          └──> Dashboard Refresh (Power BI)
```

*(7 marks: 1 mark per method category, 2 marks for the diagram)*

---

#### **Part (b) — Email and Database Output — Real-World Scenario (7 Marks)**

**Scenario:** *Automated Customer Onboarding in a Bank.*

When a new customer submits a KYC form:

**Step 1 — Email Output (SMTP):**
The bot composes a welcome email using an HTML template, attaches the customer ID card PDF, and sends it via SMTP (port 587 with TLS) to the customer's email address. The email is also CC'd to the relationship manager.

**Pseudo-flow:**

$$\text{Bot} \xrightarrow{\text{SMTP CONNECT (587)}} \text{Mail Server} \xrightarrow{\text{AUTH}} \text{Bot} \xrightarrow{\text{MAIL FROM / RCPT TO}} \text{Mail Server} \xrightarrow{\text{DATA (HTML + PDF)}} \text{Recipient}$$

**Step 2 — Database Output (SQL):**
Simultaneously, the bot opens an ODBC connection to the bank's Oracle DB and executes:

```sql
BEGIN TRANSACTION;
INSERT INTO Customers (CustomerID, Name, KYC_Status, Onboarded_At)
VALUES ('CUST10234', 'Rahul Menon', 'VERIFIED', SYSDATE);
COMMIT;
```

If the insert fails, the bot executes `ROLLBACK` to maintain ACID compliance and routes the record to an exception queue.

**Step 3 — Audit Log:**
Both I/O operations are logged in the orchestrator's audit trail with timestamps, transaction IDs, and user context for regulatory compliance (RBI audit requirements).

*(7 marks: 1 mark for scenario setup, 2 marks for SMTP flow, 2 marks for SQL flow, 1 mark for audit, 1 mark for diagram/pseudo-code clarity)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing SMTP with IMAP:** Students often write "SMTP is used to read emails." This is **wrong** — SMTP is *send-only*. IMAP/POP3 are for *reading*.
> 2. **Forgetting port numbers:** Examiners reward students who mention **port 587 (SMTP), 993 (IMAP)** — these are high-yield facts.
> 3. **Missing ACID compliance in DB writes:** Any database output answer *must* mention `BEGIN`, `COMMIT`, `ROLLBACK`. Omitting this loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Not drawing a diagram:** A 14-mark answer without a block diagram loses 1–2 marks. Always include a labelled flowchart.
> 5. **Vague "Bot does everything" answers:** Examiners want **specific activity names** (e.g., `HTTP Request`, `Send SMTP Mail Message`, `Execute Query`) — not generic statements.
> 6. **Ignoring error/exception handling:** Every I/O method has failure modes. Mention *retry logic*, *timeout*, and *exception queue*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 3: Input/Output Methods**

- **Definition:** I/O Methods = *how a bot reads data (input) and writes data (output).*
- **Seven Core I/O Categories:** GUI, API, File, Database, Email, OCR/Screen Scraping, Sensor/IoT.
- **API I/O is preferred** over GUI I/O whenever possible — faster, more reliable, more secure.
- **HTTP Methods:** `GET` (read), `POST` (create), `PUT` (update), `DELETE` (remove).
- **Email I/O is asymmetric:** **IMAP/POP3** for input, **SMTP** for output.
- **Standard Secure Ports:** SMTP = `587 (TLS) / 465 (SSL)`, IMAP = `993`, POP3 = `995`.
- **Database I/O** must follow **ACID** properties — always use `BEGIN`, `COMMIT`, `ROLLBACK`.
- **File I/O Formats:** `.xlsx`, `.csv`, `.json`, `.xml`, `.pdf`, `.docx`, `.txt`.
- **OCR Confidence** depends on image resolution (DPI ≥ 150 recommended).
- **Throughput Formula:** $TP = N_{tx} / T_{total}$ — must satisfy SLA.
- **Error Rate Formula:** $E_r = (E_{io} / N_{io}) \times 100\%$ — must be < 1% in production.
- **Security Best Practices:** Use **OAuth 2.0 / JWT** for APIs, **TLS** for email, **encrypted connections** for databases.
- **Audit Logging:** Every I/O operation must be logged for compliance (GDPR, RBI, HIPAA).
- **Common Tools:** UiPath, Automation Anywhere, Blue Prism, Power Automate, Selenium, Tesseract OCR.
- **Fallback Strategy:** If API is unavailable, fall back to GUI automation. If GUI is also unavailable, escalate to a human via email/SMS.
- **KTU-Favourite Keywords:** "UI selectors," "REST endpoints," "IMAP/SMTP," "ACID transaction," "OCR confidence," "audit trail."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Screen Scraping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Screen Scraping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Screen Scraping** in Robotic Process Automation (RPA) is the technique of programmatically **extracting structured data from the visual user interface (UI)** of an application — such as text, values, images, or tables rendered on a screen — when direct access to backend systems (APIs, databases, or logs) is unavailable, restricted, or impractical.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Screen Scraping is classified as a **non-invasive data acquisition methodology** used by software robots (bots) to read information displayed on legacy terminals, desktop GUIs, web pages, terminal emulators (like Mainframe green screens), PDFs, or Citrix/VDI environments.

### Types of Screen Scraping (as per RPA platforms)

| Type | Mechanism | Best For |
|------|-----------|----------|
| **Full Text Scraping** | Reads all visible text with formatting | Standard desktop apps, browsers |
| **Native Scraping** | Uses underlying UI element attributes (Accessibility APIs) | Web apps, .NET/WPF apps |
| **OCR Scraping** | Uses Optical Character Recognition on rendered pixels | Citrix, VDI, scanned images, Mainframes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** UiPath's official documentation categorizes scraping into exactly three methods above. This is a **frequently asked 3-mark question** in KTU ESE.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Robot's Reading Glasses"**
>
> Imagine you are a robot working in a bank. The bank's core ledger system is a 30-year-old Mainframe that only shows green text on a black screen (a 3270 terminal). Nobody gave you the keys to the database. You cannot install any software on it. You cannot ask it to "export to Excel."
>
> What can you do? You can **look at the screen and read what's written on it** — just like a human teller would. Screen Scraping is literally that: the bot looks at the pixels/elements on the screen and copies down the information, the same way a human's eyes and brain work.
>
> - **Native Scraping** = the bot has perfect 20/20 vision and can read the structured layout directly.
> - **OCR Scraping** = the bot is reading handwritten scribbles, so it needs an OCR "translator" first.
> - **Full Text Scraping** = the bot grabs a photograph of the page and extracts everything visible.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

- **Accuracy Threshold (OCR):** Industry standard ≥ **95% character recognition accuracy** for production-grade bots.
- **Anchor Base:** The reference UI element from which the target data is located (e.g., label "Account Number:" is the anchor; the value next to it is the target).
- **Scrape Relative Position:** Offset (X, Y) pixels from the anchor element.
- **Get Visible Text / Get Full Text:** Two fundamental methods returning string outputs.
- **Citrix Environment:** Virtual Desktop Infrastructure where only rendered pixels are accessible (forces OCR).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How Screen Scraping Works (Step-by-Step)

A screen scraping activity in any RPA tool (UiPath, Automation Anywhere, Blue Prism) follows this generic operational pipeline:

1. **Target Application Identification** — The bot identifies the application window (e.g., `wnd`, `web`, `java`, `sap`, `acrobat` selector prefixes in UiPath).
2. **Indicator/Anchor Definition** — A unique static UI element is selected as the reference (the "indicator"). This ensures the bot can relocate the target even if window positions change.
3. **Scraping Method Selection** — The tool/engineer decides between Native, Full Text, or OCR based on the application's accessibility tree.
4. **Data Extraction** — The bot traverses the UI element tree (DOM for web, MSAA/UIA for Windows) and pulls text/values.
5. **Output Structuring** — Extracted data is returned as a string, a `DataTable`, or written to a variable/Excel/queue.
6. **Exception Handling** — If the element is not found, the "Element Not Found" exception is caught and a retry/alternative path is triggered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Screen Scraping is the "Last Resort" in RPA Architecture**
> Best practice: Use APIs → Databases → Direct file reading → Screen Scraping (in that priority order). Screen scraping is invoked when the application lacks modern integration points. This priority is a common KTU exam question.

---

## 2.2 Comparison Matrix of Scraping Methods

| Parameter | Native Scraping | Full Text Scraping | OCR Scraping |
|-----------|----------------|--------------------|--------------|
| **Speed** | Fastest | Fast | Slowest |
| **Accuracy** | 100% (if element exists) | High (95%+) | Variable (85–99%) |
| **Dependency** | UI Accessibility tree | Screen coordinates + OCR engine | Pixel bitmap + OCR engine |
| **Citrix/VDI Support** | ❌ Not supported | ✅ Supported | ✅ Supported (only viable option) |
| **Hidden Text Visible** | No (only visible UI) | Yes (can include hidden) | No (only visible pixels) |
| **Computational Cost** | Low | Medium | **High** |
| **Implementation Complexity** | Low | Medium | High |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** OCR engines (Tesseract, Google Cloud Vision, Microsoft Read API, Abbyy) consume **3–10× more CPU/RAM** than native methods, which is why production RPA deployments prefer native scraping whenever technically feasible.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Application |
|---------|---------------------|-------|-------------|
| OCR Confidence Score | $C = \frac{N_{correct}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage | Measures scraping accuracy |
| Scrape Offset | $P_{target} = P_{anchor} + (\Delta x, \Delta y)$ | Pixels | Locating data relative to anchor |
| Levenshtein Distance (string matching) | $d(i,j) = \min\begin{cases} d(i-1,j) + 1 \\ d(i,j-1) + 1 \\ d(i-1,j-1) + \delta \end{cases}$ | Edit operations | Fuzzy match validation |
| String Similarity Ratio | $S = 1 - \frac{d_{Levenshtein}}{\max(\vert L_1 \vert, \vert L_2 \vert)}$ | Ratio $\in [0,1]$ | Post-scrape validation |
| Retry Logic Limit | $N_{retry} \leq 3$ (typical KTU safe value) | Integer | Avoid infinite loops |
| OCR Engine Latency | $T_{OCR} \approx 200\text{ms} \text{ to } 1500\text{ms}$ per region | Milliseconds | Process timing estimation |

> **Where the pipe character was needed (`|x|`), the LaTeX `\vert` command has been used to preserve markdown table integrity.**

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking & Finance:** Extracting data from legacy Mainframe (IBM AS/400, Unisys) green screens for reconciliation bots.
- **Healthcare:** Reading patient data from Electronic Health Record (EHR) systems that lack HL7/FHIR APIs.
- **Insurance:** Pulling claim data from PDF forms (uses PDF + OCR hybrid).
- **BPO Operations:** Migrating data from one vendor's portal to another without any backend access.
- **Government:** Extracting data from public-facing legacy portals for citizen-service chatbots.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: OCR Confidence Score Calculation

Let us assume a screen scraping bot processes **1,000 characters** from a scanned invoice image. The OCR engine misreads **23 characters** (typos, broken pixels, similar glyphs like `0` vs `O`).

**Step 1 — Identify variables**
Let $N_{correct} = 1000 - 23 = 977$ and $N_{total} = 1000$.

**Step 2 — Apply the confidence formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{N_{correct}}{N_{total}} \times 100\% \\
  &= \frac{977}{1000} \times 100\% \\
  &= 0.977 \times 100\% \\
  &= 97.7\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Decision Rule (KTU Standard)**
- If $C \geq 95\%$, scraping is **production-ready**.
- If $90\% \leq C < 95\%$, **human-in-the-loop validation** is mandated.
- If $C < 90\%$, the scraping method is **rejected**; reconsider anchor selection or image preprocessing (binarization, deskew, denoising).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough: Scrape Relative Position Logic

Suppose a banking screen displays:

```
[ANCHOR] Account Number:  [VALUE] 4587-2390-1123
```

The anchor's top-left pixel coordinate is $(x_a, y_a) = (120, 340)$ and the target value box is at $(x_t, y_t) = (320, 340)$.

**Step 1 — Compute horizontal offset**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta x &= x_t - x_a = 320 - 120 = 200 \text{ pixels}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute vertical offset**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta y &= y_t - y_a = 340 - 340 = 0 \text{ pixels}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the scraping target coordinate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{target} &= P_{anchor} + (\Delta x, \Delta y) \\
           &= (120, 340) + (200, 0) \\
           &= (320, 340)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why this matters:** If the window is moved or DPI scaling changes, the bot must re-resolve $P_{anchor}$ dynamically, then apply the **same relative offset** to extract the data. This is the foundation of **Anchor Base Scraping**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough: String Similarity for Post-Scrape Validation

After scraping, the bot must confirm the extracted value is reasonable. Suppose the expected string is `"INVOICE_2024"` and the scraped string is `"INVOI_E_2024"` (two characters missing/duplicated).

**Step 1 — Levenshtein Distance calculation**
One plausible edit sequence: `INVOI_E_2024` → insert `C` at position 5 → insert `E` at position 6 → `"INVOICE_2024"`. So $d = 2$.

**Step 2 — Apply the similarity formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= 1 - \frac{d_{Levenshtein}}{\max(\vert L_1 \vert, \vert L_2 \vert)} \\
  &= 1 - \frac{2}{12} \\
  &= 1 - 0.1667 \\
  &= 0.8333
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Decision Rule**
Since $S = 0.8333 < 0.95$, the bot should **flag this for human review** rather than auto-process. This protects against silent data corruption in production bots.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Decision Matrix for Method Selection

| Scenario | Recommended Method | Justification |
|----------|-------------------|---------------|
| Web app with stable DOM | Native | DOM provides direct element access, 100% accuracy |
| Desktop .NET/WPF app | Native | MSAA/UIA tree is accessible |
| Citrix/VDI hosted app | OCR | No accessibility tree exposed |
| PDF (digital, not scanned) | Full Text / PDF text extraction | Text layer exists in PDF metadata |
| Scanned image / image-only PDF | OCR | Only pixels are available |
| Mainframe green-screen (3270/5250) | OCR / Terminal Scraping | No GUI in traditional sense |
| SAP GUI for Windows | Native (SAP-specific selectors) | SAP exposes BAPI/IDoc-like access via scripting |
| Java Swing/AWT app | Native (with Java Access Bridge) | Requires JAB to be enabled |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Screen Scraping Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[botStart] --> B[Identify Target Application Window]
    B --> C{Environment Type}
    C -->|Web / Desktop| D[Native Scraping Path]
    C -->|Citrix / VDI| E[OCR Scraping Path]
    C -->|PDF Document| F[Full Text Scraping Path]
    D --> D1[Traverse UI Accessibility Tree]
    D1 --> D2[Locate Anchor Element]
    D2 --> D3[Apply Relative Offset to Target]
    D3 --> G[Extract Data as String]
    E --> E1[Capture Screen Region Bitmap]
    E1 --> E2[Image Preprocessing - Binarize, Deskew]
    E2 --> E3[Run OCR Engine - Tesseract, Abbyy, Google Vision]
    E3 --> G
    F --> F1[Read PDF Text Layer]
    F1 --> F2{Full Text or Scanned}
    F2 -->|Scanned| E1
    F2 -->|Digital| F3[Parse Text Coordinates]
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Store in Variable or DataTable]
    H --> I{Confidence Check}
    I -->|High - 95 percent and above| J[Pass to Next Activity]
    I -->|Low - below 95 percent| K[Flag for Human Review]
    J --> L[botEnd]
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow: Which Scraping Method to Choose?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Start Decision] --> Q2{Is the app hosted in Citrix or VDI}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R1[Use OCR Scraping]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Does the app expose an accessibility tree}
    Q3 -->|Yes| R2[Use Native Scraping]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Is the source a PDF}
    Q4 -->|Yes Digital PDF| R3[Use Full Text Scraping]
    Q4 -->|Yes Scanned PDF| R4[Use OCR Scraping]
    Q4 -->|No| R5[Use Full Text Scraping as Default]
    R1 --> Z[End]
    R2 --> Z
    R3 --> Z
    R4 --> Z
    R5 --> Z
```

## 4.3 Anchor-Based Scraping Concept (Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U1[User Screen Layout] --> A1[Anchor Element - Static Label]
    A1 --> O1[Offset Delta X and Delta Y]
    O1 --> T1[Target Data Field]
    T1 --> V1[Scraped Variable]

    style U1 fill:#e6f3ff
    style A1 fill:#fff4cc
    style T1 fill:#d4f4dd
    style V1 fill:#f4d4d4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The anchor is the "anchor of a ship" — it never moves. The target data is the "boat" — it may drift if window resizes, but relative to the anchor, its position is constant. This is the entire philosophy of **Anchor Base** in UiPath scraping.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Screen Scraping. List and briefly explain its types. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Screen Scraping is a technique used in RPA to extract data displayed on the visual user interface of an application when direct backend access is not available.
- **Types (2 marks):**
  1. **Native Scraping** — Uses the application's accessibility/API tree to read UI element attributes.
  2. **Full Text Scraping** — Extracts all visible (and optionally hidden) text along with screen coordinates.
  3. **OCR Scraping** — Uses Optical Character Recognition to read text from rendered pixels (essential for Citrix/VDI).

---

### Question 2: When is OCR-based screen scraping preferred over Native scraping? `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
OCR-based scraping is preferred when:
1. The application is hosted in **Citrix, VDI, or Remote Desktop** environments where the accessibility tree is not exposed to the bot (1 mark).
2. The application is a **legacy Mainframe (green-screen)** terminal that only renders text pixels (1 mark).
3. The source is a **scanned image or image-only PDF** without an underlying accessibility or text layer (1 mark).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall**
> - Students often confuse **Native** and **Full Text** scraping. Remember: Native uses accessibility attributes; Full Text grabs everything visible.
> - When asked for OCR, **always mention the preprocessing step** (binarization, deskew). Examiners deduct marks if you skip it.
> - In 14-mark answers, **draw a block diagram** wherever possible. It typically earns 2 free marks.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Study of Screen Scraping

**(a)** Explain in detail the three methods of screen scraping in RPA with suitable examples. Compare their accuracy, speed, and applicability. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** Screen scraping is the extraction of data from a UI when API/DB access is unavailable. RPA tools like UiPath offer three methods.
2. **Native Scraping (2 marks):**
   - Uses the underlying accessibility tree (UIA, MSAA, DOM).
   - **Example:** Reading the value of a text box in a Windows Forms app.
   - Pros: 100% accuracy, fastest, low CPU.
   - Cons: Fails in Citrix/VDI.
3. **Full Text Scraping (2 marks):**
   - Captures every visible text string with its (X, Y) screen coordinates.
   - **Example:** Extracting all visible labels and values from a SAP GUI screen.
   - Pros: Works in some Citrix, can include hidden text.
   - Cons: Slower than native, depends on screen position.
4. **OCR Scraping (2 marks):**
   - Converts pixel bitmaps to text using OCR engines (Tesseract, Abbyy, Google Vision).
   - **Example:** Reading invoice numbers from scanned PDFs.
   - Pros: Universal — works on anything visible.
   - Cons: Lowest speed, highest CPU, accuracy varies (85–99%).
5. **Conclusion:** Method choice depends on environment; OCR is the universal fallback.

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the operational workflow of an OCR-based screen scraping bot. Discuss the challenges and best practices. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

1. **Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

   **Step 1:** Bot captures the target screen region as a bitmap.
   **Step 2:** Image preprocessing — convert to grayscale, binarize (threshold), deskew, denoise.
   **Step 3:** OCR engine processes the cleaned image and outputs text with confidence scores.
   **Step 4:** Bot applies regex/anchors to isolate the required field.
   **Step 5:** Validation via Levenshtein similarity or expected pattern.
   **Step 6:** Output to variable/DataTable or flag for human review if confidence is low.

2. **Challenges (2 marks):**
   - Low-resolution or compressed screen images reduce accuracy.
   - Font similarity (0/O, 1/l/I) causes misreads.
   - Language and locale mismatches with OCR training data.
   - Dynamic UI changes (color, position) break anchors.

3. **Best Practices (2 marks):**
   - Always use **Anchor Base** to handle UI drift.
   - Set a **confidence threshold ≥ 95%** before auto-processing.
   - Preprocess images: increase contrast, fix DPI scaling.
   - Use **Try-Catch + Retry** with max 3 attempts.
   - Implement **human-in-the-loop** for low-confidence extractions.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks): Alternative — Screen Scraping in Specific Environments

**(a)** What are the challenges of screen scraping in **Citrix/VDI environments**? How does OCR overcome them? Explain with a use case. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

1. **What is Citrix/VDI (1 mark):** Virtual desktop environments stream only rendered pixels to the user's screen; the underlying OS, file system, and accessibility APIs are hidden from the client.
2. **Challenges (3 marks):**
   - **No DOM/Accessibility Tree:** Native selectors fail because UIA/MSAA are not exposed.
   - **Pixel-Only Output:** The bot sees only what a human sees — bitmaps.
   - **Resolution Variability:** Different VDI clients use different compression; the same screen may look slightly different.
   - **Color/Theme Drift:** UI elements may change color, breaking visual indicators.
3. **How OCR Overcomes These (2 marks):**
   - OCR reads the rendered pixels directly, bypassing the need for an accessibility tree.
   - Image preprocessing (deskew, binarization) standardizes the input.
   - Anchor-based scraping provides a stable reference for dynamic UIs.
4. **Use Case (1 mark):** A bank runs a 30-year-old Mainframe application via Citrix. The bot uses OCR to read the "Account Number" displayed on the green screen, anchors it to the static label, and feeds the value to a downstream reconciliation process.

**(b)** Differentiate between **Anchor Base activity** and **traditional selector-based scraping** in UiPath. Why is anchor base recommended for screen scraping? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

1. **Selector-Based Scraping (2 marks):**
   - Uses XPath/CSS/UI Automation selectors to locate elements.
   - **Pros:** Fast, precise, deterministic.
   - **Cons:** Brittle — if the DOM changes (dynamic IDs), the selector fails. Cannot work in Citrix where there is no DOM.
2. **Anchor Base Scraping (2 marks):**
   - Uses a stable **anchor element** (e.g., the label "Total Amount:") as a reference point.
   - The target data is extracted by a **relative offset** (X, Y pixels) or fuzzy match from the anchor.
   - **Pros:** Robust against layout shifts; works in Citrix when combined with OCR.
3. **Tabular Comparison (2 marks):**

   | Feature | Selector-Based | Anchor Base |
   |---------|---------------|-------------|
   | Robustness to UI changes | Low | High |
   | Works in Citrix | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (with OCR) |
   | Speed | Faster | Slightly slower |
   | Implementation | Easy | Moderate |

4. **Why Anchor Base is Recommended (1 mark):** Because screen-scraping scenarios (especially Citrix, legacy systems, dynamic UIs) have unstable selectors; anchors provide a stable reference that survives layout, theme, and resolution changes.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Definition:** Screen scraping = extracting UI-displayed data when backend access is unavailable.
- ✅ **Three Types:** **Native** (accessibility tree), **Full Text** (all visible text + coordinates), **OCR** (pixel-to-text).
- ✅ **OCR is the universal fallback** — works everywhere but is the slowest and most CPU-intensive.
- ✅ **Native scraping is fastest and most accurate** — but fails in Citrix/VDI.
- ✅ **Citrix/VDI forces OCR** — no accessibility tree is exposed.
- ✅ **Anchor Base** is the recommended approach for robust scraping — it survives UI drift.
- ✅ **Confidence threshold ≥ 95%** is the KTU industry standard for production-grade scraping.
- ✅ **Image preprocessing** (binarization, deskew) is mandatory for high OCR accuracy.
- ✅ **Priority order for data extraction:** API → Database → File → **Screen Scraping (last resort)**.
- ✅ **Levenshtein distance** is used for post-scrape string validation.
- ✅ **Key UI selector prefixes in UiPath:** `wnd` (Windows), `html` (web), `java` (Java), `sap` (SAP), `acrobat` (PDF).
- ✅ **Human-in-the-loop** is mandated when confidence falls below threshold.
- ✅ **Common exam trap:** Do NOT confuse **Full Text** with **Native** — Full Text captures hidden/background text; Native reads only visible UI elements via accessibility.
- ✅ **Tools/Engines to remember:** UiPath, Automation Anywhere, Blue Prism, Tesseract OCR, Abbyy, Google Cloud Vision, Microsoft Read API.
- ✅ **KTU exam pattern:** Expect a 3-mark definition question + a 7+7 long answer with diagram in every ESE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Scraping - Scraping Advanced Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Data Scraping - Scraping Advanced Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Data Scraping — Scraping Advanced Techniques

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Data Scraping* in Robotic Process Automation (RPA) refers to the automated extraction of structured or semi-structured data from web pages, documents, PDFs, or applications by intelligently identifying repeating patterns (such as tables, lists, or search results) and converting them into machine-readable formats like **DataTables**, **Excel sheets**, or **CSV files**. *Advanced Scraping Techniques* extend this capability to handle pagination, AJAX-based dynamic content, multi-page navigation, login-protected portals, and pattern-based extraction with anchors.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Catalogue Robot" 🏛️

Imagine you are asked to copy the title, author, and price of **every book** from a massive online library that displays results across **50 pages**, where each page holds **20 books**. Doing this manually would take hours.

Now, think of **Advanced Data Scraping** as a smart robotic librarian that:
- **Walks** through each aisle (page) automatically.
- **Recognizes** that every book card has the same shape (title on top, author below, price at the bottom-right).
- **Copies** the data into a single neat spreadsheet.
- **Stops** only when there are no more shelves (end of pagination).

You, the RPA developer, only need to teach it the **shape of the first record** — the robot figures out the rest!

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - **Screen Scraping** → Extracts text from a *specific region* of the screen (pixel/text-based).
> - **Data Scraping** → Extracts *structured, repeating* records (rows) from a source (web, PDF, Excel, app).
> - **Advanced Data Scraping** → Adds *intelligence* — pagination handling, pattern matching, AJAX handling, and multi-source extraction.

### Standard Metrics in Data Scraping (KTU High-Yield Terms)

- **Maximum Number of Results** — The cap on rows extracted (default: **1000** in UiPath).
- **Delay Between Pages** — Wait time (in **milliseconds**) inserted between page navigations.
- **Selector Accuracy** — The percentage of correctly identified UI elements (target: **≥ 95%** for production bots).
- **Extraction Timeout** — Maximum wait (default: **30000 ms**) before the wizard aborts.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
