The date `2024-12-15` will change daily. We declare a `String` variable `reportDate` and assign it the value of `DateTime.Today.ToString("yyyy-MM-dd")`.

**Stage 3 — Substitute the Variable:**

```
<html title='MIS Report {{reportDate}}' url='https://reports.kerala.gov.in/mis/{{reportDate}}/report.pdf' />
<webctrl tag='A' aaname='Download' />
```

**Stage 4 — Add Defensive Wildcards:**
Browsers may append tab indicators to titles, so we add margin wildcards:

```
<html title='MIS Report*{{reportDate}}*' url='https://reports.kerala.gov.in/mis/{{reportDate}}/report.pdf' />
<webctrl tag='A' aaname='Download' />
```

**Stage 5 — Validate and Test:**
Run the workflow with `reportDate = "2024-12-16"`. The runtime-resolved selector is:

```
<html title='MIS Report*2024-12-16*' url='https://reports.kerala.gov.in/mis/2024-12-16/report.pdf' />
<webctrl tag='A' aaname='Download' />
```

The robot successfully locates the download link — $N_{matches} = 1$, click is dispatched, file is saved.

*(7 marks: 2 for scenario + Stage 1, 2 for variable substitution, 2 for wildcarding, 1 for validation logic.)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
> List and explain **three common reasons why dynamic selectors fail** in production. For each reason, suggest a specific remedy.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Over-Wildcarding:** When the `*` wildcard is placed too liberally, the selector matches more than one element ($N_{matches} > 1$). Example: `aaname='*'` matches *every* button on the page, not just the intended one.
   - **Remedy:** Replace `*` with a longer anchor prefix, or restrict the wildcard's scope. For example, `aaname='View *'` is more restrictive than `aaname='*'`.

2. **Under-Wildcarding:** When one hard-coded fragment remains that the application changes, the selector matches zero elements ($N_{matches} = 0$). Example: a date selector that still has a hard-coded year.
   - **Remedy:** Audit every fragment; replace every dynamic portion with either a wildcard or a `{{Variable}}`.

3. **Context / Frame Loss:** The selector itself is correct, but the parent window or browser tab is no longer the active one. The robot clicks the wrong page.
   - **Remedy:** Add an `Attach Browser` (for web) or `Attach Window` (for desktop) activity immediately before the click, ensuring the parent context is bound.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often answer part (b) with generic answers like *"selectors fail due to application changes."* This is **worth zero marks**. The examiner expects the **three specific named failure modes** — Over-Wildcarding, Under-Wildcarding, Context Loss — each with a *concrete* remedy. Memorize the names and pair them with examples.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
> Compare and contrast **wildcard-based dynamic selectors** and **variable-based dynamic selectors** in UiPath. Construct a 2-column comparison table covering syntax, use case, performance, and limitations.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Wildcard-Based Selector | Variable-Based Selector |
|:-------|:------------------------|:------------------------|
| **Syntax** | `aaname='*'` or `aaname='View?'` | `aaname='{{ButtonName}}'` |
| **Use Case** | When the changing value is **unknown to the workflow** (e.g., a randomly generated session ID) | When the changing value is **computed inside the workflow** (e.g., loop counter, scraped name) |
| **Performance** | Slightly slower at runtime because the engine must **regex-evaluate** the pattern | Marginally faster because it is just a **string replacement** before matching |
| **Readability** | Harder for non-author reviewers to interpret | Clearer — the variable name documents intent |
| **Risk** | Over-wildcarding can produce ambiguous matches | Variable may be empty or mis-typed, causing zero matches |
| **Maintenance** | Edit only the `.xaml` selector string | Edit only the variable assignment in code |
| **KTU Recommendation** | Use as fallback when variables are not available | **Preferred** for new development (cleaner) |

*(7 marks: 2 for the table itself, 3 for explanatory notes, 2 for KTU recommendation.)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
> Describe, with a Mermaid-style or textual flowchart, the **runtime pipeline** that UiPath's Selector Resolution Engine follows when executing an activity with a dynamic selector. Highlight the **exception paths**.

**Model Answer:**

The pipeline consists of the following stages (textual flowchart):

```
[1] Activity fires (e.g., Click)
        |
        v
[2] Load Selector XML from .xaml
        |
        v
[3] Scan for {{ }} tokens
        |-- Yes --> [4a] Replace with variable runtime value
        |-- No  --> (proceed unchanged)
        |
        v
[5] Scan for * and ? wildcards
        |-- Yes --> [6a] Convert to internal regex
        |-- No  --> (proceed unchanged)
        |
        v
[7] Query UI Automation tree
        |
        v
[8] Count matches N
        |
        +-- N = 0 ---> EXCEPTION: SelectorNotFoundException
        |                    (Remedy: increase Timeout, add WaitForReady)
        |
        +-- N = 1 ---> [9] Dispatch UI event  --> SUCCESS
        |
        +-- N > 1 ---> EXCEPTION: AmbiguousSelectorException
                             (Remedy: add ancestor <webctrl> tags)
```

**Key exception paths:**

- **SelectorNotFoundException** — the engine found **zero** elements. Usually means the dynamic part is still under-wildcarded, or the target page has not yet loaded.
- **AmbiguousSelectorException** — the engine found **more than one** element. Usually means the wildcard is too broad, or two elements genuinely share the targeted attribute.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Warning:** Do not confuse the *exceptions* (UI-Automation-level) with the *Activity-level* outcomes. A `SelectorNotFoundException` does **not** mean the workflow stops unless the activity's `ContinueOnError` is set to `False` (the default). Many students lose marks by claiming the "robot crashes" — in reality, the developer can catch and retry.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 3: Dynamic Selectors**

- ✅ **Selector** = XML-like string of `tag:attr="value"` triples that uniquely identifies a UI element.
- ✅ **Static Selector** = hard-coded, brittle, fails on dynamic UIs.
- ✅ **Dynamic Selector** = uses `*`, `?`, or `{{Variable}}` to remain flexible.
- ✅ **`*` wildcard** matches **0 or more characters** (variable length).
- ✅ **`?` wildcard** matches **exactly 1 character** (fixed length, position known).
- ✅ **Variable substitution syntax** is `{{VariableName}}` — double-curly-brace mustache style.
- ✅ **Selector uniqueness rule:** $N_{matches} = 1$ is the only valid success state.
- ✅ **Three failure modes:** Over-Wildcarding, Under-Wildcarding, Context Loss — memorize these by name.
- ✅ **Two exceptions:** `SelectorNotFoundException` ($N=0$) and `AmbiguousSelectorException` ($N>1$).
- ✅ **Maximum recommended wildcards per selector:** 3 — beyond this is fragile.
- ✅ **Best practice:** Prefer *anchor* activities and *variables* over *bare wildcards*.
- ✅ **KTU Viva question to expect:** *"When would you choose a wildcard over a variable?"* — Answer: when the changing value is **not generated by the workflow** itself (e.g., a server-assigned GUID).
- ✅ **Engineering utility:** Dynamic selectors are the *enabling technology* of data-driven RPA — without them, `For Each` loops over records are impossible.
- ✅ **Draw the pipeline** (Load → Variable Sub → Wildcard → DOM Query → Match Count → Dispatch) for any 7-mark question on this topic.

> **End of Module 3 Topic Notes — Dynamic Selectors. Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme / NEP 2020 outcome-based education guidelines.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Partial Selectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Partial Selectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Partial Selectors** are UI element descriptors in Robotic Process Automation (RPA) platforms (specifically UiPath) in which one or more attribute values within a selector are **replaced with wildcard characters** (`*` and `?`) so that the selector can match a *family of similar* UI elements rather than a single, exact target.

In UiPath's selector architecture, a *full selector* uniquely identifies a single UI element using exact attribute-value pairs (e.g., `title='Calculator'`, `cls='CalcFrame'`). A **partial selector** relaxes this constraint by introducing wildcards, allowing the robot to dynamically adapt to UI changes such as dynamic window titles, dynamic IDs, version-specific class names, or iteratively generated text.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Name Tag" System**
> Imagine you walk into a company where every employee wears a name tag. A *full selector* would say: "Find the person whose tag reads *exactly* 'Rahul Menon, Emp-ID 1042, Floor 5, Department Finance." If that person is on vacation, the robot fails.
>
> A **partial selector** is like saying: "Find anyone whose tag contains 'Rahul' on **Floor 5**." Now the robot can find Rahul Menon OR Rahul Krishnan — the matching is *flexible*. The asterisk (`*`) acts like the phrase "anything can go here", and the question mark (`?`) acts like "any single character can go here."

### 1.3 The Two Wildcard Characters in UiPath Selectors

| Symbol | Name | Meaning | Example Selector Fragment |
|:------:|:----:|:--------|:--------------------------|
| `*` | Asterisk (Star wildcard) | Replaces **zero or more** characters | `title='*Report*'` |
| `?` | Question mark (Single-char wildcard) | Replaces **exactly one** character | `cls='Button??'` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wildcards in UiPath selectors operate at the attribute-value level**, NOT at the attribute-name level. You cannot wildcard an attribute *name* (like replacing `title` with `*`), but you *can* wildcard its *value* (like replacing `'Calculator'` with `'Calc*'`).

### 1.4 Why Partial Selectors Are Essential

Partial selectors are the **backbone of dynamic UI automation**. The three primary use-cases are:

- **Dynamic IDs/Window Titles**: Applications that append timestamps, session IDs, or user names to window titles (e.g., `Document1 - John_Doe.docx`).
- **Iterative List Processing**: Selecting rows in a table, items in a list, or emails in an inbox where indices change.
- **Version-Independent Automation**: Class names or attribute structures that change between software releases (e.g., Chrome's `Chrome_RenderWidgetHostHWND` evolving across versions).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a UiPath Selector

A UiPath selector is a structured XML-like descriptor composed of `<wnd>`, `<ctrl>`, `<uia>`, `<java>`, or `<html>` tags, each containing attributes.

```xml
<html app='chrome.exe' title='Invoice *' />
<webctrl tag='TABLE' class='data-grid' />
<webctrl tag='TR' idx='5' />
```

In this example:
- The `*` in `title='Invoice *'` is a partial selector wildcard.
- It will match titles like "Invoice 1001", "Invoice ABC", "Invoice March 2025" — anything starting with "Invoice ".

### 2.2 Operational Logic of Wildcards — Step-by-Step

**Asterisk (`*`) behavior:**
- **Logical Rule:** Substitutes for any sequence of zero or more characters at the position it occupies.
- **Position matters:**
  - `*Report` → matches "Daily Report", "Sales Report", "Report"
  - `Report*` → matches "Report 2024", "Report.docx", "Report"
  - `*Report*` → matches anywhere in the string
  - `Report` (no asterisk) → exact match only

**Question mark (`?`) behavior:**
- **Logical Rule:** Substitutes for exactly one character.
- `Button?` → matches "Button1", "ButtonA" but NOT "Button12"
- `2024-??-15` → matches any date in 2024 on the 15th of any month

### 2.3 Full vs. Partial Selector — The Strict Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> A **Full Selector** is generated when the target application is attached as the *root* container (typically via the **Attach Browser** / **Attach Window** activity that opens its own scope).
>
> A **Partial Selector** is generated when the target element is inside an already-open container (typically a **Use Application/Browser** scope or an **Attach Window** scope where the parent is implicit).

In practice, the term "partial selector" is a **double-meaning** in UiPath:

1. **Architectural Partial Selector:** The selector stored in an activity *inside* a container scope (no `<wnd>` or `<html>` root tag for the application).
2. **Wildcard Partial Selector:** A selector that uses `*` or `?` to allow flexible matching.

Both meanings often intersect — wildcards are most commonly used *within* partial selectors to add flexibility to container-scoped automation.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax / Value | Purpose | Exam-Relevance |
|:--------|:--------------:|:--------|:---------------|
| Full Selector | Contains `<html app='...'> or <wnd app='...'>` as root | Independent, attachable selector | **High** |
| Partial Selector (Architectural) | Begins with `<webctrl>` or `<ctrl>` | Lives inside a parent container scope | **Very High** |
| Star Wildcard `*` | `attr='value*'` or `*value*'` | Zero or more chars | **Very High** |
| Question Mark `?` | `attr='va?ue'` | Exactly one character | **High** |
| `idx` attribute | `idx='2'` | Zero-based index of sibling | **High** |
| `aaname` attribute | `aaname='Submit'` | Automation-friendly accessible name | **High** |
| `innertext` attribute | `innertext='*'` | Matches visible text of element | **Medium** |
| Wildcards in attribute *names* | ❌ NOT ALLOWED | Only in attribute *values* | **High** |
| Wildcards in tag names | ✅ Allowed (e.g., `<*>`) | Matches any tag | **Medium** |
| Selector Editor Toggle | `aaname`, `innertext`, etc. checkboxes | Add/replace attributes in UI | **Medium** |

### 2.5 Real-World RPA Engineering Utility

In enterprise RPA deployments (Banking, Insurance, Healthcare), **partial selectors with wildcards** are used to:

- Process hundreds of invoices with dynamic filenames: `title='Invoice *.pdf'`
- Click the "OK" button in any popup regardless of its parent window: `title='*' role='button' name='OK'`
- Iterate over dynamic web tables: `tag='TR' idx='{{rowIndex}}'`
- Handle version-updated applications where the class name changes partially: `cls='Chrome_WidgetWin*'`
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Selector Construction

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Dynamic Window Title Matching

**Problem:** Automate a process that opens files named `Report_2024.xlsx`, `Report_2025.xlsx`, etc. The window title changes yearly.

**Step 1 — Identify the dynamic attribute.**

The `title` attribute in `<wnd>` changes: `title='Report_2024 - Excel'`, `title='Report_2025 - Excel'`.

**Step 2 — Decide wildcard position.**

Since the year is the only changing segment, we replace it with `*`.

**Step 3 — Construct the partial selector.**

```xml
<wnd app='excel.exe' title='Report_* - Excel' />
<wnd cls='XLMAIN' />
<ctrl name='Sheet1' role='PAGE TAB' />
```

**Step 4 — Logical verification.**

$$\text{Match Set} = \{ s \mid s \text{ contains the substring "Report\_"} \wedge s \text{ ends with " - Excel"} \}$$

For any input $s$, the matching condition is:
$$\text{isMatch}(s) = \begin{cases} \text{True} & \text{if } s \text{ satisfies the wildcard pattern} \\ \text{False} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

- `Report_2024 - Excel` → ✅ matches `Report_* - Excel`
- `Report_Nov - Excel` → ✅ matches
- `Summary_2024 - Excel` → ❌ does not match (must start with "Report_")

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Iterating a Dynamic Table Row

**Problem:** Click the 3rd, 4th, and 5th rows of a data grid in a web application.

**Step 1 — Full selector for a single row (generated by UiPath).**

```xml
<html app='chrome.exe' title='Customer Portal' />
<webctrl tag='TABLE' class='data-grid' />
<webctrl tag='TR' idx='2' />
```

**Step 2 — Convert to a reusable partial selector with parameterization.**

The `idx` is 0-based in UiPath, so the 3rd row = `idx='2'`. We use a UiPath variable `rowIndex`:

```xml
<webctrl tag='TR' idx='{{rowIndex}}' />
```

**Step 3 — Step-by-step transition (manual walkthrough).**

Initial state, $i = 2$:
$$\text{Row Click} = \text{Selector}(i) = \texttt{<webctrl tag='TR' idx='}i\texttt{' />}$$

For loop iterations:
$$\begin{aligned}
i=2 &: \text{clicks row 3 (since index is 0-based)} \\
i=3 &: \text{clicks row 4} \\
i=4 &: \text{clicks row 5} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Combine with wildcards for ultimate flexibility.**

If the table rows have unique IDs but the table class name sometimes includes a version suffix, we can wild-card that:

```xml
<html app='chrome.exe' title='Customer Portal' />
<webctrl tag='TABLE' class='data-grid*' />
<webctrl tag='TR' idx='{{rowIndex}}' />
```

This matches `data-grid`, `data-grid-v2`, `data-grid-light`, etc.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — `?` Wildcard for Fixed-Length Dynamic Characters

**Problem:** A button's automation ID is `btnSave01`, `btnSave02`, ..., `btnSave99` and we want a selector that matches *any* of them.

**Construction:**

```xml
<webctrl id='btnSave??' tag='BUTTON' />
```

**Verification:**

- `btnSave01` → ✅ exactly 2 chars after "btnSave"
- `btnSave99` → ✅
- `btnSave100` → ❌ 3 chars, does not match `??`
- `btnSaveA1` → ✅ `?` matches any single character (letters OR digits)

> [!NOTE]
> **Counting Rule for `?`:** The number of `?` symbols must **exactly equal** the number of variable characters in the actual value. `?` is positional and has no "zero-or-more" semantics.

### 3.4 Edge Cases & Substitution Logic

For a string $S$ and a pattern $P$ containing wildcards, the matching function $M(S, P)$ evaluates as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M(S, P) &= \text{True} \iff \forall \text{ position } p \in P, \text{ the character constraint is satisfied.} \\
\text{For } `*' &: \text{ matches any suffix (zero or more chars from } S \text{ at position } p).\\
\text{For } `?' &: \text{ matches exactly one character from } S \text{ at position } p.\\
\text{For literal } c &: \text{ must equal } S[p].
\end{aligned}
$$

**Edge-case examples to memorize for exams:**

| Pattern | Matches | Does NOT Match | Reason |
|:--------|:--------|:---------------|:-------|
| `*` | Any string | — | Universal wildcard |
| `*Report*` | "Report", "Daily Report", "XReportY" | "report" (lowercase) | Case-sensitive |
| `File?.txt` | "File1.txt", "FileA.txt" | "File12.txt" | `?` = one char only |
| `*.docx` | "a.docx", "report.docx" | ".docx" (zero chars before) — ✅ actually matches | `*` allows zero |
| `2024-??-15` | "2024-03-15" | "2024-3-15" | `??` = 2 chars exactly |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Selector Hierarchy — Full vs. Partial (Architectural View)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Robot Execution Starts] --> B{Target Application Open?}
    B -- No --> C[Use Attach Window / Attach Browser]
    B -- Yes --> D[Use Application/Browser Scope]
    C --> E[Full Selector Generated]
    D --> F[Partial Selector Generated]
    E --> G[Selector contains wnd or html root tag]
    F --> H[Selector begins with ctrl or webctrl]
    G --> I[Wildcards applied for dynamic attributes]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Final Flexible Selector Ready]
    J --> K[UI Element Identified and Interacted]
```

### 4.2 Wildcard Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Need Flexible Selector] --> Q1{How many variable characters?}
    Q1 -- Zero or more --> Star[Use asterisk star wildcard]
    Q1 -- Exactly one --> QM[Use question mark wildcard]
    Q1 -- Fixed count, more than one --> QM2[Use multiple question marks]
    Star --> Pos1{Where is the variation?}
    QM --> Pos1
    QM2 --> Pos1
    Pos1 -- Start of value --> P1[pattern star value]
    Pos1 -- End of value --> P2[value star pattern]
    Pos1 -- Middle of value --> P3[prefix star middle star suffix]
    Pos1 -- All over --> P4[star value star]
    P1 --> Validate[Test in UIExplorer]
    P2 --> Validate
    P3 --> Validate
    P4 --> Validate
    Validate --> Done[Selector Validated]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Partial Selector Resolution

```mermaid
graph LR
    Input[Raw Selector XML with Wildcards] --> Parser[UiPath Selector Parser]
    Parser --> Tokenizer[Tokenizer Splits Attributes and Wildcards]
    Tokenizer --> Expand[Wildcard Expansion Engine]
    Expand --> Regex[Internal Regex Pattern Compilation]
    Regex --> Match[Match Against Live UI Element Tree]
    UI[Live UI Tree from Target App] --> Match
    Match --> Result{Match Found?}
    Result -- Yes --> Action[Perform Activity Action]
    Result -- No --> Retry[SelectorNotFoundException or Retry]
    Action --> End[Success]
    Retry --> End2[Failure logged to Orchestrator]
```

### 4.4 Anatomy of a Wildcarded Partial Selector (Annotated)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[html tag root] --> B[app attribute exact value]
    A --> C[title attribute with star wildcard]
    C --> C1[Static prefix Invoice space]
    C --> C2[Wildcard star matches variable suffix]
    A --> D[webctrl child tag]
    D --> E[tag attribute exact TABLE]
    D --> F[class attribute with star wildcard]
    F --> F1[Static prefix data hyphen grid]
    F --> F2[Wildcard star matches version suffix v1, v2, etc.]
    D --> G[idx attribute dynamic via variable]
```

### 4.5 Lifecycle of a Partial Selector During RPA Run

```mermaid
graph TD
    Phase1[Phase 1 Design Time] --> P1A[Developer uses Indicate Element]
    P1A --> P1B[UiPath generates raw selector]
    P1B --> P1C[Developer injects wildcards star or question]
    P1C --> P1D[Selector validated in UIExplorer]
    P1D --> Phase2[Phase 2 Run Time]
    Phase2 --> P2A[Robot loads target application]
    P2A --> P2B[Parent scope provides application context]
    P2B --> P2C[Partial selector appended to context]
    P2C --> P2D[Wildcard engine matches UI element]
    P2D --> P2E{Unique Match?}
    P2E -- Yes --> P2F[Activity executes successfully]
    P2E -- No --> P2G[Ambiguous match or exception thrown]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define the term "partial selector" in UiPath. How does it differ from a full selector?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **partial selector** in UiPath is a UI element descriptor that does **not** include the root container tag (like `<wnd app='...'>` or `<html app='...'>`). It begins directly with a child tag such as `<ctrl>` or `<webctrl>` and is meant to be used **inside an already-open container scope** (e.g., a `Use Application/Browser` or `Attach Window` activity).

**Difference from Full Selector:**

| Aspect | Full Selector | Partial Selector |
|:-------|:--------------|:-----------------|
| Root tag | Present (`<wnd>`, `<html>`) | Absent |
| Self-sufficient | Yes — contains application context | No — needs parent scope |
| Usage | Standalone activities | Inside container activities |
| Reusability | Lower (tied to app) | Higher (reusable across apps) |

**(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for differences)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Explain the role of wildcard characters `*` and `?` in UiPath partial selectors with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Wildcard characters allow partial selectors to **flexibly match multiple UI elements** whose attribute values may vary at runtime.

- **`*` (Asterisk):** Replaces **zero or more characters** at its position.
  - *Example:* `title='Invoice *'` matches "Invoice 1001", "Invoice March", "Invoice".

- **`?` (Question mark):** Replaces **exactly one character** at its position.
  - *Example:* `id='btnSave??'` matches "btnSave01", "btnSaveAB" but NOT "btnSave123" (3 chars).

**(3 marks: 1 mark each for explanation + 1 mark for correct example)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **anatomy of a UiPath selector** and clearly mark the location where wildcards can be applied. Discuss any two real-world scenarios where partial selectors with wildcards become essential. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Construct a **partial selector with appropriate wildcards** to click the *Submit* button on a web form whose:
   - Browser is Chrome
   - Page title is `Registration Form — Session A1B2C3` (the alphanumeric session ID changes per user)
   - Submit button has `id='btnSubmit_v2024'`, `id='btnSubmit_v2025'`, etc.
   - Use a `Use Application/Browser` scope. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a) — Anatomy of a UiPath Selector (7 marks)

A UiPath selector is an **XML-like descriptor** that uniquely identifies a UI element by listing its attributes and parent hierarchy.

**General structure:**

```xml
<root_tag app='application' title='window title'>
    <child_tag attribute1='value1' attribute2='value2' />
    <grandchild_tag attribute='value' />
</root_tag>
```

**Common tags:**
- `<wnd>` — for desktop applications (Windows)
- `<html>` — for web pages
- `<webctrl>` — child element within HTML
- `<ctrl>` — child element within Windows apps
- `<uia>`, `<java>` — for Java/UIA-based apps

**Wildcard placement locations:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Selector XML] --> B[Tag Name - wildcards NOT allowed]
    A --> C[Attribute Name - wildcards NOT allowed]
    A --> D[Attribute Value - wildcards ALLOWED here]
    A --> E[Tag attribute itself - wildcards ALLOWED]
    D --> D1[Example title equals star Report star]
    E --> E1[Example web star tag]
```

**Two real-world scenarios:**

1. **Dynamic Window Titles in Document Processing:** When the title bar contains user-specific data (e.g., `Document_JohnDoe.docx - Word`), a partial selector like `title='Document_*.docx - Word'` handles all users.

2. **Iterative Web Table Row Selection:** In an invoice approval workflow, clicking row N of a 50-row table requires `idx='{{N}}'` (a variable). Wildcards on the table's `class` attribute (e.g., `class='data-grid*'`) further decouple the robot from CSS version updates.

**(Mark distribution: 3 marks diagram + 2 marks wildcard locations + 2 marks scenarios)**

#### Part (b) — Constructing the Submit Button Selector (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify the dynamic portions:**
- Window title: `Registration Form — Session A1B2C3` → the `A1B2C3` part changes
- Button ID: `btnSubmit_v2024`, `btnSubmit_v2025` → the `v2024`/`v2025` part changes

**Step 2 — Since the browser is already attached via a parent scope, we omit the `<html>` root tag (making it a *partial selector*).**

**Step 3 — Construct the selector:**

```xml
<webctrl tag='BUTTON' id='btnSubmit_v*' aaname='Submit' />
```

**Step 4 — Verification by enumeration:**

| Actual Button ID | Pattern `btnSubmit_v*` | Match? |
|:-----------------|:----------------------|:------:|
| `btnSubmit_v2024` | `btnSubmit_v` + any suffix | ✅ |
| `btnSubmit_v2025` | `btnSubmit_v` + any suffix | ✅ |
| `btnSubmit_vBeta` | `btnSubmit_v` + any suffix | ✅ |
| `btnSave_v2024` | Must start with `btnSubmit_` | ❌ |

**Step 5 — Full activity structure:**

```
[Use Application/Browser: 'Registration Form — Session *']
        └── [Click: selector uses <webctrl id='btnSubmit_v*'>]
```

**Justification for wildcards:**

- `title='Registration Form — Session *'` (in the parent scope) — handles variable session ID
- `id='btnSubmit_v*'` (in the click activity) — handles version year changes
- `aaname='Submit'` (kept exact) — ensures we are clicking the *Submit* button and not, say, a "Submit Draft" button

**(Mark distribution: 2 marks identifying dynamic parts + 3 marks correct selector XML + 2 marks justification)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Differentiate between `*` and `?` wildcards. Construct **two partial selectors** — one using `*` and one using `?` — for an SAP GUI automation where:
   - The window title is always "SAP Easy Access" but the system name (e.g., "PRD", "QAS", "DEV") appears in the title
   - The transaction code field is a text box with `id='okcd'` whose default value is `/n` followed by a 4-character transaction code (e.g., `/nVA01`, `/nMM01`) **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **"Use Application/Browser" scope activity** and describe how it enables the use of partial selectors. Discuss **one limitation** of partial selectors in real-world automation. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a) — Wildcard Differentiation and SAP Selectors (7 marks)

**Differentiation table:**

| Aspect | `*` (Asterisk) | `?` (Question mark) |
|:-------|:---------------|:--------------------|
| Replaces | Zero or more characters | Exactly one character |
| Positional flexibility | Yes (start, end, middle, alone) | Yes (one char per `?`) |
| Typical use | Variable-length values | Fixed-length variable values |
| Example | `Invoice*` | `VA??` |

**Selector 1 — Using `*` (for window title with variable system name):**

```xml
<wnd app='saplogon.exe' title='SAP Easy Access*' />
<wnd cls='SAP_FRONTEND_SESSION' />
<sap id='okcd' />
```

The `*` after "SAP Easy Access" absorbs any system suffix (e.g., "SAP Easy Access - PRD").

**Selector 2 — Using `?` (for transaction code field):**

Since the transaction code after `/n` is *exactly 4 characters*, we use four `?` symbols:

```xml
<wnd app='saplogon.exe' title='SAP Easy Access*' />
<wnd cls='SAP_FRONTEND_SESSION' />
<sap id='okcd' aaname='????' />
```

Alternatively, if the field contains the full value `/nVA01`, we wildcard the variable part:

```xml
<sap id='okcd' aaname='/n' />
```

*(Note: `aaname` in SAP often refers to the field's visible text, so the choice of `*` vs `?` depends on whether the value length is fixed or variable.)*

**Matching function walkthrough:**

For an actual transaction code $T = c_1 c_2 c_3 c_4$ (4 characters) and pattern $P = \texttt{????}$:

$$M(T, P) = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{4} (\text{position } i \text{ of } T \text{ matches wildcard at position } i \text{ of } P)$$

Since `?` accepts any single character:

$$M(T, \texttt{????}) = \text{True} \quad \forall T \text{ where } \vert T \vert = 4$$

**(Mark distribution: 2 marks differentiation + 2.5 marks selector 1 + 2.5 marks selector 2)**

#### Part (b) — Use Application/Browser Scope & Partial Selector Limitations (7 marks)

**Use Application/Browser Activity — Definition:**

> The **Use Application/Browser** activity is a **container scope** in UiPath that opens (or attaches to) a target application/browser window and provides a **shared context** for all child activities. It exposes the application's UI tree to its children, enabling the use of **partial selectors** (selectors without the application root tag).

**How it enables partial selectors:**

- The container holds the `<wnd app='...'>` or `<html app='...'>` tag *implicitly*.
- All child activities (Click, Type Into, Get Text) reference elements using *partial selectors* that begin with `<webctrl>`, `<ctrl>`, `<sap>`, etc.
- This **reduces duplication** — the application context is written *once* at the container, not repeated in every child activity.

**Visualization:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    Container[Use Application Browser: app equals chrome.exe] --> A1[Click Activity - partial selector only]
    Container --> A2[Type Into Activity - partial selector only]
    Container --> A3[Get Text Activity - partial selector only]
    A1 --> S1[webctrl tag equals BUTTON]
    A2 --> S2[webctrl id equals txtUsername]
    A3 --> S3[webctrl id equals lblStatus]
```

**Limitation of Partial Selectors:**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Limitation — Ambiguity & Indistinguishability**
>
> Partial selectors **omit the application context**, which means:
> - If multiple applications or browser windows are open simultaneously, a partial selector **may match an unintended element** in the wrong window.
> - **Mitigation:** UiPath's modern architecture largely handles this via the container scope, but legacy scenarios and dual-monitor environments still pose risks. Best practice is to combine partial selectors with **explicit container scoping** and **attribute uniqueness** (`aaname`, `id`).
>
> **Mark loss zone:** Students often forget to mention that partial selectors require a *parent scope to be active* at runtime — this is a 2-mark deduction in valuation.

**(Mark distribution: 2 marks Use Application/Browser definition + 2 marks how it enables partial selectors + 1 mark limitation statement + 2 marks mitigation/example)**

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on "Partial Selectors" questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating the two meanings of "partial selector":** Many students mix up the *architectural* meaning (no root tag, inside a container) with the *wildcard* meaning (uses `*` or `?`). KTU expects you to **know BOTH** and be able to discuss them separately. *(–2 marks if confused)*
>
> 2. **Forgetting that wildcards are case-sensitive:** `title='*Report*'` will NOT match "daily report" (lowercase r). *(–1 mark in selector construction questions)*
>
> 3. **Using `?` loosely:** Writing `id='btn?'` thinking it matches "btnSave01" — it does NOT. `?` matches exactly one character. *(–1 to 2 marks)*
>
> 4. **Not mentioning the parent scope requirement:** A partial selector (architectural) **cannot function standalone** — the examiner will deduct marks if you do not mention the `Use Application/Browser` or `Attach Window` scope.
>
> 5. **Writing `*` inside attribute names** (e.g., `*name='Submit'`) — this is **invalid syntax** and earns zero marks for that selector.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Partial Selector = Architectural meaning** (no root app tag) **OR Wildcard meaning** (uses `*` or `?`). Both are testable.
- ✅ **`*` (asterisk)** = zero or more characters; **`?` (question mark)** = exactly one character.
- ✅ Wildcards work in **attribute VALUES** only — not in attribute names; tag name wildcards are limited.
- ✅ Wildcard matching is **case-sensitive** in UiPath.
- ✅ Partial selectors (architectural) **must live inside a container scope** — they are not self-sufficient.
- ✅ The container scope (`Use Application/Browser`, `Attach Window`, `Open Browser`) **implicitly provides the root tag** (`<wnd>` or `<html>`).
- ✅ Common attributes for dynamic matching: `title`, `aaname`, `innertext`, `id`, `cls`, `name`.
- ✅ `idx` is **0-based** — `idx='0'` is the first element, `idx='1'` is the second.
- ✅ Use `*` for **variable-length** dynamic values, use `?` for **fixed-length** dynamic values.
- ✅ Best practice: combine wildcards with stable attributes (e.g., `tag='BUTTON' id='btnSubmit_v*'`) to avoid ambiguity.
- ✅ KTU 2024 emphasizes: differentiating full vs. partial, explaining `*` vs. `?`, constructing selectors in scenario questions, and explaining container scope architecture.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RPA Challenge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** RPA Challenge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Official Definition**
> The **RPA Challenge** is a standardized benchmarking web-application scenario (hosted at *rpachallenge.com*) used to evaluate the ability of a Robotic Process Automation (RPA) bot to interact with **dynamically mutating Graphical User Interfaces (GUIs)**. The platform deliberately reorders, renames, and repositions form input fields across successive submissions within a single session, requiring the bot to employ robust **dynamic selector strategies** rather than static XPath/UI-Element references.

The challenge is built on three engineering pillars:

1. **Data-Driven Iteration:** Consume an external spreadsheet (typically an `.xlsx` file) row-by-row using a `For Each Row` control structure.
2. **Dynamic Element Resolution:** Identify form fields whose `title`, `name`, or `aria-label` attributes persist logically but whose absolute position in the DOM tree changes after every `Submit`.
3. **Exception-Resilient Execution:** Tolerate transient timing issues, stale selectors, and post-submit DOM refreshes without terminating the workflow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this is a "Module 3" topic in KTU PEAIT415:** This topic integrates the **Selector Engineering**, **UI Automation**, and **DataTable handling** concepts introduced in earlier modules into a single, exam-style industrial use case. It is one of the highest-weightage practical scenarios in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Shuffled Exam OMR Sheet"**
> Imagine you are filling out a 10-question **OMR sheet** in a college exam. The catch: **after every question you answer, the invigilator reprints the next OMR sheet with the bubbles in completely different positions** (Question 1's bubble may now be at slot 7, Question 2 at slot 2, etc.). The *question text* on the sheet stays the same, but *its location* shuffles. A human, reading the label and finding the correct bubble, takes a few extra seconds. A poorly designed robot (one that memorized *"always click slot 1"*) fails instantly. A smart robot that **reads the label, searches for the matching bubble, and only then marks it** succeeds.

The **RPA Challenge** is precisely this shuffled-OMR metaphor, digitized:
- The **Excel spreadsheet** = your answer key (Company Name, Role, Email, Phone, Address, First/Last Name).
- The **Web form** = the shuffled OMR sheet whose labels persist but whose DOM positions shuffle.
- The **RPA bot** = the "smart robot" that must perform **label-based field resolution** for 10 rounds and then receive a final time score.

---

## 1.3 Standard KTU High-Yield Metrics (Memorize these)

> [!NOTE]
> **Benchmarking Constants used in RPA Challenge scoring**
> - **Total Rounds:** **10 records** per challenge instance.
> - **Typical Field Set:** **7 fields per row** (First Name, Last Name, Company Name, Role in Company, Address, Email, Phone Number).
> - **Selector Mutation Cycle:** Position shuffle occurs **after every successful `Submit` click**.
> - **Master Performance Target:** Top-tier bots complete the 10 rounds in **< 1.5 seconds average per round** (a benchmark often quoted in UiPath Academy coursework that KTU follows).
> - **Failure Tolerance:** Bots are scored on **completion percentage** and **average per-record time**, not just pass/fail.

---

## 1.4 Prerequisite Knowledge (KTU Module 1 & 2 Recall)

Before tackling the challenge, the KTU student must be fluent in:

| Prerequisite Skill | Source Module | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| Recording vs. Drag-and-Drop activities | Module 1 | Recording produces brittle selectors unsuitable for this challenge |
| `Open Browser` / `Attach Browser` | Module 2 | Must choose the right one for performance |
| Excel `Read Range` / `Build DataTable` | Module 2 | Source data ingestion |
| `For Each Row` loop | Module 1 | Iterative data input |
| `Anchor Base` / `Find Element` | Module 2 | Dynamic selector resolution |
| `Element Exists` + `On Element Appear` | Module 2 | Wait conditions for DOM refresh |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Engineering Problem in Formal Terms

Let the challenge instance be defined as the tuple:

$$ C = \langle D, F, \sigma, T \rangle $$

Where:

- $D = \{d_1, d_2, \dots, d_{10}\}$ is the input **Dataset** of 10 rows from the Excel file.
- $F = \{f_1, f_2, \dots, f_7\}$ is the logical **Field Set** (First Name, Last Name, …, Phone).
- $\sigma: F \rightarrow P$ is the **Shuffle Function** that maps each field $f_i$ to a physical pixel position $p_i$ in the rendered DOM. Crucially, $\sigma$ changes after every submission, i.e., $\sigma_{k+1} \neq \sigma_k$.
- $T$ is the total **Tolerance time** before the bot must complete all 10 rounds.

The naive approach (recording a workflow once) produces selectors of the form:

$$ s_{\text{static}} = \texttt{html > body > div[3] > form > input[1]} $$

This selector resolves $p_i$ based on **positional index**, which breaks when $\sigma$ permutes. The robust solution requires **attribute-based** selectors of the form:

$$ s_{\text{dynamic}}(f) = \texttt{//input[@ng-reflect-name="}f \texttt{"]} \quad \text{or} \quad \texttt{//label[text()="}f \texttt{"]/following::input[1]} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** The selector strategy is THE most frequently examined concept from this topic. Marks are awarded for correctly identifying *which* `activity` is used (e.g., Anchor Base) and *why* it is preferred over a static selector.

---

## 2.2 The Three Selector Strategies (Comparative Analysis)

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategy 1 — Static Positional Selectors**
> Generated automatically when a workflow is **recorded**. These bind to absolute indices in the DOM tree. They are **fast but fragile**, and they will fail the RPA Challenge within 1–2 rounds because the field positions shuffle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategy 2 — Anchor Base Activity (UiPath)**
> The recommended KTU-canonical approach. You define an **Anchor** (a stable label like *"First Name"*) and an **Activity** (the corresponding input box). The runtime engine **searches the page at execution time** for the anchor's text and locates the activity **relative to it**, regardless of its position in the DOM. This is robust to $\sigma$ changes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategy 3 — Find Element + Set Attribute / Type Into**
> A more **programmatic** approach. The bot executes a `Find Element` activity with a fuzzy XPath query (e.g., `//label[contains(., "Email")]/following::input`), then performs a `Type Into` on the returned element. This offers finer control and is preferred when labels share a common prefix (e.g., `"Phone Number"` vs `"Phone"`).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — it is the entire quantitative content examiners can ask from this topic.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Average Round Time | $\bar{t} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} t_k$ where $N = 10$ | Performance metric on the result page |
| 2 | Total Run Time | $T_{\text{total}} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} t_k$ | Displayed after final round |
| 3 | Success Rate | $S = \dfrac{N_{\text{passed}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | KPI for bot quality |
| 4 | Selector Robustness Score | $R = 1 - \dfrac{N_{\text{breaks}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | Higher $R$ ⇒ more reliable |
| 5 | DataTable Row Count (Excel) | $n = \text{Rows}(DT)$ | Used to bound the `For Each Row` loop |
| 6 | Time per Record Optimization | $t_{\text{opt}} = t_{\text{base}} - \Delta t_{\text{selector}}$ | Impact of switching static → dynamic selector |
| 7 | UI Element Staleness Probability | $P_{\text{stale}} \approx 1 - e^{-\lambda \Delta t}$ | Justifies the need for `On Element Appear` |

> **Note on Symbols:**
> - $t_k$ = time taken for the $k^{\text{th}}$ round in **milliseconds**.
> - $\lambda$ = rate of DOM mutation per second (empirically observed).
> - $\Delta t$ = time elapsed since the last selector was resolved.

---

## 2.4 The Engineering "Why" — Real-World Utility

The same dynamic-selector skill that defeats the RPA Challenge is used to automate:

1. **Insurance claim portals** where input sections expand/collapse based on claim type.
2. **Banking KYC forms** where additional fields appear conditionally (e.g., PAN field appears only for Indian residents).
3. **ERP systems (SAP, Oracle)** where the same logical field can be in a different tab or sub-screen depending on context.
4. **Government e-filing portals** that frequently rebrand or reflow their layouts without changing field semantics.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Hint:** When a KTU Part B question says *"Design an RPA workflow to fill a dynamic web form"*, the examiner is essentially testing the **RPA Challenge** pattern. Mention **Anchor Base** explicitly, justify *why* you avoided a static selector, and you will score full methodology marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Implementation & Analytical Walkthrough

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note to the KTU 2024 Student:** The KTU Board does not require code execution in the exam — it requires a **detailed workflow description**. The following walkthrough uses UiPath Studio terminology (the de-facto KTU-prescribed platform) and shows the *logical* steps. Each step is also tagged with the **implied engineering reasoning** to help you write your own answer.

---

## 3.1 Step 1 — Data Ingestion (Excel Read)

**Action:** Read the downloaded `challenge.xlsx` into a `DataTable` variable named `dt_Employees`.

**Implementation Steps:**
1. Use the `Read Range` activity (Excel Application Scope or Workbook Read Range).
2. Set `SheetName = "Sheet1"`, leave `Range` blank to read all data.
3. Store the output in `dt_Employees`.

**Why this matters:**
The Excel file is the **single source of truth**. The number of iterations of the entire workflow is determined by `dt_Employees.Rows.Count`. If the file has 10 rows, the workflow runs 10 rounds.

**Verification Logic:**

$$ \text{Loop Bound} = \text{Number of Records} = \text{Rows}(dt\_Employees) = 10 $$

---

## 3.2 Step 2 — Browser Initialization

**Action:** Open the challenge URL and attach to the main window.

**Implementation Steps:**
1. `Open Browser` activity with URL = `"http://www.rpachallenge.com/"`.
2. Browser type = `Chrome` (or `Edge` as per KTU lab convention).
3. Optional: Maximize the window for consistent selector resolution.
4. Use `Attach Browser` to re-attach on retry.

**Why this matters:**
A clean, maximized window ensures that all 7 form fields are visible and interactable without scrolling. The DOM may render differently when fields are off-screen.

---

## 3.3 Step 3 — Build the Dynamic Selector

**Action:** Create reusable selectors that resolve fields by their **stable text label**, not by position.

**Implementation Steps:**
1. For each field (e.g., *First Name*), right-click the field in UiExplorer.
2. Replace any positional attributes (`idx`, `tableRow`, `tableCol`) with **attribute-based** queries.
3. Example for "Phone Number":

$$ s_{\text{phone}} = \texttt{<html title='Rpa Challenge'>} \quad \texttt{<webctrl aaname='Phone Number'} \quad \texttt{tag='LABEL' />} \quad \texttt{<nav up='1' />} \quad \texttt{<webctrl tag='INPUT' />} $$

4. Alternatively, the **Anchor Base** activity encapsulates this entire pattern automatically.

**Why this matters:**
This is the **core innovation** of solving the challenge. Without this step, the bot is doomed to fail on Round 2.

---

## 3.4 Step 4 — The Main Loop (For Each Row)

**Action:** Iterate through `dt_Employees`, populating the form for each row.

**Implementation Steps:**

For each `row` in `dt_Employees`:
1. For each of the 7 fields $f \in F$:
   - `Anchor Base` with `Anchor = label of $f$` and `Activity = Type Into` with `row[f].ToString`.
2. After all 7 fields are typed, click the `Submit` button.
3. **Critical:** Use an `On Element Appear` activity pointing to the *next* form instance (or simply use a small `Delay` of 500 ms) to wait for the DOM to settle.
4. The loop automatically re-anchors all selectors in Round $k+1$ because Anchor Base re-searches the DOM.

**Why this matters:**
The `On Element Appear` is a **race-condition eliminator**. After `Submit`, the page re-renders. Typing into a stale reference would throw a `UiPath.Core.Activities.OrchestratorHttpException` or a `SelectorNotFoundException`.

---

## 3.5 Step 5 — Result Validation

**Action:** Confirm successful completion of all 10 rounds.

**Implementation Steps:**
1. After the loop, use `Get Text` on the result div (e.g., `//div[@class='result-content']`).
2. Parse the text using `Regex.Match` to extract the percentage score:

$$ \text{Score} = \text{Regex.Match}\left(\text{resultText}, \quad \texttt{"(\d{1,3})\%"}\right) $$

3. Log the result via a `Log Message` activity with `Level = Info`.

**Why this matters:**
Validation is what separates an **RPA script** from an **RPA solution**. Production bots must self-verify.

---

## 3.6 Step 6 — Exception Handling (KTU-Mandated)

**Action:** Wrap the main loop in a `Try-Catch` block.

**Implementation Steps:**
1. `Try` block: Steps 4–5 above.
2. `Catch` block: Catch `SelectorNotFoundException` and `BrowserOperationException`.
3. On catch: Use `Retry Scope` (3 attempts with 1-second delay).
4. After 3 failures, log to a `Send Email` activity or write to an `Error_Log.txt` file.

**Why this matters:**
KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education explicitly evaluates **robustness**. A bot that crashes on Round 5 is worth 0 marks in a practical exam, even if the workflow logic is correct.

---

## 3.7 Pseudocode Representation (Logic-Only, No Python)

```
START
  ├─ Download challenge.xlsx from the portal
  ├─ Read Range → dt_Employees
  ├─ Open Browser → rpachallenge.com
  ├─ FOR EACH row IN dt_Employees:
  │     ├─ Anchor Base (First Name)  → Type Into row("First Name")
  │     ├─ Anchor Base (Last Name)   → Type Into row("Last Name")
  │     ├─ Anchor Base (Company)     → Type Into row("Company Name")
  │     ├─ Anchor Base (Role)        → Type Into row("Role in Company")
  │     ├─ Anchor Base (Address)     → Type Into row("Address")
  │     ├─ Anchor Base (Email)       → Type Into row("Email")
  │     ├─ Anchor Base (Phone)       → Type Into row("Phone Number")
  │     ├─ Click Submit
  │     └─ On Element Appear (Next Form) → Wait
  ├─ Get Text → Result div
  ├─ Regex Match → Extract Score %
  └─ Log Message → Display Score
END
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no Python?** KTU 2024 explicitly disallows introducing unrelated programming languages into RPA practicals. The pseudocode above is **language-agnostic** and acceptable across all RPA platforms (UiPath, Automation Anywhere, Blue Prism).

---

## 3.8 Analytical Decision Matrix (Use this in exam answers)

| Step | Wrong Choice (Common Mistake) | Right Choice (KTU Standard) | Marks Justified By |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Recording the workflow | Building with `Anchor Base` | 3 |
| 2 | Static `Type Into` selector | Dynamic attribute-based selector | 4 |
| 3 | No wait between Submit and next round | `On Element Appear` or `Delay 500ms` | 2 |
| 4 | No `Try-Catch` | Full exception handling with `Retry Scope` | 3 |
| 5 | Hard-coded row index | `For Each Row` data-driven loop | 2 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `A1`, `B2`), no reserved keywords are used, and all labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text inside double quotes.

## 4.1 High-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["START BOT EXECUTION"]
    A2["DOWNLOAD CHALLENGE XLSX"]
    A3["READ RANGE INTO DT EMPLOYEES"]
    A4["OPEN BROWSER NAVIGATE TO RPA CHALLENGE"]
    A5["FOR EACH ROW IN DT EMPLOYEES"]
    A6["RESOLVE FIRST NAME LABEL ANCHOR"]
    A7["TYPE INTO FIRST NAME INPUT"]
    A8["RESOLVE LAST NAME LABEL ANCHOR"]
    A9["TYPE INTO LAST NAME INPUT"]
    A10["RESOLVE COMPANY NAME LABEL ANCHOR"]
    A11["TYPE INTO COMPANY NAME INPUT"]
    A12["RESOLVE ROLE LABEL ANCHOR"]
    A13["TYPE INTO ROLE INPUT"]
    A14["RESOLVE ADDRESS LABEL ANCHOR"]
    A15["TYPE INTO ADDRESS INPUT"]
    A16["RESOLVE EMAIL LABEL ANCHOR"]
    A17["TYPE INTO EMAIL INPUT"]
    A18["RESOLVE PHONE LABEL ANCHOR"]
    A19["TYPE INTO PHONE INPUT"]
    A20["CLICK SUBMIT BUTTON"]
    A21["ON ELEMENT APPEAR WAIT FOR NEW FORM"]
    A22["END OF FOR EACH LOOP"]
    A23["GET TEXT FROM RESULT DIV"]
    A24["REGEX EXTRACT SCORE PERCENTAGE"]
    A25["LOG MESSAGE DISPLAY FINAL SCORE"]
    A26["END BOT EXECUTION"]

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6 --> A7 --> A8 --> A9 --> A10 --> A11 --> A12 --> A13 --> A14 --> A15 --> A16 --> A17 --> A18 --> A19 --> A20 --> A21
    A21 --> A5
    A5 -- "All Rows Processed" --> A22 --> A23 --> A24 --> A25 --> A26
```

## 4.2 Dynamic Selector Resolution Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant BOT as RPA BOT
    participant DOM as WEB PAGE DOM
    participant EXC as EXCEL DATATABLE

    BOT->>EXC: READ RANGE GET ROW 1
    EXC-->>BOT: RETURN DICTIONARY WITH 7 FIELDS
    BOT->>DOM: FIND LABEL TEXT FIRST NAME
    DOM-->>BOT: RETURN POSITION COORDINATE A
    BOT->>DOM: TYPE INTO INPUT NEAR A
    DOM-->>BOT: ACKNOWLEDGED
    BOT->>DOM: REPEAT FOR OTHER 6 FIELDS
    BOT->>DOM: CLICK SUBMIT
    DOM-->>BOT: DOM REFRESHED NEW LAYOUT
    BOT->>DOM: FIND LABEL TEXT FIRST NAME AGAIN
    DOM-->>BOT: RETURN NEW POSITION COORDINATE B
    Note over BOT,DOM: POSITION B IS DIFFERENT FROM A
    BOT->>DOM: TYPE INTO INPUT NEAR B
    DOM-->>BOT: ACKNOWLEDGED
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"]
        I1["CHALLENGE XLSX FILE"]
        I2["WEB FORM HTML DOM"]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS["PROCESSING LAYER"]
        P1["EXCEL READER MODULE"]
        P2["DYNAMIC SELECTOR ENGINE"]
        P3["FORM FILLING ORCHESTRATOR"]
        P4["EXCEPTION HANDLER MODULE"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        O1["SUBMITTED FORM ENTRIES"]
        O2["RESULT SCORE LOG"]
        O3["ERROR LOG TXT"]
    end

    I1 --> P1 --> P3
    I2 --> P2 --> P3
    P3 --> O1
    P3 --> O2
    P4 --> P3
    P3 -.->|"ON FAILURE"| P4 --> O3
```

## 4.4 Selector Strategy Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["SELECTOR STRATEGY DECISION"]
    S2["DOES THE UI LAYOUT CHANGE"]
    S3["USE STATIC POSITIONAL SELECTORS"]
    S4["USE ANCHOR BASE ACTIVITY"]
    S5["USE FIND ELEMENT WITH XPATH"]
    S6["RPA CHALLENGE USES THIS"]

    S1 --> S2
    S2 -- "NO" --> S3
    S2 -- "YES" --> S4
    S2 -- "YES WITH COMPLEX LABELS" --> S5
    S4 --> S6
    S5 --> S6
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT** describe a workflow based on *recording* — this implies static selectors and loses 4–5 marks.
> - **Do NOT** omit the `On Element Appear` / `Delay` step. After `Submit`, the DOM re-renders; typing into a stale element throws a runtime exception.
> - **Do NOT** forget to mention `Try-Catch` with `Retry Scope`. A bot that crashes after Round 5 demonstrates zero industrial readiness.
> - **Do NOT** confuse **Anchor Base** with **Anchor** activity. The **Anchor Base** activity contains BOTH an anchor AND an action — the Anchor activity is only for finding the anchor element.
> - **Memorize the 7 fields** in the exact order: First Name, Last Name, Company Name, Role in Company, Address, Email, Phone Number. Examiners often ask "List the fields in the RPA Challenge data file."

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is the RPA Challenge and why is it considered a benchmark for RPA bots?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **RPA Challenge** is a public web-based benchmarking platform (rpachallenge.com) that tests an RPA bot's ability to fill a web form whose input fields are dynamically reordered after every submission. It is considered a benchmark because it simulates real-world enterprise scenarios where the UI is unstable (e.g., conditional form sections, rebranded portals) and forces the developer to use **dynamic selector strategies** like the `Anchor Base` activity. The platform provides a downloadable Excel file with 10 records and a final result page displaying the bot's score and time, making it a measurable, repeatable, and skill-proving test.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between static selectors and dynamic selectors in the context of the RPA Challenge.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **static selector** is generated during workflow recording and binds to absolute positional attributes in the DOM, such as `idx='5'` or `tableRow='3'`. It is fast but **breaks** the moment the DOM mutates. The RPA Challenge shuffles the field positions after every `Submit`, so a static selector fails within 1–2 rounds.

A **dynamic selector** is built manually (or via the `Anchor Base` activity) using **attribute-based queries** such as `aaname='First Name'` or XPath expressions like `//label[text()='Email']/following::input[1]`. It re-resolves the target element at runtime based on its **logical label**, not its physical position, and is therefore robust against the position-shuffling behavior of the challenge.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

> **[Internal Choice Instruction]** *Answer ANY ONE of the following:*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3, Modified]**

**(a)** Design a complete RPA workflow (UiPath or any KTU-prescribed platform) to solve the RPA Challenge. List the activities in the order they should appear, justify the use of dynamic selectors, and explain why recording is unsuitable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss exception handling and validation steps. What happens if the bot fails to find a field? How does `On Element Appear` help in post-submit DOM refresh? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

1. **Workflow Design (3 marks):**
   The workflow is structured as follows:
   - `Open Browser` to the challenge URL.
   - `Excel Application Scope` → `Read Range` to ingest the downloaded `.xlsx` file into `dt_Employees`.
   - `For Each Row` over `dt_Employees`:
     - For each of the 7 fields, an `Anchor Base` activity where the **Anchor** is the field's label (e.g., *"First Name"*) and the **Activity** is `Type Into` with the corresponding column value.
     - `Click` on the `Submit` button.
     - `On Element Appear` activity pointing to the first input field of the next form to wait for the DOM to stabilize.
   - `Get Text` on the result div → `Matches` regex to extract the score.
   - `Log Message` to print the final score.

2. **Justification for Dynamic Selectors (2 marks):**
   Static selectors rely on positional indices that change after every `Submit`. The challenge is intentionally designed to defeat recording-based workflows. Dynamic selectors use **attribute matching** (e.g., `aaname='First Name'`) which is invariant to position changes. The `Anchor Base` activity encapsulates this pattern efficiently by combining anchor search and target action in a single block.

3. **Why Recording is Unsuitable (2 marks):**
   Recording captures the absolute screen coordinates and DOM indices at the moment of recording. After Round 1, the form's internal layout mutates, making the recorded selectors stale. Furthermore, the recorded selector's `idx` attribute is brittle to even minor UI changes, causing the workflow to break on Round 2 or 3. A hand-crafted dynamic selector, on the other hand, is **self-healing** because it re-resolves at runtime.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

1. **Exception Handling Design (3 marks):**
   The `For Each Row` loop is wrapped in a **`Try-Catch` with `Retry Scope`** block:
   - **Try block:** The form-filling and submit logic.
   - **Catch block:** Catches `SelectorNotFoundException`, `BrowserOperationException`, and `IOException`.
   - **Retry Scope:** Configured for 3 retries with a 1-second delay between attempts.
   - **Final Catch:** If 3 retries fail, the bot logs the failure to a `.txt` file and sends an email via SMTP `Send SMTP Mail Message` activity, then proceeds to the next record gracefully.

2. **Why `On Element Appear` is Critical (4 marks):**
   After clicking `Submit`, the browser triggers a **full or partial DOM re-render** to display the next form with shuffled fields. The previous input elements are destroyed and recreated. If the bot attempts a `Type Into` on a now-stale element, it throws a `UiPath.Core.Activities.ElementOperationException`.
   The `On Element Appear` activity acts as a **synchronization barrier** — it pauses execution until a specific target element (e.g., the "First Name" label of the new form) appears in the DOM. This ensures that the subsequent `Anchor Base` activity finds a live, interactive element. The alternative — a hard `Delay` — is **non-deterministic** and may either over-wait (slowing the bot) or under-wait (causing race conditions).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of the `Anchor Base` activity. How does it work internally to handle dynamic UI elements in the RPA Challenge? Provide an example selector. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the `Anchor Base` activity with the `Find Element` + `Type Into` pattern. Under what circumstances would you prefer one over the other? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

1. **Definition of Anchor Base (2 marks):**
   The `Anchor Base` activity in UiPath is a composite activity that pairs a **stable reference element (Anchor)** with an **action element (Activity)**. The runtime engine first locates the anchor using its selector, then searches the surrounding region of the DOM for the action element using its own selector, and finally executes the action. This pattern is ideal for forms where the position of input boxes is volatile but their associated labels are stable.

2. **Internal Working (3 marks):**
   - Step 1: UiPath evaluates the anchor's selector (e.g., `aaname='Email'`) and finds the matching label element in the DOM.
   - Step 2: It defines a **search region** around the anchor (default: same parent container, configurable in properties).
   - Step 3: It searches within this region for the action element (e.g., the `INPUT` tag with `type='text'`).
   - Step 4: Once the action element is found, it performs the configured activity (e.g., `Type Into`) on it.
   - This decoupling of "what to find" from "where to find it" makes the workflow **position-invariant**.

3. **Example Selector (2 marks):**
   For the **"Phone Number"** field:
   - **Anchor selector:** `<webctrl aaname='Phone Number' tag='LABEL' />`
   - **Action selector:** `<webctrl tag='INPUT' />` (within the anchor's parent)
   - The combined result: the bot always finds the input box *next to* the label "Phone Number," regardless of whether it is the 1st, 4th, or 7th field in the rendered form.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

| Aspect | Anchor Base | Find Element + Type Into |
|---|---|---|
| **Ease of Use** | Higher — UI-driven configuration | Lower — requires XPath/selector expertise |
| **Flexibility** | Limited to a single anchor-action pair | High — can chain multiple operations programmatically |
| **Reusability** | Can be saved as a reusable component | Often embedded inline in the workflow |
| **Performance** | Slightly faster (single search) | Slightly slower (two activity calls) |
| **Error Recovery** | Built-in retry within the activity | Must be explicitly coded with `Retry Scope` |

1. **Prefer Anchor Base when (3 marks):**
   - The form has **distinct, stable text labels** for each field (as in the RPA Challenge).
   - You want a **no-code / low-code** approach that is easy to maintain.
   - The action is a simple `Type Into` or `Click` with no complex logic.

2. **Prefer Find Element + Type Into when (4 marks):**
   - The field labels are **dynamic or i18n-translated** (e.g., labels change based on user language).
   - You need to perform **multiple actions** on the resolved element (e.g., `Type Into` followed by `Get Text` for validation).
   - The form has **overlapping or duplicate labels** that require XPath predicates like `[1]` or `[last()]`.
   - You need to **store the element** in a variable for use elsewhere in the workflow.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This**

- [ ] The **RPA Challenge** is a web app at `rpachallenge.com` with a **10-row Excel file** and a form with **7 fields** whose positions shuffle after every submit.
- [ ] The 7 fields are: **First Name, Last Name, Company Name, Role in Company, Address, Email, Phone Number**.
- [ ] **Recording the workflow is the WRONG approach** — it produces static selectors.
- [ ] The **CORRECT approach** uses the **`Anchor Base` activity** with attribute-based selectors.
- [ ] Use **`For Each Row`** to iterate the DataTable (data-driven execution).
- [ ] Use **`On Element Appear`** to synchronize the bot with post-submit DOM refresh.
- [ ] Use **`Try-Catch` with `Retry Scope`** for industrial-grade robustness.
- [ ] Use **`Get Text` + `Regex Match`** to extract the final score from the result page.
- [ ] The benchmark **average per-round time** for a top-tier bot is **< 1.5 seconds**.
- [ ] **Static selectors** = `idx`, `tableRow`, `tableCol` based → **avoid**.
- [ ] **Dynamic selectors** = `aaname`, `innertext`, attribute-based → **use**.
- [ ] The **Anchor Base** activity encapsulates the anchor + action pattern in a single block.
- [ ] The **`Find Element`** pattern is preferred for complex, i18n, or duplicate-label scenarios.
- [ ] The **performance formula** is $\bar{t} = \frac{1}{N}\sum t_k$ with $N = 10$.
- [ ] The **success rate formula** is $S = \frac{N_{\text{passed}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$.
- [ ] **KTU marks distribution:** Selector design (4), workflow logic (4), exception handling (3), result validation (3).
- [ ] **Always** mention that the bot must **self-validate** via `Get Text` on the result div.
- [ ] **Always** mention that selectors are **runtime-resolved**, not hardcoded.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Image, Text and Advanced Citrix Automation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Image, Text and Advanced Citrix Automation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Image Automation in RPA **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** *Image Automation* in Robotic Process Automation is a computer-vision-driven interaction technique where the software bot identifies and manipulates on-screen UI elements by comparing pixel patterns against stored reference images, rather than relying on DOM selectors, accessibility APIs, or XML element attributes. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** Image automation becomes mandatory when working inside virtualized environments (Citrix, VMware Horizon, RDP), legacy terminal emulators (mainframes, AS/400), or any application whose UI is rendered as a streamed bitmap without exposing a selector tree. **Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for First-Time Readers:** Think of Image Automation as **recognizing your friend in a crowded railway station by looking at their face**. You do not interrogate the railway database or ask for an Aadhaar ID — you simply *look at pixels of light* (eyes, nose, smile) and match it to your memory. RPA bots do exactly the same: they take a screenshot of a tiny region of the screen and compare it pixel-by-pixel to a reference image saved during design time. If the match is above a confidence threshold, the bot "sees" the button and clicks it. **Key Technical Constants & Metrics:** - **Default Confidence Threshold:** **0.8 (80%)** in UiPath; range **0.0 to 1.0**. - **WaitForReady:** *INTERACTIVE* / *COMPLETE* / *NONE*. - **Accuracy Factor (UiPath Computer Vision):** dynamically calculated, default **0.8**. - **Image Format supported:** PNG (lossless, mandatory for reference images). - **CV Engine backends:** *Basic* (legacy pixel-based), *Modern* (CNN-based — UiPath's bundled CV), *Custom Endpoint* (user-trained model). --- ## 1.2 Text Automation in RPA **Formal Definition:** *Text Automation* is the umbrella term for RPA activities that **extract, validate, search, type, manipulate, or compare textual data** appearing on the screen, inside PDF documents, or within image-based screen regions. It encompasses three sub-disciplines: *Native Text Scraping*, *Full-Text / Visible-Text extraction*, and *OCR (Optical Character Recognition)*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Text Automation matters in KTU exam context:** Bots often need to read data they did not generate — invoices, claim numbers, policy IDs, ledger amounts — without a clean database. Text automation is the bridge between unstructured visual data and structured robotic decision-making. **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine you are filling out a bank form in a language you do not understand. You use a **translation app that points at a word on the paper and tells you what it means**. Text Automation is that translation app: it points at characters on the screen and converts them into machine-readable strings that the bot can store in an Excel cell, compare against a database, or pass to another activity. **Core Sub-Techniques:** - **Screen Scraping** — extract text directly from the rendering tree of the application (fastest, 100% accurate). - **OCR Engines** — Google Tesseract, Microsoft MODI, Abbyy FineReader, UiPath OmniPage. - **Anchor Base Activity** — couples an UI selector with a nearby text/element anchor to disambiguate visually similar elements. --- ## 1.3 Advanced Citrix Automation **Formal Definition:** *Advanced Citrix Automation* refers to the family of RPA techniques specifically engineered to drive **virtualized desktop environments** (Citrix XenApp/XenDesktop, VMware Horizon, Azure Virtual Desktop, Amazon WorkSpaces) where the underlying application's selectors, accessibility tree, and DOM are not exposed to the host operating system. The UI is presented to the bot as a streaming bitmap inside a remote desktop window. **Conceptual Analogy:** Driving a **remote-controlled toy car while watching it through a CCTV camera** is precisely what Citrix automation feels like. The bot cannot reach into the application's internals (no selectors, no API hooks). It can only see pixels, press physical-looking keyboard shortcuts, and hope the cursor lands on the right icon. Therefore, Citrix automation is built from a **hybrid toolkit** of image recognition + OCR + native keyboard automation + computer-vision-based element detection. > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Misconception:** "Citrix automation is just clicking images slowly." *Incorrect.* Modern Advanced Citrix Automation uses the **UiPath Computer Vision neural engine** which builds a *synthetic selector tree* from the screen bitmap, allowing selector-like reliability even inside a pixel stream. **Key CV Activities in Citrix:** - **CV Click, CV Type Into, CV Get Text, CV Element Exists, CV Screen Scope** — all powered by the *Modern CV Engine*. - **Keyboard Shortcuts:** `{TAB}`, `{ENTER}`, `{Alt+F4}`, `{Ctrl+Shift+S}` — hardware-level key injection. - **Hotkey Trigger Scope:** event-driven keypresses for trigger automation. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 When to Use What — Decision Matrix | Application Type | Recommended Technique | Fallback Technique | Reasoning | |---|---|---|---| | Native Windows / Web with full DOM | **Standard Selector** | Image / Text | Selectors are 10× faster & deterministic | | Java / SAP GUI scripting enabled | **UI Automation Selector** | Image | Accessibility APIs are exposed | | Virtualized (Citrix / VMware) | **Computer Vision** | Image + OCR hybrid | No selector tree available | | Legacy Terminal (3270/5250) | **Terminal Session Activities** | Screen scraping + keyboard | TN3270E / TN5250 protocols | | PDFs (text layer present) | **Read PDF Text** | OCR if scanned | Text layer gives 100% fidelity | | Scanned PDFs / Images | **OCR Engine** | Manual data entry | Only pixels available | | SAP GUI scripting disabled | **Image Automation** | SAP BAPI / Excel upload | Common bank/enterprise scenario | --- ## 2.2 Image Automation — Core Activities & Properties | Activity | Purpose | Critical Property | |---|---|---| | **Click Image** | Click a UI element identified by image | `Accuracy`, `Timeout`, `WaitForReady` | | **Find Image** | Locate coordinates of image, return `Rectangle` | Returns *ImageFound* / *Position* | | **Hover Image** | Move mouse without clicking | Used for menus / tooltips | | **Wait Image Vanish** | Block until reference image disappears | Loading-screen synchronization | | **Drag & Drop Image** | Drag from source image to target image | File upload scenarios | | **Highlight Image** | Debug-only visual annotation | Developer aid | **Step-by-Step Operational Logic:** 1. **Capture** — developer takes a 24×24 to 200×200 PNG snapshot of the target element. 2. **Anchor Setup** — optional reference image used to handle screen scaling. 3. **Match** — runtime engine slides the reference across the screen, computing similarity. 4. **Threshold Check** — if `MatchScore >= Accuracy`, treat as found. 5. **Action Execution** — perform click, hover, drag, or wait. 6. **Retry Loop** — re-attempt for `Timeout` duration with `Delay` between tries. --- ## 2.3 Text Automation — Three Extraction Paradigms $$\text{Native Scraping} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Speed} \gg \text{Portability} \;\gg\; \text{OCR Scraping}$$ | Paradigm | Engine | Speed | Accuracy | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Full Text** | Application's own accessibility | Fastest (ms) | **100%** | Web, modern WinForms | | **Visible Text** | UI Automation API | Fast | 99% | WPF, Qt apps | | **OCR** | Tesseract / OmniPage / Google Vision | Slow (200–800 ms) | 85–98% | Citrix, scanned docs | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Quick-Recall:** Always scrape natively first. Only switch to OCR when the application refuses to expose its text model. OCR is **CPU-intensive** and can drop to **60–70% accuracy** on low-resolution screenshots. **The Anchor Base Activity (Disambiguation Engine):** When two text fields look identical (e.g., multiple "Amount" columns in a tabular SAP view), a plain selector will throw *AmbiguousMatchException*. Anchor Base solves this by pairing: - A **primary UI element** (the target to act on). - An **anchor element** (a uniquely-located nearby label or icon). The bot finds the anchor first, computes a relative offset, then acts on the primary — eliminating ambiguity geometrically. --- ## 2.4 Advanced Citrix Automation — Architecture Layers | Layer | Function | Tools/Activities | |---|---|---| | **L1 — Window Layer** | Locate the Citrix receiver window | `Attach Window`, `Activate` | | **L2 — Vision Layer** | Perceive buttons/fields as objects | `CV Screen Scope`, `CV Click` | | **L3 — Keyboard Layer** | Inject keystrokes for speed | `Type Into`, `Send Hotkey` | | **L4 — Data Layer** | Extract structured data | `CV Get Text`, `CV Screen Scraping` | | **L5 — Synchronization Layer** | Handle network lag | `Wait Image Vanish`, `Delay`, `Retry` | --- ## 2.5 Computer Vision Engine — The Modern Backbone The **UiPath Modern Computer Vision Engine** is a convolutional neural network trained on 1.5M+ UI screenshots. It performs: - **Element Localization** — bounding boxes. - **Type Classification** — button / text-box / dropdown / checkbox. - **State Detection** — enabled / disabled / focused. - **OCR fallback** — integrated Tesseract 4.x with LSTM. **Required Setup:** - **Modern CV Server URL:** `https://cv.uipath.com/` (cloud) **or** on-prem Docker container. - **API Key:** issued per tenant. - **Scaling Factor:** must be set if host DPI ≠ client DPI (common 1.0 / 1.25 / 1.5 / 2.0). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Field Utility:** Computer-vision-driven RPA is now the standard for **BPO process automation** (insurance claims, medical billing, mortgage processing) where back-office systems run inside Citrix farms. India's IT/BPM industry (TCS, Infosys BPM, Wipro) runs millions of such transactions daily. # 3. Step-by-Step Workflow Derivations ## 3.1 Worked Workflow — Image-Based Login (Citrix App) **Scenario:** A bot must log in to a legacy ERP rendered inside Citrix Receiver. There are no selectors. **Step 1 — Capture Reference Images** (Design Time) ``` 1. Login screen is opened. 2. Developer uses UiExplorer → "Indicate Element" → drags cursor to draw a rectangle around the Username textbox. 3. UiPath saves "username_field.png" (e.g. 180×30 PNG). 4. Same for Password field, OK button, error toast. ``` **Step 2 — Configure the Click Image Activity** ``` Properties: Image = "username_field.png" Accuracy = 0.9 // 90 % match threshold Timeout = 30000 // 30 seconds DelayBetweenScreenshots = 200 // ms WaitForReady = INTERACTIVE ``` **Step 3 — Runtime Execution Path** The runtime engine executes the following algorithmic sequence: $$\text{Step A:} \quad t = 0, \quad \text{cap} = 30000 \text{ ms}$$ $$\text{Step B:} \quad \text{screenshot}_t = \text{CaptureDesktopWindow}(\text{Citrix Receiver handle})$$ $$\text{Step C:} \quad \text{score}_{x,y} = \frac{\sum_{i,j} I_{\text{ref}}(i,j) \cdot I_t(x+i,\, y+j)}{\sqrt{\sum I_{\text{ref}}^2 \cdot \sum I_t^2}} \quad \text{(Cosine Similarity)}$$ $$\text{Step D:} \quad (x^*, y^*) = \arg\max_{x,y} \text{score}_{x,y}$$ $$\text{Step E:} \quad \text{if } \text{score}_{x^*,y^*} \ge 0.9 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{found} = \text{True}; \;\text{else } t \leftarrow t + 200, \text{ goto Step B}$$ $$\text{Step F:} \quad \text{MoveCursor}(x^* + w/2,\; y^* + h/2)$$ $$\text{Step G:} \quad \text{MouseDown}() \;|\; \text{MouseUp}()$$ > **Explanation of each transition:** > - **Step A** initializes the search clock and the timeout budget. > - **Step B** captures a fresh bitmap of the Citrix window (not the entire desktop, to avoid multi-monitor noise). > - **Step C** computes the *cosine similarity* between the reference patch and every candidate region. Mathematically, this is the normalized cross-correlation — robust against brightness scaling. > - **Step D** finds the peak-match coordinates. > - **Step E** enforces the **confidence threshold**; if unmet, the loop retries after a 200 ms pause. > - **Step F–G** translate the *visual coordinate* into a hardware cursor event. **Step 4 — Compose Full Workflow** ``` Sequence: ├── Attach Window "Citrix Receiver" │ ├── Click Image "username_field" (Accuracy 0.9) │ ├── Type Into CurrentWindow ("admin_user") │ ├── Click Image "password_field" │ ├── TypeSecure CurrentWindow (Credential asset) │ ├── Click Image "ok_button" │ └── Wait Image Vanish "loading_spinner" └── Log Message "Login complete" ``` --- ## 3.2 Worked Workflow — OCR-Based Invoice Extraction **Step 1 — Launch application & open invoice.** **Step 2 — Indicate the invoice number region** using `CV Get Text`. ``` Properties: CVSelector = "invoiceNumber " // CV-engine generated selector OCR Engine = Tesseract 4 (LSTM) Scale = 1.0 Language = eng Output → variable invoiceNum (String) ``` **Step 3 — Validate extracted text using a Regex Match.** $$\text{Regex Pattern} = \texttt{INV\text{-}2024\text{-}\textbackslash d\{6\}}$$ $$\text{isValid} = \text{Regex.IsMatch}(\text{invoiceNum},\;
