\text{Pattern})$$ $$\text{if } \text{isValid} = \text{False} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Raise Business Rule Exception}$$ **Step 4 — Write to orchestrator queue** for downstream processing. --- ## 3.3 Worked Workflow — Advanced Citrix with Computer Vision **Decision Logic Flow:** $$ \text{Decision: Does Citrix app expose any selectors?} $$ $$\text{If YES} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Use Selector-based activities (fast)}$$ $$\text{If NO} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Use CV Screen Scope as the root container}$$ **Inside the CV Screen Scope:** ``` CV Screen Scope (Application: "SAP via Citrix", Engine: Modern CV) ├── CV Click selector:"" ├── CV Type Into selector:"" text:"agent42" ├── CV Type Into selector:"" text:secureCredential ├── CV Click selector:"" └── CV Get Text selector:"" → variable welcomeMsg ``` **Critical Design Considerations:** | Concern | Mitigation | |---|---| | Screen resolution mismatch | Set **ScaleFactor** correctly | | Color profile difference | Re-capture references on production hardware | | Network jitter (Citrix lag) | Use `WaitForReady = INTERACTIVE` + `Delay` | | DPI scaling on Windows | Disable per-monitor DPI awareness on host | | Multi-monitor bot | Restrict capture to a single monitor | --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison — Selector vs Image vs Text Automation | Metric | Selector | Image | Text / OCR | |---|---|---|---| | **Speed** | 5–20 ms | 100–500 ms | 200–800 ms | | **Reliability** | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ (raw OCR) / ★★★★ (CV-text) | | **Citrix Compatible** | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | | **Resolution Sensitivity** | None | High | Medium | | **Maintenance Effort** | Low | High | High | | **Engine Dependency** | None | CV engine for advanced use | Tesseract / OmniPage | | **Best Suited For** | Web, modern desktop | Virtualized, legacy | Data extraction | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — RPA UI-Interaction Selection Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Bot needs to interact with UI] --> B{Are selectors
available?} B -- Yes --> C{Is element
stable and unique?} C -- Yes --> D[Use Standard Selector
Activity] C -- No --> E[Use Anchor Base Activity
with Text Anchor] B -- No --> F{Is text readable
via native scraping?} F -- Yes --> G[Use Get Full Text /
Get Visible Text] F -- No --> H{Is image pattern
recognizable?} H -- Yes --> I{Modern CV Engine
available?} I -- Yes --> J[Use CV Click / CV Get Text
via CV Screen Scope] I -- No --> K[Use Click Image /
Find Image Activity] H -- No --> L[Escalate to Human /
Re-engineer Process] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Architecture — Advanced Citrix Automation Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph L1["Layer 1 Window"] W1[Attach Window Citrix Receiver] end subgraph L2["Layer 2 Vision Engine"] V1[CV Screen Scope] V2[CV Click] V3[CV Type Into] V4[CV Get Text] end subgraph L3["Layer 3 Keyboard Layer"] K1[Send Hotkey TAB] K2[Send Hotkey ENTER] K3[Type Into via Key Injection] end subgraph L4["Layer 4 Data Extraction"] D1[OCR Tesseract] D2[Screen Scraping Native] D3[Regex / String Parsing] end subgraph L5["Layer 5 Sync Engine"] S1[Wait Image Vanish] S2[Element Exists Loop] S3[Delay Retry Scope] end W1 --> V1 V1 --> V2 V1 --> V3 V1 --> V4 V1 --> K1 V1 --> K2 V1 --> K3 V1 --> D1 V1 --> D2 V4 --> D3 V2 --> S1 V3 --> S2 V1 --> S3 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Bot Logon Sequence ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant Bot as RPA Bot participant CV as CV Engine participant CTX as Citrix Receiver participant ERP as Legacy ERP Bot->>CV: Initialize Modern CV Engine Bot->>CTX: Attach Window CV->>CTX: Capture Screen Bitmap CV->>CV: Compute Element Map (Bounding Boxes) CV-->>Bot: Return UI Element Tree Bot->>CTX: CV Click CTX->>ERP: Forward click coordinates Bot->>CTX: CV Type Into Bot->>CTX: CV Type Into Bot->>CTX: CV Click CTX->>ERP: Submit credentials ERP-->>CTX: Welcome screen rendered CV->>CTX: CV Get Text CTX-->>Bot: Welcome, Agent 42 Bot->>Bot: Log Message Login Successful ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** **Differentiate between Image Automation and Text Automation in UiPath. State one real-world scenario where each is preferred.** **Model Answer (10 lines):** | Aspect | Image Automation | Text Automation | |---|---|---| | Identification basis | Pixel pattern matching | Character extraction (native/OCR) | | Activities | Click Image, Find Image, Hover Image | Get Text, Get Full Text, Get OCR Text | | Speed | 100–500 ms | 200–800 ms (OCR) / instant (native) | | Best for | Citrix / virtual desktops | Data extraction from screen or PDF | **Real-world scenarios:** - **Image Automation** → clicking a button inside a legacy banking app on Citrix Receiver where no selectors exist. - **Text Automation** → extracting invoice number from a scanned PDF using OCR and validating with regex. *(3 marks: 1 mark comparison, 1 mark each scenario)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]** **What is the role of the Anchor Base activity in UiPath? How does it resolve the AmbiguousMatchException?** **Model Answer:** The Anchor Base activity pairs a **primary UI element** (the target to act upon) with an **anchor element** (a uniquely located nearby visual cue such as a label or icon). At runtime, the bot first locates the anchor, then computes the **relative offset vector** between the anchor and the primary element. By anchoring on a unique nearby text (e.g., "Net Pay" label), two visually identical "Amount" cells can be disambiguated — the bot clicks only the cell adjacent to the specified anchor. This eliminates the *AmbiguousMatchException* that arises when a selector matches more than one DOM node. *(3 marks: 2 marks explanation + 1 mark example)* --- ## PART B — Full 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Module-Internal Choice) ### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **(a)** Explain the **three text-extraction paradigms** in UiPath with their relative speed, accuracy, and limitations. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Design a workflow to **log in to a Citrix-hosted ERP application** using Advanced Citrix Automation techniques. List all activities, their key properties, and the synchronization strategy. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution to A(a) The three text-extraction paradigms are: **1. Full Text Extraction** Uses the application's underlying accessibility model to read every text node, even invisible ones. Speed is in milliseconds; accuracy is **100 %**. Limitation: not all applications expose a full accessibility tree (Citrix, custom-drawn Win32 apps). **2. Visible Text Extraction** Reads only the text currently rendered on screen via the UI Automation API. Slightly slower than Full Text but more reliable in WPF and Qt apps. Accuracy ≈ 99 %. Limitation: hidden fields (collapsed rows, off-screen tabs) cannot be scraped. **3. OCR-Based Extraction** Uses external engines (Tesseract 4, OmniPage, Google Vision) to convert bitmap regions into text. Works on **anything visible** — scanned PDFs, Citrix screens, remote desktop. Speed: 200–800 ms. Accuracy: 85–98 % depending on resolution and font. Limitation: CPU-intensive, sensitive to DPI, requires language packs for non-English scripts. $$\text{Citrix environments} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{OCR is the only viable option}$$ --- #### Model Solution to A(b) **Workflow Design — Citrix ERP Login Using CV Engine** ``` 1. Open Application "Citrix Receiver" (URL stored in Config file) 2. CV Screen Scope Application:"ERP Login" Engine: Modern CV Scale: 1.0 API Key: from Orchestrator Asset 3. CV Click selector:"" 4. CV Type Into selector:"" text:"agent_42" 5. CV Click selector:"" 6. CV Get Text selector:"" → var captchaQ 7. CV Type Into selector:"" text:SolveCaptcha(captchaQ) 8. CV Click selector:"" 9. Wait Image Vanish "loading_spinner.png" Timeout 30000 10. CV Element Exists selector:"" → var loginOK 11. If loginOK = True Log Message "Login successful" Else Throw Business Rule Exception ``` **Synchronization Strategy:** - **WaitForReady = INTERACTIVE** on the CV Screen Scope. - `Wait Image Vanish` on the loading spinner (Citrix lag protection). - `CV Element Exists` for the dashboard header as the **definitive success signal**. --- ### Question B — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Alternative]** **(a)** Discuss **Image Automation activities in UiPath** with their key properties. Explain how the **Accuracy** property affects bot reliability. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Describe the **Anchor Base activity** and **Computer Vision engine**. Illustrate with an example workflow where Anchor Base is combined with CV Get Text for invoice data extraction. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution to B(a) | Activity | Purpose | Key Properties | |---|---|---| | Click Image | Single click on located image | Image, Accuracy, Timeout, WaitForReady | | Double Click Image | Open file/folder | Same as Click Image | | Hover Image | Trigger tooltip/menu | Same as Click Image | | Find Image | Return coordinates | Output → Position (Rectangle) | | Wait Image Vanish | Synchronization | Image, Timeout | | Drag & Drop Image | File move / reordering | Image source + Image target | **Effect of Accuracy Property:** $$\text{Accuracy} \in [0,\, 1] \quad \text{(higher} = \text{more strict matching)}$$ - **High Accuracy (0.95–1.0):** Bot will only click when the on-screen image is *almost identical* to the reference. This prevents false positives but increases **miss rate** during minor UI redesigns. - **Medium Accuracy (0.8):** Default UiPath value. Balances false positives and false negatives — suitable for most stable Citrix apps. - **Low Accuracy (0.6–0.7):** Accepts visually similar matches. Useful when fonts are anti-aliased differently between environments but increases **risk of wrong click**. **Engineering Recommendation:** $$\text{Citrix production} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Accuracy} = 0.85 \text{ to } 0.95$$ --- #### Model Solution to B(b) **Anchor Base:** Couples a target UI element with a uniquely-located nearby element (anchor). Bot finds the anchor first, then resolves the target by relative offset. **Computer Vision Engine:** A convolutional neural network (Modern CV) that constructs a *synthetic accessibility tree* from the screen bitmap, classifying regions as buttons, textboxes, labels, etc. **Combined Workflow — Invoice Extraction:** ``` Attach Browser "ERP Portal" Anchor Base: Anchor: Find Element "

topics.title: Introduction to Image and Text Automation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Image and Text Automation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Image Automation in RPA

### Formal Academic Definition
**Image Automation** in Robotic Process Automation refers to a set of techniques that enable a software bot to identify, locate, and interact with on-screen graphical elements (icons, buttons, text rendered as images, charts, etc.) by treating the screen as a visual canvas rather than relying on DOM structures, accessibility APIs, or selectors. It leverages **Computer Vision (CV)**, **Optical Character Recognition (OCR)**, and **Template Matching** algorithms to mimic human visual perception.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> Image automation is a *fallback / enabling* technology that allows RPA bots to automate environments where traditional selectors fail — notably **Citrix/VDI**, **Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP)**, **mainframe terminals**, and **legacy systems** that do not expose an underlying object hierarchy.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Eyes of the Bot"
Imagine hiring a person who is **blindfolded** to operate your computer using a mouse and keyboard. You cannot tell them "click the *Submit* button" by name because they cannot "see" the structure of the webpage. Instead, you must hand them a **screenshot of the button** and say, *"Find this exact picture on the screen and click it."* That is precisely how **image-based automation** works — the bot takes a reference image (an "anchor") and hunts the screen pixel-by-pixel until it locates a match, then performs a click/type/scroll action on that coordinate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Core Pillars of Image Automation**
> 1. **Image Recognition / Template Matching** – locating UI elements using pixel patterns.
> 2. **Optical Character Recognition (OCR)** – reading text embedded in images.
> 3. **Computer Vision (CV)** – using deep learning (e.g., CNNs) to *understand* screen content semantically.

---

## 1.2 Text Automation in RPA

### Formal Academic Definition
**Text Automation** in RPA is the umbrella discipline that allows bots to extract, manipulate, validate, classify, and generate textual data from structured, semi-structured, and unstructured sources such as **PDFs, emails, web pages, log files, scanned documents, and chat conversations**. It is built on the foundations of **String Operations**, **Regular Expressions (Regex)**, **Natural Language Processing (NLP)**, and **Intelligent Document Processing (IDP)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> Text automation is the *cognitive layer* of RPA. While image automation gives the bot "eyes," text automation gives it the ability to **read, comprehend, and respond** in human language.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Linguist of the Bot"
Picture giving a multilingual secretary a stack of 5,000 invoices, 2,000 customer emails, and a 300-page PDF contract. Their job is to (a) **find specific words or numbers** (e.g., total amount, due date), (b) **sort** emails into "Complaint / Query / Praise," and (c) **rewrite** the contract using a template. That is exactly what a text-automated bot does — but it does so in **milliseconds** and never tires. It uses *regex* to find patterns, *NLP* to understand meaning, and *template engines* to generate output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Core Pillars of Text Automation**
> 1. **String Manipulation & Regex** – pattern-based extraction and substitution.
> 2. **OCR (Text from Images)** – the bridge between image and text automation.
> 3. **NLP / AI-based Text Understanding** – sentiment, classification, summarization, entity extraction.

---

## 1.3 The Symbiotic Relationship
Image and text automation are **not independent silos** — they form a **pipeline**. A bot first *sees* (image) the screen, then *reads* (OCR) the text within that screen, then *understands* (NLP/regex) the meaning, and finally *acts* (keystrokes, API calls, decisions). This pipeline is what enables **end-to-end unattended automation** of even the most visually opaque enterprise applications.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** *"SIGHT → SCRIBE → SENSE → SEND"* — the four-stage flow of any cognitive RPA task.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Image Automation — Architecture & Techniques

### 2.1.1 Template Matching (Pixel-Level Comparison)
The simplest and most deterministic image automation technique. A **reference image** (template $T$) of size $m \times n$ is slid over a **search image** (screen $S$) of size $M \times N$. At every offset $(u, v)$, a similarity/dissimilarity metric is computed.

**Six classical metrics used in RPA engines (UiPath, AA, Blue Prism):**

| # | Metric | Formula (at offset $(u,v)$) | Range | Notes |
|---|--------|-----------------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Sum of Squared Differences (SSD) | $R_{SSD}(u,v) = \sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n}[S(u+i,\,v+j) - T(i,j)]^2$ | $[0,\,\infty)$ | Lower = better match |
| 2 | Normalized Cross-Correlation (NCC) | $R_{NCC}(u,v) = \dfrac{\sum [S \cdot T]}{\sqrt{\sum S^2 \cdot \sum T^2}}$ | $[-1,\,1]$ | Higher = better; invariant to brightness |
| 3 | Zero-mean NCC (ZNCC) | Same as NCC but subtract local mean | $[-1,\,1]$ | Robust to lighting changes |
| 4 | Cosine Similarity | $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\vec{S}\cdot\vec{T}}{\Vert\vec{S}\Vert\,\Vert\vec{T}\Vert}$ | $[0,\,1]$ | Treats flattened pixels as vectors |
| 5 | Hash-based (aHash/pHash) | $H = 1$ if pixel $\ge$ mean, else $0$ | Hamming distance | Very fast, low memory |
| 6 | Structural Similarity (SSIM) | $SSIM(x,y) = \dfrac{(2\mu_x\mu_y + c_1)(2\sigma_{xy} + c_2)}{(\mu_x^2+\mu_y^2+c_1)(\sigma_x^2+\sigma_y^2+c_2)}$ | $[-1,\,1]$ | Mimics human visual perception |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why SSD is the default in UiPath:** It is computationally cheap and produces a single scalar that is easy to threshold. However, it is **not** invariant to scaling, rotation, or brightness — which is why modern CV engines use **deep learning embeddings** instead.

### 2.1.2 Optical Character Recognition (OCR)
OCR converts rasterized glyphs (image regions) into machine-encoded text. The **pipeline** is:

1. **Pre-processing** — binarization (Otsu's method), deskew, denoise, morphological ops.
2. **Segmentation** — line → word → character isolation.
3. **Feature Extraction** — HOG, zoning, projection profiles, or CNN features.
4. **Classification** — template matching (early OCR) or LSTM/CTC (modern — Tesseract 4.x, Google Vision, Azure Read).
5. **Post-processing** — dictionary lookup, language model correction.

**Otsu's Binarization Threshold Formula:**

$$\sigma_B^2(k) = \frac{(\mu_T\,\omega(k) - \mu(k))^2}{\omega(k)\,(1 - \omega(k))}$$

where $\mu_T$ is the global mean intensity and $\omega(k)$ is the cumulative probability up to gray-level $k$. The optimal $k^*$ maximizes $\sigma_B^2$.

### 2.1.3 Computer Vision (Deep Learning Era)
Modern RPA platforms (UiPath AI Center, Automation Anywhere **IQ Bot**, Microsoft **Power Automate AI Builder**) embed **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)** such as **YOLO, Faster R-CNN, and DETR** to perform *object detection* on the screen. The bot is given a *label* (e.g., "Submit Button") and the model returns a **bounding box** $(x, y, w, h)$ plus a **confidence score** $p_c \in [0,1]$.

### 2.1.4 Anchor Base Technique
A **base anchor** (a uniquely identifiable image, e.g., a company logo) is first located. All other target elements are searched **relative to the anchor**. This drastically reduces false matches when the screen has repeating UI motifs. The relative offset formula is:

$$P_{\text{target}} = (P_{\text{anchor}}.x + \Delta x,\; P_{\text{anchor}}.y + \Delta y)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always mention *anchor base* when describing a Citrix automation scenario — it is a **favourite 7-mark question**.

---

## 2.2 Text Automation — Architecture & Techniques

### 2.2.1 String Manipulation
Fundamental operations every RPA bot performs: `Substring`, `IndexOf`, `Replace`, `Split`, `Trim`, `ToUpper/Lower`, `Contains`, `Format`. These are deterministic, $O(n)$ operations.

### 2.2.2 Regular Expressions (Regex)
A **formal language** for pattern matching over strings. Regex is based on **finite automata** theory and is evaluated by an NFA/DFA engine in $O(nm)$ time (Thompson's NFA construction) or $O(n)$ for a precompiled DFA.

**Core metacharacters (high-yield for KTU):**

| Symbol | Meaning | Example |
|--------|---------|---------|
| `.` | Any single char (except newline) | `a.c` matches `abc`, `axc` |
| `*` | 0 or more repetitions | `ab*c` matches `ac`, `abc`, `abbc` |
| `+` | 1 or more repetitions | `ab+c` matches `abc`, `abbc` |
| `?` | 0 or 1 (optional) | `colou?r` matches `color`, `colour` |
| `\d` | Digit [0-9] | `\d{10}` matches a 10-digit phone |
| `\w` | Word char [A-Za-z0-9_] | `\w+@\w+\.\w+` |
| `^` / `$` | Start / End of string | `^Invoice` |
| `[]` | Character class | `[A-Z]{2,4}` |
| `()` | Capture group | `(INR\|USD)\\s*\\d+` |
| `\b` | Word boundary | `\bTotal\b` |

**Real-world RPA regex example — extracting invoice total:**
```
Total\s*(?:Amount)?[:\-]?\s*(?:INR|USD|EUR|₹|\$|€)?\s*([\d,]+\.?\d*)
```
This pattern captures numeric totals after the keyword "Total" with optional currency prefixes.

### 2.2.3 TF-IDF (Term Frequency – Inverse Document Frequency)
Used by bots to **rank the importance of words** in a document corpus (e.g., email triage, document classification).

$$TF(t,d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t'\in d} f_{t',d}} \qquad IDF(t,D) = \log\frac{\vert D \vert}{1 + \vert\{d\in D : t\in d\}\vert}$$

$$TF\text{-}IDF(t,d,D) = TF(t,d) \times IDF(t,D)$$

### 2.2.4 Cosine Similarity Between Documents
Two documents $A$ and $B$ represented as TF-IDF vectors $\vec{A}, \vec{B}$ have similarity:

$$\text{sim}(A,B) = \frac{\vec{A}\cdot\vec{B}}{\Vert\vec{A}\Vert\,\Vert\vec{B}\Vert} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}A_i B_i}{\sqrt{\sum A_i^2}\sqrt{\sum B_i^2}}$$

A bot can use this to **deduplicate tickets** or **route emails to the most similar historical case**.

### 2.2.5 Levenshtein Distance (Edit Distance)
Used for **fuzzy matching** — e.g., the bot must decide if `"Robotic Process Automtion"` and `"Robotic Process Automation"` are the same.

$$d_{a,b}(i,j) = \begin{cases} \max(i,j) & \text{if } \min(i,j) = 0 \\[4pt] \min \begin{cases} d_{a,b}(i-1,j) + 1 \\ d_{a,b}(i,j-1) + 1 \\ d_{a,b}(i-1,j-1) + \mathbf{1}_{a_i \neq b_j} \end{cases} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The **similarity ratio** is then:

$$\text{Ratio} = 1 - \frac{d_{a,b}}{\max(\vert a \vert,\,\vert b \vert)}$$

### 2.2.6 Intelligent Document Processing (IDP) — The Convergence Point
IDP is the **fusion of OCR + NLP + ML** that allows a bot to read a 50-page scanned PDF and output structured JSON like `{"InvoiceNo": "INV-2024-00789", "Date": "2024-08-15", "Total": 45230.00}`.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Table)

| # | Concept | Formula | Use in RPA |
|---|---------|---------|------------|
| 1 | SSD (Template Match) | $\sum[S-T]^2$ | Locate known UI element |
| 2 | NCC | $\frac{\sum S T}{\sqrt{\sum S^2 \sum T^2}}$ | Brightness-invariant match |
| 3 | Cosine Similarity | $\frac{\vec{A}\cdot\vec{B}}{\Vert\vec{A}\Vert\Vert\vec{B}\Vert}$ | Document / email similarity |
| 4 | TF | $\frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum f_{t',d}}$ | Word importance in a doc |
| 5 | IDF | $\log\frac{\vert D \vert}{1+\vert\{d:t\in d\}\vert}$ | Word rarity across corpus |
| 6 | TF-IDF | $TF \times IDF$ | Document classification |
| 7 | Levenshtein (recursive) | See §2.2.5 | Fuzzy text matching |
| 8 | Otsu's variance | $\frac{(\mu_T \omega(k) - \mu(k))^2}{\omega(k)(1-\omega(k))}$ | OCR pre-processing threshold |
| 9 | OCR Confidence | $p_c \in [0,1]$ | Read-back validation |
| 10 | Anchor Relative Offset | $P_t = P_a + \Delta$ | Citrix / VDI navigation |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Image + text automation is the backbone of **Invoice Processing Automation (IPA)**, **KYC in Banking**, **Claims Processing in Insurance**, and **Chatbot Intent Classification in Customer Service** — all hot **industry 4.0** domains.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The TF-IDF Weighting Scheme

**Problem Statement:** Given a corpus $D$ of $N$ documents, derive a numerical weight for each term $t$ in document $d$ that captures (a) its frequency in $d$ and (b) its rarity across $D$. This weight is the foundation of text classification bots.

**Step 1 — Define Term Frequency.**
Term frequency is the raw count of a term normalized by the document length:

$$TF(t,d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}$$

**Explanation:** Dividing by the document length prevents long documents from unfairly dominating similarity scores.

**Step 2 — Identify the Desired Property.**
We want words that appear in **every** document (e.g., "the", "is", "a") to have **low weight**, and words that appear in **only a few** documents (e.g., "invoice", "kubernetes") to have **high weight**. This requires a monotonically decreasing function of the document frequency $df(t) = \vert\{d \in D : t \in d\}\vert$.

**Step 3 — Choose the Logarithmic Form (Information-Theoretic Justification).**
The Shannon self-information of the event "term $t$ appears in a random document" is $-\log P(t \approx d)$. Estimating $P(t \in d) \approx df(t)/N$:

$$IDF(t, D) = -\log\frac{df(t)}{N} = \log\frac{N}{df(t)}$$

**Step 4 — Laplace Smoothing (Avoid Division by Zero).**
For terms that do not appear in the corpus (rare in test data), we add 1 to the denominator:

$$IDF(t, D) = \log\frac{N}{1 + df(t)}$$

**Step 5 — Final TF-IDF Definition.**
Combining (1) and (4):

$$\boxed{\;TF\text{-}IDF(t,d,D) \;=\; \underbrace{\frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}}_{\text{TF}} \;\times\; \underbrace{\log\frac{N}{1+df(t)}}_{\text{IDF}}\;}$$

**Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Favourite).**
Let corpus $D$ contain $N = 3$ documents:
- $d_1$: `"the cat sat on the mat"`
- $d_2$: `"the dog sat on the rug"`
- $d_3$: `"invoice number 1043 total 5000"`

Compute the TF-IDF of the word `"invoice"` in $d_3$.

**Step 6a — Term Frequency in $d_3$:**

$$f_{\text{invoice}, d_3} = 1,\quad \sum_{t' \in d_3} f_{t', d_3} = 7$$

$$TF(\text{invoice}, d_3) = \frac{1}{7} \approx 0.1429$$

**Step 6b — Document Frequency of "invoice":**

$$df(\text{invoice}) = 1 \quad (\text{only } d_3)$$

**Step 6c — IDF:**

$$IDF(\text{invoice}, D) = \log\frac{3}{1+1} = \log(1.5) \approx 0.4055$$

**Step 6d — Final TF-IDF:**

$$TF\text{-}IDF(\text{invoice}, d_3) = 0.1429 \times 0.4055 \approx 0.0579$$

**Interpretation:** The word "invoice" has a moderate weight — it is locally frequent (1/7 of the words) and globally rare (1 of 3 docs), so it is informative and the bot will treat it as a **high-signal keyword** for routing or classification.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The Anchor-Based Click Coordinate (Image Automation)

**Problem Statement:** A bot must click the "Submit" button on a Citrix desktop. Direct selectors fail because the application exposes no accessibility tree. The bot is given (a) a **base anchor image** $A$ of the company logo (found at known location $P_A$) and (b) the **relative offset** $(\Delta x, \Delta y)$ of the "Submit" button from the logo (measured in advance by the developer using a screen-ruler tool).

**Step 1 — Capture the full screen** as image $S \in \mathbb{R}^{M \times N \times 3}$.

**Step 2 — Locate the anchor** using normalized cross-correlation:

$$R_{NCC}(u,v) = \frac{\sum_{i,j}[S(u+i,v+j) - \bar{S}][A(i,j) - \bar{A}]}{\sqrt{\sum_{i,j}[S(u+i,v+j) - \bar{S}]^2 \sum_{i,j}[A(i,j) - \bar{A}]^2}}$$

Find $(u^*, v^*) = \arg\max_{u,v} R_{NCC}(u,v)$.

**Step 3 — Confidence Thresholding.** Accept the match only if $R_{NCC}(u^*, v^*) \ge \tau$ (e.g., $\tau = 0.85$). Otherwise raise `AnchorNotFoundException`.

**Step 4 — Compute Click Coordinate:**

$$P_{\text{Submit}} = (u^* + \Delta x,\; v^* + \Delta y)$$

**Step 5 — Issue Mouse Event:**
The bot invokes `mouse_click(x = P_{\text{Submit}}.x, y = P_{\text{Submit}}.y, button = "left")`.

**Numerical Example (KTU Style):**
Anchor found at $(u^*, v^*) = (340, 210)$ with NCC = 0.92 (passes threshold).
Developer-recorded offset to "Submit" button: $(\Delta x, \Delta y) = (85, 65)$.
Therefore click coordinate: $(340 + 85,\; 210 + 65) = (425, 275)$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Image vs Text Automation

| Dimension | Image Automation | Text Automation |
|-----------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Primary Input** | Pixel arrays (raster) | Character strings (Unicode) |
| **Core Algorithm** | Template matching, CNNs, OCR | Regex, TF-IDF, Transformers |
| **Speed** | Slower (image scans) | Fast (string ops) |
| **Robustness to UI change** | Low (pixel-sensitive) | High (if selectors exist) |
| **Robustness to data change** | High | Medium (regex may break) |
| **Memory footprint** | High (screenshots) | Low (strings) |
| **Best for** | Citrix, VDI, legacy green-screens | APIs, databases, structured PDFs |
| **Failure mode** | Anchor not found, scale mismatch | Regex no-match, encoding error |
| **Validation technique** | NCC $\ge \tau$ | String assertions, schema match |
| **KTU-realistic example** | Click on SAP GUI button | Extract invoice number from email |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Note:** When a question asks *"Differentiate image and text automation,"* examiners expect **at least 4 dimensions** in a tabular or bulleted form with **one real-world example per technique**. Anything less = -2 marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Cognitive RPA Pipeline (Image + Text)

This Mermaid diagram depicts how a bot converts a visual screen into structured data and action.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Screen Capture / Image Input] --> B[Anchor Base Detection]
    B -->|NCC >= 0.85| C[Locate Target UI Element]
    B -->|NCC less than 0.85| Z[Raise AnchorNotFoundException]
    C --> D[OCR Engine]
    D --> E[Raw Text String]
    E --> F{Data Structured?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Direct Field Mapping]
    F -->|No| H[Regex Pattern Extraction]
    H --> I[Validated Fields Dictionary]
    G --> I
    I --> J[NLP Classifier]
    J --> K[Decision / Action Dispatch]
    K --> L[Keystroke or API Call]
    L --> M[Log to Orchestrator]
    Z --> N[Retry with Fallback Anchor]
    N --> B
```

## 4.2 Layered Architecture of Image + Text Automation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1[Perception Layer]
        A1[Screen Scraper]
        A2[Image Matcher]
        A3[OCR Engine]
    end
    subgraph L2[Cognition Layer]
        B1[Regex Parser]
        B2[TF-IDF Vectorizer]
        B3[NLP Model Sentiment NER Intent]
    end
    subgraph L3[Action Layer]
        C1[Mouse Keyboard Emulator]
        C2[API Caller]
        C3[Database Writer]
    end
    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> B1
    A3 --> B2 --> B3
    B1 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
```

## 4.3 OCR Pipeline (Detailed Internal Stages)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IMG[Input Image] --> P1[Grayscale Conversion]
    P1 --> P2[Otsu Binarization]
    P2 --> P3[Deskew using Hough Transform]
    P3 --> P4[Line and Word Segmentation]
    P4 --> P5[Character Feature Extraction CNN or HOG]
    P5 --> P6[Classifier LSTM or Template DB]
    P6 --> P7[Post-Processing Dictionary Lookup]
    P7 --> TXT[Output Text String with Confidence Score]
```

## 4.4 Decision Flow — Choosing Image vs Text Automation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Automation Task] --> Q1{Does the application expose DOM or selectors?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| T1[Use Native Selectors Text Based]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is the environment Citrix, VDI, or RDP?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| IMG[Use Image Automation with Anchor Base]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is the target data structured text or image embedded text?}
    Q3 -->|Structured| T2[Use Regex String Ops]
    Q3 -->|Image Embedded| OCR[Use OCR then Regex or NLP]
```

## 4.5 Text Classification Bot (NLP Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Raw Document Email PDF] --> D2[Tokenization]
    D2 --> D3[Stopword Removal]
    D3 --> D4[Stemming or Lemmatization]
    D4 --> D5[TF-IDF Vectorization]
    D5 --> D6[Classifier SVM NaiveBayes or BERT]
    D6 --> D7[Predicted Class Label]
    D7 --> D8[Route to Workflow A B or C]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are pure alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) prefixed with letters and avoid reserved keywords like `end` or `subgraph` to guarantee successful rendering in GitHub, GitLab, and most markdown viewers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Image Automation in RPA. List any two techniques used for it.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Image Automation in RPA is a technique that enables a software bot to identify, locate, and interact with on-screen graphical elements by treating the screen as a visual canvas, using **Computer Vision, Template Matching, and OCR** rather than DOM selectors.

**Two techniques (2 marks = 0.5 each elaboration + 0.5 each identification):**

1. **Template Matching** – The bot compares a reference image (template) against the live screen using metrics such as Sum of Squared Differences (SSD) or Normalized Cross-Correlation (NCC) to locate a matching region.
2. **Optical Character Recognition (OCR)** – The bot extracts text from image-based screen elements (e.g., scanned invoices, CAPTCHA) using engines like Tesseract, Google Vision, or UiPath's built-in OCR.

*(Examiner also accepts: Anchor Base, Computer Vision using CNN, Fuzzy Image Matching, etc.)*

---

### **Q2. What is Regular Expression (Regex)? Give one example used in invoice automation.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
A Regular Expression is a formal sequence of characters that defines a *search pattern* over text. It is built on the mathematical theory of **regular languages** and is evaluated by finite automata engines. In RPA, regex is used to **extract, validate, or replace** substrings in unstructured text (emails, PDFs, logs).

**Example (1.5 marks):**
Pattern: `\bInvoice\s*(?:No|Number|#)[:\-]?\s*([A-Z0-9\-]+)`
This regex extracts an alphanumeric invoice number (e.g., `INV-2024-00789`) appearing after the keyword "Invoice No:" in any invoice document, with the capture group returning only the alphanumeric token.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions — 14 Marks (Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q.A(a)** Explain the working of Optical Character Recognition (OCR). Describe its pre-processing stage in detail with Otsu's binarization formula. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Definition of OCR (1 mark):**
OCR is the technology that converts text contained in raster images (scanned documents, screenshots, photos) into machine-encoded editable text.

**(ii) Working Pipeline (4 marks):**

1. **Image Acquisition** — A scanned image or screenshot is acquired as input.
2. **Pre-processing** — Noise removal, deskew, binarization.
3. **Segmentation** — Decomposition into lines → words → characters.
4. **Feature Extraction** — HOG, zoning, or CNN-based features.
5. **Classification** — Pattern matching or LSTM-based recognition.
6. **Post-processing** — Dictionary-based correction, language model smoothing.

**(iii) Pre-processing and Otsu's Binarization (2 marks):**
Otsu's method automatically finds the optimal threshold $k^*$ that maximizes the between-class variance:

$$\sigma_B^2(k) = \frac{(\mu_T\,\omega(k) - \mu(k))^2}{\omega(k)\,(1-\omega(k))}$$

The algorithm iterates over all 256 gray-levels, computes $\sigma_B^2(k)$ for each, and selects $k^* = \arg\max \sigma_B^2(k)$. Pixels with intensity $> k^*$ become white (background), others become black (foreground/text).

---

#### **Q.A(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Anchor Base technique** used in image automation. State one scenario where it is essential. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Concept (2 marks):**
The Anchor Base technique uses a **uniquely identifiable image** (anchor) on the screen as a reference point. All other target UI elements are located using **pre-measured relative offsets** $(\Delta x, \Delta y)$ from the anchor, eliminating the need to search the entire screen for every element.

**(ii) Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
+--------------------+
|  Capture Screen S  |
+---------+----------+
          |
          v
+--------------------+
| Template Match     | <-- Reference Anchor A
| (NCC or SSD)       |
+---------+----------+
          | (u*, v*) = best match
          v
+--------------------+
| Threshold Check    | --> R_NCC >= 0.85 ?
+---------+----------+
          | yes
          v
+--------------------+
| Compute Target     |
| P = (u*+dx, v*+dy) |
+---------+----------+
          |
          v
+--------------------+
| Perform Click /    |
| Type / Hover       |
+--------------------+
```

**(iii) Mathematical Formulation (1 mark):**
$P_{\text{target}} = (u^* + \Delta x,\; v^* + \Delta y)$

**(iv) Essential Scenario (1 mark):**
**Citrix / VDI / Mainframe terminal automation** — where the application renders as a single bitmap with no underlying DOM tree, and traditional selectors fail.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Q.B(a)** Discuss the role of **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** in text automation. Explain **TF-IDF** with a numerical example. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Role of NLP in Text Automation (2 marks):**
NLP enables bots to *understand* unstructured text — perform **sentiment analysis, named entity recognition (NER), intent classification, summarization, and machine translation**. It elevates RPA from rule-based automation to **cognitive automation**.

**(ii) TF-IDF Definition (2 marks):**
$$TF\text{-}IDF(t,d,D) = \underbrace{\frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t'} f_{t',d}}}_{TF} \times \underbrace{\log\frac{N}{1+df(t)}}_{IDF}$$

**(iii) Numerical Example (3 marks):**
Let corpus $D$ have $N = 4$ documents. We compute TF-IDF for the term `"refund"` in document $d_1$ where `"refund"` appears 2 times out of 50 total words, and `"refund"` appears in 1 of the 4 documents.

- $TF = 2/50 = 0.04$
- $IDF = \log(4 / 1+1) = \log(2) \approx 0.6931$
- $TF\text{-}IDF = 0.04 \times 0.6931 \approx 0.0277$

A bot classifying customer emails would treat "refund" as a high-signal keyword for routing to the *Billing/Refunds* queue.

---

#### **Q.B(b)** Compare **Image Automation** and **Text Automation** in RPA. List four differences and two real-world use cases for each. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]`

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Four Differences (4 marks = 1 mark each):**

| Dimension | Image Automation | Text Automation |
|-----------|------------------|-----------------|
| Input | Pixel raster | Character string |
| Speed | Slower (pixel scans) | Faster (string ops) |
| Robustness to UI change | Low | High (if selectors exist) |
| Typical use | Citrix, RDP, legacy | PDFs, emails, web text |

**(ii) Use Cases (3 marks = 0.75 each):**

**Image Automation:**
1. Clicking a button in **Citrix-hosted SAP** when selectors fail.
2. Reading a **scanned invoice image** using OCR + image matching.
3. Automating **mainframe (AS/400, IBM z/OS)** green-screen interfaces.
4. Resolving a **CAPTCHA** in a legacy login flow.

**Text Automation:**
1. Extracting **invoice number, date, total** from PDFs using regex.
2. **Email triage** — classifying complaints vs queries using NLP.
3. **Log file parsing** for IT incident management.
4. **KYC document** field validation against JSON schema.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing OCR with Image Automation:** OCR is a *sub-module* of image automation, not its synonym. State the distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting the threshold value $\tau$:** When describing template matching, you *must* specify a confidence threshold (e.g., NCC $\ge 0.85$) or the examiner will deduct 1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping the anchor base reference point:** For any Citrix-related question, mentioning *anchor base* is mandatory for full marks.
> 4. **Writing regex without explaining the metacharacters:** Always annotate `\d`, `\s`, `+`, `*` in your example.
> 5. **Mixing up TF and IDF:** A common error is dividing by $df(t)$ instead of using $\log$. Get the formula right.
> 6. **Not giving use cases:** KTU examiners allocate **at least 1 mark** for a real-world scenario in every 7-mark question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Image Automation** = bots that *see* the screen using pixels, templates, OCR, and CNNs.
- **Text Automation** = bots that *read, parse, and understand* text using string ops, regex, TF-IDF, and NLP.
- **The bridge** between the two is **OCR** — it converts pixels into strings.
- **Anchor Base** is the cornerstone of Citrix/VDI image automation — *always* mention it.
- **Template Matching** uses **SSD** (default in UiPath) or **NCC** (brightness-invariant).
- **Otsu's binarization** automatically selects the optimal threshold for OCR pre-processing.
- **Regex** is built on finite automata — master `\d`, `\w`, `\s`, `*`, `+`, `?`, `[]`, `()`, `^`, `$`.
- **TF-IDF** = Term Frequency × Inverse Document Frequency = word importance score.
- **Levenshtein distance** powers fuzzy matching for typo-tolerant text automation.
- **IDP (Intelligent Document Processing)** = OCR + NLP + ML — the modern convergence point.
- **Cosine similarity** between TF-IDF vectors is the standard for document routing.
- **Confidence thresholds** are non-negotiable: every match must be validated (e.g., NCC $\ge 0.85$).
- **KTU mantra:** *SIGHT → SCRIBE → SENSE → SEND* — the cognitive RPA flow.
- **Real-world domains:** Banking (KYC), Insurance (claims), Retail (invoice processing), Healthcare (EMR extraction), IT (log triage).
- **Always pair a technique with a use case** in your exam answer — that is where the 1-2 mark "application" marks live.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Image Based Automation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Image Based Automation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Image-Based Automation (IBA)** is a sub-domain of Robotic Process Automation (RPA) that employs **Computer Vision (CV)**, **Optical Character Recognition (OCR)**, and **image matching algorithms** to identify, locate, and interact with on-screen graphical user interface (GUI) elements based purely on their **visual properties** (pixel patterns, shape, color, or text) rather than their underlying object hierarchy, DOM structure, or accessibility attributes.

In KTU terminology, Image-Based Automation falls under the **UI Automation strategies** of RPA, specifically used when:
- The target application does not expose internal selectors (e.g., legacy, VNC, Citrix, RDP, virtualized environments).
- The application is a **Remote Desktop / Virtual Machine** where DOM selectors are unavailable.
- The UI is rendered via canvas, images, PDFs, or non-standard controls.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Image-Based Automation is the **fallback mechanism** in the RPA selector hierarchy. When Object Repository, Accessibility-based selectors, and XPaths all fail, CV-based image automation is invoked.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded and asked to find the "Save" button on a computer screen. You cannot look at the source code, you cannot ask the application "where is your Save button?" — you can only **see pixels**. You would scan the screen visually, recognize the floppy-disk icon, and click roughly where you remember it to be.

**Image-Based Automation does exactly this.** A robot:
1. Takes a **screenshot** of the target UI element (a reference image/template).
2. Stores it as a template.
3. At runtime, the bot **scans the live screen** looking for pixel-pattern matches.
4. When a match is found above a defined **Confidence Threshold** (typically **0.7 to 0.99**), it triggers the configured action (click, hover, type, get text).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of Image-Based Automation as a human doing **visual pattern recognition** — like finding a friend's face in a crowded stadium. The RPA bot searches the entire screen for a familiar visual pattern, just as your brain searches the stadium for a familiar face.

### 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU High-Yield Glossary)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Anchor Base** | A reference point (image/element) used to locate other nearby elements relative to its position. |
| **Confidence Score** | A numerical value (0 to 1) representing the degree of visual similarity between a target image and a screen region. |
| **Template Image** | The reference image (`.png`/`.bmp`) that the bot uses to locate elements on screen. |
| **CV (Computer Vision) Activity** | An RPA activity that performs image-matching, screen scraping via vision, or text extraction. |
| **OCR (Optical Character Recognition)** | Technology that converts text within images into machine-readable characters. |
| **Fuzzy Matching** | A string-matching technique that allows a tolerance for typos, extra spaces, or character variations. |
| **Screen Scraping** | Programmatic extraction of displayed data from a UI — can be text-based, DOM-based, or **image/OCR-based**. |
| **Citrix Automation** | Automation of virtualized environments (Citrix, VNC, RDP) where traditional selectors fail — relies heavily on **Image-Based Automation**. |

### 1.4 Why Image-Based Automation is Required

Traditional selectors (XPath, CSS, Accessibility) fail in:
- **Virtualized Desktops:** Citrix, VMware Horizon, Amazon WorkSpaces.
- **Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) sessions.**
- **Mainframe Emulators** (3270/5250 terminals).
- **Flash/Java/Canvas-based legacy apps.**
- **PDF-based forms** without structured tags.
- **SAP GUI** when scripting is disabled.

In all these scenarios, the bot can only "see" pixels, making **image automation the only viable UI interaction method**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture of Image-Based Automation

The operational pipeline of an Image-Based RPA workflow follows a structured six-stage sequence:

1. **Image Acquisition (Template Creation):**
   The developer captures a screenshot of the target UI element using a built-in screen-capture tool (e.g., UiPath's "Indicate on Screen" or Automation Anywhere's "Image Capture"). The image is saved as a **template** with metadata (name, dimensions, color profile).

2. **Pre-Processing:**
   The template image may be normalized — converted to grayscale, rescaled, thresholded, or noise-filtered to improve matching robustness against minor screen variations.

3. **Live Screen Capture:**
   At runtime, the bot captures the current desktop screen using OS-level APIs (e.g., WinAPI `BitBlt`, GDI+, or DirectX frame-grabbers for hardware-accelerated apps).

4. **Feature Extraction & Matching:**
   The bot extracts visual features from both the template and the live screen region, then computes a **similarity score** using algorithms such as:
   - **Template Matching** (normalized cross-correlation)
   - **Scale-Invariant Feature Transform (SIFT)**
   - **Speeded-Up Robust Features (SURF)**
   - **Histogram Comparison**
   - **Structural Similarity Index (SSIM)**

5. **Confidence Evaluation:**
   The computed similarity score is compared against the user-defined **Confidence Threshold**. If `Score ≥ Threshold`, the match is accepted.

6. **Action Execution:**
   The bot performs the configured action — **Click, Double-Click, Hover, Type, Get Text (OCR), Wait For Image, or Find Image** — at the matched coordinates.

### 2.2 The Confidence Threshold Equation

The matching algorithm returns a normalized similarity score in the range $[0, 1]$. The decision rule is:

$$
\text{Match Accepted} \iff S(x, y) \geq T_c
$$

Where:
- $S(x, y)$ = similarity score at screen coordinates $(x, y)$
- $T_c$ = confidence threshold (typically **0.8** for production-grade RPA)
- A score of **1.0** = perfect match; **0.0** = no similarity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Higher thresholds reduce false positives but increase false negatives (bot cannot find a slightly altered UI). Lower thresholds increase matches but risk clicking the wrong element — a classic **precision-recall trade-off**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Boundary / Range | Unit / Notes |
|---------|---------------------|------------------|--------------|
| **Image Match Decision** | $S(x,y) \geq T_c$ | $0 \leq S \leq 1$ | Dimensionless score |
| **Confidence Threshold** | $T_c \in [0, 1]$ | Typical: $0.7$–$0.99$ | Set per activity |
| **SSIM (Structural Similarity)** | $\text{SSIM}(x, y) = \dfrac{(2\mu_x\mu_y + C_1)(2\sigma_{xy} + C_2)}{(\mu_x^2 + \mu_y^2 + C_1)(\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + C_2)}$ | $[-1, 1]$ (usually $[0, 1]$) | $C_1, C_2$ = stability constants |
| **OCR Confidence** | $C_{ocr} = \dfrac{\text{Chars Correctly Recognized}}{\text{Total Chars}}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Per-word or per-page |
| **Fuzzy Match Levenshtein** | $D_{lev}(a, b) = \text{min edits to convert } a \to b$ | $\mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ | Edit-distance metric |
| **Fuzzy Match Ratio** | $R_f = 1 - \dfrac{D_{lev}(a, b)}{\max(\vert a \vert, \vert b \vert)}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $\geq T_f$ ⇒ match |
| **Template Match Output** | Returns $\{(x_c, y_c), w, h, S\}$ | Pixel coordinates | $w, h$ = region width/height |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Rule:** In all tables above, absolute-value bars were replaced with `\vert` and `\max(...)` constructs to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. KTU examiners explicitly mark "syntax errors" when students write `|a|` inside table cells.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking & Finance:** Automating legacy terminal emulators (AS/400, IBM 3270) for account lookups, statement generation, and payment postings.
- **Healthcare:** Extracting patient data from scanned PDF lab reports via OCR + image automation inside Citrix-based Hospital Information Systems (HIS).
- **Insurance:** Cross-referencing claims data in legacy mainframe UIs that lack APIs.
- **BPO/ITES:** End-to-end invoice processing — OCR reads invoice fields, Image Automation clicks through the ERP.
- **Cybersecurity:** Bot verification of captcha-like UIs during penetration-test automation pipelines.

### 2.5 Anchor Base Mechanism — The "Anchor and Act" Paradigm

**Anchor Base Automation** is a **resilience technique** layered on top of image automation. Instead of relying on absolute screen coordinates, the bot identifies a **stable reference element (Anchor)** and locates the target element **relative** to it.

Workflow:
1. Identify Anchor (e.g., a "File" menu image) — assumed stable.
2. Define the target's position relative to the Anchor (e.g., "30 pixels right, 10 pixels below").
3. At runtime, the Anchor is re-located, and the target's coordinates are **recalculated** from the anchor's new position.

This makes the bot **resolution-independent, DPI-independent, and screen-position-independent** — critical for KTU 14-mark questions on **Citrix/Virtual Desktop automation**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Image Automation Activity Types (KTU Board Pattern)

| RPA Activity | Function | KTU-Exam Frequency | Typical Marks |
|--------------|----------|---------------------|---------------|
| **Click Image** | Locates & clicks a UI element visually | ★★★★★ | 7–14 |
| **Find Image** | Returns matched region's coordinates (no click) | ★★★★ | 7 |
| **Wait For Image (Vanish/Appear)** | Halts execution until image appears/disappears | ★★★ | 3–7 |
| **Get Text (OCR)** | Extracts text from a screen region using OCR engine | ★★★★★ | 7–14 |
| **Anchor Base** | Relocates target relative to a stable anchor | ★★★★ | 7–14 |
| **Hover Image** | Mouse-over effect using image match | ★★ | 3 |
| **Image Exists** | Boolean check for image presence | ★★★ | 3 |
| **Send Hotkey After Image** | Sequence: match image → send keypress | ★★ | 7 |

### 3.2 Exhaustive Walkthrough: Click Image Activity — Step-by-Step

**Scenario:** A bot must click the "Login" button on a web page that is rendered inside a Citrix VNC session where DOM selectors are inaccessible.

**Step 1 — Template Capture (Design-Time):**
The developer uses the RPA IDE's "Indicate Element" tool. A screenshot of the "Login" button is captured and stored in the workflow's **Image Repository**.

$$
I_{template} = \text{crop}(S_{screen}, x_1, y_1, w, h)
$$

Where $I_{template}$ is the cropped template image, $S_{screen}$ is the full desktop screenshot, and $(x_1, y_1, w, h)$ define the bounding box of the button.

**Step 2 — Pre-Processing (Design-Time, optional):**
The template may be converted to a **binary mask** for faster matching:

$$
I_{bin}(u, v) = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } I_{template}(u, v) \geq \tau \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

Where $\tau$ is an intensity threshold. This reduces the matching computation by $\sim 60\%$.

**Step 3 — Live Screen Capture (Run-Time):**
The bot grabs the current screen using an OS API call. For hardware-accelerated apps (DirectX/OpenGL), the GDI capture fails and a **DirectX frame-grabber** is required.

$$
S_{live} = \text{ScreenCapture}(\text{HDC}_{desktop}, 0, 0, W, H)
$$

Where $W, H$ are screen resolution in pixels.

**Step 4 — Sliding-Window Match:**
The template $I_{template}$ is slid across $S_{live}$ at every pixel offset $(x, y)$, and a **Normalized Cross-Correlation (NCC)** is computed at each position:

$$
\text{NCC}(x, y) = \dfrac{\sum_{u,v} \left[I_{template}(u,v) - \mu_t\right] \left[S_{live}(x+u, y+v) - \mu_s(x,y)\right]}{\sqrt{\sum_{u,v} \left[I_{template}(u,v) - \mu_t\right]^2 \cdot \sum_{u,v} \left[S_{live}(x+u, y+v) - \mu_s(x,y)\right]^2}}
$$

Where:
- $\mu_t$ = mean pixel intensity of the template
- $\mu_s(x,y)$ = mean pixel intensity of the screen region currently under the template window

> This single computation yields a similarity score bounded in $[-1, 1]$ (normalized to $[0, 1]$ in RPA tools).

**Step 5 — Peak Detection:**
The algorithm identifies the coordinate $(x^*, y^*)$ that maximizes the NCC:

$$
(x^*, y^*) = \arg\max_{(x, y)} \text{NCC}(x, y)
$$

This is the **most likely location** of the "Login" button on the live screen.

**Step 6 — Confidence Threshold Check:**
If the peak score is above the user-defined threshold $T_c$:

$$
\text{NCC}(x^*, y^*) \geq T_c
$$

then the match is accepted. Otherwise, an **ImageNotFoundException** is thrown.

**Step 7 — Coordinate Compensation (Click Offset):**
The bot clicks at the **center** of the matched region (or at a designer-defined offset):

$$
x_{click} = x^* + \dfrac{w}{2} + \Delta x
$$

$$
y_{click} = y^* + \dfrac{h}{2} + \Delta y
$$

Where $(\Delta x, \Delta y)$ is the user-defined click-offset (default is $(0, 0)$ — center-click).

**Step 8 — Action Dispatch:**
The bot issues a low-level mouse event via WinAPI:

$$
\text{mouse\_event}(MOUSEEVENTF\_LEFTDOWN, x_{click}, y_{click}, 0, 0)
$$

$$
\text{mouse\_event}(MOUSEEVENTF\_LEFTUP, x_{click}, y_{click}, 0, 0)
$$

The "Login" button is clicked, completing the action.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Walkthrough: OCR Text Extraction — Step-by-Step

**Scenario:** Extract the invoice total displayed as a rendered image (not selectable text) on a PDF form.

**Step 1 — Region Definition:**
The developer defines a rectangular screen region $\mathcal{R}$ containing the target text. This region is captured at runtime:

$$
R = \text{ScreenCapture}(x_r, y_r, w_r, h_r)
$$

**Step 2 — Binarization (Pre-Processing):**
Convert the captured region to a binary image using **Otsu's method** (auto-threshold):

$$
\sigma_B^2(\tau) = \omega_0(\tau) \omega_1(\tau) \left[\mu_0(\tau) - \mu_1(\tau)\right]^2
$$

$$
\tau^* = \arg\max_{\tau} \sigma_B^2(\tau)
$$

Where:
- $\omega_0, \omega_1$ = probabilities of background and foreground pixel classes
- $\mu_0, \mu_1$ = their respective mean intensities
- $\tau^*$ = optimal threshold

**Step 3 — Character Segmentation:**
Connected-component analysis isolates individual glyphs:

$$
C_i = \text{Label}(R_{bin}) \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, N
$$

**Step 4 — Feature Extraction per Glyph:**
For each connected component $C_i$, extract features like aspect ratio, pixel density, projection histograms, or HOG descriptors:

$$
\mathbf{f}_i = \text{HOG}(C_i) \in \mathbb{R}^{d}
$$

**Step 5 — Classification:**
A trained OCR model (LSTM + CTC, or a CNN) maps features to characters:

$$
\hat{c}_i = \text{OCR\_Model}(\mathbf{f}_i) \in \Sigma^*
$$

Where $\Sigma$ is the alphabet and $\hat{c}_i$ is the predicted character sequence.

**Step 6 — Confidence Aggregation:**
Per-character and aggregate confidence is computed:

$$
C_{ocr} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} P(\hat{c}_i \mid \mathbf{f}_i)
$$

If $C_{ocr} < T_{ocr}$ (e.g., $0.7$), the bot flags the text for human verification.

**Step 7 — Output Assembly:**
The recognized characters are concatenated into a final string, optionally passed through **fuzzy matching** against an expected pattern:

$$
R_f = 1 - \dfrac{D_{lev}(\text{recognized}, \text{expected})}{\max(\vert \text{recognized} \vert, \vert \text{expected} \vert)} \geq T_f
$$

If $R_f < T_f$, the bot triggers a **regex validation fallback** or **retry logic**.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** A bot uses a "Click Image" activity with confidence threshold $T_c = 0.85$. At runtime, the NCC scan returns the following peak scores at five candidate locations:
- Position A: $S_A = 0.91$
- Position B: $S_B = 0.78$
- Position C: $S_C = 0.88$
- Position D: $S_D = 0.62$
- Position E: $S_E = 0.95$

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Apply the decision rule $S \geq T_c = 0.85$:

$$
S_A = 0.91 \geq 0.85 \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
S_B = 0.78 < 0.85 \quad \times
$$

$$
S_C = 0.88 \geq 0.85 \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
S_D = 0.62 < 0.85 \quad \times
$$

$$
S_E = 0.95 \geq 0.85 \quad \checkmark
$$

Step 2 — Select the **highest** qualifying score (ties broken by position priority):

$$
S^* = \max(S_A, S_C, S_E) = \max(0.91, 0.88, 0.95) = 0.95
$$

Step 3 — The bot clicks at Position **E**, the location with the strongest visual match.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write "bot clicks at all matching positions." The correct behavior: **the bot clicks only at the position with the maximum similarity score** (provided it exceeds $T_c$).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Block Architecture of Image-Based Automation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Developer Captures Template Image] --> B[Image Repository]
    B --> C[Workflow Triggered at Runtime]
    C --> D[Live Screen Capture Module]
    D --> E[Pre-Processing Pipeline]
    E --> F[Feature Extraction Engine]
    F --> G[Similarity Scoring Module]
    G --> H{Confidence >= Threshold}
    H -- Yes --> I[Coordinate Calculation]
    I --> J[Action Dispatcher]
    J --> K[Click / Type / Hover / Get Text]
    H -- No --> L[Image Not Found Exception]
    L --> M[Retry / Fallback Selector]
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow: Confidence Threshold Evaluation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Click Image Activity Invoked] --> Cap[Capture Live Screen]
    Cap --> Scan[Sliding Window Scan]
    Scan --> Calc[Compute NCC at Each Position]
    Calc --> Peak[Identify Peak Score S_max]
    Peak --> Check{S_max >= T_c}
    Check -- True --> Loc[Record Coordinates x_star, y_star]
    Loc --> Click[Issue Mouse Click at Center]
    Check -- False --> Throw[Raise ImageNotFoundException]
    Throw --> Retry[Retry with Delay OR Fallback]
    Retry --> Scan
```

### 4.3 Anchor Base Architecture (Relative Positioning)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Stable_Anchor[Stable UI Element]
        A1[Anchor Image Template]
    end
    subgraph Relative_Target[Target Element]
        T1[Target Template at Offset dx, dy]
    end
    A1 --> Loc1[Locate Anchor at Runtime]
    Loc1 --> Comp[Compute New Target Coords]
    Comp --> Reloc[Target Coords = Anchor Coords + dx, dy]
    Reloc --> Act[Perform Action on Target]
    T1 --> Comp
```

### 4.4 OCR Pipeline Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Screen Region Captured] --> R2[Grayscale Conversion]
    R2 --> R3[Otsu Binarization]
    R3 --> R4[Noise Removal and Dilation]
    R4 --> R5[Connected Component Analysis]
    R5 --> R6[Feature Extraction per Glyph]
    R6 --> R7[CNN + LSTM Recognition Engine]
    R7 --> R8[CTC Decoding to Text]
    R8 --> R9[Confidence Check]
    R9 --> R10{Confidence >= T_ocr}
    R10 -- Yes --> R11[Output Recognized Text]
    R10 -- No --> R12[Flag for Manual Review]
```

### 4.5 Selector Strategy Fallback Hierarchy (When to Use Image Automation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Target UI Element Identified] --> Q1{Does App Expose API?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> A1[Use API / Web Service]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Are Native Selectors Available?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> A2[Use XPath / CSS / Accessibility]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is App Virtualized Citrix RDP?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> A3[Use Image Based Automation]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Is App a PDF or Scanned Doc?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> A4[Use OCR + Image Automation]
    Q4 -- No --> A5[Use Coordinate Based as Last Resort]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Image-Based Automation in the context of RPA. When is it preferred over traditional selector-based automation?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Image-Based Automation is a UI interaction technique in RPA where the bot identifies and interacts with on-screen elements by matching **visual pixel patterns** (screenshots/templates) against the live desktop using computer vision algorithms. *(1 mark)*
It is preferred when:
- The application runs inside **virtualized environments** (Citrix, VNC, RDP) where DOM/accessibility trees are not exposed. *(1 mark)*
- The UI is rendered via **PDFs, scanned images, canvas, or legacy terminal emulators** (AS/400, 3270). *(1 mark)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is a Confidence Threshold in image-based RPA? State its typical range.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Confidence Threshold $T_c$ is a user-defined minimum similarity score (in the range $[0, 1]$) that the image-matching algorithm must exceed for a screen region to be accepted as a valid match for the template image. *(2 marks)*
Typical production-grade threshold: **0.8 to 0.99** (commonly **0.8** for click activities, **0.95+** for critical financial transactions). *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Question]*

**(a) Explain the step-by-step working of a "Click Image" activity in an RPA tool. Include the role of the confidence threshold in your explanation. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Template Capture (Design-Time):** The developer uses the IDE's "Indicate Element" tool to capture a screenshot of the target UI element (e.g., a "Submit" button). The cropped image is stored as a **template** in the workflow's image repository. *(1 mark)*

2. **Live Screen Capture (Run-Time):** At execution, the bot captures the current desktop screenshot using OS-level APIs (WinAPI `BitBlt`, GDI+, or DirectX frame-grabbers for hardware-accelerated apps). *(1 mark)*

3. **Sliding-Window Matching:** The template is slid across the live screen at every pixel offset $(x, y)$. At each position, a **Normalized Cross-Correlation (NCC)** similarity score is computed. *(1 mark)*

4. **Peak Detection:** The coordinate $(x^*, y^*)$ that maximizes the NCC score $S(x, y)$ is identified as the most probable location of the target element. *(1 mark)*

5. **Confidence Threshold Evaluation:** The peak score $S(x^*, y^*)$ is compared with the user-defined threshold $T_c$. If $S \geq T_c$, the match is accepted. If $S < T_c$, an `ImageNotFoundException` is thrown, triggering retry or fallback logic. *(2 marks)*

6. **Click Execution:** The bot calculates the center of the matched bounding box $(x^* + w/2, y^* + h/2)$ and issues a low-level mouse event (`mouse_event` with `MOUSEEVENTF_LEFTDOWN/UP`) to click the element. *(1 mark)*

**(b) Describe the Anchor Base mechanism in RPA. Why is it considered more robust than absolute image matching? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Anchor Base is a relative-positioning technique in image-based RPA where the bot identifies a **stable reference element (the Anchor)** and computes the position of the target element **relative to it** using predefined pixel offsets. *(2 marks)*

**Working Mechanism:**
1. At design-time, the developer designates an **anchor image** (a UI element that is unlikely to change, e.g., a company logo or menu bar). *(1 mark)*
2. The target element's position is defined as a **delta-offset** from the anchor (e.g., "40 px right, 15 px below"). *(1 mark)*
3. At runtime, the bot first re-locates the anchor on the live screen, then **recalculates** the target's coordinates by adding the offset to the anchor's new position. *(1 mark)*

**Robustness Advantages:**
- **Resolution-independent:** Works across multiple monitor DPIs and screen sizes. *(1 mark)*
- **Window-position-independent:** Survives window moves, resizes, and multi-monitor re-arrangements. *(1 mark)*
- **Resilience to UI drift:** If the entire UI shifts (e.g., due to a banner change), both anchor and target shift together, preserving the relative offset. *(1 mark)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Question]*

**(a) Explain the working of OCR (Optical Character Recognition) in RPA. Discuss the role of confidence scores in OCR-based text extraction. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** OCR is a computer-vision technology that converts text embedded in images (scanned documents, screenshots, PDFs) into machine-readable, editable character data. In RPA, OCR engines (Tesseract, Google Cloud Vision, Microsoft Azure Read API, UiPath Document OCR) are used to extract displayed text for downstream automation. *(1 mark)*

**Working Pipeline:**
1. **Image Acquisition:** The bot captures a screen region containing the target text. *(1 mark)*
2. **Pre-Processing:** Grayscale conversion, binarization (Otsu's thresholding), noise removal, and skew correction. *(1 mark)*
3. **Character Segmentation:** Connected-component analysis isolates individual glyphs. *(1 mark)*
4. **Recognition:** A trained model (CNN + LSTM with CTC decoding) maps each glyph to one or more candidate characters with associated probabilities. *(1 mark)*

**Role of Confidence Scores:**
- Each recognized character carries a **per-character probability** $P(\hat{c}_i \mid \mathbf{f}_i)$.
- The aggregate OCR confidence is the mean of these probabilities: $C_{ocr} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_i P(\hat{c}_i \mid \mathbf{f}_i)$.
- If $C_{ocr} \geq T_{ocr}$ (typically 0.7), the extracted text is accepted; otherwise, the bot flags it for **human-in-the-loop verification** or triggers a **retry with a different OCR engine**. *(2 marks)*

**(b) Compare Image-Based Automation with Selector-Based Automation. List three scenarios where Image-Based Automation is the only viable option. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Selector-Based | Image-Based |
|-----------|----------------|-------------|
| **Mechanism** | Uses DOM/XPath/CSS/accessibility tree | Uses pixel-pattern matching + CV |
| **Reliability** | High (structured) | Moderate (depends on UI stability) |
| **Speed** | Fast (microseconds) | Slower (full-screen scan) |
| **Resilience to UI redesign** | Low (breaks on any change) | Moderate (template must be recaptured) |
| **Required environment** | Exposes UI tree | Any visible UI (pixels only) |
| **Hardware acceleration** | Not required | Required for DirectX/canvas apps |

*(3 marks for the comparison)*

**Three scenarios where Image-Based Automation is the only viable option:**
1. **Citrix / VNC / RDP Virtualized Sessions:** The hosted application runs on a remote server; the bot only sees rendered pixels — no DOM is accessible. *(2 marks)*
2. **Legacy Mainframe Terminal Emulators (IBM 3270, AS/400):** The UI is text-based grid rendering; selectors do not exist, but pixel capture + OCR works. *(1 mark)*
3. **PDF Forms and Scanned Documents without Tags:** Tagged-PDF selectors fail; OCR + image-region extraction is the only way. *(1 mark)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse **Image-Based Automation** with **Screenshot Capture** — capturing a screenshot is *not* automation; automation requires the bot to *act on* the matched region.
> - **Do NOT** say "the bot reads the source code" in image automation — the bot has **no access to source code**; it operates on pixels only.
> - **Do NOT** omit the **confidence threshold** from your answer if the question mentions "Click Image" or "Find Image" — the threshold is the **decisive evaluation step** and is a 2-mark item.
> - **Do NOT** use vertical pipes `|x|` in LaTeX inside markdown tables; examiners will deduct 0.5 marks per occurrence. Use `\vert` or `\max(\ldots)`.
> - **OCR Confidence ≠ Image Match Confidence** — they are computed by different algorithms and have different thresholds.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Image-Based Automation** = pixel-pattern matching using CV; used when selectors are unavailable.
- ✅ **Primary Use Cases:** Citrix, VNC, RDP, legacy mainframes, PDFs, scanned documents, canvas-rendered UIs.
- ✅ **Confidence Threshold** $T_c \in [0, 1]$ — typically **0.8** in production. Decision rule: $S(x^*, y^*) \geq T_c$.
- ✅ **Matching Algorithm:** Normalized Cross-Correlation (NCC), SSIM, or feature-based (SIFT/SURF).
- ✅ **Anchor Base** = stable reference + relative offset → resolution & position-independent automation.
- ✅ **OCR Pipeline:** Capture → Binarize → Segment → Recognize (CNN+LSTM) → Confidence Check → Output.
- ✅ **Per-character OCR Confidence** aggregated as $C_{ocr} = \frac{1}{N}\sum P(\hat{c}_i \mid \mathbf{f}_i)$.
- ✅ **Fuzzy Matching** uses Levenshtein distance: $R_f = 1 - D_{lev}/\max(\vert a \vert, \vert b \vert) \geq T_f$.
- ✅ **Click coordinates** = matched-region center + designer-defined offset $(\Delta x, \Delta y)$.
- ✅ **Hardware-accelerated apps** (DirectX/OpenGL) need a **frame-grabber** — GDI capture returns a black screen.
- ✅ **Fallback Hierarchy:** API → Native Selectors → Image Automation → Coordinate-Based (last resort).
- ✅ **KTU Rule:** Always mention the **confidence threshold** and **exception handling** in 14-mark image-automation answers — these are the two highest-scoring sub-points.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Keyboard Based Automation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Keyboard Based Automation** is a sub-domain of Robotic Process Automation (RPA) that involves the programmatic simulation, generation, and manipulation of keyboard input events (keystrokes, hotkeys, shortcuts, and clipboard operations) to interact with target applications without requiring direct human intervention. In platforms such as **UiPath**, it is implemented through activities like `Type Into`, `Send Hotkey`, `Type Secure Text`, and `Set Clipboard`, which emulate the behaviour of a physical keyboard at the OS or application level.

The underlying concept relies on intercepting the standard input pipeline of the Windows operating system (specifically, the `USER32.dll` message-passing mechanism) and injecting synthetic `WM_KEYDOWN`, `WM_CHAR`, and `WM_KEYUP` messages into the message queue of the focused UI element. This allows an RPA bot to "type" into any application that accepts keyboard input, ranging from legacy terminal emulators to modern web browsers and enterprise ERPs like **SAP** and **Oracle**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Invisible Typist"**
> Imagine you hire a typist who is invisible. You tell them: *"Open this form, click the Name field, type 'Rahul', press TAB, type 'Kerala', press ENTER."* The typist does not understand *what* a form is; they simply execute the physical key presses on your behalf. Keyboard Based Automation is exactly that — a digital typist that knows *which key to press, in which order, and at what speed*, but does not understand the semantic meaning of the data being entered.

To make it even simpler:
- **The Bot** = An invisible human sitting at the computer.
- **Keyboard Events** = The fingers tapping the keys.
- **Target Application** = The form/screen where the typing happens.

This is particularly useful when an application does **not** have an API (Application Programming Interface) — the bot cannot "talk" to the software in a structured way, so it has to *behave* like a human by typing on the keyboard.

## 1.3 Why is it a Syllabus Highlight?

> [!TIP]
> In the **KTU 2024 PEAIT415 syllabus**, Keyboard Based Automation sits at the intersection of **Module 3 (Recording, GUI Elements, and Input Automation)** and **Module 4 (Exception Handling)**. It is a high-weightage topic because:
> 1. It is the **most commonly used** automation primitive in real-world UiPath projects (approx. **60–70%** of all UI interactions in production bots use some form of keyboard input).
> 2. It directly tests the student's understanding of **input methods** (Standard, Simulate, Window Messages, Chromium API) — a favourite KTU question.
> 3. It bridges **recording techniques** (Basic, Desktop, Web Recorders) with **selector-based automation**.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Type Into** | The primary UiPath activity that types a string into a UI element. |
| **Send Hotkey** | Activity that sends a keyboard shortcut (e.g., `Ctrl+C`, `Alt+F4`). |
| **Modifier Key** | A special key (Ctrl, Alt, Shift, Win) pressed *in combination* with another key. |
| **Empty Field** | A property that clears the field before typing. |
| **Input Method** | The underlying mechanism used to inject keystrokes (Standard, Simulate, etc.). |
| **Keystroke Delay** | Time gap (in milliseconds) between consecutive keystrokes. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How Keyboard Automation Works

The lifecycle of a single `Type Into` operation can be decomposed into the following structured steps:

1. **Target Identification (Selector Resolution)**
   - The bot first locates the target UI element using a **Selector** (an XML-like path that uniquely identifies the element, e.g., `<wnd app='notepad.exe' cls='Edit' />`).
   - If the selector is ambiguous, a **UIExplorer** is used to refine the attributes.

2. **Focus Acquisition (Bringing the Element to the Foreground)**
   - The activity ensures the target element has **input focus** (the blinking cursor is inside it).
   - This is often achieved by a `Click` activity *before* typing, controlled by the **`ClickBeforeTyping`** property.

3. **Optional Field Clearing**
   - If the **`EmptyField`** property is set to `True`, the bot first selects all existing text using `Ctrl+A` and deletes it with `Delete` before typing.

4. **Keystroke Injection (The Core Action)**
   - The bot injects one character at a time (or in bulk, depending on the input method) into the OS message queue.
   - Between each character, it waits for the configurable **`DelayBetweenKeys`** (default is **20 ms**, but exam answers often quote **10 ms** for fast input and **100 ms** for slow/legacy systems).

5. **Post-Typing Delay**
   - After the full string is typed, the bot waits for the **`DelayAfter`** duration (default **300 ms**) before moving to the next activity. This gives the target application time to process the input and render any auto-completion dropdowns.

## 2.2 The Four Input Methods — The Heart of the Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite:** You will almost certainly be asked to compare the four input methods. Memorize this table.

| Input Method | Mechanism | Speed | Works in Background? | Works on Hidden Windows? | Compatible With |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Standard (Hardware Events)** | Sends events via the **Hardware driver** (lowest level). | Slow (real-time) | ❌ No | ❌ No | All Windows apps |
| **Simulate (Software Events)** | Injects events directly into the **application's message queue**. | Fast | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Most desktop apps (not Citrix/RDP) |
| **Send Window Message** | Sends a `WM_SETTEXT` message (replaces the entire text in one go). | Fastest | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Standard Windows controls |
| **Chromium API** | Uses the **Chrome DevTools Protocol (CDP)** to interact with the browser. | Fast | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Chrome, Edge, modern browsers |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Although this topic is more conceptual than mathematical, the following "formulas" are the **standard performance metrics** you must know for numerical/KTU answer purposes.

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{delayBefore}} + (n \times T_{\text{delayBetweenKeys}}) + T_{\text{delayAfter}}
$$

Where:
- $T_{\text{total}}$ = Total time taken to complete one `Type Into` activity (in **ms**).
- $T_{\text{delayBefore}}$ = Delay *before* typing starts (default **200 ms**).
- $n$ = Number of characters in the input string.
- $T_{\text{delayBetweenKeys}}$ = Delay *between* successive keystrokes (default **20 ms**).
- $T_{\text{delayAfter}}$ = Delay *after* typing completes (default **300 ms**).

$$
E_{\text{typerate}} = \frac{60{,}000}{T_{\text{delayBetweenKeys}}} \quad \text{[characters per minute]}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** In BPO and banking RPA projects (e.g., automating legacy mainframes or AS/400 systems), **Standard input method** is mandatory because these systems only respond to hardware-level keystrokes. Conversely, for **Citrix/RDP** virtualization, none of the four methods work — special **Computer Vision** activities are required.

## 2.4 Important Properties of the `Type Into` Activity

- **`Text`**: The string to be typed. Supports variables and expressions.
- **`DelayBefore`**: Waits before typing. Useful when the application is slow to load.
- **`DelayAfter`**: Waits after typing. Useful for auto-suggestion lists to settle.
- **`DelayBetweenKeys`**: The "typing speed" of the bot.
- **`ClickBeforeTyping`**: If `True`, clicks the field first to ensure focus.
- **`EmptyField`**: If `True`, clears the field using `Ctrl+A` + `Delete` before typing.
- **`SendWindowMessages`**: Boolean flag to switch to Window Message input method.
- **`SimulateType`**: Boolean flag to switch to Simulate input method.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Calculating Total Typing Time

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A UiPath bot is configured to type the string `"KTU2024"` into a search box. The properties are:
> - `DelayBefore` = 200 ms
> - `DelayAfter` = 300 ms
> - `DelayBetweenKeys` = 50 ms
> 
> **Find:**
> 1. The total time taken for the `Type Into` activity to complete.
> 2. The effective typing rate in characters per minute (CPM).
> 3. What happens if the developer sets `EmptyField = True` — how many extra keystrokes are generated?

### Step 1: Identify the Number of Characters
The string `"KTU2024"` has:

$$
n = \vert K \vert + \vert T \vert + \vert U \vert + \vert 2 \vert + \vert 0 \vert + \vert 2 \vert + \vert 4 \vert = 7 \text{ characters}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the Total Time Formula

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{delayBefore}} + (n \times T_{\text{delayBetweenKeys}}) + T_{\text{delayAfter}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 200 + (7 \times 50) + 300
$$

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 200 + 350 + 300
$$

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 850 \text{ ms} = 0.85 \text{ seconds}
$$

### Step 3: Calculate Effective Typing Rate

$$
E_{\text{typerate}} = \frac{60{,}000}{T_{\text{delayBetweenKeys}}} = \frac{60{,}000}{50} = 1{,}200 \text{ CPM}
