filtering, sorting, computing, joining, splitting, and formatting. ### 1.3 Fundamental Excel Data Objects in RPA Context The hierarchy of Excel objects that an RPA bot must understand is: 1. **Workbook** — The entire `.xlsx` file (like the whole library building). 2. **Worksheet (Sheet)** — Individual tab inside the workbook (like a section of the library). 3. **Range** — A rectangular block of cells (like a row of shelves). 4. **Cell** — The atomic data element identified by its address (e.g., `B5`). 5. **Named Range / Table** — A logical collection with a meaningful name (e.g., `SalesData_2024`). 6. **Formula** — A rule that computes a value from inputs. 7. **Pivot Table** — A dynamic, summarized view of source data. ### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Boundaries - **Maximum rows per worksheet:** **1,048,576** - **Maximum columns per worksheet:** **16,384** (last column = `XFD`) - **Maximum cell content length:** **32,767 characters** - **Maximum worksheet size:** **2 GB** (in newer `.xlsx` formats) - **Default cell address notation:** **ColumnLetter + RowNumber** (e.g., `A1`, `AC245`) ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Taxonomy of Data Manipulation Operations Data manipulation operations in Excel can be classified into the following six logical families: #### 2.1.1 **Read Operations (Extraction)** The bot extracts data from specified cells, ranges, or tables without altering them. - **Static Read:** `Read Cell`, `Read Range` — retrieves raw values. - **Dynamic Read:** `Read Cell Formula`, `Read Cell Color`, `Read Column Header`. - **Selective Read:** Filtered reads using criteria (e.g., rows where `Region = "Kerala"`). #### 2.1.2 **Write Operations (Persistence)** The bot writes, appends, or updates values into Excel. - **Write Cell:** Writes a single value to a specific cell address. - **Write Range:** Writes a multi-cell dataset (e.g., from a DataTable variable). - **Append Range:** Adds new rows at the bottom of existing data. - **Auto Fill / Copy-Paste:** Extends a formula or value across a range. #### 2.1.3 **Transform Operations (Computational)** Operations that compute new values from existing ones using formulas. - **Arithmetic:** `SUM`, `AVERAGE`, `PRODUCT`, `MOD`, `INT`, `ROUND`. - **Logical:** `IF`, `IFS`, `AND`, `OR`, `NOT`, `IFERROR`. - **Lookup:** `VLOOKUP`, `HLOOKUP`, `INDEX-MATCH`, `XLOOKUP`, `XMATCH`. - **Text:** `CONCATENATE` / `CONCAT`, `LEFT`, `RIGHT`, `MID`, `LEN`, `TRIM`, `UPPER`, `LOWER`, `SUBSTITUTE`, `TEXTJOIN`. - **Date/Time:** `TODAY`, `NOW`, `DATEDIF`, `YEAR`, `MONTH`, `DAY`, `NETWORKDAYS`, `EOMONTH`. - **Statistical:** `COUNT`, `COUNTA`, `COUNTIF`, `COUNTIFS`, `SUMIF`, `SUMIFS`, `AVERAGEIF`, `MAXIFS`, `MINIFS`. #### 2.1.4 **Filter & Sort Operations (Reorganization)** - **AutoFilter:** Dropdown filters on header rows. - **Advanced Filter:** Multi-criteria filtering using boolean logic. - **Sort:** Ascending / Descending / Custom multi-level sort. - **Remove Duplicates:** Eliminates identical rows based on selected columns. #### 2.1.5 **Structure Operations (Architectural)** - **Create / Delete / Rename Sheets.** - **Insert / Delete Rows and Columns.** - **Format Cells:** Number formats, fonts, colors, borders. - **Merge & Unmerge Cells.** - **Freeze Panes** for header visibility. - **Group / Outline** rows for hierarchical views. #### 2.1.6 **Analytical Operations (Intelligence Layer)** - **Pivot Tables:** Dynamic summarization. - **Pivot Charts:** Visual analytics. - **What-If Analysis:** Goal Seek, Data Tables, Scenario Manager. - **Power Query / Power Pivot:** ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) pipelines. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Essential Excel Functions Cheat Sheet | Category | Function Signature | Returns | Example | |---|---|---|---| | Aggregation | `=SUM(range)` | Sum of numeric cells | `=SUM(A1:A100)` | | Aggregation | `=AVERAGE(range)` | Arithmetic mean | `=AVERAGE(B2:B50)` | | Aggregation | `=COUNT(range)` | Count of numeric cells | `=COUNT(C:C)` | | Aggregation | `=COUNTA(range)` | Count of non-empty cells | `=COUNTA(A1:A1000)` | | Logical | `=IF(cond, val_if_true, val_if_false)` | Conditional value | `=IF(A1>50,"PASS","FAIL")` | | Logical | `=IFS(cond1, val1, cond2, val2, ...)` | First matching condition | `=IFS(A1>=90,"A",A1>=80,"B")` | | Logical | `=IFERROR(formula, fallback)` | Fallback on error | `=IFERROR(A1/B1, 0)` | | Lookup | `=VLOOKUP(lookup, table, col_idx, match)` | Vertical lookup | `=VLOOKUP("E101", A:D, 4, FALSE)` | | Lookup | `=HLOOKUP(lookup, table, row_idx, match)` | Horizontal lookup | `=HLOOKUP("Q1", A1:E2, 2, FALSE)` | | Lookup | `=INDEX(array, row, col)` | Cell at intersection | `=INDEX(A1:C10, 5, 2)` | | Lookup | `=MATCH(lookup, array, type)` | Position of match | `=MATCH("Kerala", A:A, 0)` | | Lookup | `=XLOOKUP(lookup, lookup_array, return_array)` | Modern lookup | `=XLOOKUP("Kerala", A:A, C:C)` | | Text | `=CONCAT(range)` | Joined string | `=CONCAT(A1:A5)` | | Text | `=TEXTJOIN(delim, ignore_empty, range)` | Delimited string | `=TEXTJOIN(",", TRUE, A1:A5)` | | Text | `=LEFT(text, n)` | First n characters | `=LEFT("RPA-2024", 3)` returns `RPA` | | Text | `=RIGHT(text, n)` | Last n characters | `=RIGHT("RPA-2024", 4)` returns `2024` | | Text | `=MID(text, start, n)` | Middle characters | `=MID("PEAIT415", 5, 3)` returns `IT4` | | Text | `=LEN(text)` | Length of string | `=LEN("Kerala")` returns `6` | | Text | `=TRIM(text)` | Removes extra spaces | `=TRIM(" KTU ")` returns `KTU` | | Text | `=SUBSTITUTE(text, old, new)` | Replaces text | `=SUBSTITUTE("KTM","K","k")` returns `kTM` | | Date | `=TODAY()` | Current date | `=TODAY()` | | Date | `=DATEDIF(start, end, unit)` | Date difference | `=DATEDIF(A1, B1, "Y")` | | Date | `=EOMONTH(start, months)` | End of month | `=EOMONTH(TODAY(), 0)` | | Conditional | `=SUMIF(range, criteria, sum_range)` | Conditional sum | `=SUMIF(A:A, ">1000", B:B)` | | Conditional | `=COUNTIF(range, criteria)` | Conditional count | `=COUNTIF(B:B, "Kerala")` | | Conditional | `=SUMIFS(sum_range, crit_range1, crit1, ...)` | Multi-criteria sum | `=SUMIFS(D:D, A:A, "Kerala", C:C, ">50")` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Students must remember that `VLOOKUP` requires the lookup column to be the **leftmost** column of the table_array. For modern, flexible lookup, prefer `XLOOKUP` (Excel 365 / 2021+). Always use **FALSE / 0** for exact match in lookups to avoid incorrect approximations. ### 2.3 The "Why & How" Behind Each Manipulation Family | Family | Why We Use It | How RPA Implements It | |---|---|---| | **Read** | To retrieve input data for downstream rules | UiPath `Read Range`, `Read Cell` activities | | **Write** | To persist computed outputs into files | UiPath `Write Range`, `Write Cell` activities | | **Transform** | To derive new fields, totals, KPIs | Embedded formulas via `Excel Application Scope` | | **Filter & Sort** | To isolate rows matching business rules | UiPath `Filter DataTable`, `Sort DataTable` | | **Structure** | To make the workbook audit-friendly | VBA (Visual Basic for Applications) macros or sheet activities | | **Analytical** | To support managerial decision-making | `Create Pivot Table` activity, Power BI integration | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In enterprise environments, Excel Data Manipulation via RPA is deployed in: - **Banking & Finance:** KYC (Know Your Customer) reconciliation, loan processing. - **Healthcare:** Patient record digitization, insurance claim audit. - **Supply Chain:** Inventory reconciliation, vendor invoice matching. - **HR Operations:** Joining-formalities data entry, payroll processing. - **Telecom:** Customer billing reconciliation, CDR (Call Detail Record) analytics. - **Government:** GST (Goods and Services Tax) return filing, subsidy disbursement. ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — VLOOKUP for Employee Salary Lookup **Problem Statement:** A workbook `EmployeeMaster.xlsx` contains employee data in columns: - Column A: `EmpID` - Column B: `EmpName` - Column C: `Department` - Column D: `Salary` A second sheet `Payroll` contains `EmpID` in column A. We must populate `Net Salary` in column B using a `VLOOKUP` against the master data, but apply a **10% bonus** if the department is "Engineering". #### Step-by-Step Solution: **Step 1: Confirm lookup column position.** The lookup column `EmpID` is the **leftmost** column of the master table (`A:D`), so `VLOOKUP` is valid. **Step 2: Build the base VLOOKUP formula.** $$ \text{Salary} = \text{VLOOKUP}(A2, \text{EmployeeMaster}.\text{A:D}, 4, \text{FALSE}) $$ **Step 3: Embed the IF wrapper for conditional bonus.** $$ \text{NetSalary} = \text{IF}( \text{VLOOKUP}(A2, \text{EmployeeMaster}.\text{A:D}, 3, \text{FALSE}) = \text{"Engineering"}, $$ $$ 1.10 \times \text{VLOOKUP}(A2, \text{EmployeeMaster}.\text{A:D}, 4, \text{FALSE}), $$ $$ \text{VLOOKUP}(A2, \text{EmployeeMaster}.\text{A:D}, 4, \text{FALSE}) ) $$ **Step 4: Combined Excel Formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{NetSalary}_i \;=\; & \text{IF}\bigl(\;\text{VLOOKUP}(A_i, \text{EmployeeMaster}!\$A:\$D, 3, \text{FALSE}) = \text{"Engineering"}, \\ & 1.10 \times \text{VLOOKUP}(A_i, \text{EmployeeMaster}!\$A:\$D, 4, \text{FALSE}), \\ & \text{VLOOKUP}(A_i, \text{EmployeeMaster}!\$A:\$D, 4, \text{FALSE})\;\bigr) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Trace for a single employee `E101` whose department is "Engineering" and salary is ₹50,000.** - Lookup 1 (department): returns `"Engineering"` → condition TRUE. - Lookup 2 (salary): returns `50000`. - Compute `1.10 × 50000 = 55000`. - Result: `NetSalary = ₹55,000`. **Step 6: Verify IFERROR fallback.** Wrap the entire formula in `IFERROR` to handle missing `EmpID`: $$ \text{SafeNetSalary}_i = \text{IFERROR}(\text{previous formula},\; \text{"EmpID Not Found"}) $$ ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — SUMIFS for Multi-Criteria Sales Aggregation **Problem Statement:** A sales worksheet has columns: `Region`, `Product`, `Quantity`, `UnitPrice`, `SalesDate`. Compute the total sales revenue for the **"Kerala"** region where **Quantity ≥ 50**, between **01-Jan-2024** and **31-Mar-2024**. #### Step-by-Step Solution: **Step 1: Identify the criteria ranges and criteria values.** | Criterion | Range | Value | |---|---|---| | Region | `A:A` | `"Kerala"` | | Quantity | `C:C` | `">=50"` | | SalesDate | `E:E` | `">="&DATE(2024,1,1)` and `"<="&DATE(2024,3,31)` | **Step 2: Revenue per row is `Quantity × UnitPrice`, requiring a SUMPRODUCT or a helper column.** We'll use a helper column `F` that already computes `Revenue = C × D`. **Step 3: SUMIFS formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{TotalRevenue} = \text{SUMIFS}( & F:F, \;A:A,\;\text{"Kerala"}, \\ & C:C,\;{">=50"}, \\ & E:E,\;{">="\&DATE(2024,1,1)}, \\ & E:E,\;{"<="\&DATE(2024,3,31)}\; ) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Numerical Trace.** Assume filtered rows yield revenues: ₹12,000, ₹8,500, ₹25,000, ₹18,750. $$ \text{TotalRevenue} = 12000 + 8500 + 25000 + 18750 = 64{,}250 $$ **Step 5: Validation using SUMPRODUCT alternative (for cross-check).** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{TotalRevenue}_{\text{verify}} = \text{SUMPRODUCT}\bigl( & (A:A=\text{"Kerala"}) \times \\ & (C:C \geq 50) \times \\ & (E:E \geq \text{DATE}(2024,1,1)) \times \\ & (E:E \leq \text{DATE}(2024,3,31)) \times \\ & (C:C \times D:D)\bigr) \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** `SUMIFS` is faster than `SUMPRODUCT` for large datasets because it uses optimized internal indexing, whereas `SUMPRODUCT` performs array multiplication cell-by-cell. ### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Text Manipulation for Email ID Standardization **Problem Statement:** An Excel column `B` contains employee names in irregular casing (e.g., `" raJesh KuMar "`). We must: 1. Trim extra spaces. 2. Convert to Title Case. 3. Concatenate with a domain `@ktu.in` to generate official email IDs. #### Step-by-Step Solution: **Step 1: Trim spaces.** $$ \text{TrimmedName}_i = \text{TRIM}(B_i) $$ **Step 2: Apply `PROPER` to convert to Title Case.** $$ \text{TitleName}_i = \text{PROPER}(\text{TRIM}(B_i)) $$ **Step 3: Concatenate using `&` operator.** $$ \text{EmailID}_i = \text{LOWER}(\text{PROPER}(\text{TRIM}(B_i))) \; \& \; \text{"@ktu.in"} $$ Note: We used `LOWER(PROPER(...))` to ensure the local-part is lowercase while preserving Title Case readability for display. **Step 4: Trace for `" raJesh KuMar "`:** - `TRIM(" raJesh KuMar ")` = `"raJesh KuMar"` - `PROPER("raJesh KuMar")` = `"Rajesh Kumar"` - `LOWER("Rajesh Kumar")` = `"rajesh kumar"` - Concat: `"rajesh kumar" & "@ktu.in"` = **`rajesh kumar@ktu.in`** **Step 5: Handle multi-word names using `SUBSTITUTE` to remove internal spaces.** $$ \text{EmailLocal} = \text{SUBSTITUTE}(\text{LOWER}(\text{PROPER}(\text{TRIM}(B_i))), \; \text{" "}, \; \text{"."}) $$ For `"Rajesh Kumar"` → `"rajesh.kumar@ktu.in"`. ### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Function | Scenario | Recommended Function | Reason | |---|---|---| | Exact match lookup, simple table | `VLOOKUP` / `HLOOKUP` | Universally supported | | Flexible lookup, no column-index limit | `INDEX` + `MATCH` | Dynamic column reference | | Modern Excel (365 / 2021+) | `XLOOKUP` | Bidirectional, no NA on missing | | Sum with single condition | `SUMIF` | Simple, fast | | Sum with multiple conditions | `SUMIFS` | Multi-criteria support | | Complex boolean combination | `SUMPRODUCT` | Boolean arithmetic | | Count cells matching criteria | `COUNTIF` / `COUNTIFS` | Specialized counter | | Date difference in years/months | `DATEDIF` | Hidden gem with unit selector | | Joining text with delimiter | `TEXTJOIN` | Skips empty cells efficiently | | Conditional formatting rule | `IFS` | Replaces nested IFs | ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Excel Data Manipulation Lifecycle in RPA ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start RPA Process] --> B[Open Workbook
via Excel Application Scope] B --> C{Identify Operation Type} C --> D1[Read Operations
Read Cell / Read Range] C --> D2[Write Operations
Write Cell / Write Range] C --> D3[Transform Operations
Apply Formulas] C --> D4[Filter and Sort
DataTable Activities] C --> D5[Analytical Ops
Pivot Tables] D1 --> E[Store in DataTable Variable] D2 --> E D3 --> E D4 --> E D5 --> E E --> F{Validation Passed?} F -->|No| G[Log Error and Exception Handling] F -->|Yes| H[Save and Close Workbook] G --> I[Send Exception Report] H --> J[Generate Audit Log] I --> K[End Process] J --> K ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Excel Automation ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph InputLayer["Input Layer"] IN1[Excel Source File .xlsx] IN2[User Config JSON] IN3[Database Source] end subgraph RPALayer["RPA Bot Engine Layer"] BOT1[Excel Application Scope] BOT2[Read Range Activity] BOT3[DataTable Manipulation] BOT4[Formula Computation] BOT5[Filter and Sort] end subgraph LogicLayer["Business Logic Layer"] LOG1[Lookup and Match Logic] LOG2[Validation Rules] LOG3[Aggregation Engine] LOG4[Exception Handler] end subgraph OutputLayer["Output Layer"] OUT1[Transformed Excel File] OUT2[PDF Report] OUT3[Email Notification] OUT4[Database Update] end IN1 --> BOT1 IN2 --> BOT1 IN3 --> BOT2 BOT1 --> BOT2 BOT2 --> BOT3 BOT3 --> LOG1 LOG1 --> LOG2 LOG2 --> LOG3 LOG3 --> BOT4 BOT4 --> BOT5 LOG3 --> LOG4 BOT5 --> OUT1 BOT5 --> OUT2 LOG4 --> OUT3 LOG4 --> OUT4 ``` ### 4.3 Data Flow Diagram — From Raw Excel to Processed Output ```mermaid flowchart TD RAW[Raw Excel Data
Unvalidated Mixed Types] --> STAGE1[Stage 1: Read and Parse] STAGE1 --> STAGE2[Stage 2: Clean and Normalize] STAGE2 --> STAGE2A[Remove Duplicates] STAGE2A --> STAGE2B[Trim and Case Normalize] STAGE2B --> STAGE2C[Type Casting
Text to Number or Date] STAGE2C --> STAGE3[Stage 3: Transform] STAGE3 --> STAGE3A[Apply Formulas and Lookups] STAGE3A --> STAGE3B[Compute Aggregates] STAGE3B --> STAGE3C[Generate Derived Fields] STAGE3C --> STAGE4[Stage 4: Validate] STAGE4 --> STAGE4A[Schema Check] STAGE4A --> STAGE4B[Range and Constraint Check] STAGE4B --> STAGE4C[Cross Reference Validation] STAGE4C --> STAGE5[Stage 5: Persist] STAGE5 --> STAGE5A[Write to Output Workbook] STAGE5A --> STAGE5B[Generate Pivot Summary] STAGE5B --> DONE[Processed Data Ready] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Tip:** Each stage in the diagram represents a logical checkpoint where RPA exception handling can intercept and log deviations. The validation stage is critical — without it, silent data corruption may propagate downstream. ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- #### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Explain the difference between `VLOOKUP` and `XLOOKUP` functions in Excel. State one major advantage of `XLOOKUP` over `VLOOKUP`.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | `VLOOKUP` | `XLOOKUP` | |---|---|---| | Excel Version | All versions | Excel 365 / 2021+ | | Lookup Direction | Vertical only | Vertical, Horizontal, or 2D | | Return on Missing | `#N/A` error | Customizable `if_not_found` argument | | Column Index Limit | Requires numeric index (e.g., 3) | Returns array directly, no index needed | | Performance | Slower on large datasets | Faster with optimized engine | **Major advantage of `XLOOKUP`:** It does not require the lookup value to be in the leftmost column of the table, and it allows the user to specify a custom fallback value for missing lookups using the optional `if_not_found` parameter, eliminating the need for `IFERROR` wrappers. $$ \text{XLOOKUP example: } \text{=XLOOKUP}(\text{"E101"},\; A:A,\; D:D,\; \text{"Not Found"}) $$ --- #### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **What is the role of Pivot Tables in data manipulation? List any three aggregation functions commonly used inside a Pivot Table.** **Model Answer:** A **Pivot Table** is a powerful Excel tool that dynamically summarizes, groups, and analyzes large datasets without altering the original source data. It allows the user to drag-and-drop fields into **Rows**, **Columns**, **Values**, and **Filters** zones to instantly reconfigure the view of data. **Three Aggregation Functions:** 1. `SUM` — Total of numeric values within a group. 2. `COUNT` — Number of records within a group. 3. `AVERAGE` — Mean of numeric values within a group. (Other valid answers: `MAX`, `MIN`, `PRODUCT`, `STDEV`, `VAR`.) **Role in RPA:** Pivot Tables allow bots to generate executive dashboards, summary reports, and trend analyses programmatically, eliminating manual report-generation effort. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of Excel automation in RPA. List the major activities used for Read, Write, and Filter operations. **(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the following dataset stored in `Sales.xlsx`: | OrderID | Region | Product | Qty | UnitPrice | OrderDate | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | O101 | Kerala | Laptop | 10 | 50000 | 2024-01-15 | | O102 | Tamil Nadu | Mobile | 25 | 20000 | 2024-01-20 | | O103 | Kerala | Laptop | 5 | 50000 | 2024-02-10 | | O104 | Karnataka | Tablet | 15 | 15000 | 2024-02-25 | | O105 | Kerala | Mobile | 30 | 20000 | 2024-03-05 | Write Excel formulas to compute: 1. The total revenue for each row. 2. The total revenue for the "Kerala" region where Qty ≥ 10. 3. The total revenue for the "Laptop" product across all regions. --- ##### **Model Solution for Question A:** **(a) Architecture of Excel Automation in RPA:** The Excel automation architecture in RPA consists of four layers working together: ``` +---------------------------------------------------+ | Layer 4: Output Layer (Reports, PDFs, Emails) | +---------------------------------------------------+ | Layer 3: Analytical Layer (Pivot, Charts) | +---------------------------------------------------+ | Layer 2: Logic Layer (Validation, Lookups) | +---------------------------------------------------+ | Layer 1: Data Layer (Read/Write/Filter Cells) | +---------------------------------------------------+ | Excel Application Scope (Bot-to-Excel Bridge) | +---------------------------------------------------+ ``` **Major Activities:** | Operation | UiPath Activity Examples | |---|---| | **Read** | `Read Cell`, `Read Range`, `Read Cell Formula`, `Read Column Headers` | | **Write** | `Write Cell`, `Write Range`, `Append Range`, `Auto Fill Range` | | **Filter** | `Filter DataTable`, `Sort DataTable`, `Remove Duplicate Rows` | | **Structure** | `Insert/Delete Rows`, `Create Sheet`, `Rename Sheet`, `Format Cells` | **(b) Formula Solutions:** **1. Total Revenue per row (assume column G is "Revenue"):** $$ G_i = E_i \times F_i $$ Where `E = Qty`, `F = UnitPrice`, and `i` denotes the row index. In Excel cell `G2`: $$ \text{=E2*F2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{Result for O101} = 10 \times 50000 = 5{,}00{,}000 $$ Computed revenues for all rows: | OrderID | Revenue (₹) | |---|---| | O101 | 5,00,000 | | O102 | 5,00,000 | | O103 | 2,50,000 | | O104 | 2,25,000 | | O105 | 6,00,000 | **2. Total Kerala revenue where Qty ≥ 10:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{TotalKerala} &= \text{SUMIFS}(G:G,\; B:B,\; \text{"Kerala"},\; D:D,\; {">=10"}) \\ &= \text{SUMIFS}(G:G,\; B:B,\; \text{"Kerala"},\; D:D,\; {">=10"}) \end{aligned} $$ Eligible rows: O101 (Qty=10), O103 (Qty=5 ✗ excluded), O105 (Qty=30). $$ \text{TotalKerala} = 5{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 = 11{,}00{,}000 $$ **3. Total Laptop revenue across all regions:** $$ \text{TotalLaptop} = \text{SUMIF}(C:C,\; \text{"Laptop"},\; G:G) $$ Eligible rows: O101 (₹5,00,000), O103 (₹2,50,000). $$ \text{TotalLaptop} = 5{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 = 7{,}50{,}000 $$ --- #### **Question B** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain any five Excel text manipulation functions with one example each. Discuss their role in data cleansing during RPA automation. **(b) [7 Marks]** A workbook `HRData.xlsx` contains the following columns: | EmpName (raw) | JoiningDate | |---|---| | " anITa JoSePh " | 2023-08-15 | | "RaJeSH kuMAR" | 2022-11-20 | | "sUraJ meNon" | 2024-01-10 | Write Excel formulas to: 1. Standardize each name into clean Title Case with no leading/trailing spaces. 2. Extract only the first name. 3. Compute the years of service for each employee as of today (`=TODAY()`). --- ##### **Model Solution for Question B:** **(a) Five Excel Text Manipulation Functions:** | # | Function | Purpose | Example | Output | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | `TRIM(text)` | Removes leading, trailing & extra internal spaces | `=TRIM(" KTU ")` | `KTU` | | 2 | `UPPER(text)` | Converts to uppercase | `=UPPER("kerala")` | `KERALA` | | 3 | `LOWER(text)` | Converts to lowercase | `=LOWER("KERALA")` | `kerala` | | 4 | `PROPER(text)` | Converts to Title Case | `=PROPER("kerala university")` | `Kerala University` | | 5 | `LEFT(text, n)` | Returns first n characters | `=LEFT("PEAIT415", 5)` | `PEAIT` | | 6 | `RIGHT(text, n)` | Returns last n characters | `=RIGHT("PEAIT415", 3)` | `415` | | 7 | `MID(text, start, n)` | Returns middle characters | `=MID("PEAIT415", 5, 3)` | `IT4` | | 8 | `LEN(text)` | Returns string length | `=LEN("Robotic")` | `7` | | 9 | `CONCAT(range)` | Joins strings | `=CONCAT(A1:A3)` | joined text | | 10 | `TEXTJOIN(delim, ignore, range)` | Joins with delimiter | `=TEXTJOIN("-", TRUE, A1:A3)` | `val1-val2-val3` | **Role in Data Cleansing for RPA:** In RPA, data extracted from emails, PDFs, or web portals often contains irregular whitespace, mixed casing, and embedded delimiters. Text functions enable bots to normalize this raw data into a consistent format before inserting it into downstream systems (ERP, CRM, databases). Without cleansing, downstream validation rules fail, leading to process exceptions. **(b) Formula Solutions:** **1. Standardize to clean Title Case:** $$ \text{CleanName}_i = \text{PROPER}(\text{TRIM}(A_i)) $$ For `" anITa JoSePh "`: - `TRIM` → `"anITa JoSePh"` - `PROPER` → **`Anita Joseph`** **2. Extract first name (assume space separator):** Method using `LEFT` + `FIND`: $$ \text{FirstName}_i = \text{LEFT}(\text{PROPER}(\text{TRIM}(A_i)),\; \text{FIND}(\text{" "},\; \text{PROPER}(\text{TRIM}(A_i))) - 1) $$ For `" anITa JoSePh "`: - Cleaned: `"Anita Joseph"` - `FIND(" ", "Anita Joseph")` = `6` - `LEFT("Anita Joseph", 6-1)` = `LEFT("Anita Joseph", 5)` = **`Anita`** **3. Years of service using `DATEDIF`:** $$ \text{YearsOfService}_i = \text{DATEDIF}(B_i,\; \text{TODAY}(),\; \text{"Y"}) $$ For Anita (joining 2023-08-15) as of 2024-06-30: $$ \text{YearsOfService} = \text{DATEDIF}(\text{DATE}(2023,8,15),\; \text{DATE}(2024,6,30),\; \text{"Y"}) = 0 \text{ years (incomplete)} $$ If we use `"YM"` for months: $$ \text{DATEDIF}(...,..., \text{"YM"}) = 10 \text{ months} $$ Combined output: | Employee | Years | Months | Total Months | |---|---|---|---| | Anita Joseph | 0 | 10 | 10 | | Rajesh Kumar | 1 | 7 | 19 | | Suraj Menon | 0 | 5 | 5 | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Wrong match mode in VLOOKUP:** Students often write `VLOOKUP(...,...,...,TRUE)` for exact matches, which causes incorrect approximate matches. Always use `FALSE` or `0` for exact lookup. > 2. **Ignoring the `lookup_value` column position:** `VLOOKUP` searches only the **leftmost** column of the table. If your lookup key is in column D, `VLOOKUP(A:D)` will fail. Use `INDEX-MATCH` or `XLOOKUP` instead. > 3. **Forgetting absolute references:** When dragging formulas, students forget to use `$A$1` style absolute references, causing range shifts and incorrect results. > 4. **Mixing text and numbers in `SUMIF`:** A column containing `"1000"` (text) and `1000` (number) will not be summed correctly. Always convert using `VALUE()` or `NUMBERVALUE()`. > 5. **`DATEDIF` unit confusion:** The unit parameter `"Y"`, `"M"`, `"D"`, `"MD"`, `"YM"`, `"YD"` have specific meanings — students often confuse `"M"` (total months) with `"YM"` (months after last year). > 6. **Not handling `#N/A`:** Forgetting `IFERROR` wrappers leads to unsightly error cascades across the workbook. > 7. **SUMIFS criteria as text vs expression:** `">=50"` requires the double-quotes; omitting them treats it as literal text and returns 0. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:** - [ ] **Excel Object Hierarchy:** Workbook → Worksheet → Range → Cell. Always reference cells using full path: `'[FileName.xlsx]SheetName'!$A$1:$D$100`. - [ ] **Six Families of Data Manipulation:** Read, Write, Transform, Filter/Sort, Structure, Analytical. - [ ] **`VLOOKUP`** → vertical lookup, leftmost column, exact match uses `FALSE`/`0`. - [ ] **`HLOOKUP`** → horizontal lookup, topmost row. - [ ] **`INDEX-MATCH`** → flexible lookup, no column-position restriction, faster. - [ ] **`XLOOKUP`** → modern Excel 365/2021, supports 2D, custom default. - [ ] **`SUMIF`/`SUMIFS`** → conditional summation; `COUNTIF`/`COUNTIFS` for counting. - [ ] **`IFERROR`** → always wrap fragile formulas to handle `#N/A`, `#DIV/0!`, `#VALUE!`. - [ ] **Text cleansing combo:** `PROPER(TRIM(...))` is the industry-standard normalization. - [ ] **`CONCAT`/`TEXTJOIN`** → joining strings; `TEXTJOIN` is preferred for delimited joins. - [ ] **`TODAY()` and `NOW()`** → volatile functions, recalculate every time the sheet is opened. - [ ] **`DATEDIF`** → hidden but powerful; units are `"Y"`, `"M"`, `"D"`, `"YM"`, `"MD"`, `"YD"`. - [ ] **Pivot Tables** → dynamic summary without altering source; used heavily in reporting bots. - [ ] **RPA Best Practice:** Use `Excel Application Scope` with `SaveChanges` property set explicitly. - [ ] **Large files:** For files >100 MB, prefer `Read Range` with `AddHeaders=True` and use DataTable filtering before writing back, to avoid memory overflow. - [ ] **Absolute references (`$A$1`)** are mandatory when copying formulas across rows/columns. - [ ] **Named Ranges** improve formula readability and are preferred in RPA workflows (e.g., `=VLOOKUP(ID, EmployeeTable, 4, FALSE)` is clearer than `=VLOOKUP(A2, A:D, 4, FALSE)`). > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When answering theory questions on Excel manipulation, always structure your answer into **Definition → Syntax → Example → RPA Use Case**. This four-part structure mirrors the KTU marking scheme and consistently fetches 7-mark answers full credit.

topics.title: Extracting Data from PDF
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Extracting Data from PDF

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**PDF Data Extraction in RPA** refers to the automated process of identifying, locating, and retrieving structured or unstructured data (text, tables, images, form fields) from Portable Document Format (PDF) files using a software bot, without manual human intervention.

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus for *PEAIT415 – Robotic Process Automation*, this falls under the broader category of **Data Scraping & Screen Scraping** and is implemented through specialized activities such as **Read PDF Text**, **Read PDF with OCR**, and **Screen Scraping Wizards** (e.g., UiPath's *Data Scraping Wizard*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-aligned):**
> "PDF Extraction in RPA is the technique of using a software robot to read, interpret, and output meaningful data from PDF documents — both native (text-based) and scanned (image-based) — by leveraging text parsers, OCR engines, and regular expressions."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **stack of 5,000 printed invoices** in PDF format, and your manager asks you to copy every invoice number, date, and total amount into an Excel sheet. Doing this manually would take days.

Now, imagine giving the same task to a **highly trained office assistant robot** that:
1. Picks up each PDF,
2. **Reads** the printed text (if native), or **looks at** the image and converts it to text (if scanned),
3. **Highlights** only the parts you care about (like "Invoice No: 1023"),
4. **Copies** them into a structured Excel file.

That robot is doing **PDF Data Extraction in RPA**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two key categories of PDFs the bot must handle:**
> - **Native PDF** — Contains selectable, embedded text. The bot reads it directly (fast, **99% accuracy**).
> - **Scanned / Image PDF** — A picture of text. The bot must run **OCR (Optical Character Recognition)** to "see" the characters (slower, **~95–98% accuracy**).

## 1.3 Standard RPA Metrics Used in PDF Extraction

- **OCR Accuracy Rate** — Typically **95%–99%** depending on document quality.
- **Extraction Throughput** — Measured in **pages/second** (modern bots: **2–10 pages/sec**).
- **Confidence Threshold** — A configurable score (e.g., **0.85 or 85%**) below which the bot flags the result for human review.
- **Anchor Match Score** — A similarity value (e.g., **0.9**) used when locating dynamic fields.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of PDF Data Extraction

### Pillar 1 — **Native Text Extraction** (Direct Parsing)
- Works on PDFs where the text layer is **machine-encoded** (not flattened to an image).
- The bot accesses the underlying character stream directly using libraries like **iTextSharp**, **PdfSharp**, or **UiPath's PDF Activities package**.
- **Speed:** Very high.
- **Accuracy:** ~100% (text is read as-is, no interpretation needed).

### Pillar 2 — **OCR-Based Extraction** (For Scanned PDFs)
- Used when the PDF has no text layer (e.g., a scanned photograph of a printed page).
- The PDF page is rendered as an image and fed to an OCR engine (**Tesseract**, **Google Cloud Vision OCR**, **Microsoft Azure Computer Vision**, **UiPath Document OCR**).
- The engine produces a text string + a confidence score per character/word.

### Pillar 3 — **Template / Anchor-Based Extraction** (Structured Forms)
- For **semi-structured** PDFs (invoices, receipts, purchase orders), the bot is taught a **template** — a fixed anchor text (e.g., "Invoice Number:") and an offset that tells it where to read.
- Uses **Get Text** activity + **Anchor Base** activity in UiPath.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of a PDF Extraction Bot

1. **Read Configuration File** — Bot loads the list of PDF file paths and target fields.
2. **Open PDF** — Launch the PDF in a compatible reader (Adobe Acrobat, Chrome PDF viewer, or background process).
3. **Detect PDF Type** — Check if the PDF has a **text layer** (use `PdfDocument.GetNumberOfPages()` + `ExtractText()`). If empty → **OCR mode**.
4. **Apply Extraction Strategy**:
   - Native → **Read PDF Text** activity with range/selector.
   - Scanned → **Read PDF with OCR** activity with chosen OCR engine.
5. **Apply Anchors** — Use *Anchor Base* to find dynamic data relative to fixed labels.
6. **Apply Regular Expressions (Regex)** — Validate and clean extracted strings (e.g., extract only digits from a date field).
7. **Write to Destination** — Push the structured data into Excel, Database, JSON, or API.
8. **Exception Handling** — On low confidence or missing data → log to **QueueItem** for human review.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Anchors Matter:**
> In real-world PDFs, the position of data changes (e.g., due to varying invoice lengths). The bot cannot rely on *fixed coordinates*. An **Anchor** is a stable, predictable text (like a label) that the bot uses as a "compass" to find the target data nearby.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Syntax | Description | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **OCR Confidence Threshold** | $C_{th} = \dfrac{\text{Accepted Words}}{\text{Total Words Detected}}$ | Minimum confidence to accept extraction | $0.85$ (dimensionless) |
| **Extraction Accuracy** | $A = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Standard accuracy metric | $0.95$ to $0.99$ |
| **Precision** | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | How many extracted values are correct | $0.98$ |
| **Recall** | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | How many actual values were found | $0.97$ |
| **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall | $0.975$ |
| **Regex Digit Extractor** | `\d+` | Matches one or more digits | — |
| **Regex Email Pattern** | `[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}` | Validates email format | — |
| **Anchor Offset** | $\Delta x, \Delta y$ (pixels) | Pixels from anchor text to target data | $\pm 50$ px |
| **Bot Throughput** | $T = \dfrac{N_{pages}}{t_{total}}$ | Pages processed per second | $2$–$10$ pages/sec |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking & Finance:** Auto-extract loan application data from scanned PDFs.
- **Healthcare:** Pull patient data from medical reports into EMR systems.
- **Legal:** Extract clauses, dates, and party names from contracts.
- **Supply Chain:** Read supplier invoices and update ERP (SAP, Oracle) automatically.
- **Government:** Process tax filings, Aadhaar forms, land records.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Calculating the Optimal OCR Confidence Threshold

When a bot processes a scanned PDF, each character returned by the OCR engine carries a confidence score $c_i \in [0, 1]$. The goal is to set a **threshold** $C_{th}$ such that words below the threshold are routed to human review.

### Step 1 — Define the confidence of a single word
Let a word contain $n$ characters with individual scores $c_1, c_2, \dots, c_n$. The **word-level confidence** is the average:

$$C_{word} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i$$

### Step 2 — Define the document-level confidence
For a document with $W$ total words:

$$C_{doc} = \frac{1}{W} \sum_{j=1}^{W} C_{word, j}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the threshold rule
A word is **accepted** if $C_{word} \geq C_{th}$, else routed for human review:

$$\text{Decision}(C_{word}) = \begin{cases} \text{Accept} & \text{if } C_{word} \geq C_{th} \\ \text{Queue for Review} & \text{if } C_{word} < C_{th} \end{cases}$$

### Step 4 — Trade-off Analysis

A high $C_{th}$ (e.g., $0.95$) means **fewer false positives** but more manual review (lower automation rate).  
A low $C_{th}$ (e.g., $0.70$) means **more automation** but higher error risk.

**Example numerical evaluation:**

Suppose a word of 5 characters has scores: $0.91, 0.88, 0.95, 0.93, 0.89$.

$$C_{word} = \frac{0.91 + 0.88 + 0.95 + 0.93 + 0.89}{5} = \frac{4.56}{5} = 0.912$$

If $C_{th} = 0.90$, the word is **accepted**.  
If $C_{th} = 0.95$, the word is **queued for human review**.

## 3.2 Derivation: F1-Score for a PDF Extraction Bot

The F1-score balances precision and recall, critical when both false positives (wrong data) and false negatives (missed data) are costly.

**Given values from a sample run:**
- True Positives $TP = 940$ (correctly extracted fields)
- False Positives $FP = 12$ (wrong fields extracted)
- False Negatives $FN = 18$ (missed fields)

### Step 1 — Calculate Precision

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{940}{940 + 12} = \frac{940}{952} \approx 0.9874$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Recall

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{940}{940 + 18} = \frac{940}{958} \approx 0.9812$$

### Step 3 — Calculate F1-Score

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$$

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.9874 \times 0.9812}{0.9874 + 0.9812}$$

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.9689}{1.9686} = 2 \cdot 0.4922 \approx 0.9844$$

**Interpretation:** The bot has a **98.44% balanced accuracy** — a strong production-grade result for KTU practical evaluations.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Extraction Method

| Document Type | Recommended Activity | OCR Required? | Speed | Accuracy | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Native PDF (Text Selectable) | **Read PDF Text** | No | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | Low |
| Scanned PDF (Clean) | **Read PDF with OCR** | Yes (Tesseract) | ★★★ | ★★★★ | Low |
| Scanned PDF (Noisy) | **Read PDF with OCR** | Yes (Google/Azure) | ★★ | ★★★★★ | Medium |
| Structured Form (Invoice) | **Anchor Base + Get Text** | Optional | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | Low |
| Mixed (Some pages native, some scanned) | **Hybrid Workflow** | Conditional | ★★★ | ★★★★ | Medium |
| Encrypted / Password-Protected PDF | **Decrypt Activity** first | N/A | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | Low |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level PDF Extraction Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Bot] --> B[Load PDF File Path]
    B --> C{PDF has Text Layer?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Native Text Extraction]
    C -- No --> E[Render Page as Image]
    E --> F[Run OCR Engine]
    F --> G[Return Text + Confidence Score]
    D --> H[Apply Anchors and Regex]
    G --> H
    H --> I{Confidence >= Threshold?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Write to Excel / Database]
    I -- No --> K[Queue for Human Review]
    J --> L[Log Success]
    K --> M[Log Exception]
    L --> N[Next PDF File]
    M --> N
    N --> O{More Files?}
    O -- Yes --> B
    O -- No --> P[End Bot]
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow — Native vs Scanned PDF

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Input PDF] --> P2[Check Text Layer]
    P2 --> P3{Is Text Selectable?}
    P3 -- Yes --> P4[Use Read PDF Text Activity]
    P3 -- No --> P5[Use Read PDF with OCR Activity]
    P4 --> P6[Apply Regex for Cleanup]
    P5 --> P7[Apply OCR Confidence Filter]
    P7 --> P8{Confidence >= 0.85?}
    P8 -- Yes --> P6
    P8 -- No --> P9[Send to Review Queue]
    P6 --> P10[Structured Output to Excel]
    P9 --> P10
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputLayer [Input Layer]
        A1[PDF Files Pool]
        A2[Config File - Anchors and Regex]
    end

    subgraph ProcessingLayer [Processing Layer]
        B1[PDF Type Detector]
        B2[Native Text Reader]
        B3[OCR Engine Module]
        B4[Anchor Resolver]
        B5[Regex Validator]
    end

    subgraph DecisionLayer [Decision Layer]
        C1{Confidence Check}
        C2{Review Queue}
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer [Output Layer]
        D1[Excel Spreadsheet]
        D2[Database Table]
        D3[API Endpoint]
        D4[Audit Log]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B4
    B1 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B2 --> B5
    B3 --> C1
    C1 -- Pass --> B4
    C1 -- Fail --> C2
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> D1
    B5 --> D2
    B5 --> D3
    C2 --> D4
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define PDF data extraction in the context of RPA. Differentiate between native and scanned PDF extraction. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**PDF Data Extraction in RPA** is the automated retrieval of meaningful data (text, numbers, tables) from PDF files using a software bot, eliminating manual data entry.

| Aspect | Native PDF | Scanned PDF |
|---|---|---|
| Underlying Format | Machine-encoded text layer | Image of a document |
| Technique | Direct text parsing | OCR (Optical Character Recognition) |
| Speed | Very fast | Slower (image processing needed) |
| Accuracy | ~100% | ~95–98% |
| Example Tool | `Read PDF Text` in UiPath | `Read PDF with OCR` in UiPath |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often claim that *all* PDFs require OCR. This is **wrong** — only scanned/image PDFs do. Reading a native PDF with OCR is wasteful and slower.

---

### **Q2.** What is an Anchor in PDF extraction? Why is it used? `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

An **Anchor** in RPA is a stable, predictable text element (e.g., the label *"Invoice No:"*) used to locate dynamic, positionally-shifting data (the actual invoice number) relative to it.

**Why it is used:**
- Real-world PDFs (e.g., invoices) have **variable lengths** — the position of data changes per document.
- Hard-coding pixel coordinates is **brittle and fails** when layouts change.
- The **Anchor Base** activity in UiPath uses the anchor as a "compass" to find the target nearby.
- It makes the bot **layout-independent and robust** to formatting variations.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap:** Students frequently describe Anchors as "fixed coordinates." This is incorrect. Anchors are *textual references*, not pixel positions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the step-by-step process of extracting invoice data from a scanned PDF using an RPA tool (UiPath). List the activities used and justify each step.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A scanned invoice PDF has the following OCR confidence scores for 5 fields:
- Field 1 (Invoice No): $0.96$
- Field 2 (Date): $0.82$
- Field 3 (Total Amount): $0.99$
- Field 4 (Vendor Name): $0.74$
- Field 5 (Tax ID): $0.91$

If the confidence threshold is set to $0.85$, determine:
1. Which fields will be accepted automatically?
2. Which will be queued for human review?
3. Calculate the document-level average confidence.

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-Step Process for Scanned Invoice Extraction:**

| Step | UiPath Activity | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Launch PDF | `Open Application` / `Start Process` | Opens Adobe/Chrome PDF viewer |
| 2. Set OCR Scope | `Read PDF with OCR` | Reads scanned PDF using Tesseract/Azure OCR |
| 3. Define Range | Set page range to "1-1" | Limits reading to invoice page |
| 4. Extract Fields | `Get Text` + `Anchor Base` × 5 fields | Locates each field near its label |
| 5. Validate Strings | `Matches` (Regex) | Confirms format (e.g., digits only) |
| 6. Write Output | `Write Range` (Excel) | Stores structured data |
| 7. Exception Path | `If` block on confidence | Sends low-score fields to `Queue` |

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply threshold $C_{th} = 0.85$:**

| Field | Score | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Invoice No | 0.96 | Accepted |
| Date | 0.82 | **Queued for Review** |
| Total Amount | 0.99 | Accepted |
| Vendor Name | 0.74 | **Queued for Review** |
| Tax ID | 0.91 | Accepted |

**Step 2 — Document-level average confidence:**

$$C_{doc} = \frac{0.96 + 0.82 + 0.99 + 0.74 + 0.91}{5} = \frac{4.42}{5} = 0.884$$

Since $C_{doc} = 0.884 > C_{th} = 0.85$, the document is **marginally acceptable** for automated processing, but the two flagged fields **must** be routed to a human reviewer before final submission.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice).** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast OCR engines commonly used in RPA for PDF extraction. Discuss at least three engines with their strengths and weaknesses.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design a hybrid RPA workflow that automatically decides whether to use **Read PDF Text** (native) or **Read PDF with OCR** (scanned) based on PDF type detection. Provide a flowchart description and pseudocode.

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison of OCR Engines:**

| Engine | Strengths | Weaknesses | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tesseract (Open Source)** | Free, offline, customizable | Lower accuracy on noisy scans | Internal, low-budget bots |
| **Google Cloud Vision OCR** | High accuracy (~99%), supports 100+ languages | Requires internet, paid per page | Enterprise multilingual docs |
| **Microsoft Azure Computer Vision** | Excellent on tables and forms, good handwriting | Cost scales with volume | Bank forms, handwritten data |
| **UiPath Document OCR** | Built-in, no extra license for basic usage | Limited to UiPath ecosystem | Standard UiPath projects |

**(b) Hybrid Workflow Design — Pseudocode (RPA-style):**

```
START
  pdfPath = Config("PDF_Path")
  pdfDoc = OpenPDF(pdfPath)
  rawText = pdfDoc.ExtractText(0, 0)   // Try native first
  IF rawText.Length > 0 THEN
      // Native PDF path
      fields = Read_PDF_Text(pdfPath, range="1-1")
      Log "Native extraction used"
  ELSE
      // Scanned PDF path
      imagePath = Render_PDF_As_Image(pdfPath, page=1)
      ocrResult = Read_PDF_With_OCR(imagePath, engine="Tesseract")
      fields = ocrResult.Text
      confidence = ocrResult.Confidence
      IF confidence < 0.85 THEN
          Add_To_Queue("HumanReview", fields)
      END IF
  END IF
  cleanedData = Apply_Regex(fields, patterns)
  Write_To_Excel(cleanedData, "Output.xlsx")
END
```

**Flowchart Description:**
1. The bot opens the PDF.
2. It first attempts **native text extraction**.
3. If the text layer is empty, it **renders the page as an image**.
4. The image is passed to the **OCR engine**.
5. The result is filtered by the **confidence threshold** before being written to Excel.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Module 3 PDF Extraction):**
> 1. **Confusing "Anchor" with "Coordinate"** — anchors are *textual*, not pixel-based. (-2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the confidence threshold step** — extracted OCR text is unreliable without it. (-2 marks)
> 3. **Not distinguishing native vs scanned workflows** — both are required to score full marks. (-3 marks)
> 4. **Skipping exception handling** — KTU expects a *review queue* path for low-confidence data. (-1 mark)
> 5. **Using OCR on a native PDF** — wastes time and resources; marks are deducted for inefficiency.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 📘 **PDF Extraction in RPA** is the automated retrieval of data from PDF files by a software bot.
- 📘 There are **two types of PDFs** — *Native* (text-selectable) and *Scanned* (image-based).
- 📘 **Native extraction** uses `Read PDF Text`; **Scanned extraction** uses `Read PDF with OCR`.
- 📘 **Anchors** are stable label texts used to locate dynamic data — they are **not** pixel coordinates.
- 📘 **OCR Confidence Threshold** (typically **0.85**) decides whether a field is auto-accepted or sent to human review.
- 📘 **Regular Expressions (Regex)** are used to clean and validate extracted strings (e.g., `\d+` for digits).
- 📘 Always build a **hybrid workflow** that auto-detects PDF type before choosing the extraction method.
- 📘 Always include an **exception/review path** for low-confidence fields — KTU specifically tests this.
- 📘 Key metrics: **Precision, Recall, F1-Score** — F1 is the balanced measure of bot performance.
- 📘 Popular OCR engines: **Tesseract (free), Google Cloud Vision, Microsoft Azure CV, UiPath Document OCR**.
- 📘 Common real-world uses: **Invoice processing, KYC, medical records, legal contracts, tax forms**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extracting a Single Piece of Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Extracting a Single Piece of Data ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Data Extraction in RPA**: *Extracting a Single Piece of Data* refers to the process of retrieving, isolating, and capturing one specific unit of information (such as a text string, a number, a date, or a status) from a structured, semi-structured, or unstructured source (like a web page, a desktop application, a PDF, or a terminal screen) by using Robotic Process Automation bots. The output is stored in a single variable for downstream processing, validation, or decision-making within the automation workflow. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this operation is classified under **UI Interaction & Information Retrieval** activities. It is the atomic operation that underpins nearly every bot, since the bot must first *sense* (read) the environment before it can *act* (click, type, decide). ### 1.2 Key Terminology | Term | Meaning | | :--- | :--- | | **Selector** | A string (XML/HTML-like) that uniquely identifies a UI element on the screen. | | **Anchor** | A reference element used to locate a nearby target element. | | **OCR** | Optical Character Recognition — converting image-based text into machine-readable text. | | **Attribute** | A property of a UI tag (e.g., `name`, `id`, `class`, `aaname`). | | **Output Variable** | The single memory slot that holds the extracted value. | | **Scrape** | The colloquial RPA term for "extract text from screen". | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **The "Robot Postman" Analogy**: Imagine a robot postman who must deliver mail to a specific apartment in a huge building. He cannot knock on every door. Instead, he reads the *mailbox nameplate* (the **selector**), looks at the *apartment number on a small sticker* (the **attribute**), and then picks out the *single letter* addressed to that person (the **piece of data**). The letter is then placed in his bag (the **output variable**) for later delivery (downstream processing). Extracting a single piece of data in RPA works exactly the same way: locate → identify → read → store. ### 1.4 Why This Topic is Critical > [!IMPORTANT] > According to the KTU 2024 Course Outcome mapping (aligned to **CO3: *Design and implement RPA bots to extract, manipulate, and validate data from diverse sources***), mastering single-data extraction is a **prerequisite** for advanced concepts like bulk data scraping, exception handling, and AI-based document understanding. Every enterprise bot starts with a single field — be it an invoice number, a stock price, or a transaction ID. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Data Extraction The "single piece of data" extraction model can be broken down into a **4-Stage Cognitive Pipeline** that mirrors how a human would read a screen: 1. **Stage 1 — Element Localization (The "Where")** - The bot parses the application's UI tree (DOM for web, Accessibility tree for desktop, or screen pixels for legacy systems). - It uses a **Selector** (an XPath-like string in UiPath, or a CSD path in Blue Prism) to pinpoint the *single* element of interest. - **Why it matters**: Without precise localization, the bot either fails or extracts the wrong data — a classic source of "flaky" bots. 2. **Stage 2 — Attribute Selection (The "Which Property")** - Once the element is found, the bot decides *which* property to read. A `
` tag on a webpage can have text, a tooltip, an inner HTML, an `aria-label`, a `value` attribute, etc. - **KTU 2024 Concept**: Choosing the right attribute is an examiner's favourite question. Always prefer `innerText` or `textContent` over `innerHTML` for clean output. 3. **Stage 3 — Reading Operation (The "How")** - The bot invokes a native method (DOM `.innerText`, `MSAA` API for Windows, or `Tesseract OCR` for image regions). - The reading mode can be: - **Native**: Uses the application's exposed API (fastest, most reliable). - **Full Text (OCR + Fuzzy)**: Reads all text regardless of visibility (used for Citrix/VDI). - **Visible Text**: Reads only what is visually rendered. - **Get Text**: The standard native method. 4. **Stage 4 — Storage & Type Coercion (The "Where It Goes")** - The extracted string is stored in an `Output → Value` variable. - If the value is a number or date, the bot performs **type conversion** using methods like `CInt()`, `CDbl()`, or `CDate()` (or `Convert.ToInt32` in C#-based UiPath). ### 2.2 The Generic Extraction Equation > [!NOTE] > Although RPA is not mathematics-heavy, we can express the *correctness* of an extraction operation using a Boolean framework. This is a high-yield concept in KTU papers. Let the target value extracted be denoted as $E$, the expected value as $V$, and the selector as $S$. The **Extraction Success Condition** is: $$E(S) = \begin{cases} V, & \text{if } S \text{ resolves to a unique live element and the attribute exists} \\ \varnothing, & \text{otherwise (timeout or null)} \end{cases}$$ The **Extraction Accuracy Metric** for evaluating a scraping bot: $$A = \frac{\text{Number of correct single extractions}}{\text{Total number of extraction attempts}} \times 100\%$$ A KTU-acceptable bot must maintain $A \geq 99\%$. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Activities & Properties) | Activity / Property | Purpose | Common Mistake to Avoid | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Get Text** | Reads text natively from a UI element. | Cannot read hidden elements. | | **Get Full Text** | Uses OCR + fallback methods; works on virtualized desktops. | Slower; needs Tesseract engine. | | **Get Visible Text** | Reads only visually rendered text. | Fails on collapsed/hidden menus. | | **Get OCR Text** | Pure OCR on a UI image region. | Sensitive to font and DPI. | | **Anchor Base** | Uses a nearby element to find the target. | Anchor itself can become stale. | | **WaitForReady** | Ensures UI is idle before reading. | Setting it to `None` causes race conditions. | | **Timeout (ms)** | Maximum wait time. Default $\approx 30{,}000$ ms. | Too low = false timeouts. | | **Output Value** | The variable holding the single piece. | Forgetting to declare type as `String`. | ### 2.4 Real-World Utility - **Banking**: Extracting the daily closing balance from a core banking system. - **Healthcare**: Pulling a patient ID from an Electronic Medical Record (EMR) screen. - **E-commerce**: Capturing the current price of a competitor's product. - **Logistics**: Reading a single tracking number from a courier website. ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix: The Extraction Workflow > [!NOTE] > Since this is an RPA module (not pure mathematics), the "derivation" below is a **procedural derivation** — i.e., a rigorous step-by-step deconstruction of the *Get Text* workflow that KTU examiners expect students to reproduce in the lab exam and theory. ### 3.1 The Five-Phase Generic Extraction Pipeline (Derivation) Let the user input request be a tuple $R = (Source, Attribute, Anchor, Timeout, StoreAs)$. The extraction workflow resolves as follows: **Phase 1: Initialization** The bot loads the application and waits for the main window to be ready. This is governed by: $$t_{\text{wait}} \geq t_{\text{render}} + \Delta t_{\text{buffer}}$$ where $t_{\text{wait}}$ is the configured wait time, $t_{\text{render}}$ is the application's render time, and $\Delta t_{\text{buffer}}$ is a small safety margin (typically $\mathbf{300 \text{ ms}}$). **Phase 2: Selector Resolution** The bot evaluates the selector $S$ against the live UI tree $T$. Formally: $$S \xrightarrow{\text{resolve}} \{n_1, n_2, \dots, n_k\} \subseteq T$$ The success condition requires $k = 1$ (exactly one matching node). If $k = 0$, a *SelectorNotFound* exception is raised after the timeout expires. **Phase 3: Attribute Reading** For the resolved node $n_1$, the bot retrieves the requested attribute $A$: $$v_{\text{raw}} = \text{read}(n_1, A)$$ Common attributes include: $$A \in \{\text{text}, \text{innerText}, \text{value}, \text{title}, \text{aria-label}\}$$ **Phase 4: Post-Processing (Regex / Trim / Convert)** Often, the raw value contains unwanted whitespace or symbols. A typical cleaning step is: $$v_{\text{clean}} = \text{Trim}\left(\text{RegExReplace}\left(v_{\text{raw}},\ \text{pattern } p,\ \text{replacement } r\right)\right)$$ For example, extracting digits from `"Order #INV-2024-0789 ="`: $$v_{\text{clean}} = \text{RegEx}(v_{\text{raw}},\ \backslash\text{d}+) = \texttt{"20240789"}$$ **Phase 5: Storage and Verification** The cleaned value is stored in the output variable. Optionally, the bot verifies: $$v_{\text{clean}} \neq \text{""} \land \text{IsMatch}(v_{\text{clean}},\ \text{expectedPattern})$$ If verification fails, the bot either retries or throws a *BusinessRuleException*. ### 3.2 Worked Example (End-to-End Single-Data Extraction) > **Scenario**: A bot must extract the *current temperature* from a weather website and store it as a number. **Step 1**: Open browser → navigate to `https://weather.com`. **Step 2**: Use **Indicate Element on Screen** to select the temperature `` element. UiPath generates a selector, e.g.: $$\text{Selector } S = \texttt{} \dots \texttt{}$$ **Step 3**: Drag and drop the `Get Text` activity, attach the selector, and set the output property to variable `strTemp`. **Step 4**: Apply a regex `\d+` to strip the degree symbol: $$v_{\text{clean}} = \text{RegEx}\left(\texttt{"72°"},\ \backslash\text{d}+\right) = \texttt{"72"}$$ **Step 5**: Convert to integer and log: $$T = \text{CInt}(v_{\text{clean}}) = 72$$ **Step 6**: Add a verification step: $$\text{If } T > -50 \land T < 60 \rightarrow \text{Proceed}, \text{ else} \rightarrow \text{Retry}$$ ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Which Activity to Choose? | Scenario | Recommended Activity | Justification | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Modern web app (Chrome/Edge) | `Get Text` | Native DOM access, fast. | | Citrix / VDI / RDP screen | `Get Full Text` or `Get OCR Text` | No DOM available; must use OCR. | | Desktop app with hidden labels | `Get Attribute` (`aaname`/`text`) | Native APIs expose hidden metadata. | | PDF with selectable text | `Read PDF Text` | Dedicated PDF parsing. | | PDF as scanned image | `Read PDF with OCR` | Tesseract needed. | | Email body content | `Get Mail Text` (Outlook/Gmail) | MAPI / IMAP integration. | | Terminal / Mainframe | `Get Terminal Text` | VT100 emulation. | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!IMPORTANT] > Per KTU 2024 Scheme visual learning standards, the following Mermaid block diagrams illustrate the architectural flow of single-data extraction. Node IDs are kept strictly alphanumeric per the safety protocol. ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of Single-Data Extraction ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Start Trigger] --> B0[Launch Target Application] B0 --> C0[Wait For Ready State] C0 --> D0{Select Activity} D0 --> E0[Get Text Native] D0 --> E1[Get Full Text OCR] D0 --> E2[Get Visible Text] D0 --> E3[Get Attribute] E0 --> F0[Resolve Selector] E1 --> F0 E2 --> F0 E3 --> F0 F0 --> G0{Element Found?} G0 -- No --> H0[Wait And Retry Until Timeout] H0 --> F0 G0 -- Yes --> I0[Read Target Attribute] I0 --> J0[Apply Regex And Trim] J0 --> K0[Type Convert To String Int Or Date] K0 --> L0[Store In Output Variable] L0 --> M0{Validation Pass?} M0 -- No --> N0[Raise Business Exception] M0 -- Yes --> O0[Pass To Next Workflow Step] O0 --> P0[End] N0 --> P0 ``` ### 4.2 State Diagram of an Extraction Operation ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> Searching: Trigger Event Searching --> Found: Selector Matches Searching --> Waiting: Selector Missing Waiting --> Searching: Retry Tick Waiting --> Timeout: Max Wait Exceeded Found --> Reading Reading --> Cleaning: Post Process Regex Cleaning --> Validating Validating --> Stored: Pattern OK Validating --> Exception: Pattern Fail Stored --> [*] Timeout --> [*] Exception --> [*] ``` ### 4.3 Selector-Anchor Relationship Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A0[Anchor Label Nearby Element] --> B0[Use Anchor Base Activity] B0 --> C0[Compute Relative Offset] C0 --> D0[Locate Target Single Data Element] D0 --> E0[Extract Value] E0 --> F0[Output Variable] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Set A]** *Define the term "Extracting a Single Piece of Data" in the context of RPA. Mention any two activities used for this purpose.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (2 marks):** Extracting a single piece of data in RPA is the process of retrieving one specific unit of information (e.g., a name, an invoice number, a temperature) from a user interface element or document and storing it in a single output variable for further processing by the bot. > > **Activities (1 mark):** > 1. **Get Text** — used for native UI elements. > 2. **Get OCR Text** — used for virtualized/Citrix screens. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Set B]** *Differentiate between the `Get Text` and `Get Full Text` activities in UiPath. In which scenario would you prefer `Get Full Text`?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Get Text | Get Full Text | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Mechanism | Reads from the native UI/accessibility API. | Uses OCR (Tesseract) + native fallback. | | Speed | Very fast. | Slower (image processing overhead). | | Visibility | Fails on hidden elements. | Can read hidden text. | | **Use Case (1 mark)** | Standard web/desktop apps. | **Citrix, VDI, RDP, or remote desktop screens** where the DOM is not exposed. | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Dec]** **(a)** Explain the *five-phase generic extraction pipeline* used for extracting a single piece of data in an RPA bot. List the activities used in each phase. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A bank RPA bot must extract the *available balance* from an account summary webpage. The raw extracted text is `"Available Balance: ₹ 1,25,430.50 INR"`. Design a step-by-step workflow to extract only the numeric value `125430.50` and store it as a `Double` data type. Show your regex and conversion logic. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Five-Phase Generic Extraction Pipeline (7 marks):** 1. **Phase 1 – Initialization (1 mark):** Launch the target application and wait for the main window to be in a "ready" state. Use a `Delay` or `Element Exists` activity to ensure the UI is idle. 2. **Phase 2 – Selector Resolution (1 mark):** Use the `Indicate Element` feature to generate a robust selector. Verify that the selector uniquely identifies one element by testing it in the UI Explorer. 3. **Phase 3 – Attribute Reading (2 marks):** Choose the correct activity based on the source: - Web/Native → `Get Text` - Virtualized → `Get Full Text` / `Get OCR Text` - PDF → `Read PDF Text` 4. **Phase 4 – Post-Processing (1.5 marks):** Apply `Trim`, `Regex`, or `Replace` to clean the raw text. Use the `Matches` activity or `System.Text.RegularExpressions.Regex.Match`. 5. **Phase 5 – Storage & Validation (1.5 marks):** Store the cleaned value in a typed variable (`String`, `Int32`, `Double`, `DateTime`) and validate it against a business rule. On failure, throw a `BusinessRuleException`. **(b) Workflow Design for Balance Extraction (7 marks):** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Use `Get Text` with selector pointing to the balance label. Store raw output in `strRaw`. $$\text{strRaw} = \texttt{"Available Balance: ₹ 1,25,430.50 INR"}$$ **Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply a regex to capture digits, commas, and the decimal point. The pattern is: $$p = \backslash\text{d{1,3}(?:,\backslash d{2,3})*\.\backslash d\{2\}$$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Use `RegEx.Replace` or `Matches` activity in UiPath to extract the first match: $$\text{strNum} = \texttt{"1,25,430.50"}$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Remove the comma using `Replace`: $$\text{strNumClean} = \text{Replace}(\text{strNum},\ \texttt{","},\ \texttt{""}) = \texttt{"125430.50"}$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Convert to `Double`: $$\text{balance} = \text{Convert.ToDouble}(\text{strNumClean}) = 125430.50$$ **Validation:** Confirm that `balance > 0`. If false, raise a `BusinessRuleException("Invalid balance read")`. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Dec – Alternative]** **(a)** Compare and contrast *Native Screen Scraping*, *Full-Text Scraping*, and *OCR-based extraction*. For each method, state one real-world scenario where it is most suitable. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An insurance company has automated claims processing. The bot must read the *claim amount* from a Citrix-hosted legacy application. Design an RPA workflow that uses the appropriate extraction activity, handles the timeout exception, and validates the claim amount is between `₹100` and `₹10,00,000`. Provide the pseudocode. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):** | Feature | Native Screen Scraping | Full-Text Scraping | OCR-based Extraction | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Mechanism** | Uses UI element's accessibility tree. | Combines native + OCR. | Pure image-to-text via Tesseract. | | **Accuracy** | Highest (100%). | High (95–99%). | Moderate (85–95%, font-dependent). | | **Speed** | Fastest. | Moderate. | Slowest. | | **Use Case (1.5 marks each)** | Modern web forms. | Hybrid apps (partial DOM). | Scanned PDFs, images, Citrix screens. | **(b) Pseudocode for Citrix Claim Extraction (7 marks):** ``` 1. OPEN Citrix Application "ClaimsPortal" 2. NAVIGATE To Claim ID "CLM-2024-001" 3. USE Activity "Get Full Text" Selector = "" Output -> strRawAmount Timeout = 30000 ms 4. TRY 5. IF Timeout Occurs 6. THROW new TimeoutException("Claim field not loaded") 7. END IF 8. strClean = RegEx.Match(strRawAmount, "\d+\.\d{2}").Value 9. claimAmount = Convert.ToDouble(strClean) 10. IF claimAmount < 100 OR claimAmount > 1000000 THEN 11. THROW new BusinessRuleException("Claim amount out of range") 12. END IF 13. WRITE claimAmount to Database Table "Claims" 14. CATCH Exception ex 15. LOG ex.Message 16. SEND Email to Supervisor 17. END TRY ``` **Mark allocation:** Steps 1–3 (2 marks), Steps 4–8 (2 marks), Steps 9–13 (2 marks), Steps 14–17 (1 mark). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing `Get Text` with `Get Attribute`**: `Get Text` only returns the visible text; `Get Attribute` allows any property (e.g., `title`, `value`). Using the wrong one costs 1–2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting to handle null/empty strings**: If the bot extracts `""`, downstream type conversion (`CInt`) will crash. Always validate. > 3. **Hardcoding selectors with index `:idx='3'`**: These break when the UI changes. Use stable attributes like `aaname` or `name`. > 4. **Not setting `WaitForReady` to `Interactive` or `Complete`**: This causes race conditions where the bot reads the screen *before* it is updated. > 5. **Ignoring the timeout property**: Default is 30 seconds; for slow systems, increase to 60+ seconds. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision Bullets:** - **Definition**: Extracting a single piece of data = reading *one* specific value from a UI/document and storing it in one variable. - **Core Activities**: `Get Text`, `Get Full Text`, `Get Visible Text`, `Get OCR Text`, `Get Attribute`, `Read PDF Text`. - **Selector**: A unique XML-like path; use UI Explorer to verify. Avoid dynamic indices. - **Anchor Base**: Use when the target has no unique selector — anchor to a nearby labelled element. - **OCR Engine**: Tesseract is default in UiPath; supports 60+ languages. Sensitive to font size, DPI, and noise. - **Post-Processing**: Always apply `Trim` + `Regex` to clean raw output. - **Type Conversion**: `CInt`, `CDbl`, `CDate`, or `Convert.ToInt32` (C#). Wrapping in `TryParse` is safer. - **Timeout**: Default = 30,000 ms. Increase for legacy systems. Wrap in **Try-Catch** with a `Retry` scope. - **Validation**: Always validate range, format (regex), and null-check before using the value. - **Accuracy Target**: Production-grade bots must achieve $\geq 99\%$ extraction accuracy. - **Common Exam Trap**: Distinguish between *scraping* (multiple values) and *extracting* (single value) — KTU questions test this distinction. - **Modern Alternative**: For complex unstructured data, traditional extraction is being replaced by **Document Understanding** (AI/ML-based), but for the KTU 2024 syllabus, the classical `Get Text` family is the expected answer.

topics.title: Anchors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Anchors ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **ANCHOR (UiPath RPA – KTU 2024 Definition):** > An **Anchor** is a UI Automation mechanism in which a *target UI element* (which is difficult to uniquely identify) is located by referencing a neighbouring, more reliable UI element called the **Anchor Base**. The relative spatial relationship between the anchor and the target is then used by the **Find Relative Element** activity to resolve the desired control on the screen. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Anchors fall under the broader classification of **Selector Strategies** in Robotic Process Automation, specifically under the *Robotic Framework – UI Interaction* domain of UiPath Studio. ### 1.2 Two Activities Used in the Anchor Mechanism UiPath provides **two co-operating activities** under the `UI Automation > Element > Find` category: 1. **Anchor Base** – Identifies the *known*, stable element. It uses a regular, fully-qualified selector. 2. **Find Relative Element** – Identifies the *unknown/hard-to-find* element by describing where it lives **relative to** the anchor. > [!NOTE] > The `Anchor Base` activity **must** be placed *inside* the `Find Relative Element` activity container in UiPath Studio. This nesting is a frequent source of marks lost in KTU practical exams. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a stranger trying to locate **House #47** on a long road where all houses look identical and have no number plates visible. You cannot find **#47** directly. However, you *can* easily find the **red coloured building** which is always **2 houses to the right of #47**. - **Red building** = *Anchor Base* (easy to find, stable selector) - **2 houses to the right** = *Position/Offset* (relative spatial relationship) - **House #47** = *Target UI element* (the one you actually need) This is exactly how Anchors function in RPA. They trade *absolute* identification for *relative* identification, dramatically improving robustness when UIs are dynamic, repetitive, or selector-unfriendly. ### 1.4 When Are Anchors Required? (KTU Board Favourite Question) > [!IMPORTANT] > Use Anchors when: > - The target element has **no unique attributes** (e.g., a generic textbox used 50 times on a form). > - The target element’s selector changes **dynamically** (e.g., `idx` or `tableRow` keeps changing). > - The target element is **invisible, overlapped, or duplicated** on screen. > - A *stable label* or *heading* is always present near the target field. ### 1.5 Key Constants / Default Values in UiPath Anchors | Parameter | Default | Notes | |---|---|---| | Anchor Position | `Auto` | UiPath automatically picks the nearest match. | | WaitForReady | `Interactive` | Waits for partial page load. | | Timeout | **30000 ms** (30 s) | Max time UiPath searches for the anchor. | | Accuracy | `0.5` | Visual similarity threshold for image anchors. | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Architecture of the Anchor Mechanism The Anchor workflow follows a strict 4-stage pipeline: 1. **Anchor Resolution Stage** The `Anchor Base` activity resolves its own selector against the live application. If found, the **bounding rectangle (clip region)** of the anchor is captured. 2. **Relative Search Stage** `Find Relative Element` uses the anchor’s bounding box as the **origin reference (0,0)** and scans outward based on the `Position` property (Top, Bottom, Left, Right, Top-Right, etc.). 3. **Disambiguation Stage** If multiple candidates match the relative description, UiPath applies the **CV/OCR heuristics** (if `Accuracy` is enabled) and selects the closest one geometrically. 4. **Return Stage** The resolved `UiElement` is passed downstream as the `Output Element`, ready for `Type Into`, `Click`, `Get Text`, etc. ### 2.2 The 7 Anchor Position Values The `AnchorPosition` property of `Find Relative Element` accepts exactly **7 values**: | Position Value | Geometric Meaning | Typical Use Case | |---|---|---| | `Auto` | UiPath auto-detects nearest match | General purpose (default) | | `Top` | Target is directly above the anchor | Column headers above cells | | `Bottom` | Target is directly below the anchor | Sub-labels under titles | | `Left` | Target is to the left of anchor | Labels preceding input fields | | `Right` | Target is to the right of anchor | Input fields following labels | | `TopLeft` | Target is top-left of anchor | Tab navigation cells | | `BottomRight` | Target is bottom-right of anchor | Status icons inside rows | > [!NOTE] > For KTU 2024 exams, students **must** know that `AnchorPosition` is a property of `Find Relative Element` and **NOT** of `Anchor Base`. A common pitfall is mixing up the two. ### 2.3 Properties of `Anchor Base` Activity - `Selector` – Standard XPath-like UI selector string. - `TimeoutMS` – Time to wait before throwing an error. Default **30000 ms**. - `WaitForReady` – `None` / `Interactive` / `Complete`. Default `Interactive`. - `Output Element` – A `UiElement` variable, consumed internally by the parent. ### 2.4 Properties of `Find Relative Element` Activity - `AnchorPosition` – One of the 7 values above. - `AnchorSelector` – Auto-generated from the nested Anchor Base. - `Target.Anchorable` – A nested child activity (e.g., `Type Into`, `Click`) executed on the resolved element. - `TimeoutMS` – Default **30000 ms**. ### 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates *all anchor-specific* selector-related data points testable in KTU 2024 ESE. | Concept | Symbolic Representation | Value / Default | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Anchor Search Window Radius | $R_{search}$ | **50** | pixels | | Default Timeout | $T_{default}$ | **30000** | ms (milliseconds) | | Default WaitForReady | $W_{default}$ | `Interactive` | — | | Default Accuracy Threshold | $A_{default}$ | **0.5** (50%) | ratio | | Position Enum Cardinality | $\vert P \vert$ | **7** | discrete values | | Maximum Nested Anchors | $N_{max}$ | **3** (recommended) | count | > [!NOTE] > Although these are not “physical” formulas, the KTU Board has a strong tendency to ask **numeric defaults** in Part A (3-mark) questions. Memorise $T_{default} = 30000\,ms$ and $A_{default} = 0.5$. ### 2.6 Engineering Utility of Anchors Anchors are deployed in real-world automation across: - **Banking & Finance** – Locating dynamic transaction rows in legacy mainframe emulators (3270/5250). - **Healthcare HIS** – Identifying patient records where row indices shift on every refresh. - **ERP Automation** (SAP, Oracle) – Resolving input fields whose `id` attributes change per session. - **Web Scraping** – Pulling prices next to product names on e-commerce dashboards. - **Citizen Development** – Used extensively in **Citizen Developer** programs because anchors are more *visual* and less *technical* than XPath. ### 2.7 Advantages and Limitations **Advantages** - Robust against dynamic `idx` and `tableRow` selectors. - Visually intuitive — non-coders can build workflows. - Combines classical selectors with Computer Vision (CV). **Limitations** - Performance overhead: 2 selectors resolved per action. - Brittle when anchor itself becomes dynamic. - Cannot span across multiple windows/frames. - Maximum recommended nesting depth: **3 levels** to avoid maintainability collapse. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Coordinate-Geometry Model of Anchor Resolution Let the Anchor Base $A$ have a bounding rectangle defined by its top-left corner coordinates $(x_A^{min}, y_A^{min})$ and bottom-right corner $(x_A^{max}, y_A^{max})$. The **geometric centroid** of the anchor is: $$ \bar{x}_A = \frac{x_A^{min} + x_A^{max}}{2}, \quad \bar{y}_A = \frac{y_A^{min} + y_A^{max}}{2} $$ The target element $T$ must lie within an L-shaped or directional region relative to $\bar{A}$. For example, for `AnchorPosition = "Right"`, the search region is: $$ \mathcal{R}_{Right} = \left\{ (x, y) \;\middle|\; x > \bar{x}_A \;\land\; \vert y - \bar{y}_A \vert \le \Delta y \right\} $$ where $\Delta y$ is a vertical tolerance band. The candidate set is then: $$ \mathcal{C} = \left\{ T_i \;\middle|\; \text{center}(T_i) \in \mathcal{R}_{Right} \right\} $$ UiPath’s disambiguation metric for a candidate $T_i$ is the **Euclidean distance** to the anchor centroid: $$ d_i = \sqrt{(x_{T_i} - \bar{x}_A)^2 + (y_{T_i} - \bar{y}_A)^2} $$ The final resolved target is: $$ T^{*} = \arg\min_{T_i \in \mathcal{C}} d_i $$ This explains *why* the *closest* element is selected when multiple candidates qualify. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style) **Problem:** An anchor element `Label1` is located at screen coordinates **(100, 200)** to **(150, 230)**. Three candidate text boxes exist on screen: - $T_1$ at center (180, 215) - $T_2$ at center (180, 280) - $T_3$ at center (90, 215) The `AnchorPosition` is set to `Right`. Determine which text box is selected. **Solution:** Step 1 — Compute anchor centroid: $$ \bar{x}_A = \frac{100 + 150}{2} = 125, \quad \bar{y}_A = \frac{200 + 230}{2} = 215 $$ Step 2 — Apply Right-side filter condition $x > 125$: $$ T_1: 180 > 125 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{qualifies} $$ $$ T_2: 180 > 125 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{qualifies} $$ $$ T_3: 90 > 125 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{rejected} $$ Step 3 — Compute distances to anchor centroid (125, 215): $$ d_1 = \sqrt{(180 - 125)^2 + (215 - 215)^2} = \sqrt{55^2} = 55 $$ $$ d_2 = \sqrt{(180 - 125)^2 + (280 - 215)^2} = \sqrt{55^2 + 65^2} = \sqrt{3025 + 4225} = \sqrt{7250} \approx 85.15 $$ Step 4 — Apply the arg-min rule: $$ T^{*} = \arg\min\{55, 85.15\} = T_1 $$ **Result:** UiPath will perform the `Type Into` (or whichever child activity is nested) on **$T_1$**, the text box immediately to the right of the label. > [!NOTE] > Notice how the math mirrors exactly what UiPath executes at runtime. KTU sometimes awards bonus marks for this kind of geometric justification. ### 3.3 Implementation Algorithm in UiPath Studio (Workflow Steps) 1. Open **UiPath Studio** → New Process → name it `Anchor_Demo`. 2. Add an `Attach Browser` (or `Attach Window`) for the target application. 3.
