> **[Q3.B. (b)] List and explain the steps to publish a UiPath project using the Publish Utility, including all key fields in the Publish dialog. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

---

**Solution to Q3.B(a) – 7 Marks:**

**Role of `Monitor Events` Activity:**
The `Monitor Events` activity is a *container* (parent) in UiPath Studio that hosts one or more **trigger child activities**. It performs the following functions:
- It **opens a "Trigger Scope"** in which the developer places the *response logic*.
- It **binds** each child trigger to a *polling loop* governed by a single `PollingInterval`.
- It **manages the state machine** of all triggers (`Idle → Monitoring → Fired`).
- It **propagates exceptions** in a unified `Try-Catch` envelope to the parent scope.

**Comparison Table – Image vs Element Trigger:**

| Parameter | **Image Trigger** | **Element Trigger** |
|---|---|---|
| Detection Source | Screen bitmap (pixels) | UIA / AA tree (selectors) |
| Algorithm | NCC (Normalized Cross-Correlation) | Selector resolution against live tree |
| Robustness to Resize | **Low** – fails on DPI change | **High** – relative selectors adapt |
| Typical Environment | Citrix, RDP, legacy green-screen | Win32, WPF, SAP, browsers |
| Firing Accuracy Control | *Accuracy* (0.0 – 1.0) | *Selector validity* (boolean) |
| CPU Overhead | **High** (screen capture + NCC) | **Low** (tree query) |
| Polling Default | 500 ms | 500 ms |
| When Selector Fails | Use as fallback | Use as primary |

> **Real-World Insight:** Image triggers are a *last-resort* mechanism; the *Robotic Operating Model (ROM)* always encourages **selector-first** design.

---

**Solution to Q3.B(b) – 7 Marks:**

**Steps to Publish using the Publish Utility:**

1. **Step 1 – Open the project in UiPath Studio.** Ensure it compiles cleanly.
2. **Step 2 – Click `Validate Project`** (or `Ctrl+Shift+V`). Resolve all errors and warnings.
3. **Step 3 – Click the `Publish` button** in the *Design* ribbon (or `Ctrl+P`).
4. **Step 4 – Configure the Publish dialog:**

   | Field | Description | Example |
   |---|---|---|
   | **Package Name** | Name of the `.nupkg` (must match Studio project name) | `ImageTriggerDemo` |
   | **Version** | Semantic version `Major.Minor.Patch` | `1.0.0` |
   | **Project Type** | Process / Library / Template | `Process` |
   | **Source** | Where the package is uploaded | `Orchestrator Tenant Process Feed` |
   | **Entry Point** | The `.xaml` invoked at runtime | `Main.xaml` |
   | **Description** | Release notes | `v1.0 – Image + Element triggers` |
   | **Icon** | 16×16 PNG (auto) | – |
   | **Labels** | Tags for governance | `Finance`, `Production` |

5. **Step 5 – Click `Publish`.** A progress bar runs; a *toast* message confirms success. The `.nupkg` is stored at:
   - `%ProgramData%\UiPath\Packages\ImageTriggerDemo_1.0.0.nupkg`
   - **and** the connected Orchestrator tenant feed (if logged in).
6. **Step 6 – Verify** in Orchestrator → `Tenant` → `Packages` → search by name. A green status indicates a *signed, validated* package.
7. **Step 7 – Promote to Production** by creating a *Process* and assigning to a *Robot + Folder*.

> **Common Pitfall:** Students often forget that *Version* follows *SemVer 2.0*; incrementing a build requires the `Patch` digit to increase. A duplicate `1.0.0` publish will **overwrite** the previous package (unless *auto-versioning* is enabled in Orchestrator).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here):**
> 1. **Confusing "Publishing" with "Deploying"** – Publishing *creates* the `.nupkg`; deployment is the *Orchestrator-level* allocation. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the formula** $\text{NCC} \geq A$ – an examiner will deduct 1 mark if you only describe *image similarity* qualitatively.
> 3. **Not mentioning PollingInterval** – a topper always includes the *500 ms* default.
> 4. **Mixing up Element vs Image triggers** – the *Citrix* scenario always requires an **Image** trigger, not an Element one. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Skipping the cyclic Maintenance arrow** in the lifecycle diagram – examiners award a *bonus mark* for showing the feedback loop.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember (Exam-Focused Highlights):**

- **Trigger Types (3):** `System / Hotkey`, `Element / UI`, `Image / Pixel` – all are children of the **`Monitor Events`** activity.
- **Image Trigger Algorithm:** **NCC (Normalized Cross-Correlation)**.
- **Firing Condition:** $\text{NCC}(I, T) \geq A$ where $A$ is the user-set **Accuracy** (default `0.8`).
- **Polling Interval (Δt):** Default `500 ms`; range `100 ms – 5,000 ms`. Lower = faster but higher CPU.
- **Element Trigger Modes:** `Appeared` and `Disappeared`.
- **Image Trigger Strength:** Universal (any visible pixel).
- **Element Trigger Strength:** Stable, low CPU, selector-based.
- **Publish Utility Output:** A **`.nupkg`** (NuGet Open Packaging Convention).
- **Publish Key Fields:** *Package Name*, *Version* (SemVer), *Source*, *Entry Point*, *Description*, *Project Type*.
- **Default Local Feed Path:** `%ProgramData%\UiPath\Packages\`.
- **Deployment Lifecycle:** `Studio → Validate → Publish → Orchestrator → Process → Robot → Trigger → Execute → Monitor → Maintain`.
- **Maintenance Activities:** Daily job review, weekly log mining, monthly selector refactoring, quarterly credential rotation.
- **Citrix Rule of Thumb:** *Always use Image triggers in Citrix*; selectors will fail.
- **Real-Time Tip:** A *Repeat Trigger* value of `True` makes the trigger re-arm after firing – useful for *batch* scenarios.
- **Best Practice:** *Selector-first*, Image-as-fallback.
- **SemVer:** Patch `1.0.0 → 1.0.1` (bug-fix), Minor `1.0.0 → 1.1.0` (feature), Major `1.0.0 → 2.0.0` (breaking).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creation of Server
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Creation of Server

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🖥️ Creation of Server in Robotic Process Automation (RPA)

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> In the context of Robotic Process Automation (RPA), a **Server** refers to a centralized computing environment (either on-premise or cloud-hosted) that acts as the command-and-control hub for managing, scheduling, monitoring, and orchestrating automated bots (software robots) across an enterprise. In UiPath, this server is the **UiPath Orchestrator**; in Automation Anywhere, it is the **Control Room**; and in Blue Prism, it is the **Application Server / Runtime Resource**.

A server in RPA is a **multi-tenant, web-based management console** built on a service-oriented architecture (SOA) that exposes REST APIs for bot lifecycle management, credential vaulting, queue management, and audit logging.

### 1.2 Key Technical Constituents of an RPA Server

| Component | Role | Common Implementation |
|---|---|---|
| **Web Application** | User Interface (UI) for administrators and developers | ASP.NET / Java EE / Angular |
| **Database** | Persistent storage of robots, queues, logs, schedules | SQL Server, PostgreSQL, MongoDB |
| **Application Server** | Hosts business logic and REST APIs | IIS (Windows) / Tomcat (Linux) |
| **Message Broker** | Communication between Orchestrator and Studio/Robot | WebSocket, SignalR, gRPC |
| **Identity Provider** | Authentication & Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) | Active Directory, LDAP, OAuth 2.0 |
| **Credential Vault** | Secure storage of robot credentials | HashiCorp Vault, CyberArk, Azure Key Vault |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Control Tower

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:**
> Think of an RPA Server as the **Air Traffic Control Tower** of a busy international airport.
> - The **towers (servers)** don't fly the planes themselves; they **coordinate, monitor, and guide** them.
> - The **planes (software robots/bots)** do the actual "flying" — executing the business tasks on your applications.
> - The **airport managers (RPA developers/admins)** sit inside the tower watching radar screens (dashboards) and assigning take-off slots (schedules), runways (machines), and flight plans (processes).
> - Just as a tower needs radar, runways, and licensed controllers, an RPA server needs a database, robots, and a licensed orchestrator.

### 1.4 Why a Server is Required in RPA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without a central server, RPA deployments suffer from the **"Island of Automation"** problem — bots run unmanaged, unmonitored, and unscheduled on individual desktops, leading to no scalability, no governance, and no ROI measurement. The server solves this by providing **Centralized Governance**.

**Standard Metrics in RPA Server Management:**
- **Uptime SLA:** **99.9%** (Industry Standard)
- **Concurrent Bot Capacity:** **1 to 10,000+** depending on license tier
- **Authentication:** **OAuth 2.0 / SAML 2.0**
- **Communication Protocol:** **HTTPS (port 443) / WSS (port 443)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2️⃣ Deep Theoretical Analysis — Architecture & Operational Layers

## 2.1 The Three-Tier Architecture of an RPA Server

The creation of a server is fundamentally the instantiation of a **three-tier architecture**:

### Tier 1 — Presentation Layer (The Web Console)
- Browser-based dashboard for tenant admins, robot managers, and developers.
- Built on modern front-end frameworks (Angular, React).
- Communicates with backend via **RESTful JSON APIs** over **HTTPS**.

### Tier 2 — Application Layer (The Business Logic)
- Handles authentication, authorization, scheduling, queueing, and logging.
- Exposes services via REST endpoints.
- Uses **WebSocket (WSS)** for real-time push notifications (e.g., job status updates) to the robots.

### Tier 3 — Data Layer (The Persistent Store)
- Stores all configuration: Tenants, Robots, Machines, Processes, Queues, Schedules, Logs, Audit Trails.
- Uses **SQL Server / PostgreSQL** as the primary RDBMS.
- Uses **Elasticsearch / Splunk** for log indexing (optional but enterprise-grade).

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of Server Creation

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind each step:**
> RPA server creation is not a single click — it follows a deterministic sequence where each prerequisite enables the next.

1. **Infrastructure Provisioning** — Allocate VM/Cloud instance (Azure VM, AWS EC2, on-prem) with minimum **8 vCPU, 16 GB RAM, 100 GB SSD**.
2. **OS Hardening** — Install Windows Server 2019/2022 or Linux (Ubuntu 20.04 LTS) with latest security patches.
3. **Database Deployment** — Install & configure SQL Server 2019, create database `UiPath` with collation `SQL_Latin1_General_CP1_CI_AS`.
4. **Application Server Setup** — Install IIS (Internet Information Services) with required roles.
5. **Orchestrator Binary Deployment** — Deploy the Orchestrator `.zip` package to IIS.
6. **Connection String Configuration** — Provide DB credentials in `web.config` or `appsettings.json`.
7. **License Activation** — Apply Enterprise/Community license XML.
8. **Tenant & Folder Provisioning** — Create logical isolation units for departments.
9. **Robot Unattended Setup** — Install UiPath Robot service on target machines and connect via **Machine Key**.
10. **High Availability (HA) Configuration** — Set up load balancer (e.g., F5, Azure Application Gateway) across **N+1** server nodes.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Server Capacity & Sizing Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Standard | Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Number of Bot Servers** | $N_{server} = \lceil \frac{B_{total} \times L_{avg}}{C_{single} \times U_{target}} \rceil$ | Count | Hardware sizing |
| **DB Storage per Year** | $S_{db} = B_{active} \times L_{avg} \times 365 \times C_{log}$ | GB | Capacity planning |
| **License Compliance Ratio** | $R_{LC} = \frac{B_{concurrent}}{L_{purchased}} \leq 1$ | Ratio | Audit metric |
| **Mean Time To Recovery** | $MTTR = \frac{\sum T_{down_i}}{N_{failures}}$ | Hours | SLA tracking |
| **Server Uptime** | $U_{pct} = \left(1 - \frac{T_{down}}{T_{period}}\right) \times 100$ | % | SLA metric |
| **Concurrent Session Limit** | $S_{max} = \min(C_{cpu}, C_{ram}, L_{max})$ | Sessions | Bottleneck identification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Variable Definitions for the Formulas above:**
> - $B_{total}$ = Total number of bots
> - $L_{avg}$ = Average load (transactions/day per bot)
> - $C_{single}$ = Single server capacity
> - $U_{target}$ = Target utilization (typically **0.70** to **0.80**)
> - $C_{log}$ = Average log size per transaction (**~2 KB**)
> - $T_{period}$ = Total measurement period in hours

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking Sector:** Server manages 500+ bots processing loan applications 24/7.
- **Healthcare:** Schedules patient data migration bots during off-peak hours (2 AM – 5 AM).
- **BPO/ITES:** Centralized queue distribution across 10,000+ concurrent attended bots.
- **Supply Chain:** Disaster-recovery orchestrated across geographically distributed data centers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3️⃣ Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Sizing the Number of Servers

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A mid-sized insurance firm wants to deploy **200 bots**. Each bot executes an average of **500 transactions per day**. A single server node can handle **50 concurrent bots** at a target utilization of **75%**. Calculate the number of server nodes required.

### Step 1 — Identify Given Parameters
- $B_{total} = 200$ bots
- $L_{avg} = 500$ transactions/day/bot
- $C_{single} = 50$ concurrent bots per server
- $U_{target} = 0.75$

### Step 2 — Apply the Sizing Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{server} &= \left\lceil \frac{B_{total} \times L_{avg}}{C_{single} \times U_{target}} \right\rceil \\[8pt]
&= \left\lceil \frac{200 \times 500}{50 \times 0.75} \right\rceil \\[8pt]
&= \left\lceil \frac{100{,}000}{37.5} \right\rceil \\[8pt]
&= \lceil 2666.67 \rceil \\[8pt]
&= 2667 \text{ servers (theoretical maximum)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Realistic Bottleneck Adjustment

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{max} &= \min(C_{cpu}, C_{ram}, L_{max}) \\[8pt]
&= \min(64, 128, 200) \\[8pt]
&= 64 \text{ concurrent sessions (RAM-limited)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The theoretical 2667 comes from a 1:1 transaction-to-server assumption, which is unrealistic. In practice, a single server can multiplex multiple transactions. The bottleneck is usually RAM (128 GB / 2 GB per session = 64 sessions).

### Step 4 — Final Practical Answer

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{server,practical} &= \left\lceil \frac{B_{total}}{S_{max}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{200}{64} \right\rceil = \lceil 3.125 \rceil = 4 \text{ servers}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The firm needs **4 server nodes** in an N+1 redundancy model → **5 total servers** (1 hot standby).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Database Storage Estimation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> Calculate the annual database storage required for an RPA deployment with **150 active bots**, each producing an average of **300 log entries per day**, where each log entry is approximately **2 KB** in size.

### Step 1 — Given Parameters
- $B_{active} = 150$ bots
- $L_{avg} = 300$ logs/day/bot
- $C_{log} = 2$ KB = $2 \times 1024$ bytes
- Days in year = 365

### Step 2 — Apply Storage Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{db} &= B_{active} \times L_{avg} \times 365 \times C_{log} \\[8pt]
&= 150 \times 300 \times 365 \times 2 \text{ KB} \\[8pt]
&= 150 \times 300 \times 365 \times 2 \\[8pt]
&= 32{,}850{,}000 \text{ KB} \\[8pt]
&= \frac{32{,}850{,}000}{1024 \times 1024} \text{ GB} \\[8pt]
&= 31.33 \text{ GB/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Add 40% Index & Backup Overhead

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{total} &= S_{db} \times 1.40 = 31.33 \times 1.40 = 43.86 \text{ GB/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** With a **3-year retention policy**, total storage = $43.86 \times 3 \approx 131.6$ GB. Recommended provisioning: **500 GB SSD** with quarterly archiving.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Server Uptime & SLA Compliance

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> An RPA server was down for a total of **4 hours 22 minutes** in a **30-day month**. Determine if it meets the **99.9%** SLA.

### Step 1 — Compute Total Period in Hours

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{period} &= 30 \text{ days} \times 24 \text{ hours/day} = 720 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Convert Downtime to Hours

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{down} &= 4 + \frac{22}{60} = 4.3667 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Uptime Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{pct} &= \left(1 - \frac{T_{down}}{T_{period}}\right) \times 100 \\[8pt]
&= \left(1 - \frac{4.3667}{720}\right) \times 100 \\[8pt]
&= \left(1 - 0.006065\right) \times 100 \\[8pt]
&= 99.3935\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compare with SLA

$$
\begin{aligned}
99.3935\% &< 99.9\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{SLA BREACHED}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Allowable downtime per month for 99.9% SLA:**
> $$T_{down,allowed} = 720 \times 0.001 = 0.72 \text{ hours} = 43.2 \text{ minutes}$$
> The actual downtime of **4h 22m** is **6× over the limit**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4️⃣ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Server Creation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Infrastructure Request] --> B[Provision VM / Cloud Instance]
    B --> C[Install OS Windows Server 2019]
    C --> D[Install IIS Web Server]
    D --> E[Install SQL Server 2019]
    E --> F[Create Orchestrator Database]
    F --> G[Deploy Orchestrator Web App]
    G --> H[Configure Connection Strings]
    H --> I[Apply License]
    I --> J[Create Tenant and Folders]
    J --> K[Define Roles and Permissions]
    K --> L[Register Unattended Robots]
    L --> M[Create Machine Templates]
    M --> N[Deploy First Process]
    N --> O[Enable Monitoring and Alerts]
    O --> P[Server Ready for Production]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style P fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style I fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
    style L fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
```

## 4.2 Three-Tier Server Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PRES[Presentation Layer - Browser]
        UI1[Admin Dashboard]
        UI2[Developer Portal]
        UI3[Tenant Console]
    end

    subgraph APP[Application Layer - IIS Orchestrator]
        AUTH[Authentication Service]
        SCHED[Scheduler Service]
        QUEUE[Queue Manager]
        LOG[Logging Service]
        API[REST API Gateway]
    end

    subgraph DATA[Data Layer - SQL Server]
        DB1[(Tenant DB)]
        DB2[(Robot DB)]
        DB3[(Queue DB)]
        DB4[(Audit Log DB)]
    end

    subgraph BOT[Bot Layer - Robot Machines]
        R1[Unattended Robot 1]
        R2[Unattended Robot 2]
        R3[Attended Robot N]
    end

    UI1 --> API
    UI2 --> API
    UI3 --> API
    API --> AUTH
    API --> SCHED
    API --> QUEUE
    API --> LOG
    AUTH --> DB1
    SCHED --> DB2
    QUEUE --> DB3
    LOG --> DB4
    SCHED --> R1
    SCHED --> R2
    QUEUE --> R3

    style PRES fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0
    style APP fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style DATA fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
    style BOT fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
```

## 4.3 High-Availability (HA) Deployment Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LB[Load Balancer - F5 or Azure App Gateway] --> S1[Orchestrator Node 1]
    LB --> S2[Orchestrator Node 2]
    LB --> S3[Orchestrator Node 3]

    S1 --> DBS[(Primary SQL Server)]
    S2 --> DBS
    S3 --> DBS

    DBS -.AlwaysOn Replication.-> DBS2[(Secondary SQL Server DR Site)]

    S1 --> ELK[(Elasticsearch Log Cluster)]
    S2 --> ELK
    S3 --> ELK

    style LB fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style DBS fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style DBS2 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
    style ELK fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The Load Balancer distributes incoming HTTPS requests across **3 Orchestrator nodes** for horizontal scalability. All nodes share a **Primary SQL Server**, which replicates asynchronously to a **Disaster Recovery (DR)** site via SQL Server **Always On Availability Groups**. Logs are streamed to **Elasticsearch** for searchable audit trails.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5️⃣ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define an RPA Server. List any four of its core functions.**

**Model Answer:**
> An **RPA Server** (e.g., UiPath Orchestrator) is a centralized web-based management console that governs the lifecycle of software robots. **Four core functions are:**
> 1. **Robot Provisioning** — Registering and managing attended/unattended bots.
> 2. **Process Scheduling** — Triggering jobs via cron-like schedules or queues.
> 3. **Queue Management** — Distributing transactional workloads using FIFO/longest-wait logic.
> 4. **Centralized Logging & Monitoring** — Real-time dashboards with SLA breach alerts.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**What is the role of a Machine Template in server-based bot deployment?**

**Model Answer:**
> A **Machine Template** is a logical grouping that defines the **Machine Key, Robot credentials, and runtime version** shared by all bots deployed on a physical or virtual host. It enables **one-to-many bot registration** from a single machine, simplifies license allocation, and supports **High-Density Robots** (multiple bots on one VM) — reducing infrastructure cost by up to **60%**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### 📘 Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **three-tier architecture** of an RPA server with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of each tier in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A manufacturing company plans to deploy **300 unattended bots** to process invoices 24/7. Each bot handles **1,200 transactions/day** and the target utilization is **80%**. A single server node supports a maximum of **75 concurrent sessions** (RAM-bottlenecked). Calculate:
- (i) The number of server nodes required.
- (ii) The annual database storage in GB (assume 1.5 KB/log, 1 log/transaction, with 50% index/backup overhead).
- (iii) The total servers needed with N+1 redundancy.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Three-Tier Architecture Explanation [7 Marks]:**
The RPA server is built on a classic **three-tier architecture**:

1. **Presentation Tier (1 Mark):** Browser-based UI built on Angular/React; renders dashboards for tenants, robots, queues, and logs. Uses **HTTPS (port 443)** for secure transport.

2. **Application Tier (3 Marks):** Hosted on IIS (Windows) or Tomcat (Linux); contains the core services:
   - **Authentication Service** — Validates OAuth 2.0 / SAML tokens.
   - **Scheduler Service** — Cron-based job triggers.
   - **Queue Manager** — Distributes transactional items to available bots.
   - **Logging Service** — Streams events to Elasticsearch.

3. **Data Tier (2 Marks):** SQL Server 2019 stores tenants, robots, processes, schedules, queue items, and audit logs. Indexed for sub-second query response on millions of rows.

4. **Supporting Layer (1 Mark):** A separate **Robot Layer** of unattended machines consumes jobs via SignalR WebSocket connections.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**(i) Number of Server Nodes (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{server} &= \left\lceil \frac{B_{total}}{S_{max}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{300}{75} \right\rceil = \lceil 4 \rceil = 4 \text{ nodes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Annual DB Storage (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{db,raw} &= 300 \times 1200 \times 365 \times 1.5 \text{ KB} \\[6pt]
&= 197{,}100{,}000 \text{ KB} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{197{,}100{,}000}{1024 \times 1024} \text{ GB} \\[6pt]
&= 187.94 \text{ GB/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{total} &= 187.94 \times 1.50 = 281.91 \text{ GB/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) N+1 Redundancy (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{total} &= N_{server} + 1 = 4 + 1 = 5 \text{ servers}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** **4 active server nodes + 1 standby = 5 servers; 281.91 GB/year DB storage required.**

---

### 📗 Question B `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]` — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **step-by-step procedure to create an RPA Orchestrator server** (using UiPath as reference) for an enterprise deployment. Include infrastructure, installation, and configuration phases.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **High Availability (HA) and Disaster Recovery (DR)** strategies for an RPA server. How does the **N+1 redundancy model** differ from **Active-Active** clustering?

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Step-by-Step Server Creation Procedure [7 Marks]:**

| Step | Phase | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Infrastructure** (1 Mark) | Provision a Windows Server 2019 VM with **8 vCPU, 16 GB RAM, 100 GB SSD**. |
| 2 | **OS Hardening** (1 Mark) | Apply Windows updates, disable unnecessary services, configure firewall rules (allow 443, 3389). |
| 3 | **Database Setup** (1.5 Marks) | Install SQL Server 2019; create DB `UiPath`; configure `web.config` connection string. |
| 4 | **IIS Deployment** (1 Mark) | Install IIS with ASP.NET 4.7+ role; deploy Orchestrator `.zip` package to `C:\inetpub\Orchestrator`. |
| 5 | **License & Tenant** (1 Mark) | Apply Enterprise license; create the first **Tenant** (e.g., `FinanceDept`). |
| 6 | **Folder & Role** (0.5 Mark) | Create **Folders** with **Roles** (Robot User, Robot Admin) for RBAC. |
| 7 | **Robot Registration** (1 Mark) | Install Robot on target VM; copy **Machine Key**; bind to Machine Template. |

---

**(b) HA & DR Strategies [7 Marks]:**

**High Availability (HA) Strategies (3 Marks):**
- **Load Balancer** (F5, HAProxy, Azure App Gateway) distributes traffic across N Orchestrator nodes.
- **AlwaysOn Availability Groups** in SQL Server replicate data synchronously to a secondary replica.
- **Active health probes** remove failed nodes from the pool within **30 seconds**.

**Disaster Recovery (DR) Strategies (2 Marks):**
- **Geo-redundant SQL replication** to a secondary region (RPO ≤ 5 min, RTO ≤ 1 hour).
- **Daily encrypted backups** stored in Azure Blob with **7-year retention** for compliance.
- **DNS failover** using Traffic Manager or Route 53.

**N+1 vs Active-Active (2 Marks):**
- **N+1 Redundancy:** N active nodes + 1 **hot standby** that takes over only on failure → **50% over-provisioning on standby is wasted** in normal operation.
- **Active-Active:** All N nodes serve traffic simultaneously; capacity is fully utilized; failover is seamless with **zero downtime**. However, it requires **stateless application design** and **distributed session management** (e.g., Redis Cache).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. ❌ **Confusing "Machine" with "Machine Template"** — A Machine is a single host; a Template is a reusable config for many hosts. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this mix-up.
> 2. ❌ **Forgetting to convert KB → GB** — Always show the $1024 \times 1024$ division step. Skipping it costs **1 mark**.
> 3. ❌ **Omitting the ceiling function** $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ — A server count of `3.125` is impossible; you **must** round up to `4`.
> 4. ❌ **Ignoring index/backup overhead** in storage problems — Real-world DBs need **30%–50%** extra space. Skipping this loses **1 mark**.
> 5. ❌ **Writing `N+1` as `N-1`** — This is a frequently reversed sign error; always double-check.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Creation of Server":**

- 🔹 An **RPA Server** = Centralized command-and-control hub (Orchestrator / Control Room / Runtime Resource).
- 🔹 Built on a **three-tier architecture**: Presentation (Web UI) + Application (IIS/REST APIs) + Data (SQL Server).
- 🔹 Communication with bots uses **HTTPS (port 443) + WebSocket (WSS)** for real-time job dispatch.
- 🔹 **Pre-requisites for server creation**: Windows Server 2019/2022, IIS, SQL Server 2019, .NET Framework 4.7+, 16 GB RAM minimum.
- 🔹 **Machine Template** ≠ Machine; it is a reusable configuration enabling **High-Density Robots**.
- 🔹 **Standard SLA** for production servers = **99.9% uptime** → max **43.2 min downtime/month**.
- 🔹 **N+1 redundancy** = N active + 1 standby; **Active-Active** = full capacity utilization, requires stateless design.
- 🔹 **Sizing formula**: $N_{server} = \lceil B_{total} / S_{max} \rceil$ where $S_{max}$ is the RAM-bottlenecked session limit.
- 🔹 **Storage formula**: $S_{db} = B_{active} \times L_{avg} \times 365 \times C_{log}$, then add **30%–50%** overhead.
- 🔹 **Tenant** is the topmost logical isolation unit in a multi-tenant RPA server.
- 🔹 **Queue-based dispatch** uses FIFO or priority logic to distribute transactional work.
- 🔹 **Credential Vaulting** is mandatory for unattended bots — never hardcode passwords.
- 🔹 Always apply **OAuth 2.0 / SAML 2.0** for authentication; avoid basic auth.
- 🔹 **Audit logs** must be retained for **7 years** in BFSI and **5 years** in healthcare (compliance).
- 🔹 For KTU exams, always **show units, intermediate steps, and ceiling rounding** in numerical answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using Server to Control the Bots - Creating a Provision Robot from the Server
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Using Server to Control the Bots - Creating a Provision Robot from the Server

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Robotic Process Automation (RPA), **Using the Server to Control the Bots** refers to the centralized orchestration paradigm where a dedicated server-side component (commonly known as the **Orchestrator**, **Control Room**, or **RPA Server**) acts as the command-and-control authority over a distributed fleet of software robots. It is responsible for **publishing, scheduling, queueing, executing, monitoring, and auditing** automated workflows on registered bot machines.

**Creating a Provision Robot from the Server** is the specific administrative operation in which a new bot instance is **provisioned** (i.e., logically created, registered, configured, licensed, and bound to a physical or virtual machine) directly from the orchestrator console, rather than from the bot machine itself. The bot becomes a "tenant" of the server and is granted permissions through **roles, environments, and licenses**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Note:** A "Provision Robot" in KTU-evaluated answers must be defined as a *server-registered, license-bound, machine-mapped execution entity* that pulls work items from the orchestrator queue and executes unattended or attended processes. Half-mark answers that omit "server-registered" or "license-bound" are penalized by examiners.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **large hospital with a Central Operations Desk** (the Server) and dozens of **ambulance drivers** (the Bots) stationed in different cities. The Central Desk does not drive the ambulances itself; instead, it:

- **Registers** each driver (creating a robot account).
- **Issues a badge / ID card** to the driver (provisioning + licensing).
- **Assigns a specific ambulance and route map** to that driver (binding to a machine template).
- **Sends dispatch instructions** ("Go pick up patient at 3 PM from City Hospital") — this is the **schedule / trigger**.
- **Tracks GPS location and fuel consumption** of every driver (monitoring & logging).
- **Sends updates back to the driver** (configuration pushes).

In this analogy, the **driver cannot operate independently for critical work** — they need the dispatch from the desk. Similarly, in RPA, the **bot needs the orchestrator to know what to run, when to run, and on what data**. The act of **creating a provision robot from the server** is exactly like the hospital desk **issuing a new ID badge, mapping it to a specific ambulance, and adding the driver to the active dispatch roster**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** The server is the "brain" that tells the bots what to do, when to do it, and on which machine to do it. Provisioning a robot means officially enrolling a new bot into the server's workforce — giving it an identity, a license, and a workstation.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in Bot Provisioning

The following **standard provisioning metrics** are universally used across enterprise RPA platforms (UiPath Orchestrator, Automation Anywhere Control Room, Blue Prism):

- **Tenant** — a logical isolation boundary inside the server hosting bots/users.
- **Robot License Type** — `Attended`, `Unattended`, `NonProduction`, `Studio`, `StudioX`.
- **Machine Template** — a named hardware profile specifying resolution, OS, and credential vault.
- **Concurrent Runtime Count** — the number of parallel jobs a single license can spawn.
- **Connection Status Polling Interval** — default **30 seconds** (heartbeat).
- **Max Retry Attempts** — default **3 retries** on job failure.
- **Standard Folder Permissions** — `Read`, `Edit`, `Create`, `Delete`, `Full Control`.
- **Credential Store Type** — `CyberArk`, `HashiCorp Vault`, `Azure Key Vault`, or `Default Orchestrator Vault`.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always mention *which server product* you are referring to (e.g., UiPath Orchestrator vs AA Control Room) because examiners reward **platform-specific terminology**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Architecture of Server-Controlled Bot Provisioning

The server-to-bot control architecture operates on a **Hub-and-Spoke topology** with three logical layers:

**Layer 1 — Orchestrator (Server-Side Control Plane)**
- Hosts the database, scheduler, queue manager, asset store, credential vault, and audit log.
- Exposes **REST APIs** and a **Web UI** for administrators.
- Manages **tenants, folders, roles, machines, robots, processes, schedules, queues, and triggers**.

**Layer 2 — Communication Channel (Transport Plane)**
- Uses **HTTPS (port 443)** with mutual TLS (mTLS) authentication.
- Bots establish a persistent **SignalR / WebSocket** connection (UiPath) or **gRPC** channel (AA).
- Heartbeat signals are sent every **30 s** by default to confirm liveness.

**Layer 3 — Bot Runtime (Execution Plane)**
- A lightweight agent service (e.g., `UiPath.Service.Host.exe`, `AA Bot Agent`) installed on the workstation.
- Polls the orchestrator for pending jobs, downloads the workflow package (`.nupkg` / `.bot` file), executes it, and reports back the status.

### 2.2 The 5 Logical Steps of Provisioning a Robot from the Server

1. **Create the Machine Template** — Define a virtual machine profile with name, description, and machine key.
2. **Generate the Machine Key** — A 64-character secret string the bot uses during `robot --connect` handshake.
3. **Create the Robot Account** — Define a unique robot name, domain/username, password (or credential), and license type.
4. **Bind Robot → Machine Template** — Link the robot entity to the machine template (1:1 mapping standard, 1:N allowed for high-density deployments).
5. **Assign Roles, Folders, and Environment** — Grant permissions so the robot can access processes, queues, and assets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Server-Side Provisioning Matters:**
> - **Centralized governance** — no rogue bots.
> - **License optimization** — concurrent licensing prevents over-licensing.
> - **Auditability** — every bot action is logged in a tamper-evident trail.
> - **Scalability** — one admin can provision **1,000+ bots** in a single day.
> - **High Availability (HA)** — primary + secondary orchestrator nodes ensure 99.9% uptime.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes the **high-yield equations and configuration parameters** used in server-based bot provisioning. These appear frequently in KTU 2024 Module 4 numerical and descriptive questions.

| # | Parameter / Formula | Definition / Unit | Default Value | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_{\text{active}} = L_{\text{concurrent}} \times R_{\text{reuse}}$ | Active bots = Concurrent Licenses × Reuse Factor (0–1) | — | Capacity planning |
| 2 | $T_{\text{polling}}$ | Heartbeat interval (seconds) | **30 s** | Connection health check |
| 3 | $N_{\text{retry}}$ | Job retry count on transient failure | **3** | Fault tolerance |
| 4 | $T_{\text{timeout}} = 3 \times T_{\text{polling}}$ | Disconnection threshold | **90 s** | Marks robot as *Disconnected* |
| 5 | $U_{\text{util}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{used}}}{N_{\text{licensed}}} \times 100\%$ | License utilization (percentage) | Target ≥ 80% | ROI measurement |
| 6 | $T_{\text{MTBR}} = \dfrac{\sum T_{\text{uptime}}}{N_{\text{breaks}}}$ | Mean Time Between Robot Restarts | Hours | SLA tracking |
| 7 | $R_{\text{throughput}} = \dfrac{J_{\text{completed}}}{T_{\text{window}}}$ | Bot throughput (jobs per minute) | — | Performance benchmarking |
| 8 | $P_{\text{over}} = \max(0, N_{\text{bots}} - L_{\text{concurrent}})$ | Over-provisioned penalty count | 0 ideal | License cost control |
| 9 | $T_{\text{handshake}} = T_{\text{TLS}} + T_{\text{auth}} + T_{\text{sync}}$ | Bot-to-server connect time | **≈ 2 s** | Connection latency |
| 10 | $C_{\text{storage}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i + L_i)$ | Storage cost = Packages + Logs (GB) | — | Server sizing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical:** LaTeX vertical bars in formulas have been deliberately escaped as `\vert` or omitted to keep the markdown table stable. Use `\lvert` and `\rvert` in your own exam scripts.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Server-based bot provisioning is the **backbone of enterprise-grade RPA deployments** in banking (e.g., automated loan processing for 5M+ customers), insurance (claims triage), healthcare (HL7 record reconciliation), and BPO industries. The provisioning model directly enables **Centre of Excellence (CoE)** governance — a single Keral-based IT services firm can manage 2,000+ bots across multiple client tenants from one orchestrator instance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Server-Controlled Bot Capacity Planning

This is the **most important KTU Module 4 numerical problem** — deriving the **maximum bot capacity** that a single orchestrator tenant can sustain under license, hardware, and network constraints.

> **Problem Context:** A KTU-evaluated design problem — *A KPO firm in Kochi wants to deploy an RPA Centre of Excellence. The firm has purchased 50 concurrent unattended licenses for UiPath Orchestrator. Each bot machine is a VM with 4 vCPU and 8 GB RAM. Each job requires 1 vCPU, 1.5 GB RAM, and runs for an average of 4 minutes. The orchestrator's heartbeat timeout is 90 s and the connection is HTTP/mTLS. Calculate the (a) theoretical max active bots, (b) license utilization if 35 bots are actually running, (c) required number of VM machines assuming 1:1 binding, and (d) throughput if jobs arrive at a Poisson rate λ = 6 jobs/min.*

---

#### Part (a) — Theoretical Max Active Bots

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{max}} &= L_{\text{concurrent}} \times R_{\text{reuse}} \\
\text{where } L_{\text{concurrent}} &= 50 \text{ (given)} \\
\text{and } R_{\text{reuse}} &= 1.0 \text{ (full reuse, all unattended)} \\
\therefore N_{\text{max}} &= 50 \times 1.0 \\
N_{\text{max}} &= \mathbf{50 \text{ active bots}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Because all 50 licenses are *Unattended* and *Non-Production = false*, each license can spawn one robot. Therefore the theoretical maximum equals the total license count.

---

#### Part (b) — License Utilization

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{\text{util}} &= \frac{N_{\text{used}}}{N_{\text{licensed}}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{35}{50} \times 100\% \\
&= 0.70 \times 100\% \\
U_{\text{util}} &= \mathbf{70\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The orchestrator dashboard will report this figure. KTU examiners want the **percentage form**, not the decimal.

---

#### Part (c) — Required Number of VM Machines (1:1 Binding)

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{VM}} &= \frac{N_{\text{max}}}{\text{Bots per VM (1:1 binding)}} \\
&= \frac{50}{1} \\
N_{\text{VM}} &= \mathbf{50 \text{ VMs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Standard provisioning uses 1 robot per VM for isolation. For high-density (multiple bots per VM) the formula becomes:
$$
N_{\text{VM}} = \left\lceil \frac{N_{\text{max}}}{B_{\text{perVM}}} \right\rceil
$$
where $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ is the ceiling function and $B_{\text{perVM}}$ = bots per VM (default = 1).

---

#### Part (d) — Throughput with Poisson Arrivals

For an M/M/1 queue (Poisson arrival, exponential service, single server per bot):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= 6 \text{ jobs/min (arrival rate)} \\
\mu &= \frac{1}{T_{\text{service}}} = \frac{1}{4 \text{ min}} = 0.25 \text{ jobs/min (single bot service rate)} \\
\rho &= \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = 6 \times 4 = 24 \text{ ... (this is wrong, redo per bot)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> ⚠️ **Correction:** The utilization is computed per **single bot** with its own arrival slice. With **50 bots** sharing load evenly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{\text{per bot}} &= \frac{\lambda_{\text{total}}}{N_{\text{max}}} = \frac{6}{50} = 0.12 \text{ jobs/min} \\
R_{\text{throughput}} &= \frac{0.12 \times 60 \text{ s}}{1} = 7.2 \text{ s per job per bot} \\
\text{or in jobs/min per bot} &= 0.12 \text{ jobs/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total system throughput:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{total}} &= \lambda_{\text{per bot}} \times N_{\text{max}} \\
&= 0.12 \times 50 \\
R_{\text{total}} &= \mathbf{6 \text{ jobs/min (system)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** This is the conservation property of queues — system throughput equals the arrival rate when the system is stable (i.e., $\rho_{\text{sys}} < 1$).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Provisioning Decision Tree

| Step | Decision Point | If YES → Action | If NO → Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Is the bot **Attended**? | License = `Attended`, no schedule needed | License = `Unattended`, schedule mandatory |
| 2 | Does the bot need **credentials**? | Store in **Orchestrator Vault** with `Get Credential` activity | Hardcode (NOT recommended, KTU penalizes) |
| 3 | Is the machine **physical or VM**? | Physical → use `Hostname`. VM → use `Machine Template` | Both options coexist |
| 4 | Does the process need **orchestrator queues**? | Bind robot to folder with queue access | Local work folder only |
| 5 | Are retries needed? | Set $N_{\text{retry}} \geq 3$ | Default = 0 (fail-fast) |
| 6 | Is there a **triggers** requirement? | Use **Queue Triggers** for event-driven jobs | Use **Schedules** for time-driven jobs |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing `Machine Template` (a server-side *profile*) with `Machine` (the actual registered workstation). A template is *cloned* into a machine when the bot first connects. KTU examiners specifically test this distinction.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Server-to-Bot Provisioning Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    admin["RPA ADMIN"] --> orchUI["ORCHESTRATOR WEB UI"]
    restapi["REST API GATEWAY"] --> orchUI
    orchUI --> tenant["TENANT DB"]
    tenant --> folder1["FOLDER 1 PRODUCTION"]
    tenant --> folder2["FOLDER 2 TESTING"]
    folder1 --> role1["ROLE ADMIN"]
    folder1 --> role2["ROLE DEVELOPER"]
    folder1 --> role3["ROLE BOT USER"]
    folder1 --> machineT1["MACHINE TEMPLATE 1"]
    folder1 --> robotA1["ROBOT A UNATTENDED"]
    folder1 --> robotA2["ROBOT B ATTENDED"]
    folder1 --> queue1["QUEUE INVOICE POOL"]
    folder1 --> schedule1["SCHEDULE 9AM DAILY"]
    folder1 --> trigger1["TRIGGER ON EMAIL"]
    machineT1 --> vm1["VM WINDOWS SERVER 1"]
    machineT1 --> vm2["VM WINDOWS SERVER 2"]
    vm1 --> agent1["BOT AGENT SERVICE"]
    vm2 --> agent2["BOT AGENT SERVICE"]
    agent1 --> channel1["HTTPS MTLS CHANNEL"]
    agent2 --> channel1
    channel1 --> orchUI
    queue1 --> orchUI
    schedule1 --> orchUI
    trigger1 --> orchUI
    agent1 --> workA["EXECUTES WORKFLOW A"]
    agent2 --> workB["EXECUTES WORKFLOW B"]
    workA --> result1["JOB RESULT LOG"]
    workB --> result2["JOB RESULT LOG"]
    result1 --> audit["AUDIT LOG"]
    result2 --> audit
```

**Reading the Diagram:** The admin creates folders → assigns roles → registers a Machine Template → provisions Robots (A and B) → binds them to VMs → installs the Bot Agent on each VM → the agent opens an **HTTPS / mTLS channel** to the orchestrator → the orchestrator pushes scheduled/triggered jobs → the agent executes workflows → results are written to the audit log.

### 4.2 Sequence Diagram: Provisioning a Robot Step-by-Step

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant ADM as Admin
    participant ORC as Orchestrator
    participant DB as Tenant DB
    participant MVT as Machine Template
    participant BOT as Bot Agent
    participant VM as Bot VM

    ADM->>ORC: Login with admin credentials
    ORC->>DB: Validate admin role
    DB-->>ORC: Access granted
    ADM->>ORC: Create Machine Template
    ORC->>MVT: Register template VM Finance01
    MVT-->>ORC: Machine Key 64 char returned
    ORC-->>ADM: Display Machine Key
    ADM->>ORC: Create Robot Robot01
    ORC->>DB: Store Robot01 record
    DB-->>ORC: RobotID generated
    ORC-->>ADM: Robot created in folder Finance
    ADM->>VM: Paste Machine Key in Bot Agent config
    VM->>BOT: Start service
    BOT->>ORC: HTTPS mTLS connect with Machine Key
    ORC->>BOT: Send tenant config and license
    BOT-->>ORC: Heartbeat every 30s
    ORC->>BOT: Job dispatched at 9 AM schedule
    BOT->>VM: Execute workflow
    VM-->>BOT: Workflow complete
    BOT-->>ORC: Job status SUCCESS
    ORC-->>ADM: Update dashboard
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: License-to-Bot Binding

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    lic["LICENSE POOL 50 SLOTS"] --> le1["UNATTENDED x 30"]
    lic --> le2["ATTENDED x 15"]
    lic --> le3["NONPROD x 5"]
    le1 --> rb1["ROBOT 01"]
    le1 --> rb2["ROBOT 02"]
    le2 --> rb3["ROBOT 03"]
    le3 --> rb4["ROBOT 04"]
    rb1 --> mt1["MACHINE TEMPLATE T1"]
    rb2 --> mt1
    rb3 --> mt2["MACHINE TEMPLATE T2"]
    rb4 --> mt1
    mt1 --> vmx["VM POOL A"]
    mt2 --> vmy["VM POOL B"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Provision Robot" in the context of server-controlled RPA. Mention any two mandatory fields required to create one.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Provision Robot** is a server-registered, license-bound execution entity in an RPA orchestrator (e.g., UiPath Orchestrator) that is created and managed from the server console and bound to a specific machine template for executing automated workflows.

**Two mandatory fields:** (1) **Robot Name** (unique identifier inside the tenant), and (2) **Machine Template** (the workstation profile to which this robot will be bound). *(2 marks for definition + 1 mark for fields.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any three responsibilities of the RPA Orchestrator (Server) in controlling the bots.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Scheduling and triggering** automated jobs on registered bots based on time, queue events, or external triggers.
2. **Distributing and versioning** process packages (`.nupkg` / `.bot` files) to the correct bot machines.
3. **Monitoring and logging** every bot's connection status, job execution time, and result for audit and SLA reporting.
*(1 mark each.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question — Module 4 Internal Choice (14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **5-step process of provisioning a robot from the RPA Server**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A firm has deployed **40 unattended RPA bots** in production managed by UiPath Orchestrator. Each license allows **3 concurrent runtimes** and the **reuse factor is 0.85**. Calculate:
   (i) Maximum number of **simultaneously active runtimes** the firm can sustain.
   (ii) **License utilization** if 85 runtimes are currently active.
   (iii) If each job takes **2 minutes** and the system receives **90 jobs per hour** under steady state, compute the **system throughput in jobs/hour** and comment on whether the system is stable. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) 5-Step Provisioning Process (7 marks):**

1. **Create Tenant & Folder** (1 mark) — The admin logs into the orchestrator, creates (or selects) a tenant and a logical folder (e.g., `Finance-Production`) to scope permissions.

2. **Create Machine Template** (1.5 marks) — A named hardware profile is registered with the orchestrator. The server generates a unique **64-character Machine Key** which the bot agent will later use to authenticate.

3. **Create the Robot Account** (1.5 marks) — The admin defines the robot with fields: Robot Name, Domain\Username, Password (or Credential asset), and License Type (`Unattended`).

4. **Bind Robot → Machine Template** (1.5 marks) — The robot record is associated with the machine template (1:1 or 1:N binding), enabling the server to know *where* to dispatch jobs.

5. **Assign Role, Folder, and Environment** (1.5 marks) — The robot receives a role (e.g., `RobotRole`) granting `Execute` permission on processes and `Read` on queues/assets inside the assigned folder. The bot agent on the VM uses the Machine Key to establish the **HTTPS/mTLS channel** and is now live.

*(Block diagram: use the architecture diagram from Section 4.1.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Max Active Runtimes:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{max}} &= L_{\text{concurrent}} \times R_{\text{reuse}} \\
&= (40 \times 3) \times 0.85 \\
&= 120 \times 0.85 \\
N_{\text{max}} &= \mathbf{102 \text{ runtimes}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) License Utilization:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{\text{util}} &= \frac{85}{102} \times 100\% \\
&= 83.33\% \\
U_{\text{util}} &\approx \mathbf{83.3\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** A utilization of **83.3% > 80%** is considered healthy under KTU/industry benchmarks (target = 75–85%).

**(iii) Throughput & Stability:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{\text{per runtime}} &= \frac{1}{T_{\text{service}}} = \frac{1}{2 \text{ min}} = 30 \text{ jobs/hour} \\
\lambda_{\text{system}} &= 90 \text{ jobs/hour} \\
\lambda_{\text{per runtime}} &= \frac{90}{102} = 0.882 \text{ jobs/hour} \\
\rho_{\text{per runtime}} &= \frac{0.882}{30} = 0.0294 \ll 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\rho_{\text{per runtime}} \ll 1$ (well below 1), the system is **stable and far from saturation**. The firm has comfortable headroom of $\approx 96\%$ spare capacity per runtime.

$$
R_{\text{system}} = \lambda_{\text{system}} = \mathbf{90 \text{ jobs/hour (steady state)}}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)**
**[KTU University Exam – Sessional Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **Attended** and **Unattended** robots in terms of provisioning parameters, trigger mechanism, and typical use cases. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **role of Machine Templates, Folders, Roles, and Credentials** in server-side robot provisioning with a labeled diagram. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Attended vs Unattended Robots (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Attended Robot | Unattended Robot |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Manual (user clicks) or attended trigger | Schedule / Queue Trigger / API |
| **License** | `Attended` (cheaper) | `Unattended` (premium) |
| **User Presence** | Required at the workstation | Not required |
| **Credentials** | User's own (no vault) | Stored in Orchestrator Vault |
| **Provisioning Path** | `Robot` → `Machine` → `User` | `Robot` → `Machine Template` → `Folder` |
| **Use Case** | Front-desk agent assist | Back-office batch processing |
| **Concurrent Runtime** | 1 per user license | 1–N per license (high density) |

*(1 mark per relevant row, capped at 7 marks.)*

**(b) Server-Side Provisioning Components (7 marks):**

- **Machine Template** (2 marks): A reusable server-side hardware profile that defines resolution, OS, and the **Machine Key** the bot uses for authentication. Multiple robots can share one template (1:N binding).
- **Folders** (2 marks): Logical containers that scope which robots, processes, queues, and assets a group of users can access. Enables multi-tenant governance inside a single orchestrator.
- **Roles** (1.5 marks): A bundle of permissions (`Execute`, `View`, `Edit`, `Delete`) assigned to users and robots. Roles are folder-scoped.
- **Credentials** (1.5 marks): Secrets (usernames, passwords, API tokens) stored encrypted inside the Orchestrator Vault and retrieved at runtime by `Get Credential` activities, preventing hardcoding.

*(Diagram: use the provisioning architecture from Section 4.1.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> - **❌ Do NOT** confuse `Machine Template` with `Machine`. A *Template* is the *blueprint* on the server; a *Machine* is the *runtime instance* cloned from the template on the actual bot VM. Examiners allocate 1–2 marks for this distinction.
> - **❌ Do NOT** say *"the bot pulls work from the server"* without specifying the **transport** (HTTPS/mTLS) and **authentication** (Machine Key). KTU 2024 values technical precision.
> - **❌ Do NOT** omit the **role and folder** binding in your provisioning steps. The robot is *useless* without permission to access the process/queue.
> - **❌ Do NOT** compute throughput using $T_{\text{service}} \times N_{\text{max}}$ as the bottleneck; for an M/M/1 queue, the **per-server** utilization $\rho$ must be $< 1$ for stability.
> - **✅ DO** use the exact KTU 2024 term "Provision Robot" rather than "create bot" or "register bot" — vocabulary carries marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] A **Provision Robot** = server-registered, license-bound, machine-mapped execution entity.
- [ ] The **Orchestrator** is the brain; the **Bot Agent** is the muscle.
- [ ] Communication is **HTTPS on port 443** with **mutual TLS** authentication.
- [ ] **Heartbeat interval** = **30 s**; **Disconnection threshold** = **90 s** (3 × polling).
- [ ] **Machine Key** = 64-character secret generated by the server, pasted into the bot agent.
- [ ] Provisioning steps: **Tenant/Folder → Machine Template → Robot → Bind → Role/Environment**.
- [ ] License types: `Attended`, `Unattended`, `NonProduction`, `Studio`, `StudioX`.
- [ ] $N_{\text{max}} = L_{\text{concurrent}} \times R_{\text{reuse}}$ is the **single most important formula**.
- [ ] $U_{\text{util}}$ target = **75–85%**; < 50% = over-licensed; > 95% = under-licensed.
- [ ] **M/M/1 stability condition**: per-server utilization $\rho = \lambda/\mu < 1$.
- [ ] **Folder** = logical scope; **Role** = permission bundle; **Credential** = encrypted secret in Vault.
- [ ] KTU expects the **UiPath Orchestrator vocabulary** by default — `Tenant, Folder, Robot, Machine Template, Queue, Trigger, Schedule`.
- [ ] Default **retry** on job failure = **3 attempts**; default **timeout** for response = variable.
- [ ] Server-side provisioning enables **governance, audit, scalability, and HA** — the 4 pillars of enterprise RPA.
- [ ] In Part B answers, always include a **block diagram** of the architecture — KTU awards 1–2 marks for figures.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Connecting a Robot to Server
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Connecting a Robot to Server

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Connecting a Robot to Server** in Robotic Process Automation (RPA) refers to the process of establishing a secure, authenticated communication link between an RPA Robot (a software agent executing automated workflows) and a centralized **Orchestration Server** (e.g., UiPath Orchestrator) so that the robot can receive job assignments, report execution status, download process packages, and log activities in a governed, centralized manner.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (Course: *PEAIT415 – Robotic Process Automation*, Module 4), this topic typically uses **UiPath** as the reference platform. The server referred to is the **UiPath Orchestrator**, a web-based platform that acts as the central command and control hub for all robots (bots) deployed across an enterprise.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Taxi Dispatcher Analogy** 🚖
>
> Imagine you own a fleet of self-driving taxis (the **Robots**). Each taxi can drive on its own, but without a central system, you have no idea where they are, what jobs they are doing, or which one is free.
>
> - The **Orchestrator** is the **Taxi Dispatch Center** (the Server).
> - The **Robot** is the **Taxi** (the worker).
> - **Connecting a Robot to Server** is the act of switching on the **GPS + Radio Transmitter** in the taxi and registering it with the dispatch center.
> - Once connected, the dispatch center can send jobs (passenger requests), track the robot's heart-beat (status), download the latest route maps (process packages), and collect trip reports (logs).

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Constants

In UiPath RPA, the following standard parameters govern a robot-server connection:

| Metric / Parameter | Standard Value | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Default Orchestrator URL** | `https://cloud.uipath.com/` | Base URL for cloud-based Orchestrator |
| **Default Tenant Port** | **443** (HTTPS) | Secure communication port |
| **Robot Heart-beat Interval** | **30 seconds** | Default ping frequency |
| **Session Timeout** | Configurable (default **30 minutes**) | Idle disconnect threshold |
| **Standard Robot Types** | **Attended / Unattended / Studio / StudioX** | Licensing categories |
| **Authentication Method** | **X.509 Certificate / Token-based** | Security protocol |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The term *"Server"* in this module predominantly means the **UiPath Orchestrator** instance, and *"Connecting"* implies both **Robot registration** (provisioning) and **Runtime connection** (live binding).

---

## 1.4 Prerequisites for Connecting a Robot

Before a robot can connect to the server, the following conditions must be met:

1. **Orchestrator Instance Available** – Either *On-Premises* or *Cloud* based.
2. **Tenant & Folder Created** – Logical organization unit inside Orchestrator.
3. **Machine Provisioned** – Either a *Standard Machine* (key-based) or *Template Machine* (modern folders).
4. **Robot Created** – With a unique *Name*, *Domain\Username*, and license type.
5. **Role Assigned** – *Robot* role must be linked to the user.
6. **UiPath Assistant / Robot Service Installed** – On the target machine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pro Tip for Exams:** In KTU theory questions, always begin your answer by defining **Orchestrator** first, then explain the connection — the examiner's marking key almost always awards 1–2 marks for the correct contextual definition.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Layer Architecture of Connection

Connecting a Robot to a Server is not a single action — it is a **three-layer architectural binding** that must succeed simultaneously. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to know all three layers.

### Layer 1: Machine Binding (Physical / Virtual Identity)
- The **Machine** represents the physical or virtual workstation where the robot resides.
- Two types:
  - **Standard Machine**: Uses a unique *Machine Key* generated in Orchestrator.
  - **Template Machine**: Used in modern folder architecture, no key required.
- The Robot Service on the workstation must be configured with this Machine Key.

### Layer 2: Robot Identity (The "Who" of the Bot)
- Defines the **identity** under which the robot will log in to the machine.
- A **Robot** entity in Orchestrator contains:
  - `Name`
  - `Domain\Username` (Windows credentials)
  - `Description`
  - `Type` (Attended, Unattended, Studio, StudioX)
  - `Hosting Type` (Standard / Floating)
- The robot is assigned to a **specific machine** OR made **floating** (any available machine).

### Layer 3: Folder / Tenant Scope (The "Where" of Execution)
- Robots are linked to a **Folder** (a logical container).
- The folder provides:
  - Process permissions
  - Queue access
  - Asset visibility
  - Trigger subscription

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters (The "Why"):** This three-layer binding ensures that a robot cannot simply "connect" to the server — it must be **provisioned (Layer 1 + 2)** by an admin and then **authenticated at runtime (Layer 3)**. This is the foundation of **enterprise-grade security** in RPA.

---

## 2.2 The Connection Workflow (End-to-End)

The connection from a Robot to the Server happens in the following structured sequence:

1. **Admin Creates Machine** → Generates a *Machine Key*.
2. **Admin Creates Robot** → Links robot to Machine, assigns Type.
3. **Admin Creates Folder** → Grants folder access to the Robot.
4. **Robot Service Installed** → On the target workstation (`UiPath.Service.Host.exe`).
5. **Configuration Step** → Machine Key + Orchestrator URL pasted in UiPath Assistant.
6. **Authentication** → Either *Connection String* or *OAuth Token* verified.
7. **Heart-beat Sent** → Robot pings server every 30 seconds.
8. **Status Displayed** → Robot appears as *"Available"* / *"Busy"* / *"Disconnected"* in Orchestrator.
9. **Job Dispatch** → Server pushes a *Job* (process execution request) to the robot.
10. **Execution & Logging** → Robot runs the workflow and uploads logs back to server.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The **heart-beat mechanism** is the single most asked concept in 14-mark questions. Always explain that without a successful heart-beat, the Orchestrator considers the robot *"Disconnected"* and will **not** dispatch any jobs to it.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Since this is a configuration/architecture topic (not numerical), the "formula sheet" represents the **decision logic** and **state transitions**.

### 2.3.1 Robot Connection State Matrix

| Current State | Event Triggered | Next State | Server Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Disconnected** | Robot Service starts + valid key | *Connecting* | Awaits authentication |
| **Connecting** | Auth successful | *Available* | Sends heart-beat ACK |
| **Available** | Job received | *Busy* | Sends Job payload |
| **Busy** | Job completed | *Available* | Receives log payload |
| **Available** | Heart-beat missed (3x) | *Disconnected* | Stops job dispatch |
| **Unresponsive** | Service crashes | *Disconnected* | Auto-reconnect attempt |

### 2.3.2 Licensing Formula (Conceptual)

$$
\text{Total Robots} = N_{\text{Attended}} + N_{\text{Unattended}} + N_{\text{Studio}} + N_{\text{Non-Production}}
$$

Where each license type has its own per-user or per-runtime cost. **Unattended** robots are the most expensive because they run 24/7 without human intervention.

### 2.3.3 Connection Success Condition

A robot is officially *"Connected"* **if and only if** ALL of the following are true:

$$
\text{Connected} \iff \big( \text{MachineKey}_{\text{valid}} \big) \land \big( \text{Auth}_{\text{passed}} \big) \land \big( \text{Folder}_{\text{granted}} \big) \land \big( \text{Heartbeat}_{\text{active}} \big)
$$

> Use `\land` in written answers, **NOT** the vertical pipe `|` which is reserved for tables in KTU markdown format.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Connecting robots to a centralized server is the **backbone of enterprise RPA deployments**. Without it:

- ❌ No centralized job scheduling
- ❌ No queue management
- ❌ No audit logs
- ❌ No role-based access control (RBAC)
- ❌ No scalability beyond a single workstation

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Use Case:** In a large bank, 500+ UiPath robots run across 20 countries. All of them are connected to a single **UiPath Automation Cloud** orchestrator, enabling the bank to schedule 10,000+ daily transactions in a governed, auditable manner — something impossible with isolated, unconnected bots.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: Deriving the Connection Logic

Since this is a configuration topic, the "derivation" below is a **logical proof of why a connection succeeds or fails** — a typical 14-mark KTU answer requires this kind of step-by-step logical reasoning.

### Step 1: Define the Problem
> *"Given a UiPath Robot Service and an Orchestrator Server, determine the conditions under which a successful connection is established."*

### Step 2: Identify the Variables

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= \text{Machine Key (UUID string)} \\
U &= \text{Orchestrator URL} \\
T &= \text{Tenant Identifier} \\
F &= \text{Folder Access Boolean} \\
R &= \text{Robot Role Assignment} \\
A &= \text{Authentication Method} \in \{\text{Token}, \text{Cert}, \text{OAuth}\} \\
H &= \text{Heart-beat Signal} \in \{0, 1\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: State the Logical Conditions

A successful connection requires the conjunction of five sub-conditions. We can express this as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Connection} &= f(K, U, T, F, R, A, H) \\
&= (K = K_{\text{valid}}) \land (U_{\text{reachable}}) \land (T_{\text{matched}}) \\
&\quad \land (F = 1) \land (R = 1) \land (A_{\text{verified}}) \land (H = 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the Failure Cases

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } K &= \text{invalid} \Rightarrow \text{Connection} = \text{False (Auth Rejected)} \\
\text{If } U_{\text{reachable}} &= 0 \Rightarrow \text{Connection} = \text{False (Network Failure)} \\
\text{If } F &= 0 \Rightarrow \text{Connection} = \text{False (No Folder Scope)} \\
\text{If } R &= 0 \Rightarrow \text{Connection} = \text{False (No Role)} \\
\text{If } H &= 0 \Rightarrow \text{Connection} = \text{True (Transient), then Disconnected}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Explanation of Logic:** Notice that the *Heart-beat* variable is special. Even if all other conditions are true at one moment, a **persistent** $H = 0$ state (no heart-beat) will eventually force the connection into a *Disconnected* state, which is why KTU examiners love this concept.

### Step 3.5: Express as a Boolean Truth Table

| $K$ | $U$ | $F$ | $R$ | $A$ | $H$ | **Connection** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | ✅ **Connected** |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | ⚠️ Transient (will fail) |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | ❌ Auth Failed |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | ❌ No Folder Scope |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | ❌ Network Error |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | ❌ Invalid Key |

Where `1 = True / Valid / Active` and `0 = False / Invalid / Inactive`.

### Step 4 (Final): Concluding Statement

$$
\boxed{\text{Connection}_{\text{stable}} \iff \bigwedge_{i=1}^{6} C_i \quad \text{where } C_i \in \{K, U, F, R, A, H\}}
$$

> **Reading:** A *stable* connection exists only when **all six conditions** remain true simultaneously. Any single failure causes connection degradation.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Connection Methods Compared

| Parameter | **Standard Robot (Key-based)** | **Modern Folder Robot (Token-based)** | **Cloud Orchestrator Robot** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Authentication** | Machine Key | OAuth 2.0 Token | OAuth 2.0 + Cloud Tenant |
| **Setup Complexity** | Low | Medium | Low (auto-provisioned) |
| **Best Suited For** | Legacy deployments | Modern enterprises | Cloud-first companies |
| **Folder Support** | Classic Folders | Modern Folders | Modern Folders |
| **High-Density Robots** | ✅ Supported | ✅ Supported | ✅ Supported |
| **Security Level** | Medium | High | Very High |
| **KTU 2024 Relevance** | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

---

## 3.3 Decision Flow Logic (Pseudo-Logical)

> [!NOTE]
> The following is a **configuration decision flow**, NOT a programming code block. It is a logical step-by-step trace that KTU examiners accept in theory answers.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 1:} \text{Identify Orchestrator type} \rightarrow \text{Cloud or On-Prem?} \\
&\textbf{Step 2:} \text{If Cloud} \rightarrow \text{Use OAuth 2.0 via cloud.uipath.com} \\
&\textbf{Step 3:} \text{If On-Prem} \rightarrow \text{Use Machine Key + Tenant ID} \\
&\textbf{Step 4:} \text{Create Machine} \rightarrow \text{Standard or Template?} \\
&\textbf{Step 5:} \text{Create Robot} \rightarrow \text{Assign Type + Link Machine} \\
&\textbf{Step 6:} \text{Configure UiPath Assistant} \rightarrow \text{Paste URL + Key} \\
&\textbf{Step 7:} \text{Test Connection} \rightarrow \text{Heart-beat check} \\
&\textbf{Step 8:} \text{If Success} \rightarrow \text{Robot Status = "Available"} \\
&\textbf{Step 9:} \text{Dispatch Test Job} \rightarrow \text{Verify execution} \\
&\textbf{Step 10:} \text{Production Deployment} \rightarrow \text{Monitor via Orchestrator Dashboard}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Robot-to-Server Connection Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User Workstation] --> B[UiPath Robot Service]
    B --> C[UiPath Assistant UI]
    C --> D{Authentication Layer}
    D -->|Machine Key| E[Standard Auth]
    D -->|OAuth Token| F[Modern Auth]
    D -->|X.509 Cert| G[Certificate Auth]
    E --> H[UiPath Orchestrator Server]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Tenant Management]
    H --> J[Folder Scoping]
    H --> K[Job Dispatcher]
    H --> L[Queue Manager]
    H --> M[Asset Repository]
    K --> N[Robot: Available]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Process Execution]
    O --> P[Logs and Audit Trail]
    P --> H
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The Robot Service on the user workstation authenticates with the Orchestrator using one of three methods (Key, Token, or Certificate). Once authenticated, the robot gains access to a Tenant, Folder scope, and the Job Dispatcher. The Orchestrator pushes jobs and the robot returns logs.

---

## 4.2 Robot Connection State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Disconnected
    Disconnected --> Connecting : Service Started + Key Provided
    Connecting --> Available : Authentication Success
    Connecting --> Disconnected : Auth Failure
    Available --> Busy : Job Received
    Busy --> Available : Job Completed
    Available --> Disconnected : Heart-beat Missed 3x
    Busy --> Disconnected : Service Crash
    Disconnected --> Connecting : Retry Triggered
    Available --> [*] : Admin Deletes Robot
```

> **Reading:** This state diagram shows all legal transitions a robot undergoes. Notice the self-correcting loop from `Disconnected → Connecting` on retry.

---

## 4.3 Three-Layer Binding Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L1[Layer 1 Machine Binding]
        M1[Physical Workstation]
        M2[Virtual Machine]
        M3[Machine Key UUID]
    end
    subgraph L2[Layer 2 Robot Identity]
        R1[Robot Name]
        R2[Domain and Username]
        R3[License Type]
    end
    subgraph L3[Layer 3 Folder Scope]
        F1[Tenant]
        F2[Folder]
        F3[Process Permissions]
    end
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> X((Connected Robot))
```

> **Reading:** The three layers must bind sequentially. A robot cannot reach Layer 3 (Folder scope) without first establishing Layer 1 (Machine) and Layer 2 (Identity).

---

## 4.4 Detailed Connection Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Workstation
    participant R as Robot Service
    participant O as Orchestrator Server
    participant DB as Database
    U->>R: Launch UiPath Assistant
    R->>O: Send Machine Key + URL
    O->>DB: Validate Key
    DB-->>O: Key Valid
    O-->>R: Auth Token Issued
    R->>O: Heart-beat Ping
    O-->>R: Heart-beat ACK
    Note over R,O: Connection Established
    O->>R: Dispatch Job Payload
    R->>U: Execute Process
    U-->>R: Execution Logs
    R-->>O: Upload Logs
    O->>DB: Persist Logs
```

> **Reading:** This sequence diagram is the **single most important visual** for a 14-mark KTU answer. Memorize this flow — it directly answers "Explain the process of connecting a robot to the server."

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Short-Answer Conceptual `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Q1.** What is meant by *"Connecting a Robot to Server"* in UiPath RPA? Name the server involved.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 mark) Connecting a Robot to Server is the process of establishing a secure, authenticated link between a UiPath Robot and the **UiPath Orchestrator**.
- (1 mark) The server involved is the **UiPath Orchestrator**, a web-based central management platform.
- (1 mark) The connection allows the robot to receive jobs, download process packages, send heart-beats, and upload execution logs.

---

### Question 2: Short-Answer Definition `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **Q2.** What is a *Machine Key* in the context of robot-server connection?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 mark) A **Machine Key** is a unique alphanumeric identifier (UUID) generated by the UiPath Orchestrator when a **Standard Machine** is created.
- (1 mark) It is used to bind a specific physical or virtual machine to the Orchestrator.
- (1 mark) The key must be entered into the **UiPath Assistant** on the target machine to enable authentication and establish the connection.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** ⚠️
>
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students describe the UiPath Studio interface instead of the **Robot Service / UiPath Assistant** when explaining connection. ❌ → This loses 3–4 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to mention the **heart-beat mechanism**. The examiner's key specifically awards 2 marks for this. ❌
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing *Standard Machine* with *Template Machine*. Always clarify the difference. ❌
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Writing a vague answer like *"robot connects via internet"* — you MUST specify **Machine Key / OAuth / X.509** authentication. ❌
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Not mentioning **Folder / Tenant** scoping. Modern Orchestrator mandates this. ❌

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

> **Q(A).** Explain in detail the process of connecting a UiPath Robot to the Orchestrator Server. Cover the prerequisites, the three-layer binding, authentication methods, and the heart-beat mechanism.

#### Part (a) — Prerequisites and Three-Layer Binding (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Prerequisites (3 Marks):**
- A running **UiPath Orchestrator** instance (Cloud or On-Prem).
- A **Tenant** account with administrator privileges.
- The **UiPath Robot Service** (`UiPath.Service.Host.exe`) must be installed on the target machine.
- A **Machine** entity must be created in Orchestrator.
- A **Robot** entity must be created with valid Windows credentials.

**2. Three-Layer Binding (4 Marks):**

- **Layer 1 — Machine Binding:** The administrator creates either a *Standard Machine* (which generates a unique Machine Key) or a *Template Machine* (used in modern folder setups). The Machine Key is copied to the Robot Service on the workstation.

- **Layer 2 — Robot Identity:** A Robot entity is created in Orchestrator with attributes: `Name`, `Domain\Username`, `Type` (Attended / Unattended / Studio), and `Hosting Type` (Standard or Floating). The Robot is linked to the Machine.

- **Layer 3 — Folder / Tenant Scope:** The Robot is granted access to a specific **Folder** under a **Tenant**. This folder defines which processes, queues, assets, and triggers the robot can see and use. Without folder access, the connection is incomplete.

#### Part (b) — Authentication Methods and Heart-beat Mechanism (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Authentication Methods (3 Marks):**

- **Machine Key Authentication:** A UUID-based key entered into UiPath Assistant; the most common method for standard machines.
- **OAuth 2.0 Token Authentication:** Used for modern folder architecture and Cloud Orchestrator; provides enhanced security with token expiry and refresh.
- **X.509 Certificate Authentication:** Used in highly secured enterprise environments; involves installing a digital certificate on the robot machine.

**2. Heart-beat Mechanism (4 Marks):**
- Once connected, the Robot Service sends a **heart-beat signal** to the Orchestrator every **30 seconds**.
- This signal confirms that the robot is alive, reachable, and ready to accept jobs.
- If the Orchestrator **misses 3 consecutive heart-beats** (i.e., ~90 seconds of silence), the robot's status changes to **"Disconnected"**.
- In the *Disconnected* state, the Orchestrator **will not dispatch any jobs** to that robot.
- The heart-beat ensures **real-time visibility** of robot health on the Orchestrator dashboard and triggers automatic reconnection on recovery.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

> **Q(B).** Discuss the architecture for connecting multiple robots to a single Orchestrator Server. Explain licensing, High-Density Robots, and the role of Tenants and Folders with a suitable diagram.

#### Part (a) — Multi-Robot Architecture and Licensing (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Multi-Robot Architecture (3 Marks):**
- A single UiPath Orchestrator can manage **thousands of robots** across multiple machines, departments, and geographies.
- Each robot registers with the Orchestrator using its unique **Machine Key** and **Robot credentials**.
- The Orchestrator acts as a **single source of truth** for all jobs, queues, schedules, and logs.

**2. Licensing Models (4 Marks):**
- **Attended Robot License:** Used for robots that work alongside humans; user-triggered.
- **Unattended Robot License:** Used for robots that run autonomously 24/7; most expensive.
- **Studio License:** Required for developers building workflows; not for production.
- **Non-Production License:** Used only in testing environments.
- **Total Active Robots** in the system is given by the formula:

$$
N_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{att}} + N_{\text{unatt}} + N_{\text{studio}} + N_{\text{non-prod}}
$$

#### Part (b) — High-Density Robots, Tenants, and Folders (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. High-Density Robots (3 Marks):**
- A **High-Density Robot** is a Windows user profile that can host multiple robot sessions **simultaneously** on a single machine.
- This is essential when one powerful server machine needs to run 5, 10, or even 20+ robots in parallel.
- The Orchestrator differentiates these robots using unique **Windows usernames** (e.g., `admin1`, `admin2`, etc.) and the **Machine Key** remains shared.
- High-Density mode maximizes hardware utilization and reduces licensing cost per process.

**2. Tenants and Folders (4 Marks):**
