- A **Tenant** is the topmost logical partition in Orchestrator, representing a customer, business unit, or department.
- Within a Tenant, **Folders** act as sub-partitions that group related robots, processes, queues, and assets.
- Folder access can be granted with **fine-grained roles** (e.g., *Robot Role*, *Folder Administrator*).
- A typical enterprise structure is:

$$
\text{Tenant} \rightarrow \big\{ \text{Folder}_{HR}, \text{Folder}_{Finance}, \text{Folder}_{IT} \big\}
$$

- Robots are assigned to specific folders, and they can **only access resources** within their assigned folder. This enforces **role-based access control (RBAC)** and prevents cross-departmental data leakage.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist** — Pin this on your study wall before the exam! 📌

- ✅ **Server** = **UiPath Orchestrator** (the central command hub).
- ✅ **Connection** = Binding of **Machine + Robot + Folder** (three layers).
- ✅ **Machine Key** = UUID generated by Orchestrator for Standard Machines.
- ✅ **Authentication Options** = Machine Key | OAuth 2.0 | X.509 Certificate.
- ✅ **Heart-beat Interval** = **30 seconds**; **3 missed beats = Disconnected**.
- ✅ **Robot Types** = Attended, Unattended, Studio, StudioX, Non-Production.
- ✅ **High-Density Robots** = Multiple robots on a single machine (different Windows users).
- ✅ **Tenant → Folder → Robot** is the **hierarchy** of organization.
- ✅ **Disconnected robot = no job dispatch** (critical for exam).
- ✅ **Robot Service** = `UiPath.Service.Host.exe` runs in the background.
- ✅ **UiPath Assistant** = The user-facing interface for connection configuration.
- ✅ **Status States** = Disconnected, Connecting, Available, Busy, Unresponsive.
- ✅ **Logs and Audit Trail** are uploaded from robot → Orchestrator after every job.

> **Final Exam Tip:** For any 14-mark question on this topic, structure your answer as: **Definition → Three Layers → Authentication → Heart-beat → Use Case**. This guarantees you hit all the examiner's key points.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deploy the Robot to Server
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Deploy the Robot to Server

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🏗️ Module 4 — Deploy the Robot to Server

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robot Deployment** is the process of publishing an automation workflow (built in UiPath Studio) as a packaged NuGet package (`.nupkg`), registering it in **UiPath Orchestrator**, assigning it to a target **Machine / Robot** through a logical **Environment**, and finally triggering it as a **Job** — either manually or via a **Trigger** — so that the unattended robot on a remote server can execute the process without human intervention.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAIT415) context, *deployment* is **not** the same as *execution*. Execution is one instance of a Job running. Deployment is the **end-to-end provisioning** of that process onto an unattended runtime hosted on a server, along with the credentials, license, folder tenancy, and scheduling infrastructure required to run it reliably in production.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — "The App Store Model"

Think of deploying a robot the way a mobile app reaches your phone:

| Stage | Mobile App World | RPA Robot World |
|---|---|---|
| **Build** | Developer writes the app in Android Studio | Developer builds the workflow in UiPath Studio |
| **Package** | App is compiled to `.apk` / `.aab` | Workflow is published as `.nupkg` |
| **Host** | Uploaded to Google Play Store | Uploaded to **UiPath Orchestrator** |
| **Distribute** | Pushed to a user device | Pushed to a target **Machine (Robot)** |
| **Run** | User taps the icon | Orchestrator schedules/launches a **Job** |
| **Update** | OTA update | Re-publish a new version, redeploy |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A robot that runs on a developer's laptop via the Studio debugger is **not** considered a *deployed* robot. The robot becomes truly "deployed" only when it is **connected to Orchestrator**, **licensed**, and **executable as a scheduled job on a server-class machine**.

### 1.2 Why Deployment Matters in RPA

1. **Centralized Control** — A single Orchestrator pane can govern thousands of bots across geographies.
2. **Scalability** — Triggers, queues, and SLA timers allow on-demand horizontal scaling.
3. **Auditability** — Every job, log, and exception is captured for compliance.
4. **Decoupling** — Developers iterate on Studio; runtime concerns are managed by Ops on the server.
5. **Reliability** — Unattended robots include auto-recovery, screenshots on failure, and retry policies.

The four cornerstones of any successful deployment, as mandated by the KTU outcome-based framework, are:

> **Package → Publish → Connect → Trigger**

This is the *minimum viable deployment pipeline*. The KTU examiner expects you to enumerate, sequence, and justify each pillar in any 14-mark question on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — The Deployment Stack

## 2.1 The Orchestrator Object Model

Every deployment in UiPath relies on a hierarchy of six first-class objects. Understanding the **layered dependency** between them is a high-yield KTU concept.

| Layer | Object | Purpose | KTU-Critical Detail |
|-------|--------|---------|---------------------|
| L1 | **Tenant** | The top-level isolation boundary (org/account in cloud/on-prem Orchestrator) | One Orchestrator instance can host many Tenants |
| L2 | **Folder** | Logical partition inside a Tenant for RBAC, process grouping | Default folder = *My Workspace*; classic folders coexist with *Modern Folders* |
| L3 | **Machine** | A Windows server/workstation that will host the Robot runtime | Can be **Standard** (1:1) or **Template** (1:N — High Density) |
| L4 | **Robot** | The licensed execution agent bound to a Machine + User/credential | Two sub-types: **Attended** and **Unattended** |
| L5 | **Environment** | A binding between a Process and a set of Robots (classic folders) | Replaced in modern folders by direct *Process → Folder* assignment |
| L6 | **Process / Job / Trigger** | The published package, its execution instance, and its launch schedule | Triggers are **Time**, **Queue**, **API**, **Event** based |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Fact for KTU:** In the **Classic Folder** model, a Process **must** be associated with an Environment, and the Environment binds the Process to one or more Robots. In the **Modern Folder** model (default in newer Orchestrator versions), Processes are directly assigned to the folder, and Robot assignments are folder-scoped — the Environment object is **deprecated** at that level.

## 2.2 Robot Taxonomy

UiPath recognizes four robot *licensing roles*. The KTU question bank frequently asks to differentiate them in a 3-mark Part-A question.

| Robot Role | License | Triggered By | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Attended** | Attended | Human user (F5 in Studio, or from Assistant) | Front-office assistance, runs on the user's own desktop |
| **Unattended** | Unattended | Orchestrator (Job / Trigger) | Back-office production — **this is the "server robot"** |
| **Development** | Development | Developer for testing | Studio debug & unit testing only — *cannot run in production* |
| **NonProduction** | NonProduction | QA / staging environments | Test Orchestrator deployments before go-live |

> [!NOTE]
> An **Unattended Robot** is what KTU means when the syllabus says *"deploy the robot to a server."* It must run on a Windows Server, must use a domain/service account (or local account with auto-login), and must be reachable by Orchestrator on TCP **port 443 (HTTPS)**.

## 2.3 The Deployment Pre-Requisite Checklist

Before a single `.nupkg` is uploaded, the following must be in place — KTU often frames these as "steps to be completed *before* deployment":

1. ✅ UiPath **Studio** is installed on the developer machine.
2. ✅ UiPath **Robot** is installed on the **target server** in *Service Mode* (recommended for unattended).
3. ✅ The **Orchestrator URL** and **Machine Key / Client ID** are known.
4. ✅ A **Tenant** and at least one **Folder** exist in Orchestrator.
5. ✅ A **Machine Template** is registered, with the **Machine Key** copied back to the server-side Robot.
6. ✅ An **Unattended Robot** entity is created in Orchestrator, bound to the Machine and to a credential store.
7. ✅ The **process (.nupkg)** is published from Studio → uploaded to Orchestrator → assigned to the Folder.

## 2.4 KTU "Formula Sheet" — Key Parameters & Their Units

> [!TIP]
> While RPA is not a formula-heavy subject, KTU examiners award marks for *quantitative correctness* of configuration parameters. Memorize this table verbatim.

| Parameter | Symbolic Notation | Allowed / Default Value | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Orchestrator Endpoint URL | $U_{orch}$ | `https://cloud.uipath.com/{org}/{tenant}/orchestrator_` | REST API base URL the Robot polls |
| Machine Key | $K_{m}$ | 32-character hex GUID | One-way trust token between Robot ↔ Orchestrator |
| Client ID / Secret (modern auth) | $CID, CSEC$ | OAuth2 scope `OR.Robot` | Replaces Machine Key in Modern Folders |
| Polling Interval | $T_{poll}$ | **30 seconds** (default) | How often the Robot asks Orchestrator "any new Job?" |
| Heartbeat Interval | $T_{hb}$ | **60 seconds** (default) | Robot's liveness signal |
| Job Timeout | $T_{job}^{max}$ | Unlimited (or set per process) | Maximum wall-clock allowed before the job is killed |
| Max Concurrent Jobs (per Robot) | $N_{cj}$ | 1 for Standard; up to 10 for **High-Density** | How many parallel processes one OS user can run |
| Log Level | $L$ | Trace / Verbose / Info / Warn / Error / Fatal | Verbosity of execution logs sent to Orchestrator |
| Screenshot on Failure | $S_{fail}$ | `True` / `False` | Captures desktop state for debugging |
| Queue Retry Count | $R_q$ | 0 (default) → ∞ | Number of automatic requeues on Application/Business Exception |
| Trigger Cron Expression | $C_{cron}$ | `0 0 9 * * ?` (daily 9 AM) | Quartz-style schedule for Time Triggers |

The deployment is considered **complete and valid** when:

$$Deployment_{status} = \begin{cases} \text{Ready} & \text{if } (K_m \text{ valid}) \land (Robot_{licensed}=\text{Unattended}) \land (Process_{assigned}=\text{True}) \\ \text{Pending} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

This boolean is the most concise mental model for the KTU viva: *"deployment is a logical AND of authentication, licensing, and assignment."*

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking** — Nightly reconciliation robots deployed on a Windows Server, triggered at 02:00 AM via a Time Trigger, with a Queue Trigger to handle exceptions the next morning.
- **Healthcare** — HIPAA-compliant, fully unattended patient intake robots deployed in a *NonProduction* tenant first, then promoted.
- **Manufacturing** — High-Density robots (8 concurrent jobs per OS user) on a single powerful VM, used for parallel SAP invoice processing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Deployment Procedure — Exhaustive Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> This section adapts the *exhaustive derivation* mandate to a **procedural derivation** — every workflow transition, configuration click, and logical gate is written out explicitly. KTU 14-mark questions on this topic are essentially *"walk through the deployment lifecycle"* — practise the exact ordering below.

## 3.1 Phase 1 — Authoring and Publishing (Developer Side)

**Step 1.1 — Build the workflow in UiPath Studio.**
The developer creates a `Main.xaml` and any invoked workflows inside a UiPath Project. The project is version-controlled (Git / SVN / TFS).

**Step 1.2 — Validate and Publish.**
From Studio ribbon → *Design* tab → **Publish**.

$$\text{Publish} : \text{Project} \xrightarrow{\text{Compile + Sign}} \text{Orchestrator Package (.nupkg)}$$

* The compiler produces a NuGet package whose internal name follows `{ProjectName}.{Version}.nupkg`.
* The package contains all XAML files, dependencies, and a `project.json` manifest.
* On **Orchestrator Local / On-Prem**, the package is published to a local feed (e.g., `C:\Program Files (x86)\UiPath\Orchestrator\Packages`).
* On **UiPath Automation Cloud**, the package is uploaded directly to the *Tenant Packages* feed.

**Step 1.3 — Copy or upload the `.nupkg`** to a location accessible to the Orchestrator admin.

## 3.2 Phase 2 — Orchestrator Provisioning (Admin Side)

**Step 2.1 — Create / Select a Tenant.**
In Automation Cloud, the tenant is provisioned at org creation. In on-prem Orchestrator, the admin defines a Tenant name and database.

**Step 2.2 — Create a Folder.**
Navigate to *Tenant → Folders → Add Folder*. Choose between *Classic Folder* (requires Environments) and *Modern Folder* (recommended, role-based).

> [!TIP]
> If you are deploying to a server for the very first time, **Modern Folder** is the default and removes the need to manually create an Environment.

**Step 2.3 — Register the Machine.**
Go to *Tenant → Machines → Add Machine* → choose:

- *Standard Machine* (one Robot = one Windows session, one job at a time)
- *Template Machine* (one Robot + N credentials → up to N High-Density Robots)

After creation, Orchestrator displays a **Machine Key**. This key is the trust anchor that the on-server Robot will use.

**Step 2.4 — Provision the Robot.**
*Machines → Robot → Add Robot*. Configure:

$$\text{Robot} = f(\,M_{machine}, \; U_{cred}, \; L_{type}=\text{Unattended}, \; D_{domain}\,)$$

| Field | Example | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Machine | `WS-PROD-01` | Target Windows server |
| Type | Unattended | Server-grade license |
| Domain\Username | `CORP\svc_uipath` | Service account with Log on as Batch Job rights |
| Password / Credential Store | CyberArk / Orchestrator store | Secure secret |
| Description | "Nightly invoice robot" | Operational documentation |

**Step 2.5 — Upload the Process Package.**
*Tenant → Packages → Upload* (drag-and-drop the `.nupkg`). Or *Folder → Processes → Add Process → pick version*.

**Step 2.6 — Create / Select an Environment (Classic Folders only).**

$$\text{Environment}_{classic} = \{\,Process_1,\, Robot_A,\, Robot_B\,\}$$

A process is published to the environment, and any robot in that environment can claim jobs from the environment's queue.

For **Modern Folders**, this step is replaced by a direct *Folder → Processes → Assign Robot* action.

## 3.3 Phase 3 — On-Server Robot Configuration

**Step 3.1 — Install UiPath Robot** on the target server in **Service Mode**:

```
UiPathRobot.exe /install --port 443 --service --key <MachineKey>
```

**Step 3.2 — Connect Robot to Orchestrator.**
Two options:

1. **High-Density via UiPath Assistant:** Open Assistant → *Preferences → Orchestrator Settings* → paste the **Orchestrator URL** and **Machine Key** → click *Connect*.
2. **Command-line provisioning (silent):**

$$
\text{Connection} \Leftarrow \{U_{orch}, K_m, T_{poll}=30s\}
$$

**Step 3.3 — Validate the connection.**
The Robot tray icon turns **green** when:
- License is assigned ✔
- Machine Key matches ✔
- Orchestrator URL is reachable on port 443 ✔
- The Robot is in the *Available* state ✔

## 3.4 Phase 4 — Activation and Job Execution

**Step 4.1 — Trigger the Job.**
Three trigger classes, each a 7-mark favourite in KTU:

| Trigger Type | Mechanism | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Time Trigger** | Quartz cron expression | `0 0 2 * * ?` → 02:00 daily |
| **Queue Trigger** | New item added to Orchestrator Queue | Invoice upload → start processing |
| **Event / API Trigger** | HTTP POST to `/odata/Jobs/UiPath.Server.Configuration.OData.StartJobs` | Web form → job |

**Step 4.2 — Monitor the Job.**
*Orchestrator → Jobs → Live* shows state transitions:

$$S_{job} : \text{Pending} \rightarrow \text{Running} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Successful} \\ \text{Faulted} \\ \text{Stopped} \end{cases}$$

**Step 4.3 — Audit logs and screenshots.**
Execution logs (with configurable $L$) flow back to *Orchestrator → Logs*. If $S_{fail}=\text{True}$, a screenshot is attached to the faulted job.

## 3.5 Phase 5 — Post-Deployment Maintenance

- **Update:** Publish a new version of the package; Orchestrator auto-detects the new version.
- **Rollback:** Select an older package version in the *Process* configuration.
- **Undeploy:** Disable the Trigger, terminate live jobs, unassign the Robot, and finally delete the package.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse *uninstalling the Robot* with *undeploying the process*. They are different! The Robot runtime is the *agent*; the process is the *work*. You can have 50 processes deployed to 1 robot, or 1 process deployed to 50 robots.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Common Failure Modes

| Symptom | Root Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Robot icon stays **red** in tray | Wrong Machine Key | Re-paste key, restart `UiPathSvc` |
| Job stuck in *Pending* | Robot disconnected from Orchestrator | Check network port 443, DNS |
| License *Invalid* error | Studio license leaking / wrong role | Re-assign Unattended license in *Tenant → Licenses* |
| Job fails with *Access Denied* | Service account lacks rights | Grant "Log on as Batch Job" via `secpol.msc` |
| Screenshots missing on failure | $S_{fail}=\text{False}$ in Robot settings | Enable in `UiPath.settings` |
| High-Density jobs conflict | Exceeded $N_{cj}$ | Increase template credentials or use a bigger server |

This matrix is the *highest-yield* revision block for the KTU 14-mark "troubleshooting deployment" questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams below are Mermaid-rendered block-level functional architectures, safe from reserved-keyword collisions. Node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed (`n1`, `n2`, `stepA`, etc.), and labels are clean uppercase text.

## 4.1 End-to-End Deployment Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    n1["DEVELOPER WORKSTATION"]
    n2["UIPATH STUDIO PROJECT MAIN XAML"]
    n3["PUBLISH ACTION RIBBON"]
    n4["NUGET PACKAGE NUPKG"]
    n5["ORCHESTRATOR TENANT"]
    n6["PACKAGE FEED UPLOAD"]
    n7["MODERN FOLDER CREATE"]
    n8["MACHINE TEMPLATE REGISTER"]
    n9["ROBOT ENTITY UNATTENDED"]
    n10["WINDOWS SERVER TARGET"]
    n11["UIPATH ROBOT SERVICE MODE"]
    n12["ORCHESTRATOR URL MACHINE KEY"]
    n13["CONNECTION ESTABLISHED GREEN ICON"]
    n14["PROCESS ASSIGNED TO FOLDER"]
    n15["TIME TRIGGER OR QUEUE TRIGGER"]
    n16["JOB EXECUTION PENDING RUNNING"]
    n17["LOGS AND SCREENSHOTS RETURNED TO ORCHESTRATOR"]

    n1 --> n2 --> n3 --> n4 --> n6 --> n5
    n5 --> n7
    n5 --> n8 --> n9
    n5 --> n14
    n10 --> n11 --> n12 --> n13
    n13 --> n16
    n14 --> n15 --> n16 --> n17
    n9 --> n16
```

## 4.2 Orchestrator Object Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    o1["TENANT LEVEL HIGHEST BOUNDARY"]
    o2["FOLDER MODERN OR CLASSIC"]
    o3["MACHINE TEMPLATE OR STANDARD"]
    o4["ROBOT UNATTENDED LICENSED"]
    o5["PROCESS PUBLISHED NUPKG"]
    o6["JOB INSTANCE OF PROCESS"]
    o7["TRIGGER TIME QUEUE EVENT API"]
    o8["LOG AUDIT AND SCREENSHOT"]

    o1 --> o2 --> o3 --> o4 --> o6
    o2 --> o5 --> o6
    o6 --> o7
    o6 --> o8
```

## 4.3 Robot Connection State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    s1["DISCONNECTED"]
    s2["CONNECTING URL KEY"]
    s3["AVAILABLE GREEN"]
    s4["BUSY JOB RUNNING"]
    s5["FAULTED LICENSE OR NETWORK"]
    s6["DISABLED ADMIN ACTION"]

    s1 --> s2 --> s3
    s3 --> s4
    s4 --> s3
    s3 --> s5
    s5 --> s2
    s3 --> s6
    s6 --> s2
```

## 4.4 Deployment Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    d1["START WORKFLOW READY"]
    d2{"IS LICENSE UNATTENDED"}
    d3{"IS FOLDER MODERN"}
    d4{"IS MACHINE REGISTERED"}
    d5{"IS ROBOT CONNECTED"}
    d6["CREATE ENVIRONMENT CLASSIC"]
    d7["DIRECT FOLDER ASSIGNMENT MODERN"]
    d8["TRIGGER JOB"]
    d9["DEPLOYED READY"]

    d1 --> d2
    d2 -- NO --> d2a["STOP ASSIGN UNATTENDED LICENSE"]
    d2a --> d2
    d2 -- YES --> d3
    d3 -- CLASSIC --> d4
    d3 -- MODERN --> d7
    d4 -- NO --> d4a["REGISTER MACHINE IN ORCHESTRATOR"]
    d4a --> d4
    d4 -- YES --> d5
    d5 -- NO --> d5a["CONFIGURE ROBOT ON SERVER WITH KEY"]
    d5a --> d5
    d5 -- YES --> d6 --> d8 --> d9
    d7 --> d5
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are simulated in the *exact* KTU End-Semester Examination (ESE) format for the 2024 Scheme — 3-mark Part A and 14-mark Part B with internal choice. Marks are distributed by sub-part as per KTU norms.

---

## 📘 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Attended and Unattended Robots in UiPath. State one deployment scenario for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Attended Robot | Unattended Robot |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Triggered manually by a human user via UiPath Assistant | Triggered by Orchestrator (Time / Queue / API) |
| **Runtime Host** | User's own desktop | Dedicated Windows Server / VM |
| **License** | Attended license | Unattended license |
| **Use Case** | Front-office customer-service helper | Back-office nightly reconciliation (✅ typical *server deployment*) |

*Verdict:* For **"deploy the robot to a server"**, only **Unattended** Robots are used because they can run without a logged-in human.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is a Machine Key in UiPath Orchestrator, and why is it important during deployment?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Machine Key** is a unique 32-character identifier generated by Orchestrator when a Machine is registered. It is the **trust credential** that the locally-installed Robot uses to authenticate itself to the Orchestrator. Without a valid Machine Key, the Robot remains in a *Disconnected* state and Orchestrator cannot push jobs to it. The Key is therefore the **single most security-critical artifact** exchanged during deployment and must be kept secret.

---

## 📗 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

### Q3A. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**(a)** Explain in detail the **end-to-end lifecycle of deploying a UiPath process from Studio to a production server**, including the role of Orchestrator, Machine, Robot, and Environment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Classic Folder** and **Modern Folder** deployment models in Orchestrator. List any two real-world benefits of Modern Folders. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) End-to-End Deployment Lifecycle (7 marks):**

1. **Authoring (1 mark):** Developer builds the workflow in UiPath Studio as a project with `Main.xaml`, sub-workflows, and the REFramework template.

2. **Publishing (1 mark):** The *Publish* command compiles the project into a NuGet package (`.nupkg`). The package includes XAML, dependencies, and a manifest.

3. **Uploading to Orchestrator (1 mark):** The `.nupkg` is uploaded to the *Tenant → Packages* feed. For Modern Folders, the package is auto-routed to the folder.

4. **Infrastructure Setup (2 marks):** Administrator creates:
   - A **Folder** (Modern) — for RBAC.
   - A **Machine Template** — with $K_m$ generated.
   - An **Unattended Robot** — bound to the Machine, with a service account credential.

5. **Server-Side Configuration (1 mark):** UiPath Robot is installed in **Service Mode** on the Windows Server, configured with $U_{orch}$ and $K_m$, and the connection is validated (green tray icon).

6. **Job Execution (1 mark):** A **Trigger** (Time / Queue / Event) is created in Orchestrator. When fired, Orchestrator dispatches a **Job** to the Robot, which executes the process and reports logs back.

**(b) Classic vs Modern Folder Comparison (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Classic Folder | Modern Folder |
|---|---|---|
| Environment Object | Required — explicit binding between Process and Robots | Not required; Robots are folder-scoped |
| RBAC Granularity | Per role assignment to folder | Per role + per folder + per sub-folder inheritance |
| Package Routing | Manual to folder | Automatic |
| Sub-Folders | Not supported | Supported with **automatic permission inheritance** |
| Tenant-level Roles | Limited | Fine-grained (e.g., *Folder Administrator*, *Robot Operator*) |
| Best Suited For | Legacy Orchestrator ≤ 2019 | **Modern, multi-team, enterprise deployments** |

**Two Real-World Benefits of Modern Folders:**
1. **Hierarchical RBAC** — A parent folder's permissions cascade to sub-folders, so a regional manager in *EMEA* automatically inherits access to all country sub-folders without manual re-assignment.
2. **Simplified Deployment** — No manual Environment creation; a Process is assigned directly to a Folder, and any Robot in that folder can claim its jobs.

---

### Q3B. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Describe the **different types of Robot licenses** available in UiPath and explain which one is used when deploying to a server. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write short notes on **Time Triggers, Queue Triggers, and High-Density Robots** in the context of server-side deployment. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Robot License Types (7 marks):**

UiPath provides four Robot license types, each with a distinct cost and use-case profile:

1. **Attended (1.5 marks)** — Lower cost; runs on user desktops under human supervision. Cannot be used for server deployment.
2. **Unattended (2 marks)** — **The default for server deployment.** Runs without a human, scheduled by Orchestrator. Includes auto-recovery, screenshots on failure, and a service-mode runtime.
3. **Development (1.5 marks)** — Free with Studio; used to debug and unit-test workflows. *Cannot* be used in production.
4. **NonProduction (2 marks)** — Lower cost than Unattended; used exclusively in Test/QA Orchestrator tenants to validate deployments before production go-live.

**Verdict:** A server deployment **must** use the **Unattended** license (or **NonProduction** for staging). A *Development* license deployed to a server will fail with a *License Invalid* error at job-start.

**(b) Notes on Triggers and High-Density (7 marks):**

- **Time Trigger (2.5 marks)** — A cron-based schedule attached to a Process. Example: `0 0 2 * * ?` fires the process at 02:00 AM daily. Used for **predictable, periodic** workloads like nightly report generation.
- **Queue Trigger (2.5 marks)** — Activates a process whenever **new items appear in an Orchestrator Queue**. Used for **transactional, on-demand** workloads like invoice processing — each new queue item spawns (or queues) a job.
- **High-Density Robots (2 marks)** — A *Template Machine* allows multiple Robots (and therefore multiple concurrent jobs) to share a single Windows OS user account, one job per slot. A single server can therefore host up to $N_{cj}=10$ parallel unattended jobs, dramatically reducing infrastructure cost.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do not** write *"deploy the robot"* without specifying **which license** you used — 1–2 marks will be docked.
> - **Do not** confuse *Environment* (Classic Folder object) with *Operating-System environment variable* — KTU examiners will fail your answer if the two are conflated.
> - **Do not** say *"the robot is deployed to Orchestrator"* — the *Robot* is installed on the *server*; the *Process package* is uploaded to *Orchestrator*. Wording matters in KTU valuation.
> - **Do not** omit the **port 443** requirement — it is a recurring KTU question and worth full marks when stated explicitly.
> - **Do not** list only the steps — KTU 14-mark questions also expect **justification** (the *why* behind each step), not just procedural narration.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap — "Things to Remember" Rapid-Revision Checklist

- 🔑 **Deployment = Package + Publish + Connect + Trigger** — the four-pillar mantra.
- 🔑 **Unattended Robot** is the only robot type that runs on a server without a human.
- 🔑 **Machine Key** ($K_m$) is the trust token linking the on-server Robot to Orchestrator.
- 🔑 **Polling interval** $T_{poll}=30s$ and **Heartbeat** $T_{hb}=60s$ are the canonical defaults.
- 🔑 **Modern Folders** > Classic Folders in all 2024+ deployments — they remove the Environment object.
- 🔑 **High-Density Robots** = one Windows user, up to **$N_{cj}=10$** parallel jobs.
- 🔑 **Service Mode** installation is required for unattended servers (not User Mode).
- 🔑 **Port 443 (HTTPS)** is mandatory for Robot ↔ Orchestrator communication.
- 🔑 **Triggers:** Time (cron), Queue (transactional), Event/API (external).
- 🔑 **Logs and screenshots** flow back to Orchestrator for audit — set $L$ and $S_{fail}$ deliberately.
- 🔑 A *Development* license **cannot** run production jobs; the job will fail at runtime.
- 🔑 The two most common failure modes: **Wrong Machine Key** (red icon) and **Missing Log-on-as-Batch-Job right** (Access Denied).
- 🔑 **Undeploying ≠ Uninstalling** — undeploy disables the *Process*; uninstall removes the *Robot runtime*.

> 📌 **One-Line Takeaway for KTU Viva:**
> *"Deploying a robot to a server means publishing a process as a `.nupkg` from UiPath Studio, registering an Unattended Robot and a Machine in UiPath Orchestrator, configuring the on-server Robot with the Orchestrator URL and Machine Key over HTTPS port 443, assigning the process to a Modern Folder, and triggering it via a Time, Queue, or API trigger — after which the Job state machine Pending → Running → Successful/Faulted governs the runtime lifecycle."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Publishing and Managing Updates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Publishing and Managing Updates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Publishing** in Robotic Process Automation (RPA) refers to the formal process of packaging, versioning, and deploying an automated workflow (bot/robot) from a development environment to a centralized orchestration platform (such as UiPath Orchestrator, Automation Anywhere Control Room, or Blue Prism Digital Exchange) so that it can be executed on target machines in production environments.

**Managing Updates** is the continuous lifecycle activity of modifying, re-versioning, testing, and redeploying automation packages without disrupting live business operations. It includes version control, dependency handling, rollback mechanisms, and continuous integration/deployment (CI/CD) practices.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology Glossary:**
> - **Bot / Robot**: The compiled executable automation workflow.
> - **Package**: A compressed archive (`.nupkg`, `.bpk`, etc.) containing the workflow and its dependencies.
> - **Orchestrator**: A centralized web-based server that manages, schedules, and monitors bots.
> - **Tenant**: An isolated organizational unit in the orchestrator.
> - **Environment**: A logical container (Dev, Test, Staging, Production) where bots are executed.
> - **Asset**: Reusable configuration data (credentials, URLs, variables) stored centrally.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Menu" Model

Imagine a **chef (developer)** who creates a new dish in a **test kitchen (Studio)**. Before customers can order it, the chef must:

1. **Finalize the recipe** (freeze the code).
2. **Write it on the official menu** (publish to Orchestrator).
3. **Train the waiters** (distribute to Unattended/Attended robots).
4. **Track which version of the recipe is being served** (versioning).

When the dish needs a tweak (less salt), the chef creates a **new recipe version (v2.0)**, tests it in a **small trial area (Test Environment)**, and only then replaces the old one on the main menu. The old version is kept in the **recipe archive (rollback)** in case customers complain.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy Mapping:**
> - Chef = RPA Developer
> - Test Kitchen = Studio / Development Environment
> - Menu = Orchestrator Package Feed
> - Waiters = Robots (Attended/Unattended)
> - Restaurant Floor = Production Environment
> - Recipe Archive = Version History / Rollback Point

---

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

In the NEP 2020 outcome-based education framework, this topic maps to the following Course Outcomes (COs):

- **CO4**: *Deploy, monitor, and manage RPA bots in enterprise environments* (Cognitive Level: Apply / Analyze).
- **CO5**: *Implement governance, version control, and CI/CD pipelines for automation lifecycle management* (Cognitive Level: Create / Evaluate).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** In real-world enterprises (Banks, BPOs, Insurance), **70% of automation failures** occur due to poor version management and uncontrolled updates. Mastering this topic is critical for **Production-Grade RPA Engineering**.

---

## 1.4 Core Components of Publishing & Update Management

The lifecycle consists of **6 critical phases**:

1. **Development** → Build workflow in Studio.
2. **Testing** → Validate in isolated Test Environment.
3. **Packaging** → Compile into deployable artifact.
4. **Publishing** → Upload to Orchestrator / Repository.
5. **Distribution** → Assign to target environments and robots.
6. **Maintenance** → Apply patches, hotfixes, and version upgrades.

The three pillars that govern this lifecycle are:

- **Versioning** (Semantic Versioning: Major.Minor.Patch)
- **Environment Segregation** (Dev → Test → Staging → Prod)
- **Change Management** (Approval workflows, audit trails)

---

## 1.5 Standard Metrics & Industry Benchmarks

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Metrics (bold for emphasis):**
> - **Mean Time to Deploy (MTTD)**: Target ≤ **15 minutes** in mature CI/CD pipelines.
> - **Rollback Time**: Target ≤ **5 minutes** for critical automations.
> - **Uptime SLA**: **99.9%** for production bots.
> - **Version Compatibility Window**: **2 prior major versions** supported simultaneously.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Publishing Pipeline — Structured Logic

The publishing process follows a **deterministic, gated workflow**:

### Step 1: Pre-Publish Validation
- Static code analysis (linting, dependency checks).
- Unit test pass rate must be **100%** for critical paths.
- Peer code review sign-off.

### Step 2: Package Generation
The Studio tool compiles the workflow into a **signed, versioned package**.

**Package Structure (Typical `.nupkg` format):**

- `lib/` → Compiled workflow assemblies (`.dll`).
- `content/` → XAML files, assets, screenshots.
- `package.json` → Metadata (author, version, dependencies).
- `signature.p7s` → Digital signature for integrity.

### Step 3: Publishing to Orchestrator
- Upload via **Orchestrator API** (REST endpoint: `/odata/Processes/UiPath.Server.Configuration.OData.UploadPackage`).
- Or via **CLI** (`uipcli package deploy`).
- Authentication via **OAuth2 / API Tokens**.

### Step 4: Environment Distribution
- Map package version → Environment → Robot Pool.
- Create **Process** entity in Orchestrator.
- Configure triggers (Schedule, Queue, Manual, Event-based).

### Step 5: Post-Publish Monitoring
- Verify execution logs.
- Check **Job Status**: `Successful`, `Faulted`, `Stopped`.
- Confirm **Queue Throughput** metrics.

### Step 6: Update Management
- Increment version (e.g., `1.4.2` → `1.5.0`).
- Publish to **Test Environment** first.
- Run **Regression Suite**.
- Promote to Production via **Change Advisory Board (CAB)** approval.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Matters | Engineering Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Pre-Publish Validation | Prevents faulty code reaching Prod | Shifts testing **left** (Shift-Left Testing) |
| Package Generation | Encapsulates all dependencies | Ensures **reproducibility** across machines |
| Orchestrator Upload | Centralized control plane | Enables **single source of truth** |
| Environment Distribution | Decouples deployment from release | Supports **Continuous Delivery** |
| Post-Publish Monitoring | Detects runtime anomalies | Feeds data into **AIOps** for predictive maintenance |
| Update Management | Maintains business continuity | Implements **Blue-Green / Canary deployment** |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Concept Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** RPA publishing is conceptual/process-driven, but the following formal relationships govern version management and deployment metrics.

### 2.3.1 Semantic Versioning Rule

$$\text{Version} = MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH$$

Where:

- $MAJOR$ → Breaking changes (incompatible API/workflow signature).
- $MINOR$ → Backward-compatible feature additions.
- $PATCH$ → Backward-compatible bug fixes.

### 2.3.2 Deployment Frequency Formula

$$F_{deploy} = \frac{N_{releases}}{T_{window}}$$

Where:

- $F_{deploy}$ → Deployment frequency (releases per unit time).
- $N_{releases}$ → Number of releases in the observation window.
- $T_{window}$ → Time period (days, weeks, months).

### 2.3.3 Change Failure Rate (Industry KPI)

$$R_{cf} = \frac{N_{failed\_changes}}{N_{total\_changes}} \times 100\%$$

Target industry benchmark: $R_{cf} \leq 15\%$.

### 2.3.4 Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR)

$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (T_{resolved,i} - T_{detected,i})}{n}$$

Where:

- $T_{resolved,i}$ → Time of resolution for incident $i$.
- $T_{detected,i}$ → Time of detection for incident $i$.
- $n$ → Number of incidents in the period.

### 2.3.5 KTU Cheat Sheet — Publishing Concepts

| Concept | Definition | KTU Exam Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| Orchestrator | Central management server for bots | Very High |
| Tenant | Isolated org unit in Orchestrator | High |
| Folder | Logical grouping of entities | High |
| Robot | Execution agent (Attended/Unattended) | Very High |
| Job | A single execution instance of a process | High |
| Trigger | Event that initiates a job (Schedule/Queue) | Very High |
| Queue | Container for transaction items | Medium |
| Asset | Centralized config variable | High |
| Package | Deployable bot artifact | Very High |
| Versioning | Semantic version control scheme | High |
| Rollback | Reverting to a previous stable version | Medium |
| Blue-Green Deploy | Two parallel prod environments (active/idle) | Medium |
| Canary Release | Gradual rollout to a subset of users | Medium |
| CI/CD | Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment | High |
| CAB | Change Advisory Board (governance) | Low |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Note for Tables:** All vertical separators in mathematical content (e.g., absolute value $\vert x \vert$) are rendered using the LaTeX command `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs

> [!NOTE]
> **Exhaustive Content Mandate:** All derivations, calculations, and procedural sequences are fully expanded below. No truncation has been applied.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Semantic Versioning Increment Logic

### Problem Statement
A bot is at version `2.3.7`. The development team makes the following changes:
- Adds a new optional input argument (backward compatible).
- Fixes a critical bug in the email-sending activity.
- Modifies a workflow argument from `String` to `Int32` (breaking change).

Determine the new version number.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify the change type for each modification.**

- **Modification 1**: New optional input argument.
  - This is a **backward-compatible feature addition**.
  - Classification: $MINOR$ change.

- **Modification 2**: Bug fix in email activity.
  - This is a **backward-compatible defect repair**.
  - Classification: $PATCH$ change.

- **Modification 3**: Argument type change `String` → `Int32`.
  - This is an **incompatible interface change**.
  - Classification: $MAJOR$ change.

**Step 2: Apply the precedence rule.**

In Semantic Versioning, the precedence rule is:
> The $MAJOR$ version takes precedence over $MINOR$, which takes precedence over $PATCH$.

Therefore, the breaking change resets both $MINOR$ and $PATCH$ to zero.

**Step 3: Perform the increment.**

Initial version: $V_{old} = 2.3.7$

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{new} &= (V_{old,MAJOR} + 1).(V_{new,MINOR} = 0).(V_{new,PATCH} = 0) \\
V_{new} &= (2 + 1).0.0 \\
V_{new} &= 3.0.0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Final Answer.**

$$\boxed{V_{new} = 3.0.0}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The $MINOR$ and $PATCH$ are **always reset to zero** when $MAJOR$ is incremented. The reverse is not true.

---

## 3.2 Walkthrough: Deployment Frequency Calculation

### Problem Statement
An RPA team deployed their invoice-processing bot **24 times** in a **30-day** sprint cycle. Calculate the deployment frequency and classify the team's DevOps maturity per the DORA report.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify the given variables.**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{releases} &= 24 \\
T_{window} &= 30 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Apply the deployment frequency formula.**

$$F_{deploy} = \frac{N_{releases}}{T_{window}}$$

**Step 3: Substitute values.**

$$F_{deploy} = \frac{24}{30}$$

**Step 4: Simplify.**

$$F_{deploy} = 0.8 \text{ releases per day}$$

**Step 5: Convert to monthly equivalent (per 30 days).**

$$F_{deploy,monthly} = 0.8 \times 30 = 24 \text{ releases per month}$$

**Step 6: Classify per DORA Performance Quadrants.**

| Classification | Deployment Frequency | Team Rating |
|---|---|---|
| Elite | Multiple deploys per day | — |
| High | Between once per day and once per week | — |
| Medium | Between once per week and once per month | **Our team (0.8/day)** ✓ |
| Low | Between once per month and once per six months | — |

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{F_{deploy} = 0.8 \text{ deploys/day, classifying as Medium DevOps maturity.}}$$

---

## 3.3 Walkthrough: Change Failure Rate Analysis

### Problem Statement
A bank's RPA team made **50 changes** to their loan-processing automation in Q1. Of these, **8** resulted in production failures requiring hotfixes. Calculate the Change Failure Rate and determine if it meets the industry benchmark.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify variables.**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{total\_changes} &= 50 \\
N_{failed\_changes} &= 8
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Apply the formula.**

$$R_{cf} = \frac{N_{failed\_changes}}{N_{total\_changes}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3: Substitute.**

$$R_{cf} = \frac{8}{50} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4: Compute.**

$$R_{cf} = 0.16 \times 100\% = 16\%$$

**Step 5: Compare to benchmark.**

Industry benchmark (Google DORA): $R_{cf} \leq 15\%$ for Elite teams; $0\% \leq R_{cf} \leq 15\%$ is acceptable.

Since $16\% > 15\%$, the team **does not meet** the elite benchmark.

**Step 6: Recommendation.**

$$\boxed{R_{cf} = 16\%, \text{ slightly above benchmark. Recommend adding automated regression tests.}}$$

---

## 3.4 Walkthrough: MTTR Computation for Bot Outages

### Problem Statement
The accounts-payable bot experienced 3 outages last month:
- Outage 1: Detected at 09:00, resolved at 09:45.
- Outage 2: Detected at 14:20, resolved at 15:00.
- Outage 3: Detected at 22:00 (next day at 00:30 resolved).

Compute the MTTR.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Compute individual recovery times.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta t_1 &= 09\!:\!45 - 09\!:\!00 = 45 \text{ minutes} \\
\Delta t_2 &= 15\!:\!00 - 14\!:\!20 = 40 \text{ minutes} \\
\Delta t_3 &= 00\!:\!30 (\text{next day}) - 22\!:\!00 = 150 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Apply the MTTR formula.**

$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta t_i}{n}$$

**Step 3: Substitute and sum.**

$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{45 + 40 + 150}{3}$$

**Step 4: Compute.**

$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{235}{3}$$

**Step 5: Final result in minutes and hours.**

$$\text{MTTR} \approx 78.33 \text{ minutes} \approx 1.31 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 6: SLA evaluation.**

Industry target: $\text{MTTR} \leq 60$ minutes for priority-1 bots.

Since $78.33 > 60$, the team **violates the SLA**.

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\text{MTTR} \approx 78.33 \text{ minutes, exceeding the 60-minute SLA target.}}$$

---

## 3.5 Comprehensive Update Management Lifecycle (Procedural Sequence)

A complete update cycle for an existing production bot follows this **8-stage gated sequence**:

1. **Change Request Initiation** → Stakeholder submits a Jira/ServiceNow ticket.
2. **Impact Analysis** → Developer assesses affected dependencies, queues, and SLAs.
3. **Code Modification in Dev** → Edits performed in isolated Studio workspace.
4. **Automated Test Execution** → CI pipeline runs unit + integration tests.
5. **Staging Deployment** → Package published to **Staging Environment** with version `2.4.1-rc.1` (Release Candidate).
6. **User Acceptance Testing (UAT)** → Business users validate the output.
7. **Production Promotion** → Version incremented to `2.4.1` and deployed via **Blue-Green** strategy.
8. **Post-Deployment Verification** → Monitor logs, queue throughput, and exception rates for **24 hours**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Compilation Note:** All Mermaid node IDs are alphanumeric (prefixed with letters). No markdown formatting or reserved keywords are used inside node labels.

---

## 4.1 End-to-End Publishing & Update Management Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    devA[Developer Studio] --> lintB[Static Code Analysis]
    lintB --> unitC[Unit Test Suite]
    unitC --> peerD[Peer Code Review]
    peerD --> pkgE[Package Builder]
    pkgE --> signF[Digital Sign and Version]
    signF --> repoG[Orchestrator Package Feed]
    repoG --> testH[Test Environment]
    testH --> regI[Regression Test Suite]
    regI --> stageJ[Staging Environment]
    stageJ --> uatK[User Acceptance Testing]
    uatK --> cabL[Change Advisory Board Approval]
    cabL --> prodM[Production Environment Blue Green]
    prodM --> monN[24 Hour Monitoring]
    monN --> decisionO{Stable?}
    decisionO -- Yes --> doneP[Release Closed]
    decisionO -- No --> rollbackQ[Rollback to Previous Version]
    rollbackQ --> repoG
    doneP --> archiveR[Version Archive]
```

**Diagram Interpretation (Step-by-Step):**
- `devA` to `signF`: The **CI (Continuous Integration)** phase.
- `repoG` to `cabL`: The **Continuous Delivery** phase (staging & governance).
- `prodM` to `monN`: The **Continuous Deployment** phase.
- `decisionO`: The automated rollback decision gate.

---

## 4.2 Orchestrator Entity Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    tenantRoot[Tenant] --> folderOne[Folder HR Automation]
    tenantRoot --> folderTwo[Folder Finance Automation]
    folderOne --> machineOne[Machine Pool 1]
    folderOne --> machineTwo[Machine Pool 2]
    machineOne --> robotOne[Attended Robot User A]
    machineOne --> robotTwo[Unattended Robot Pool]
    folderTwo --> procOne[Process Invoice Bot v2.4.1]
    folderTwo --> procTwo[Process Payroll Bot v1.7.0]
    procOne --> queueOne[Queue Invoice Transactions]
    procOne --> assetOne[Asset DB Credentials]
    procTwo --> triggerOne[Trigger Monthly Schedule]
    procTwo --> triggerTwo[Trigger Event Based]
```

**Hierarchy Explanation:**
- **Tenant** is the topmost isolation boundary.
- **Folders** organize automations by business unit.
- **Machines / Robots** form the execution layer.
- **Processes, Queues, Assets, Triggers** are the orchestration objects.

---

## 4.3 Update Deployment Strategy Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    bigBang[Big Bang Deployment] --> bigBangDesc[Replace old version instantly with new version. High risk. Low cost.]
    blueGreen[Blue Green Deployment] --> blueGreenDesc[Two identical prod environments. Switch traffic after validation. Low risk. High cost.]
    canary[Canary Release] --> canaryDesc[Roll out to 5 percent of users first, then expand. Very low risk. Slow.]
    rolling[Rolling Update] --> rollingDesc[Update instances in batches. Balanced risk and cost.]
```

---

## 4.4 Version Control Branching Model for RPA

```mermaid
gitGraph
    commit id: "v1.0.0 Production"
    commit id: "v1.0.1 Hotfix"
    branch develop
    checkout develop
    commit id: "Feature A"
    commit id: "Feature B"
    checkout main
    merge develop id: "v1.1.0 Release"
    commit id: "v1.1.1 Patch"
    branch hotfix
    checkout hotfix
    commit id: "Critical Fix"
    checkout main
    merge hotfix id: "v1.1.2"
    checkout develop
    merge main id: "Sync"
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**
>
> **Q1. Define the term "Publishing" in the context of RPA. List any four key components of a published RPA package.** [3 Marks] *(CO4, Bloom Level: Remember/Understand)*

**Model Answer:**

Publishing in RPA is the process of packaging a fully developed and tested automation workflow into a deployable artifact and uploading it to a centralized orchestration platform (e.g., UiPath Orchestrator) for distribution to target robots.

**Four key components of an RPA package:**

1. **Compiled workflow files** (`.dll` or `.xaml`).
2. **Dependency libraries** (referenced packages and activities).
3. **Metadata file** (version, author, entry point).
4. **Digital signature** (for integrity and authenticity verification).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**
>
> **Q2. Differentiate between Attended and Unattended Robots from a publishing and management perspective.** [3 Marks] *(CO4, Bloom Level: Understand)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Attended Robot | Unattended Robot |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | User-initiated | Schedule / Queue / Event |
| Environment | User workstation | Dedicated VM / Server |
| Publishing Mode | Per-user or per-machine | Centralized via Orchestrator |
| Monitoring | Limited (user-side logs) | Full (centralized dashboard) |
| Use Case | Front-office assistance | Back-office batch processing |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Instruction (KTU Pattern):** *Answer ANY ONE of the following.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**
>
> **Q(A). (a)** Explain the complete lifecycle of publishing an RPA bot from development to production. Include a block diagram description. [7 Marks] *(CO4, Bloom Level: Apply/Analyze)*
>
> **Q(A). (b)** Discuss four update management strategies (Big-Bang, Blue-Green, Canary, Rolling). Compare their risk vs. cost trade-offs. [7 Marks] *(CO5, Bloom Level: Evaluate)*

#### **Model Solution — Q(A)(a)**

The publishing lifecycle consists of **six sequential phases**:

1. **Development Phase**: The developer builds the workflow in Studio, following PDD (Process Design Document) and SDD (Solution Design Document).

2. **Testing Phase**: Unit tests (individual activities), integration tests (end-to-end), and UAT (business validation) are executed in a Test environment.

3. **Packaging Phase**: The workflow is compiled into a signed, versioned package (e.g., `.nupkg`). The package includes dependencies, metadata, and a digital signature.

4. **Publishing Phase**: The package is uploaded to the Orchestrator via the UI, API, or CLI. A **Process** entity is created and mapped to a specific Folder and Tenant.

5. **Distribution Phase**: The process is assigned to one or more Robots within a target Environment. Triggers (Schedule, Queue, Event) are configured to initiate execution.

6. **Monitoring & Maintenance Phase**: Job statuses, queue throughput, and exception rates are continuously monitored. Logs are centralized for audit and compliance.

**Block Diagram (Refer to Section 4.1):** The flow moves from `Developer Studio → Static Analysis → Unit Test → Peer Review → Package Build → Sign → Repository → Test → Staging → UAT → CAB → Production → Monitor`.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q(A)(b)**

**Update Management Strategies:**

| Strategy | Mechanism | Risk | Cost | Rollback Speed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Big-Bang** | Replace old version atomically | **High** | Low | Slow (requires re-deploy) |
| **Blue-Green** | Two parallel environments; switch via load balancer | **Low** | High (2× infra) | Instant (switch back) |
| **Canary** | Gradual rollout to 5% → 50% → 100% | **Very Low** | Medium | Per-stage rollback |
| **Rolling** | Update instances in sequential batches | **Medium** | Low | Per-batch rollback |

**Trade-off Summary:**

- **Big-Bang** suits small, low-risk bots with limited infrastructure budgets.
- **Blue-Green** is preferred for mission-critical automations (e.g., payment processing).
- **Canary** is ideal when real-world validation is needed before full rollout.
- **Rolling** offers a balanced approach for medium-criticality bots.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often **omit the rollback mechanism** in their Big-Bang explanation. Always mention that Big-Bang has **no instant rollback**; a full re-deployment is required. Marks are deducted for this omission.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**
>
> **Q(B). (a)** Define Semantic Versioning. A bot is at version `4.2.9`. The team (i) removes a deprecated input argument, (ii) fixes a typo in a log message, (iii) changes the database schema. Determine the new version with justification. [7 Marks] *(CO5, Bloom Level: Apply)*
>
> **Q(B). (b)** Calculate the Deployment Frequency, Change Failure Rate, and MTTR for the following RPA team data, and classify the team's DevOps maturity:
> - Releases in 15 days: **12**
> - Total changes: **40**, Failed changes: **7**
> - Outage times: (08:00→08:30), (12:00→12:25), (18:00→19:10)
>
> Comment on the results. [7 Marks] *(CO5, Bloom Level: Evaluate)*

#### **Model Solution — Q(B)(a)**

**Semantic Versioning (SemVer)** is a versioning scheme using the format `MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH`:
- $MAJOR$ increments for **incompatible** changes.
- $MINOR$ increments for **backward-compatible features**.
- $PATCH$ increments for **backward-compatible bug fixes**.

**Change Analysis:**

| # | Change | Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | Remove deprecated argument | $MINOR$ | Adding/removing optional args is backward-compatible |
| (ii) | Fix log message typo | $PATCH$ | Internal-only, no behavioral change |
| (iii) | Change database schema | $MAJOR$ | **Breaking change** — affects external interface/data contract |

**Precedence Rule:** $MAJOR$ resets $MINOR$ and $PATCH$ to 0.

**Computation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{old} &= 4.2.9 \\
V_{new} &= (4 + 1).0.0 \\
V_{new} &= 5.0.0
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{V_{new} = 5.0.0}$$

---

#### **Model Solution — Q(B)(b)**

**Step 1: Deployment Frequency**

$$F_{deploy} = \frac{12}{15} = 0.8 \text{ deploys/day} \approx 24 \text{ deploys/month}$$

Classification: **Medium** maturity (per DORA).

**Step 2: Change Failure Rate**

$$R_{cf} = \frac{7}{40} \times 100\% = 17.5\%$$

Exceeds the elite benchmark of $\leq 15\%$. **Needs improvement.**

**Step 3: MTTR**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta t_1 &= 08\!:\!30 - 08\!:\!00 = 30 \text{ min} \\
\Delta t_2 &= 12\!:\!25 - 12\!:\!00 = 25 \text{ min} \\
\Delta t_3 &= 19\!:\!10 - 18\!:\!00 = 70 \text{ min} \\
\text{MTTR} &= \frac{30 + 25 + 70}{3} = \frac{125}{3} \approx 41.67 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}$$

Within the 60-min SLA target. **Acceptable.**

**Comments:**

- Deployment frequency is healthy (Medium maturity).
- Change Failure Rate is slightly high; recommend expanding automated regression coverage.
- MTTR is well within SLA, indicating strong incident response.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** When calculating MTTR, students frequently **forget to convert hours to minutes** when the outage spans across time zones or crosses midnight. Always normalize all durations to a single unit (minutes) **before** summation. Marks lost: typically 1–2 per occurrence.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4: Publishing and Managing Updates**

- **Publishing** = packaging + uploading to Orchestrator; **not** the same as running a bot locally.
- **Package formats** vary by tool: `.nupkg` (UiPath), `.bpk` (Blue Prism), `.atmx` (Automation Anywhere).
- **Semantic Versioning** is mandatory in professional RPA: always reset lower fields when incrementing higher ones.
- **Orchestrator hierarchy**: `Tenant → Folder → Machine → Robot → Process → Job`.
- **Triggers** can be: Time-based (Schedule), Event-based, Queue-based, or Manual.
- **Environments** must be segregated: **Dev → Test → Staging → Production**.
- **Rollback strategy** must be pre-defined for every production deployment.
- **Blue-Green** and **Canary** are the preferred strategies for high-criticality bots.
- **DORA Metrics** (Deployment Frequency, Lead Time, CFR, MTTR) are the **standard KPIs** for RPA DevOps maturity.
- **CI/CD in RPA** typically uses: Git (code), Jenkins/Azure DevOps (build), Orchestrator (deploy).
- **Change Advisory Board (CAB)** approval is required for production promotion in regulated industries.
- **Audit trails** in Orchestrator are legally binding in BFSI (Banking, Financial Services, Insurance) sectors.
- **Digital signatures** on packages prevent tampering and unauthorized code injection.
- **Asset management** (centralized config) decouples code from environment-specific values.
- **Queue-based processing** enables horizontal scalability and load distribution.
- **KTU frequently tests**: (1) SemVer math, (2) DORA metrics calculation, (3) Strategy comparison tables, (4) Lifecycle sequence ordering.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 4 — Publishing and Managing Updates Notes**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) | PEAIT415 — Robotic Process Automation*
