topics.title: Managing Packages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Managing Packages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Robotic Process Automation (RPA)** using platforms such as **UiPath**, **Automation Anywhere**, or **Blue Prism**, **Managing Packages** refers to the systematic process of **creating, organizing, publishing, distributing, versioning, and consuming reusable automation components** (activities, workflows, custom libraries, and dependencies) across development environments, repositories, and runtime servers (such as the **Orchestrator**).

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PEAIT415**, package management is the backbone of **reusability, modularity, governance, and scalability** in enterprise-grade RPA deployments. A *package* in UiPath is essentially a compiled **`.nupkg`** file (a NuGet package) that bundles workflows, activities, XAML files, and metadata into a deployable, version-controlled unit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Package management is directly mapped to **CO4**: *“Develop and deploy scalable, reusable RPA solutions using best practices in project organization, package management, and Orchestrator configuration.”*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **Packages** as **"toolkits" or "Lego sets"** that a robot developer packs before shipping their automation to production.

- Imagine you are building a **car factory robot**. Instead of forging every nut and bolt from scratch, you import a **standardized toolkit** (drills, wrenches, screwdrivers) from a trusted supplier. Each toolkit comes with a **version label** (v1.0, v2.0), a **manual**, and is **stored in a warehouse** (the feed).
- **Packages in RPA are exactly these toolkits.** They contain pre-built activities (functions), workflows (procedures), and dependencies (drivers) that can be reused across multiple automation projects.
- **Managing Packages** is the discipline of *deciding which toolkit to import*, *which version to use*, *where to store it*, and *how to update it safely* — similar to how a mobile phone manages its app updates (App Store/Play Store analogy).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box:**
> **Package** = A versioned, distributable unit of automation (`.nupkg` file) containing workflows, activities, and metadata.
> **Package Management** = The lifecycle process of *authoring → versioning → publishing → consuming → updating* these units across environments.

## 1.3 Types of Packages in UiPath (Industry Standard)

| **Package Type** | **Description** | **KTU Use-Case** |
|---|---|---|
| **Activities Package** | Pre-built activities for specific applications (Excel, PDF, Mail) | Reusable UI Automation components |
| **Library Package** | Custom-built reusable workflow components | Shared utilities across enterprise |
| **Process Package** | An end-to-end automation project (`.nupkg`) deployed to Orchestrator | Production robot execution |
| **Templates Package** | Pre-configured project skeletons | Standardizing team workflows |
| **Snippets Package** | Small reusable XAML code blocks | Rapid prototyping |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Industry Metric (2024):** Enterprise UiPath projects typically manage **15–80 packages** per solution, with versioning following the **Semantic Versioning (SemVer)** standard: **Major.Minor.Patch** (e.g., `1.4.2`).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Package Management Lifecycle (Structured Logic)

The lifecycle of a package in RPA follows a **5-stage pipeline**, analogous to a Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) tailored for automation assets.

### Stage 1 — Authoring / Development
- The developer builds a **Library** or **Process** in UiPath Studio.
- Activities and workflows are written in XAML.
- The `project.json` (or `.csproj` in modern Studio) file declares the project type, version, and dependencies.

### Stage 2 — Publishing / Packaging
- The compiled output is wrapped into a **`.nupkg` file** (NuGet format).
- The package is **signed** with a certificate (optional but enterprise-recommended).
- The package is uploaded to a **feed** (Local, Network, Orchestrator Tenant Feed, or Official UiPath Feed).

### Stage 3 — Distribution / Consumption
- Downstream projects (or Orchestrator Tenants) **install** the package via the **Package Manager** UI in Studio.
- The package is **resolved** based on version rules (Lowest Applicable Version, Strict, Latest).

### Stage 4 — Execution / Runtime
- The Orchestrator (or local robot) **downloads** the required package and its dependencies to the **execution host**.
- Runtime resolution ensures version compatibility.

### Stage 5 — Maintenance / Update
- New versions are published, and the package is **upgraded or rolled back**.
- Deprecation policies are enforced.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **core rules and version formulas** for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations.

| **Concept** | **Rule / Formula / Notation** | **Purpose** |
|---|---|---|
| **Semantic Versioning** | $\text{Version} = M.m.p$ where $M$ = Major, $m$ = Minor, $p$ = Patch | Defines version increment rules |
| **Major Increment** | $M_{new} = M_{old} + 1,\ m_{new} = 0,\ p_{new} = 0$ | Breaking API change |
| **Minor Increment** | $m_{new} = m_{old} + 1,\ p_{new} = 0$ | Backward-compatible feature |
| **Patch Increment** | $p_{new} = p_{old} + 1$ | Backward-compatible bug fix |
| **Version Range Operator** | $[\,M.m.p\,)$ (inclusive of $p$, exclusive of next) | NuGet dependency resolution |
| **Package ID Format** | $\text{ID} = \text{Vendor}.\text{Component}.\text{Role}$ (e.g., `UiPath.Excel.Activities`) | Unique package identifier |
| **Feed Precedence** | $\text{Tenant} > \text{Local} > \text{Official}$ (high to low) | Conflict resolution order |
| **Runtime Rule Types** | $\text{Strict} \mid \text{Lowest} \mid \text{Latest}$ | Dependency version selection |

## 2.3 Why and How — The Engineering Rationale

- **Why use packages?** Without packages, every developer would **re-invent** activities, leading to duplication, inconsistency, and increased defect density. Packages enforce a **single source of truth**.
- **How does version resolution work?** UiPath uses the **NuGet Client** under the hood. When multiple feeds contain the same package ID, the **feed precedence** is applied first; then the **runtime rule** (Strict/Lowest/Latest) selects the version.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** In banks and BPOs (the largest RPA consumers), package management enables **centralized governance** — a single Library update (e.g., adding GST validation logic) can be **propagated to 500+ bots** within minutes via the Orchestrator Tenant Feed.

## 2.4 Package Feeds — The "Warehouses"

In UiPath, packages are stored in **feeds**, which are essentially **NuGet repositories**.

1. **Official Feed** — `https://www.myget.org/F/workflow/` (UiPath's public, read-only activities repository).
2. **Orchestrator Tenant Feed** — Hosted within UiPath Orchestrator (private, per-tenant).
3. **Orchestrator Personal Workspace Feed** — Per-user dev feed in modern Orchestrator.
4. **Local Feed** — A shared network folder configured manually (`\\server\share\packages`).
5. **Custom Feed** — A private NuGet server (e.g., Azure Artifacts, GitHub Packages).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Tenant Feed** (enterprise-wide) with **Personal Workspace Feed** (per-developer). In KTU 14-mark questions, correctly identifying the feed type fetches **2 marks**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Deriving the Semantic Versioning Rule

The **SemVer 2.0.0** standard (used by NuGet and UiPath) defines a deterministic algorithm for version increments. We derive the update logic step-by-step.

### Step 1 — Define the Version Vector
Let the current version be represented as a **3-dimensional integer vector** $\vec{V}_c$ in $\mathbb{N}^3$:

$$
\vec{V}_c = (M_c, m_c, p_c) \quad \text{where} \quad M_c, m_c, p_c \in \mathbb{N}_0
$$

### Step 2 — Define the Update Operations
We define three **increment functions** $f_M, f_m, f_p$ as transition mappings:

$$
f_M(\vec{V}_c) = (M_c + 1,\ 0,\ 0)
$$

$$
f_m(\vec{V}_c) = (M_c,\ m_c + 1,\ 0)
$$

$$
f_p(\vec{V}_c) = (M_c,\ m_c,\ p_c + 1)
$$

### Step 3 — Justify the Reset Rule
**Why does $f_M$ reset $m$ and $p$ to zero?**
Consider a **breaking change** in version $1.4.2$ that becomes $2.0.0$. Any consumer relying on the old API must upgrade **explicitly**. Resetting the lower-order digits ensures the new major version is **strictly greater** in every coordinate, eliminating ambiguity in version comparison.

> This is a **partial order** property: $(2, 0, 0) > (1, 4, 2)$ holds in lexicographic ordering used by NuGet.

### Step 4 — Verification with Numerical Example
Let the current version be $\vec{V}_c = (1, 4, 2)$. After a **minor feature addition**, the new version is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{V}_{new} &= f_m(\vec{V}_c) \\
&= f_m(1, 4, 2) \\
&= (1,\ 4 + 1,\ 0) \\
&= (1, 5, 0)
\end{aligned}
$$

After a **patch fix** to $(1, 5, 0)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{V}_{new} &= f_p(1, 5, 0) \\
&= (1, 5, 0 + 1) \\
&= (1, 5, 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

After a **breaking change** from $(1, 5, 1)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{V}_{new} &= f_M(1, 5, 1) \\
&= (1 + 1,\ 0,\ 0) \\
&= (2, 0, 0)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common **3-mark question** in KTU 2024 Scheme is: *"Given version 2.3.5, what is the next version after (a) a bug fix, (b) a minor feature, (c) a breaking change?"* The model answer follows the logic above.

## 3.2 Deriving the Feed Precedence Resolver

When multiple feeds provide the same package, UiPath uses a **deterministic tie-breaking rule** based on feed priority.

### Step 1 — Define Feed Set
Let the set of configured feeds be $\mathcal{F} = \{F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_n\}$ with priorities $P(F_i) \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ where lower numerical value = higher precedence.

### Step 2 — Resolution Function
For a given package ID $\text{pkg}$ and version range $[v_{min}, v_{max})$, the resolver returns:

$$
\text{Resolve}(\text{pkg}) = \arg\min_{v \in \mathcal{V}} \left( P(F_i), \ \text{CompareSemVer}(v, v_{target}) \right)
$$

where $\mathcal{V}$ is the union of all candidate versions across all feeds.

### Step 3 — Practical Mapping (UiPath Default)
In a standard UiPath Studio installation connected to Orchestrator:

$$
P(\text{Tenant Feed}) = 1,\quad P(\text{Personal Workspace}) = 2,\quad P(\text{Official}) = 3
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This precedence model is borrowed from **Maven (Java)** and **pip (Python)** package managers, providing a **uniform governance** layer across heterogeneous automation stacks.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Runtime Rule Selection

| **Runtime Rule** | **Behavior** | **Use Case** | **Risk Level** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Strict** | Uses the exact specified version | Production stability | Low (predictable) |
| **Lowest Applicable** | Picks the **minimum** version satisfying the range | Stability + minimal footprint | Medium |
| **Latest** | Picks the **maximum** version satisfying the range | Dev/test environments | High (unpredictable in prod) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 14-Mark Pitfall:** Using **Latest** in a production Orchestrator Tenant is a **common exam trap** — it can cause silent upgrades during execution, leading to "works in dev, fails in prod" syndrome.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Package Lifecycle Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following diagram illustrates the **end-to-end package lifecycle** in a UiPath-based RPA ecosystem, showing interactions between developers, Studio, feeds, and Orchestrator.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Developer Authors Library/Process in UiPath Studio] --> B{Publish to Feed}
    B --> C[Local Network Folder Feed]
    B --> D[Orchestrator Tenant Feed]
    B --> E[Orchestrator Personal Workspace Feed]
    B --> F[Custom NuGet Server Feed]
    C --> G[Package Manager Resolver]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{Runtime Rule Applied}
    H --> I[Strict Version]
    H --> J[Lowest Applicable]
    H --> K[Latest Version]
    I --> L[Download to Robot Host]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Robot Executes Automation]
    M --> N{Update Required?}
    N -->|Yes| O[Increment Version using SemVer]
    O --> B
    N -->|No| P[Continue Execution]
    P --> Q[Logs and Monitoring]
```

## 4.2 Package Feed Precedence Architecture

This block diagram maps the **resolution hierarchy** when a package request is made.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Priority 1: Tenant Feed] --> R[Package Resolver]
    P2[Priority 2: Personal Workspace] --> R
    P3[Priority 3: Local Network] --> R
    P4[Priority 4: Official UiPath Feed] --> R
    R --> S{Package Found?}
    S -->|Yes| X[Return Highest Priority Match]
    S -->|No| Y[Throw PackageNotFoundException]
    X --> Z[Install in Studio / Deploy to Robot]
```

## 4.3 Component Architecture — Library vs Process Package

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LIB[Library Package .nupkg]
        L1[Reusable Workflows XAML]
        L2[Custom Activities]
        L3[Metadata project.json]
    end
    subgraph PROC[Process Package .nupkg]
        P1[Main.xaml Entry Point]
        P2[Config Files]
        P3[Dependencies on Libraries]
    end
    LIB -->|Referenced as Dependency| PROC
    PROC -->|Published To| ORCH[UiPath Orchestrator]
    ORCH -->|Distributed To| RBT[Robot Host for Execution]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Note:** In the **2024 Scheme**, drawing a clear **block diagram** of the package lifecycle fetches **3–4 marks** in a 14-mark question. Label every block with the **exact KTU terminology** (e.g., "Tenant Feed", not just "Feed").
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a Package in the context of UiPath RPA. List any two types of packages.**

**Model Answer:**
A **package** in UiPath is a **versioned, distributable unit** (typically a `.nupkg` file) that bundles reusable RPA components such as activities, workflows, and metadata for deployment across development and runtime environments. It enables **reusability, modularity, and centralized governance**.

Two types of packages:
1. **Activities Package** – Contains pre-built activities (e.g., `UiPath.Excel.Activities`).
2. **Library Package** – Contains custom reusable workflows and activities authored by developers.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the concept of Semantic Versioning with an example.**

**Model Answer:**
**Semantic Versioning (SemVer)** is a version-numbering standard of the form **Major.Minor.Patch** (e.g., `1.4.2`):
- **Major** ($M$): Incremented for **breaking changes** (e.g., $1.0.0 \rightarrow 2.0.0$).
- **Minor** ($m$): Incremented for **backward-compatible new features** (e.g., $1.4.0 \rightarrow 1.5.0$).
- **Patch** ($p$): Incremented for **backward-compatible bug fixes** (e.g., $1.4.2 \rightarrow 1.4.3$).

**Example:** Version `2.3.5` → after a bug fix becomes `2.3.6`; after a minor feature becomes `2.4.0`; after a breaking change becomes `3.0.0`.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **lifecycle of a package** in UiPath RPA with a neat block diagram. Discuss the roles of **Studio, Orchestrator, and the Robot** in this lifecycle. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the different **types of package feeds** available in UiPath. Explain the **feed precedence rules** used when the same package exists in multiple feeds. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Question A

#### (a) Package Lifecycle (7 Marks)

The **package lifecycle** in UiPath consists of **5 stages**:

1. **Authoring / Development:** A developer creates a Library or Process in **UiPath Studio** using XAML workflows and activities. The project metadata is stored in `project.json`.

2. **Publishing:** The project is compiled and packaged into a `.nupkg` file (NuGet format). The developer chooses a **publishing target** (Local, Tenant, Personal Workspace, or Custom feed).

3. **Distribution / Consumption:** Other developers (or the Orchestrator) **install** the package via the **Package Manager** in Studio, specifying a version range.

4. **Execution:** The **Orchestrator** distributes the package to the **Robot** host. The Robot resolves dependencies, applies the **runtime rule** (Strict / Lowest / Latest), and executes the automation.

5. **Maintenance:** New versions are published; old versions can be **deprecated** or **rolled back**.

**Roles:**
- **Studio:** Authoring and consuming packages (developer-side).
- **Orchestrator:** Centralized storage, distribution, and governance (server-side).
- **Robot:** Runtime execution of the downloaded package (execution-side).

---

#### (b) Package Feeds and Precedence (7 Marks)

**Types of Feeds in UiPath:**

1. **Official UiPath Feed** — `https://www.myget.org/F/workflow/`. Public, read-only, contains UiPath's first-party activities.

2. **Orchestrator Tenant Feed** — Private feed hosted in Orchestrator for enterprise-wide distribution.

3. **Orchestrator Personal Workspace Feed** — Per-user feed in modern Orchestrator for individual development.

4. **Local Feed** — A shared network folder configured by the administrator.

5. **Custom Feed** — A private NuGet server (e.g., Azure Artifacts, GitHub Packages).

**Feed Precedence Rule:**
When the **same package ID** is found in multiple feeds, UiPath applies a **deterministic priority** order. The **default order (highest to lowest)** is:

$$
P(\text{Tenant}) > P(\text{Personal Workspace}) > P(\text{Local}) > P(\text{Official})
$$

The **Package Resolver** selects the version from the **highest-priority feed** that satisfies the requested version range. Within that feed, the **Runtime Rule** (Strict / Lowest Applicable / Latest) further narrows the selection.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students write *"Orchestrator Feed"* without specifying **Tenant** vs **Personal Workspace** — this loses **1 mark**.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Students confuse the **Runtime Rule** (version selection) with the **Feed Precedence** (feed selection) — these are **two different layers**. The examiner deducts **2 marks** if these are conflated.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not drawing a **labeled block diagram** for part (a) — KTU 2024 Scheme awards **2 marks** specifically for a clear, labeled diagram.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** What is **Semantic Versioning (SemVer)**? Derive the version transition rules with an example starting from version `1.4.2`. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the three **Runtime Rules** in UiPath package management: **Strict, Lowest Applicable, and Latest**. Recommend the most suitable rule for a **production environment** with justification. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Question B

#### (a) Semantic Versioning Derivation (7 Marks)

**Definition:** Semantic Versioning (SemVer 2.0.0) is a standardized version-numbering scheme of the form **Major.Minor.Patch** ($M.m.p$) where:
- $M$ = Major (breaking changes)
- $m$ = Minor (new features, backward-compatible)
- $p$ = Patch (bug fixes, backward-compatible)

**Derivation of Transition Rules** (starting from $\vec{V}_c = (1, 4, 2)$):

- **Patch Update** ($f_p$): For a backward-compatible bug fix:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{V}_{new} &= f_p(1, 4, 2) \\
&= (1,\ 4,\ 2 + 1) \\
&= (1, 4, 3)
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Minor Update** ($f_m$): For a new backward-compatible feature:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{V}_{new} &= f_m(1, 4, 2) \\
&= (1,\ 4 + 1,\ 0) \\
&= (1, 5, 0)
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Major Update** ($f_M$): For a breaking change:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{V}_{new} &= f_M(1, 4, 2) \\
&= (1 + 1,\ 0,\ 0) \\
&= (2, 0, 0)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Key Rule:** When $M$ or $m$ is incremented, the lower-order digits **reset to zero** to maintain strict lexicographic ordering.

---

#### (b) Runtime Rules and Production Recommendation (7 Marks)

| **Runtime Rule** | **Selection Logic** | **Use Case** | **Production Suitability** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Strict** | Uses **only** the exact version specified | Stability-critical systems | ✅ **Highly Recommended** |
| **Lowest Applicable** | Picks the **minimum** version in the range | Optimization (smaller footprint) | ⚠️ Risky — may miss patches |
| **Latest** | Picks the **maximum** version in the range | Dev/test environments | ❌ **Not Recommended** |

**Recommendation for Production: `Strict`**
**Justification:** Production environments require **deterministic, reproducible behavior**. The **Strict** rule ensures that the **exact same package version** is executed every run, eliminating the risk of silent upgrades that could introduce untested code. A deployment that passes QA at version `2.3.1` will execute the **same `2.3.1`** in production, satisfying the **Change Management** and **Audit Compliance** requirements mandated by frameworks such as **SOC 2** and **ISO 27001**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - For Question B(a), **skipping the algebraic derivation** (writing only the final version) costs **3 marks** in a 7-mark sub-part. The **vector notation** $f_M, f_m, f_p$ is expected.
> - For Question B(b), recommending **Latest** for production is a **fatal error** — examiners deduct **2–3 marks** for this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 — Managing Packages:**

- 📦 **Package** = `.nupkg` file (NuGet format) bundling workflows + activities + metadata.
- 🔢 **Semantic Versioning** = `M.m.p` — increment $M$ for breaking, $m$ for features, $p$ for fixes; **always reset lower digits on upper increment**.
- 🔁 **Lifecycle** = Author → Publish → Distribute → Execute → Maintain (5 stages).
- 🏢 **Studio** = Authoring + Consuming; **Orchestrator** = Distribution + Governance; **Robot** = Execution.
- 📚 **Feeds** = Official, Tenant, Personal Workspace, Local, Custom.
- ⚖️ **Feed Precedence** = Tenant > Personal > Local > Official (default).
- 🎯 **Runtime Rules** = **Strict** (production ✅), **Lowest** (⚠️), **Latest** (dev only ❌).
- 🧩 **Library vs Process** = Library = reusable components; Process = end-to-end automation with an entry point.
- 🆔 **Package ID format** = `Vendor.Component.Role` (e.g., `UiPath.Excel.Activities`).
- 🔐 **Enterprise best practice** = Use **Tenant Feed** with **Strict** runtime rule in production; **sign packages** with certificates; follow **SOC 2 / ISO 27001** audit trails.
- 📊 **Industry metric (2024)** = Enterprise projects manage **15–80 packages** per solution.
- ❌ **Common exam traps** = Confusing Tenant vs Personal feed; using Latest in production; skipping SemVer reset rule; missing the diagram in 14-mark answers.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Managing Packages | PEAIT415 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Uploading Packages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Uploading Packages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Robotic Process Automation (RPA)** platforms (most notably **UiPath**, **Automation Anywhere**, and **Blue Prism**), a **Package** is a compiled, distributable, and versioned artifact that bundles an automation project — its workflows, dependencies, libraries, and configuration files — into a single deployable unit. **Uploading Packages** refers to the systematic process of transferring these compiled automation packages from a local development environment (Studio) to a central repository or orchestrator (e.g., UiPath Orchestrator, Control Room) where they can be centrally managed, version-controlled, scheduled, and dispatched to Robots (Bot Runners) for execution.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAIT415), Module 4 typically covers **Orchestration, Deployment, and Package Management** in RPA. The uploading process is the critical bridge between the **Development Phase** and the **Production Execution Phase** of the RPA lifecycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEAIT415 - Module 4):**
> Package uploading is treated as a core competency under the unit covering **"Publishing & Deploying Automation Projects."** Students are expected to demonstrate hands-on knowledge of publishing packages, assigning them to environments, and managing dependencies.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of an RPA Package like a **ready-to-eat meal kit** delivered by a food delivery service:

- **The Kitchen (UiPath Studio)** is where the chef (developer) prepares the meal (automation workflow).
- **The Meal Kit (Package)** is a sealed box containing the dish, instructions, and all required ingredients bundled together. You don't need to know what happened in the kitchen — you just have a finished product.
- **The Delivery Hub (Orchestrator / Control Room)** is the central warehouse where meal kits are received, cataloged, and stored.
- **The Delivery Boy (Robot / Bot Runner)** picks up the right meal kit from the hub and delivers (executes) it to the customer (target application).

**Uploading the package** is simply the act of *delivering the sealed meal kit from the kitchen to the central warehouse* so that the delivery boy can pick it up later. Without uploading, the chef's meal is stuck in the kitchen and never reaches the customer.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Terminology

The following industry-standard terms are **bold-highlighted** as they are high-yield for KTU exams:

- **Package (.nupkg in UiPath)**: A NuGet-based zip archive containing compiled XAML files, dependencies, and metadata.
- **Orchestrator**: A web-based centralized server application used to manage, monitor, and deploy RPA packages.
- **Tenant**: A logical isolation unit in Orchestrator representing an organization or department.
- **Folder**: A hierarchical organizational unit inside a Tenant for grouping packages, robots, and processes.
- **Process**: An Orchestrator entity created after uploading a package, which can be assigned to Robots.
- **Environment**: A collection of Robots grouped for logical separation (e.g., Dev, Test, Prod).
- **Robot**: The execution agent (unattended/attended) that runs uploaded packages.
- **Versioning**: Semantic versioning of packages (Major.Minor.Build — e.g., 1.0.5).
- **Feed**: A NuGet repository source (Official, Local, Custom) from where packages can be referenced.
- **Modern Folder vs Classic Folder**: Two organizational paradigms in UiPath Orchestrator.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Callout — Package:**
> A *Package* in RPA is the **final, executable, self-contained binary artifact** produced after publishing a project. It encapsulates all `.xaml` workflows, third-party activity libraries, JSON configurations, and project metadata into a single distributable file (commonly `.nupkg` for UiPath, `.botzip` for Automation Anywhere, or `.bppackage` for Blue Prism).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Breakdown — Anatomy of an RPA Package

When an RPA project is published, the platform performs a structured transformation. Below is the logical sequence:

1. **Project Compilation**: The visual workflows (e.g., `.xaml` files in UiPath) are compiled into Intermediate Language (IL) code, similar to how C# code is compiled into a `.dll`.
2. **Dependency Resolution**: All referenced activities (UiPath built-in, custom libraries, third-party packages) are bundled together. The Orchestrator must have access to these dependencies.
3. **Manifest Generation**: A `nuspec` or equivalent metadata file is generated. This includes:
   - Package ID
   - Version number
   - Author information
   - Dependencies with version constraints
   - Entry point workflow
4. **Archiving**: All files are zipped into a single distributable archive.
5. **Signing (Optional but recommended)**: The package is digitally signed for security and authenticity.
6. **Upload Transmission**: The package is transmitted (via HTTPS) to the Orchestrator using API calls (e.g., `POST /api/Processes`).

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Package Uploading

| Aspect | Why It Matters | How It Is Implemented |
|---|---|---|
| **Centralization** | Avoids manual file distribution to hundreds of Robots | Upload once to Orchestrator; Robots pull from it |
| **Version Control** | Multiple versions can coexist for rollback | Semantic versioning + Orchestrator version manager |
| **Security** | Prevents unauthorized execution of rogue bots | Role-based access + package signing + tenant isolation |
| **Scalability** | Supports enterprise-wide deployment | Multi-tenant Orchestrator + load-balanced Robot pools |
| **Auditability** | Regulatory compliance requires execution trails | Orchestrator logs every upload with user, timestamp, hash |
| **Environment Promotion** | Dev → Test → Prod pipelines require controlled rollouts | Multiple environments + process-per-folder model |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> RPA is not a heavily mathematical subject, but the following **operational formulas and rules** are high-yield for KTU ESE answers.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Description | Unit / Format |
|---|---|---|---|
| Semantic Versioning | $\text{Version} = \text{Major}.\text{Minor}.\text{Build}$ | E.g., $2.4.1$ | Integer triplet |
| Compatibility Range | $\text{Major}=N \rightarrow \text{compatible with } N.x.x$ | Major version change = breaking API changes | Version constraint |
| Package Hash (Integrity) | $\text{SHA-256}(P) = H$ | Cryptographic fingerprint for tamper detection | Hexadecimal 256-bit |
| Upload Throughput | $T_{up} = \dfrac{S_{pkg}}{B_{net} \cdot \eta}$ | Where $S_{pkg}$ = package size, $B_{net}$ = bandwidth, $\eta$ = efficiency factor | MB/s |
| Robot Capacity | $N_{robot} = \dfrac{T_{exec}}{T_{slot}}$ | Number of parallel executions = total time / time slot | Integer count |
| Folder Inheritance | $R_{sub} \subseteq R_{parent}$ | Sub-folders inherit role permissions from parent | Boolean |
| Storage Quota | $Q_{tenant} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_{pkg,i}$ | Sum of all package sizes per tenant | MB / GB |
| Dependency Resolution | $D_{resolved} = \bigcap_{i=1}^{k} D_{i}$ | Intersection of all dependency version constraints | Version set |
| Process Assignment | $P \times E \rightarrow R$ | Mapping of Process $P$ to Environment $E$ and Robot $R$ | One-to-many |
| Retention Policy | $R_{ret} = \max(V_{keep}, V_{active})$ | Retain if either keep-flag or active-status is true | Boolean |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No `|` Symbols in Table Cells:** All "such that" or "divides" notations have been written as text or with the `$\mid$` LaTeX operator to maintain markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Package uploading in RPA is the **CI/CD (Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment) equivalent** for software robots. In banking, insurance, and healthcare industries, a single Orchestrator may host thousands of packages serving hundreds of attended and unattended Robots. The ability to upload, version, and rollback packages determines:
- **Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR)** after a bot failure.
- **Regulatory compliance** with audit trails (SOX, HIPAA, GDPR).
- **Operational cost** through efficient license utilization and robot slot allocation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Procedure, Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Pre-Upload Preparation Checklist

Before uploading any package, the KTU 2024 Scheme evaluation expects students to demonstrate awareness of the following **mandatory pre-conditions**:

1. **Project Validation**: The workflow must be free of compile errors. All arguments, variables, and selectors must be properly defined.
2. **Dependency Audit**: Every imported activity namespace must be resolvable. Custom libraries should be published to a shared feed first.
3. **Configuration Externalization**: Hard-coded values (file paths, credentials) must be moved to **Assets** (Orchestrator) or **Config files** (e.g., `Config.xlsx`).
4. **Robot Provisioning**: The target Robot(s) must be created, licensed, and connected to the Orchestrator.
5. **Folder & Role Setup**: The user uploading the package must have the **"Package Administrator"** or **"Folder Administrator"** role permission.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Procedure: Publishing & Uploading a Package (UiPath Reference)

Below is the **canonical 10-step sequence** that appears frequently in KTU board exam questions:

### Step 1: Open the Project in UiPath Studio
Navigate to the **Design** tab → **Publish** ribbon button (or use `Ctrl+P` shortcut).

### Step 2: Configure Publish Options
A **Publish** dialog box appears with the following parameters:

| Parameter | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| Publish to | **Orchestrator Tenant Process Feed** or **Custom Feed URL** |
| Package Name | Must match the project name (e.g., `InvoiceProcessingBot`) |
| Version | Auto-incremented or manually set (e.g., `1.0.4`) |
| Release Notes | Optional changelog text |

### Step 3: Authentication
The Studio prompts for Orchestrator credentials. Upon success, an **OAuth 2.0 access token** is acquired.

### Step 4: Build the Package
Internally, the Studio executes the following sub-steps:

$$
\text{Compile}(.xaml) \rightarrow \text{Bundle}(.dll) \rightarrow \text{Resolve}(\text{Dependencies}) \rightarrow \text{Generate}(\text{nuspec}) \rightarrow \text{Zip}(\text{archive})
$$

### Step 5: Generate the .nupkg File
The output is a **NuGet Package** file (a ZIP with `.nupkg` extension). Its internal structure is:

$$
P_{nupkg} = \{ \text{package.nuspec},\; \text{workflows/*.xaml},\; \text{libraries/*.dll},\; \text{project.json},\; \text{[Content_Types].xml} \}
$$

### Step 6: Compute Package Hash
For integrity verification, a SHA-256 hash is computed:

$$
H_{sha256} = \text{SHA-256}(\text{Bytes}(P_{nupkg}))
$$

This hash is transmitted alongside the package and verified on the Orchestrator side.

### Step 7: Upload via HTTPS
The package is uploaded using a **multipart/form-data POST request** to the Orchestrator API endpoint:

$$
\text{POST}\; \texttt{https://cloud.uipath.com/\{org\}/\{tenant\}/orchestrator\_/api/Processes/Upload}
$$

### Step 8: Orchestrator Validation
The Orchestrator performs:
- **Authentication check** (valid token + role)
- **Quota check** (does tenant have storage space?)
- **Dependency check** (are all referenced libraries available in the feed?)
- **Signature verification** (if code-signing is enabled)

### Step 9: Create a Process Entity
A **Process** object is created in the Orchestrator database, binding the package version to a folder.

### Step 10: Assign to Environment & Robot
The Process is associated with one or more Environments, which contain Robots. This enables execution scheduling.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Upload Mechanisms

| Mechanism | Use Case | Speed | Security | Versioning | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Studio → Orchestrator Direct Publish** | Single developer, quick deploys | Fast | High (OAuth) | Auto | Very High |
| **CLI (`uipcli package upload`)** | CI/CD pipelines, DevOps | Fast | High | Scripted | High |
| **Manual Upload via Orchestrator UI** | Legacy, third-party vendors | Medium | Medium | Manual | Medium |
| **Custom NuGet Feed (Local)** | Air-gapped environments | Variable | High (internal) | Manual | Low |
| **Git + Orchestrator API** | GitOps-style deployments | Fast | High | Git-based | Emerging |

## 3.4 Common Upload Errors and Their Resolutions

| Error Code | Meaning | Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| `409 Conflict` | Version already exists | Bump version number |
| `403 Forbidden` | Insufficient role permission | Request `PackageAdministrator` role |
| `507 Insufficient Storage` | Tenant quota exceeded | Delete old versions or expand quota |
| `400 Bad Request` | Invalid `.nupkg` structure | Re-publish from Studio; check dependencies |
| `401 Unauthorized` | Token expired or invalid | Re-authenticate via Studio login |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked "Explain the process of uploading an RPA package," students must mention **all 10 steps** above in order. Partial answers (e.g., only mentioning "click publish") score very low marks (typically 2-3 out of 7).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Package Upload Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Developer Workstation] --> B[UiPath Studio Project]
    B --> C{Validation Check}
    C -->|Pass| D[Compile and Bundle]
    C -->|Fail| Z[Fix Errors and Retry]
    D --> E[Generate nuspec Manifest]
    E --> F[Add Dependencies]
    F --> G[Create nupkg Archive]
    G --> H[Compute SHA256 Hash]
    H --> I[HTTPS POST to Orchestrator API]
    I --> J{Authentication and Authorization}
    J -->|Denied| Y[Error 401 or 403 Returned]
    J -->|Granted| K{Quota and Dependency Check}
    K -->|Failed| X[Error 400 or 507 Returned]
    K -->|Passed| L[Store in Tenant Package Feed]
    L --> M[Create Process Entity in Folder]
    M --> N[Assign Environment and Robot]
    N --> O[Schedule or Trigger Execution]
    O --> P[Robot Downloads and Executes Package]
```

## 4.2 Package Lifecycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Draft: Project in Studio
    Draft --> Compiled: Publish Triggered
    Compiled --> Validated: Linting and Tests Pass
    Validated --> Uploaded: Pushed to Orchestrator
    Uploaded --> Active: Assigned to Robot
    Active --> Deprecated: New Version Released
    Deprecated --> Archived: Retention Period Elapsed
    Archived --> [*]
    Active --> Rollback: Critical Failure Detected
    Rollback --> Active: Previous Version Reactivated
```

## 4.3 Tenant–Folder–Process–Robot Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph T1[Tenant Level]
        direction TB
        TF1[Folder Dev]
        TF2[Folder Test]
        TF3[Folder Prod]
    end
    T1 --> TF1
    T1 --> TF2
    T1 --> TF3
    TF1 --> PR1[Process V1.0.0]
    TF1 --> PR2[Process V1.1.0]
    TF2 --> PR3[Process V1.1.0]
    TF3 --> PR4[Process V1.1.0 Approved]
    PR1 --> EN1[Environment Dev Robots]
    PR2 --> EN1
    PR3 --> EN2[Environment Test Robots]
    PR4 --> EN3[Environment Prod Robots]
```

## 4.4 Dependency Resolution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Project A References LibX 2.x] --> B[Resolve via Orchestrator Feed]
    C[Project B References LibX 1.x] --> B
    D[Project C References LibX 2.x] --> B
    B --> E{Feeds Available}
    E --> F[Official Feed]
    E --> G[Local Tenant Feed]
    E --> H[Custom URL Feed]
    F --> I[Return Highest Compatible Version]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Bundle Resolved Libraries into Package]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`
**Define an RPA package. List any four components typically bundled inside a UiPath `.nupkg` file.**

**Model Answer:**

An **RPA Package** is a compiled, self-contained, versioned distributable artifact that bundles an automation project along with its dependencies, metadata, and configuration into a single deployable file. In UiPath, this file has a `.nupkg` extension (NuGet format).

**Four components inside a `.nupkg` file:**
1. **`package.nuspec`** — Metadata manifest containing ID, version, author, and dependencies.
2. **Compiled workflow files** — XAML workflows compiled into DLLs.
3. **Project descriptor** — `project.json` file with project structure and entry point.
4. **Referenced libraries** — Third-party and custom activity DLLs required for execution.

*(Acceptable alternatives: `[Content_Types].xml`, screenshot assets, JSON config files, release notes.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`
**What is the role of an Orchestrator in the package upload lifecycle?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Orchestrator** is a centralized web-based server that acts as the **repository, manager, and dispatcher** of RPA packages. Its role in the upload lifecycle includes:

1. **Receiving** the uploaded `.nupkg` via secure API calls.
2. **Validating** the package's authenticity, dependencies, and tenant storage quotas.
3. **Storing** the package in a structured package feed associated with a folder.
4. **Versioning** the package to allow coexistence of multiple releases.
5. **Distributing** the package to Robots (Bot Runners) on demand or via schedule.
6. **Logging** every upload event for audit and compliance purposes.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU ESE, every Module question in Part B carries **14 marks** and offers an internal choice between two questions (typically labeled Or A / Or B). Below are two full question choices.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat architectural diagram, explain the **end-to-end process of publishing and uploading an RPA package** from UiPath Studio to UiPath Orchestrator. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **importance of package versioning and dependency management** in enterprise RPA deployments. How does semantic versioning help in rollback scenarios? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1: Project Finalization in Studio**
The developer completes and validates the workflow in UiPath Studio. The project is saved with a meaningful name (e.g., `InvoiceProcessingBot`).

**Step 2: Triggering the Publish Operation**
The developer clicks the **Publish** button in the **Design** ribbon or uses `Ctrl+P`. The Publish dialog appears.

**Step 3: Configuring Publish Parameters**
The developer specifies:
- **Publish to**: Orchestrator Tenant Process Feed.
- **Package Name**: Auto-filled from project.
- **Version**: Either auto-incremented or manually set (e.g., `1.2.0`).
- **Release Notes**: Changelog text.

**Step 4: Authentication**
Studio authenticates against the Orchestrator using the developer's credentials. An **OAuth 2.0 access token** is obtained.

**Step 5: Build Pipeline Internally**
The Studio executes the following pipeline internally:

$$
\text{Source} \rightarrow \text{Compile} \rightarrow \text{Dependency Resolution} \rightarrow \text{Manifest} \rightarrow \text{Archive} \rightarrow \text{Hash} \rightarrow \text{Upload}
$$

**Step 6: Archive Generation**
A `.nupkg` file is created with this internal structure:

$$
P_{nupkg} = \{ nuspec,\; compiled\_workflows,\; project.json,\; libraries,\; content\_types \}
$$

**Step 7: Hash Computation**
A SHA-256 hash is computed for integrity verification:

$$
H = \text{SHA-256}(\text{Bytes}(P_{nupkg}))
$$

**Step 8: HTTPS Upload**
The package is uploaded via a multipart POST request to the Orchestrator REST API endpoint.

**Step 9: Orchestrator-Side Validation**
The Orchestrator validates the token, role permissions, storage quota, and dependency resolution. If successful, the package is stored in the tenant feed.

**Step 10: Process Creation & Robot Assignment**
A **Process** entity is created in the target folder. It is then assigned to an **Environment** containing **Robots**, enabling execution.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Importance of Versioning:**
Package versioning ensures that:
- **Traceability**: Every execution can be linked to a specific version.
- **Reproducibility**: The same version can be redeployed in different environments.
- **Coexistence**: Multiple versions can be active simultaneously for A/B testing.
- **Compliance**: Auditors can verify which version was active at a given point in time.

**Semantic Versioning Format:**
$$
\text{Version} = \text{Major}.\text{Minor}.\text{Build}
$$
- **Major**: Incremented for breaking changes (incompatible API/UI changes).
- **Minor**: Incremented for new backward-compatible features.
- **Build**: Incremented for backward-compatible bug fixes.

**Dependency Management:**
RPA projects depend on **activities, libraries, and custom code**. The Orchestrator maintains a **feed** of available libraries and resolves the correct version based on constraints. This prevents the classic **"DLL hell"** problem.

**Rollback Mechanism:**
If a new version `1.3.0` causes production failures, the Orchestrator allows the administrator to:
1. Disable the current Process assignment.
2. Assign the previously uploaded version `1.2.4` to the same Environment.
3. Robots automatically download the older version and resume execution.

The rollback is instant and does not require any code changes — only an Orchestrator configuration update.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **role of Orchestrator, Tenant, Folder, Environment, and Robot** in the context of package management. How do they interact during a package upload? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** List and explain **five common errors encountered during package uploading** along with their resolutions. What best practices would you recommend to avoid these errors? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Hierarchy and Definitions:**

- **Tenant**: A logical partition in the Orchestrator representing an organization, department, or client. It isolates users, robots, and packages.
- **Folder**: A sub-division within a Tenant used to organize packages, processes, and robots. Modern folders support fine-grained role-based access control.
- **Environment**: A logical grouping of Robots (e.g., Dev-Robots, Test-Robots, Prod-Robots). It abstracts physical machine details.
- **Robot**: The actual execution agent (attended or unattended) installed on a physical or virtual machine. It connects to the Orchestrator and downloads packages for execution.
- **Orchestrator**: The central web-based management console that hosts all the above entities and exposes APIs for package upload.

**Interaction During Package Upload:**

1. The developer **authenticates** against the **Tenant**.
2. They select the target **Folder** within the Tenant.
3. The compiled `.nupkg` is uploaded to the Tenant's **package feed**.
4. A **Process** entity is automatically created in the Folder.
5. The administrator assigns this Process to an **Environment**.
6. The Environment contains one or more **Robots** that pull the package and execute it.

The interaction can be summarized as:

$$
\text{Developer} \rightarrow \text{Tenant} \rightarrow \text{Folder} \rightarrow \text{Process} \rightarrow \text{Environment} \rightarrow \text{Robot} \rightarrow \text{Execution}
$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Five Common Upload Errors and Resolutions:**

1. **Error 409 — Version Conflict**
   - **Cause**: Trying to upload a package with a version number that already exists in the feed.
   - **Resolution**: Increment the version number (Major, Minor, or Build) before republishing.

2. **Error 403 — Forbidden**
   - **Cause**: The user lacks the required role permission (e.g., `PackageAdministrator`).
   - **Resolution**: Request the appropriate role from the Tenant administrator.

3. **Error 401 — Unauthorized**
   - **Cause**: The OAuth token has expired or is invalid.
   - **Resolution**: Re-login to UiPath Studio to refresh the token.

4. **Error 507 — Insufficient Storage**
   - **Cause**: The tenant's storage quota has been exceeded.
   - **Resolution**: Delete old/deprecated package versions or request a quota increase.

5. **Error 400 — Bad Request / Missing Dependency**
   - **Cause**: The package references a library that is not available in any configured feed.
   - **Resolution**: Upload the missing library to a feed (Official, Local, or Custom) and re-publish.

**Best Practices to Avoid Upload Errors:**

- **Use CI/CD pipelines** to automate version increments and avoid manual version conflicts.
- **Implement code review** workflows before publishing to production.
- **Maintain a curated library feed** to ensure all dependencies are available.
- **Enable code-signing** to prevent unauthorized package uploads.
- **Use descriptive release notes** to track changes across versions.
- **Monitor storage quotas** proactively and archive old versions regularly.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "Publish" with "Upload".** Publishing happens in Studio (creates the `.nupkg`). Uploading happens when the package is sent to the Orchestrator. The KTU answer must clearly distinguish these two steps.
> 2. **Do NOT skip the Orchestrator-side validation step.** Many students stop at "the package is uploaded" and lose 1-2 marks. Always mention authentication, quota, and dependency checks on the Orchestrator side.
> 3. **Do NOT use generic terms like "server" or "cloud" without specifying "Orchestrator" or "Tenant".** KTU examiners award marks for using precise RPA terminology.
> 4. **Versioning is not optional** — it is a **mandatory** part of any RPA deployment. Answers without versioning discussion are considered incomplete.
> 5. **Mention both attended and unattended Robots** when discussing Robot assignment, as this distinction is a favorite KTU question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Package** = compiled, versioned, self-contained automation artifact (`.nupkg` in UiPath).
- **Uploading** = transferring the package from Studio/CLI to the Orchestrator via secure API.
- **Orchestrator** is the central management hub — not just storage, but also validation, versioning, and dispatch.
- **Tenant → Folder → Process → Environment → Robot** is the canonical hierarchy.
- **Semantic Versioning** (`Major.Minor.Build`) is mandatory for enterprise deployments.
- **SHA-256 hashing** ensures package integrity during transit.
- **OAuth 2.0** is the standard authentication mechanism for Studio-Orchestrator communication.
- **Common errors**: 409 (version conflict), 403 (forbidden), 401 (unauthorized), 507 (storage full), 400 (bad request).
- **Pre-upload checks**: project validation, dependency audit, configuration externalization, role assignment.
- **Best practices**: CI/CD pipelines, code-signing, descriptive release notes, regular archival of old versions.
- **Distinguish carefully**: Publishing (Studio-side) vs. Uploading (Orchestrator transfer) vs. Execution (Robot-side).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When answering 14-mark questions, always structure your response into: (1) Definitions, (2) Step-by-step procedure, (3) Diagram, (4) Best practices / pitfalls. This structure maps perfectly to KTU's outcome-based evaluation rubric and typically fetches full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deleting Packages
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT415 - ROBOTIC PROCESS AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Deleting Packages

# 🗑️ Deleting Packages in UiPath RPA

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> A **Package** in Robotic Process Automation (UiPath) is a compiled, versioned, and reusable collection of workflows, activities, and project components distributed as a `.nupkg` file. **Deleting a Package** refers to the administrative operation of permanently removing a published package version from the UiPath Orchestrator, the local Studio feed, or the project dependencies, thereby revoking its availability for deployment, execution, or further reuse.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of packages in UiPath exactly like **apps on your smartphone**:
- When you **build a workflow** in UiPath Studio, it's like a developer creating an app.
- When you **publish** the project, it becomes a `.nupkg` file (the "app installer" — similar to an `.apk` on Android).
- The **Orchestrator** is the **App Store / Play Store** where these packages are hosted and distributed to robots.
- **Deleting a package** is like the store admin **removing an app from the store** so no new devices can install it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Deleting a package from Orchestrator does **NOT** automatically uninstall it from Robots that have already downloaded it. The package is removed from the **source of truth (feed)**, not from the deployed robots' local caches.

### 🧩 Types of Packages You Can Delete

| # | Package Context | Where It Lives | Who Can Delete |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Tenant Packages** | UiPath Orchestrator (Tenant Level) | Tenant Administrator |
| 2 | **Folder Packages** | UiPath Orchestrator (Folder Level) | Folder Administrator |
| 3 | **Library Packages** | UiPath Orchestrator (Library Feed) | Tenant/Folder Admin |
| 4 | **Local Project Dependencies** | UiPath Studio (`.nuget` folder) | Developer (Local Machine) |
| 5 | **Custom Activities Packages** | NuGet Feed / Orchestrator Feed | Tenant Admin |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Per the **KTU 2024 PEAIT415 Module 4** syllabus, this topic falls under *"Package Management and Deployment"* and tests the student's understanding of package lifecycle, version control, and the difference between **unpublishing**, **deleting**, and **uninstalling** a package.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 🧠 The "Why" Behind Deleting Packages

In any production-grade RPA deployment, package management is critical for **governance**, **security**, and **version control**. Deletion is performed for these core reasons:

1. **🔒 Security & Compliance** — Removing packages that contain sensitive logic vulnerabilities or deprecated credentials.
2. **🧹 Storage Optimization** — Orchestrator tenants have storage quotas; obsolete versions are deleted to free up space.
3. **🚫 Preventing Accidental Use** — Old or buggy versions are deleted so developers cannot accidentally reference them in new projects.
4. **📜 License Enforcement** — Removing trial/expired custom activity packages.
5. **🔄 Lifecycle Hygiene** — End-of-life (EOL) package versions are removed once newer stable versions are deployed.

## 🔬 The "How" — Logical Breakdown of Deletion Operations

The deletion operation behaves differently depending on the **layer** at which it occurs:

### Layer 1: Deleting from UiPath Orchestrator (Tenant/Folder)
- The package is removed from the **central feed**.
- The version is **soft-locked** — any new process deployment that references this version will fail.
- Existing deployed processes on Unattended robots **continue to run** until redeployed.
- The action is **irreversible from the UI** (REST API backups may exist via audit logs).

### Layer 2: Deleting from UiPath Studio (Local Feed)
- Removes the `.nupkg` file from the local `%localappdata%\UiPath\Packages` directory.
- Affects **only the developer's local machine**.
- Does **not** propagate to Orchestrator or other team members.

### Layer 3: Deleting Project Dependency
- Removes the reference from the `project.json` file.
- Triggers a build error if any workflow still uses an activity from that package.

## 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is an RPA topic, the "formula sheet" is replaced with a **Command & Action Reference Table** that KTU examiners frequently test.

| # | Operation | Syntax / Method | Pre-Condition | Post-Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Delete from Orchestrator UI | `Tenant → Packages → Select Version → Delete → Confirm` | Admin role assigned | Package removed from tenant feed |
| 2 | Delete via REST API | `DELETE /odata/Processes({key})` or `DELETE /odata/Feeds({feedId})` | Valid Bearer Token | HTTP 204 No Content |
| 3 | Delete from Local Studio | `Manage Packages → Search → Right-click → Uninstall` | Studio open, project closed | `.nupkg` deleted from local cache |
| 4 | Delete Project Dependency | `Project panel → Dependencies → Right-click → Remove` | Project.json accessible | Reference removed from JSON |
| 5 | Delete Folder Package | `Folder → Packages → Actions → Delete` | Folder Admin role | Package removed from folder scope |
| 6 | Unpublish (≠ Delete) | `Tenant → Packages → Unpublish` | Admin role | Hidden from feed but retained in DB |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Trap:** Students often confuse **Unpublish** with **Delete**. **Unpublishing** keeps the package in the database but hides it from the feed, while **Deleting** permanently removes it from the active feed.

## 🏭 Real-World RPA Engineering Utility

In a real **Banking BPO RPA deployment** (a common KTU case study):
- A developer publishes `InvoiceProcessing_v1.0.0.nupkg` to Orchestrator.
- After 6 months, a critical bug is found in v1.0.0.
- The admin **deletes v1.0.0** and v1.0.1 from Orchestrator.
- All unattended robots referencing these versions are flagged during the next health check.
- Developers are forced to upgrade to v2.0.0, which contains the fix.

This **forced-upgrade mechanism** is precisely why deletion is a critical governance tool in enterprise RPA.

# 3. Step-by-Step Process Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🪜 Derivation A: Deleting a Package from UiPath Orchestrator (Detailed Walkthrough)

This is the **most frequently tested KTU question pattern**. The full logical derivation is:

> **Step 1 — Authentication & Authorization Check**
>
> The system verifies that the logged-in user holds the **Tenant Administrator** or **Folder Administrator** role. Without this role, the Delete button remains disabled (grayed out) in the UI.

$$\text{Role}_{\text{required}} \in \{\text{Tenant Admin}, \text{Folder Admin}, \text{Robots Admin}\}$$

> **Step 2 — Navigation to Package Repository**
>
> In the left-hand navigation pane of Orchestrator, the user clicks `Tenant` → `Packages` (for tenant-level packages) or `Folder` → `Packages` (for folder-scoped packages). This loads the **package list grid** with columns: Name, Version, Published Date, Source, Actions.

> **Step 3 — Package Version Selection**
>
> Each published package can have **multiple versions**. The user must select the **specific version** they wish to delete. Selecting the parent package name will not delete child versions — this is a critical KTU nuance.

$$\text{Selection}_{\text{scope}} = \text{Version}_{\text{specific}}, \quad \text{NOT } \text{Package}_{\text{parent}}$$

> **Step 4 — Triggering the Delete Action**
>
> The user clicks the **trash icon (🗑️)** in the Actions column, or right-clicks and selects `Delete` from the context menu. This opens a **confirmation modal dialog**.

> **Step 5 — Confirmation & Cascade Check**
>
> Orchestrator performs a **dependency check**:
> - If any **process** is currently deployed and references this version → a **warning** is shown: *"X processes are using this version. Deletion may break them."*
> - The user must type the **package name** or click `Confirm` to proceed.

> **Step 6 — Execution & Audit Log Entry**
>
> Upon confirmation, Orchestrator executes a `DELETE` API call:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HTTP Method} &= \text{DELETE} \\
\text{Endpoint} &= \text{/odata/Processes(\{ProcessId\})} \\
\text{Headers} &= \{\text{Authorization: Bearer } \text{Token}\} \\
\text{Response} &= \text{204 No Content (Success)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step 7 — Post-Deletion State**
>
> The package version is removed from the active feed. The audit log records: *User, Timestamp, Package Name, Version, Action="Delete"*. Robots already running with this version are unaffected until the next redeployment.

---

## 🪜 Derivation B: Deleting a Package from UiPath Studio (Local Feed)

> **Step 1 — Open UiPath Studio** and load any project.
>
> **Step 2 — Click the `Manage Packages` icon** in the ribbon (📦 icon) or use `Ctrl+P` shortcut.
>
> **Step 3 — In the Package Manager window**, navigate to:
> - `Project Dependencies` tab — to remove from current project.
> - `All Packages` or `Local` tab — to uninstall from local feed.
>
> **Step 4 — Search for the target package** using the search bar.
>
> **Step 5 — Click the package** to expand its versions.
>
> **Step 6 — For Project Dependency removal:**
> - Click the package → `Uninstall` button → confirm.
>
> **Step 7 — For Local Feed uninstallation:**
> - Right-click the version → `Uninstall` from context menu.

The underlying file system operation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{File Path}_{\text{Local Cache}} &= \%\text{LOCALAPPDATA}\%\textbackslash\text{UiPath}\textbackslash\text{Packages} \\
\text{File Extension} &= .\text{nupkg} \\
\text{Action} &= \text{DeleteFile}(\text{path}) \rightarrow \text{Update project.json}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 📊 Analytical Matrix: Deletion vs Unpublish vs Uninstall

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This comparison table is a **favourite 7-mark KTU question**. Memorize the distinctions.

| Attribute | **Delete** | **Unpublish** | **Uninstall** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Orchestrator / Local | Orchestrator only | Studio / Robot only |
| **Reversible?** | ❌ No (UI level) | ✅ Yes (Republish) | ✅ Yes (Reinstall) |
| **Affects Running Robots?** | ⚠️ Only on redeploy | ⚠️ Only on redeploy | ❌ Immediate |
| **Removes from Feed?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (hidden) | ❌ No |
| **Removes from DB?** | ✅ Yes (active) | ❌ No (retained) | ❌ No |
| **Permission Required** | Admin | Publisher/Admin | Local User |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 📊 Mermaid Diagram 1: Package Deletion Workflow in UiPath Orchestrator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Admin Logs into Orchestrator] --> B{Role Check}
    B -- Not Admin --> C[Access Denied - 403 Forbidden]
    B -- Admin --> D[Navigate to Tenant or Folder]
    D --> E[Click Packages Section]
    E --> F[Package List Grid Loads]
    F --> G[Select Specific Version]
    G --> H[Click Delete Icon]
    H --> I{Dependency Check}
    I -- Has Dependencies --> J[Warning Modal Shown]
    I -- No Dependencies --> K[Confirmation Dialog]
    J --> K
    K --> L{Admin Confirms?}
    L -- No --> M[Operation Cancelled]
    L -- Yes --> N[Send DELETE API Call]
    N --> O[Orchestrator Removes .nupkg]
    O --> P[Audit Log Entry Created]
    P --> Q[Package Removed from Feed]
    Q --> R[Deployed Robots Flagged on Next Health Check]
```

## 📊 Mermaid Diagram 2: Local Studio Package Deletion (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Studio[UiPath Studio]
        S1[Developer Opens Studio] --> S2[Click Manage Packages Icon]
        S2 --> S3[Package Manager Window Opens]
        S3 --> S4[Search Target Package]
        S4 --> S5{Package Found?}
        S5 -- No --> S6[Show Not Found Message]
        S5 -- Yes --> S7[Display Version List]
        S7 --> S8[Click Uninstall Button]
        S8 --> S9[Update project.json]
        S9 --> S10[Delete .nupkg from Local Cache]
    end
    
    subgraph FS[File System]
        F1[Packages Folder] --> F2[Remove .nupkg File]
        F2 --> F3[Update Dependencies Registry]
    end
    
    S10 --> F1
    F3 --> Z[Operation Complete - Build May Fail if Activities Used]
```

## 📊 Mermaid Diagram 3: Package Lifecycle Showing Delete Position

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Created: Developer builds workflow
    Created --> Published: Publish to Orchestrator
    Published --> Deployed: Assign to Robot
    Deployed --> Running: Execution triggered
    Running --> Deployed: Job completes
    Deployed --> Published: Undeploy
    Published --> Deleted: Admin deletes version
    Published --> Unpublished: Admin unpublishes
    Unpublished --> Published: Republish
    Deleted --> [*]
    Running --> Stopped: Manual stop
    Stopped --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU Exams:** The state diagram above shows that **Deleted** is a **terminal state** — once a package is deleted, it cannot return to `Published` unless re-uploaded. This is a critical exam distinction from `Unpublished`, which can transition back to `Published`.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a package in UiPath. List any two scenarios where a package needs to be deleted from the Orchestrator.**

**Model Answer:**
A package in UiPath is a compiled, versioned file (with `.nupkg` extension) that contains reusable workflows, activities, and project components, which can be published to the Orchestrator and distributed to robots for execution.

**Two scenarios for deletion:**
1. **Security Vulnerability:** When a published package version contains exposed credentials or vulnerable code, it must be deleted to prevent further exploitation.
2. **Version Obsolescence:** When a newer stable version (e.g., v2.0.0) replaces an older one (e.g., v1.0.0), the older version is deleted to force robots to upgrade and prevent accidental deployment of the buggy version.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between "Unpublish" and "Delete" operations for a UiPath package.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Unpublish | Delete |
|---|---|---|
| Reversibility | Reversible (can republish) | Irreversible (UI level) |
| Database Retention | Retained in database | Removed from active database |
| Use Case | Temporary hide | Permanent removal |
| Audit Trail | Marked as unpublished | Marked as deleted permanently |

**Conclusion:** Unpublish is a *soft-hide* operation suitable for temporary version management, while Delete is a *hard-removal* operation used for security incidents, storage cleanup, and end-of-life version retirement.

---

## 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure to delete a package version from the UiPath Orchestrator. List the pre-conditions and post-conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the various contexts in which a package can be deleted in the UiPath ecosystem with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

### ✅ Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) — Step-by-Step Deletion from Orchestrator:

> **Pre-Conditions:**
> 1. The user must be logged in as **Tenant Administrator** or **Folder Administrator**.
> 2. The package version must be **published** to the Orchestrator tenant or folder.
> 3. The user must have **read access** to the audit log to verify deletion.

> **Procedure:**
> **Step 1:** Open the UiPath Orchestrator in a web browser and log in with admin credentials.
>
> **Step 2:** From the left navigation panel, click `Tenant` → `Packages` (or `Folder` → `Packages` for folder-scoped deletion).
>
> **Step 3:** The Packages grid displays all published packages with their versions. Locate the target package.
>
> **Step 4:** Click the **trash icon (🗑️)** in the Actions column corresponding to the specific version.
>
> **Step 5:** A confirmation dialog appears. If processes are currently using this version, a warning is shown listing the affected processes and robot count.
>
> **Step 6:** Click `Confirm` or `Delete` to proceed. The package version is removed from the feed.

> **Post-Conditions:**
> 1. The package version is **no longer visible** in the Orchestrator package feed.
> 2. Any **new process deployment** that references this version will **fail with a "Package Not Found" error**.
> 3. An **audit log entry** is created with user, timestamp, and action details.
> 4. **Already running jobs** are unaffected; only new deployments are blocked.

#### Part (b) — Various Deletion Contexts:

> **Context 1: Tenant-Level Package Deletion**
> Packages published to the tenant feed are accessible across all folders. Deletion here removes the package from the **entire organization**. *Example:* A global `CommonUtils` library used across all departments is deleted due to a critical security flaw.

> **Context 2: Folder-Level Package Deletion**
> Packages scoped to a specific folder are deleted via `Folder → Packages → Delete`. *Example:* Deleting an `HR_Onboarding` package from the HR folder after project migration.

> **Context 3: Local Studio Package Deletion**
> Developers can uninstall packages from their local UiPath Studio cache at `%localappdata%\UiPath\Packages`. *Example:* Removing a trial version of a custom activity to free local disk space.

> **Context 4: Project Dependency Removal**
> Removing a package reference from a project's `project.json` file via Studio. *Example:* Refactoring a project to no longer use the `UiPath.Mail` package after switching to SMTP-based custom activities.

> **Context 5: Programmatic Deletion via REST API**
> Using the Orchestrator API with a `DELETE` request to remove packages in bulk. *Example:* Automating the cleanup of old package versions across 50 tenants via a PowerShell script.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the lifecycle of a UiPath package and highlight the role of the Delete operation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the security implications of deleting a package from Orchestrator. What are the best practices to follow before deletion? **(7 marks)**

### ✅ Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) — Package Lifecycle with Delete Operation:

**Lifecycle Stages:**

$$\text{Create} \rightarrow \text{Develop} \rightarrow \text{Test} \rightarrow \text{Publish} \rightarrow \text{Deploy} \rightarrow \text{Execute} \rightarrow \text{Monitor} \rightarrow \text{Retire} \rightarrow \text{Delete}$$

| Stage | Description |
|---|---|
| **Create** | Developer creates a new project in UiPath Studio. |
| **Develop** | Workflows, variables, and activities are added. |
| **Test** | Debugger and Test Suite validate the logic. |
| **Publish** | Project is compiled to a `.nupkg` file and uploaded to Orchestrator. |
| **Deploy** | Package is assigned to a robot and a process is created. |
| **Execute** | Job runs on attended or unattended robot. |
| **Monitor** | Logs, queues, and SLAs are tracked. |
| **Retire** | Package is undeployed from robots. |
| **Delete** | Package version is permanently removed from the feed. |

> **Role of Delete:** Deletion is the **terminal stage** of the lifecycle. It enforces **governance**, **storage hygiene**, and **security compliance**. Once deleted, the package cannot be deployed again unless re-published.

#### Part (b) — Security Implications & Best Practices:

> **Security Implications:**
> 1. **Loss of Business Logic:** Deleting a critical package can break downstream processes, causing production failures.
> 2. **Audit Compliance Gaps:** Permanent deletion may violate retention policies (e.g., SOX, GDPR) that require historical artifact storage.
> 3. **Forced Upgrade Risk:** If a process depends on the deleted version, robots will fail, potentially impacting SLAs.
> 4. **Privilege Escalation Concern:** Any user with delete rights can disrupt the entire RPA infrastructure — least-privilege principle is critical.

> **Best Practices Before Deletion:**
> 1. ✅ **Audit Dependencies:** Use the Orchestrator's "View Dependencies" feature to identify all processes and robots using the package version.
> 2. ✅ **Communicate Stakeholders:** Inform the development and operations teams before deletion.
> 3. ✅ **Backup the .nupkg File:** Store the package in an external repository (e.g., Azure Artifacts) before deletion.
> 4. ✅ **Unpublish First:** Use Unpublish as a soft-hide before committing to permanent Delete.
> 5. ✅ **Schedule During Maintenance Window:** Perform deletion during low-traffic hours.
> 6. ✅ **Use Role-Based Access Control (RBAC):** Restrict delete permissions to Tenant Admins only.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Delete with Uninstall:** Delete removes from the *feed*; Uninstall removes from the *robot*. These are NOT the same. Examiners deduct 2-3 marks if confused.
> 2. **Forgetting to Mention Role-Based Access:** Deletion requires admin privileges. Always state the pre-condition.
> 3. **Skipping Audit Log:** KTU examiners award marks for mentioning audit trails and post-conditions.
> 4. **Not Mentioning the .nupkg File Format:** Always specify the file extension when discussing packages.
> 5. **Ignoring Folder vs Tenant Scope:** Deletion can be at tenant or folder level — both must be discussed for full marks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- 📦 A **package** is a `.nupkg` file containing reusable UiPath workflows and activities.
- 🗑️ **Deleting** a package **permanently removes** it from the Orchestrator feed — it is **irreversible from the UI**.
- 🔐 Only **Tenant Admins** or **Folder Admins** can delete packages.
- ⚠️ Deleting a package **does not stop** currently running jobs — only **future deployments** are blocked.
- 🔄 **Unpublish ≠ Delete:** Unpublish hides the package (reversible); Delete removes it (irreversible).
- 🖥️ **Local Studio deletion** affects only the developer's `%localappdata%\UiPath\Packages` folder.
- 🌐 **REST API deletion** uses `DELETE /odata/Processes` or `DELETE /odata/Feeds` with a Bearer Token.
- 🧾 Every deletion is **logged in the Audit Log** with user, timestamp, and action.
- 🧪 Best practice: **Unpublish first**, then **Backup**, then **Delete** during a maintenance window.
- 📋 Always **audit dependencies** before deletion to avoid breaking live processes.
- 🏢 **Tenant-level deletion** affects all folders; **Folder-level deletion** is scoped to one folder.
- 🔢 Select the **specific version** to delete — selecting the parent package does NOT delete child versions.
- 🧩 Deleting a project dependency updates the `project.json` file and may cause **build errors** if activities are still referenced.
- 🎯 **KTU Favourite Question Pattern:** "Differentiate between Unpublish and Delete" — always present in tabular form for full marks.
- 🛡️ **Security First:** Never hardcode credentials in packages; delete vulnerable versions immediately upon discovery.
